comment: Deleting every non-"system" entry from `sys.modules` before each zip import is very risky: this can evict already-loaded third-party libraries or shared application modules that other parts of the scheduler/webserver still hold references to, leaving the process in a partially inconsistent state. It also changes behavior far beyond the packaged DAG being loaded. Can we narrow the cleanup to modules actually loaded from this zip (or from the DAG folder) instead of globally purging `sys.modules` based on `_is_system_module()`?

This branch removes any module that `_is_system_module()` does not recognize as platform-owned, but `_is_system_module()` relies on path-prefix heuristics and a small allowlist. That means site-packages installed outside the original `sys.path`, namespace packages, editable installs, or modules with unusual `__spec__.origin` values may be incorrectly deleted. Given the issue being fixed, this broad purge seems likely to introduce hard-to-debug regressions; consider tracking/restoring only modules imported during this load instead.

Using the import-time snapshot of `sys.path` as the definition of "system" can misclassify legitimate runtime-added import locations (plugins, virtualenv/sitecustomize changes, test harnesses, etc.) as non-system and cause their modules to be purged. Since this code runs in long-lived processes, the environment may evolve after module import. A safer approach would be to reason from the modules loaded during this zip import rather than from a stale global path snapshot.

This test calls `_test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_system_inner(...)`, but the test name/docstring describe the `external` scenario. That means the `AFTER` variant for the external case is never actually exercised, and this test duplicates coverage of the system-path case instead. Please call `_test_dag_zip_flat_dependency_overrides_external_inner(DependencyPosition.AFTER)` here.

This helper is missing a docstring opening/closing delimiter, so the following line is just a bare string expression rather than a function docstring. That makes the function body harder to read and is likely an accidental edit. Please wrap the description in triple quotes or remove it.

This migration is supposed to address the `dag_code.source_code` size limit, but the code here is altering `variable.val` instead. That looks inconsistent with both the issue and PR description, and would leave the original problem unresolved while changing an unrelated table. Please verify the target table/column and update the migration accordingly.

Downgrading `source_code` back to `TEXT` can fail or truncate data if any rows exceed the 64KB limit after this migration has been applied. It would be safer to either make the downgrade explicitly non-reversible for MySQL, or add a guard/check that prevents the alter when oversized values exist and raises a clear error message.

Switching from `UnicodeText` to generic `Text` changes the SQLAlchemy type semantics, not just the MySQL storage size. On several backends/dialects this can affect Unicode handling and, unless the migration explicitly uses a MySQL-specific type override, it may also alter the column type outside MySQL where the issue does not apply. Consider keeping the model as `UnicodeText` and handling the `MEDIUMTEXT` change in the MySQL migration/dialect-specific compilation instead, so we increase capacity without weakening cross-database Unicode guarantees.

This class-level import/version check makes the rule depend on importing the installed `airflow` package at module import time. That can make the upgrade-check tool fail or behave inconsistently in environments where `airflow` isn't importable yet, or where the package version differs from the code being executed. It would be safer to obtain the version through an existing utility/config path used by the upgrade checker, or defer this logic until runtime in a method where failures can be handled explicitly.

This changelog entry rewrites the historical example env var from `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN_CMD` to `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN_CMD`. Since this is an old release note for #6801, changing it can make the changelog inaccurate for users looking up behavior in that version. Consider preserving the original text and, if needed, documenting the new section move in a new changelog or upgrade note instead.

This sentence is a bit unclear/grammatically off (`when reading new option`). Since this is upgrade guidance, it would help to explicitly say that Airflow now reads these settings from `[database]`, but will fall back to the legacy `[core]` keys with a deprecation warning for backward compatibility. That makes the migration path much easier to understand.

This switches the lookup to `[database]` only, which may break `airflow info` for users who still have `sql_alchemy_conn` configured under the legacy `[core]` section during the deprecation window. Since this PR is moving config keys rather than removing old ones outright, consider using the same compatibility path as the rest of the config system (e.g. a deprecated alias or an explicit fallback to `core`) so the command continues to report the DB URL correctly for existing installations.

The default value appears to have an accidental trailing `2` (`"utf-8"2`), which will make this YAML entry invalid and likely break config template generation/parsing. It should probably remain `"utf-8"`.

Since this change moves `sql_alchemy_conn` from `[core]` to `[database]`, please make sure backward compatibility is preserved for existing `airflow.cfg` files and environment variables. In particular, users may still have `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` set, so the config layer/docs should either keep reading the old key as a deprecated alias or clearly document the migration path; otherwise this default entry in the new section can be misleading because older deployments may silently stop picking up their configured DB URL.

This direct lookup now only checks the new `[database]` section. During the deprecation window, users may still have `sql_alchemy_conn` defined only in `[core]`, and other parts of this class already rely on `deprecated_options` to transparently resolve old locations. If `self.get()` here does not consult deprecated mappings in all call paths, this validation could fail or behave inconsistently for legacy configs. Consider using the same compatibility path explicitly (or adding a regression test) to ensure `[core] sql_alchemy_conn` still works until the deprecation is removed.

This example now only advertises the new `[database]` env var name. Since this change is a config-section migration, users upgrading existing pod templates may still be relying on `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`. If backward compatibility is still supported elsewhere in this PR, consider documenting that here (or keeping a note in the example comments/docs) so the example doesn't imply the old name stopped working immediately.

Switching the env var here is correct for the new section, but this template is user-facing and often copied verbatim. If backward compatibility still relies on `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` being accepted, consider either documenting that this template now requires the new name or temporarily including both env vars to avoid breaking existing Kubernetes secrets/manifests during upgrades.

This direct section switch can break existing deployments that still define `sql_alchemy_schema` under `[core]`. Since this PR is moving config keys, it would be safer to preserve backward compatibility here (for example, read from `[database]` first and fall back to `[core]`, ideally with a deprecation warning) until the old location is fully removed.

Reading only from `[database]` here may cause `sql_engine_collation_for_ids` to silently stop working for users who haven't migrated their config yet. Consider keeping a fallback to the old `[core]` section during the deprecation window so upgrades remain non-breaking.

This change removes compatibility with the legacy `[core] sql_alchemy_conn` setting. Because this value is used to infer MySQL/MariaDB-specific defaults, falling back to the old section is important to avoid behavior changes during upgrade for installations that haven't moved their config yet.

This switches the primary DB connection lookup to `[database]` only. Since this is a config-section migration, removing the old `[core]` lookup here can break existing installations unless `conf.get()` already transparently maps deprecated section/option names. Please confirm there is a compatibility layer for `core.sql_alchemy_conn` (including env var aliases like `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`) or add an explicit fallback/deprecation path here.

Same backward-compatibility concern applies to imported connect args: this now ignores the legacy `[core] sql_alchemy_connect_args` unless the config layer remaps deprecated keys. If that remapping is not already in place, consider checking the old location as a fallback and emitting a deprecation warning.

This reads `sql_alchemy_engine_args` from `[database]`, but that option is not listed in the issue/PR description among the settings being moved. If the templates/docs/deprecation mapping were only updated for the enumerated options, this change will create an undocumented config move. Please either keep this under `[core]` for now or make sure the new section, docs, and compatibility aliases include `sql_alchemy_engine_args` as well.

This switches `load_default_connections` to the new `[database]` section, but `initdb()` is part of the upgrade path and may run against existing installations that still only have `[core] load_default_connections` configured. Unless `conf.getboolean()` already provides a deprecation fallback for moved options, this can silently change behavior and skip creating default connections after upgrade. Please ensure this read goes through a compatibility layer (or explicitly falls back to `[core]`) until the old key is fully deprecated.

This switches the lookup to `[database]`, but it also drops compatibility with existing configs that still define `max_db_retries` under `[core]`. Since this value is read at import time, older installations may silently fall back to `3` instead of honoring the user’s configured value. Please consider using the deprecation-aware config access (or an explicit fallback to `core.max_db_retries`) so the migration preserves existing behavior.

This direct section switch can break compatibility for environments that still have `sql_alchemy_conn` only under `[core]`. Since this PR is moving config keys between sections, it would be safer to read via the same deprecation/alias mechanism used elsewhere (or fall back to `[core]`) so imports of this module don't fail before users migrate their config.

Similar to the change above, this now assumes `sql_alchemy_conn` exists in `[database]` immediately. If backward compatibility is expected for old configs, consider using a helper that honors deprecated locations or falls back to `[core]`; otherwise JSON support checks may start raising config errors during startup.

This direct section switch can break existing deployments that still define `sql_alchemy_conn` under `[core]`. Since this PR is moving config keys rather than introducing a brand-new setting, it would be safer to preserve backward compatibility here by reading `[database]` first and falling back to `[core]` (or using the config deprecation/renamed-option mechanism if one exists elsewhere in the codebase). Otherwise the web app may fail to start until users manually migrate their config.

Switching the built-in secret env var gate from `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` to only `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` can break existing Helm values files during upgrades. Users who still set `enableBuiltInSecretEnvVars.AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` will silently stop getting the metadata DB env var injected. Consider supporting both keys here for a deprecation period (e.g. `or` the old and new flags) to preserve backward compatibility while the config section migration rolls out.

Renaming this schema key to only `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` may break existing Helm values that still use `enableBuiltInSecretEnvVars.AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`. Since this PR is moving config sections, it would be safer to keep the old key as a deprecated alias (or otherwise document and handle the migration) so chart users don't lose the env var injection unexpectedly during upgrade.

Switching the built-in secret env var to `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` is correct for the new section, but this is a breaking change for existing Helm values that override `enableBuiltInSecretEnvVars.AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`. Unless the chart has compatibility logic elsewhere, those user overrides will silently stop applying after upgrade. Consider keeping the old key as a deprecated alias for one release or documenting this explicitly in the chart upgrade notes.

This update changes the documented env var to the new `[database]` section, but users upgrading may still rely on the legacy `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` alias for backward compatibility. If the code still supports the old name, it would be helpful to mention that here as a deprecated/legacy alternative to avoid making the docs look like the old variable stopped working immediately.

There is a typo in the environment variable name here: `AIRFLOW__DATABE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` is missing the `AS` in `DATABASE`. As written, users copying this value will end up with a non-functional configuration. Please change it to `AIRFLOW__DATABASE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`.

The section name in this CLI example appears to be misspelled as `databse`. This should match the new config section name `database`, otherwise the documented command will fail for users trying to verify the setting.

This example now only shows the new `[database]` env var name. Since this PR is moving a long-standing config key, it would be helpful to mention whether `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` is still accepted as a deprecated alias for backward compatibility. Otherwise readers upgrading existing deployments may assume the old variable stops working immediately and miss the migration path.

This updates the example to the new `[database]` section, but it also changes the env var name used by existing docker-compose setups. Since this file is a copy-paste entry point for many users, consider whether we should keep a temporary comment or compatibility note nearby (or in the surrounding docs) explaining that `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` was renamed, otherwise upgrades may fail silently for users following older examples.

This updates the env var reference to the new `[database]` section, but users upgrading from older versions may still rely on `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`. If backward compatibility or deprecation support still exists, it would be helpful to mention the old variable here as an alias/deprecated name to avoid confusion during upgrades.

This update reflects the new `[database]` section, but the Helm chart may still support the legacy `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN` env var for backward compatibility during upgrades. If that fallback still exists, documenting only the new variable could mislead users migrating existing deployments. Consider mentioning both variables here, or explicitly noting that the old one is no longer supported by the chart.

This updates the env var to the new `[database]` section, but please double-check backward-compatibility in the config loader for CI/bootstrap paths. If any scripts or images used by this compose file still read only `AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN`, this change could break MySQL-based CI before the deprecation alias is applied. It may be worth confirming that both section names are accepted during the transition or documenting why this file is safe to switch now.

This expectation was updated to the new `[database]` section, but the chart still exposes `AIRFLOW_CONN_AIRFLOW_DB` on the next line. Since Airflow supports both the config key and the connection-based override for the metadata DB, it would be good to verify that the Helm chart templates and any built-in secret env var mappings were updated consistently so we don't end up with mixed `CORE`/`DATABASE` names or duplicate/inconsistent behavior during upgrades.

These tests now only exercise the new `[database]` location. Since this change is a config-section migration and Airflow typically preserves backward compatibility for renamed/moved options, it would be good to add coverage that `sql_alchemy_conn` is still resolved correctly from the legacy `[core]` section as well (or explicitly assert that the old location is no longer supported). Otherwise we may miss a regression in the deprecation/alias path.

This changes the DAG-level `latest` restriction to ignore task `end_date`s unless *all* tasks define one, which fixes the reported bug but also changes behavior for mixed DAGs in a way that may be too broad. In particular, a single task without `end_date` now makes every other task's `end_date` irrelevant to DAG scheduling. Please add/verify tests for mixed cases (some tasks bounded, some unbounded, with and without `dag.end_date`) to ensure this matches the intended scheduler semantics and doesn't regress existing expectations.

The `len(end_dates) == len(self.tasks)` check is a bit fragile and the inline comment is hard to read. It also relies on `self.tasks` not changing between the list comprehension and this comparison. Consider expressing the intent directly with something like `all(t.end_date is not None for t in self.tasks)` and updating the comment/docstring to clarify why DAG `latest` should only be derived from task end dates when every task is bounded.

This changes the task eligibility check for normal scheduled runs, but `self.is_backfill` still bypasses both `start_date` and `end_date`. That means backfill runs can still create task instances outside a task's declared `end_date`, which seems inconsistent with the issue being fixed. If task-level `end_date` is meant to be respected universally, consider applying the `end_date` constraint to backfills as well, or add a test/documentation clarifying that backfill intentionally ignores it.

This test only covers cases where all task-level `end_date`s are either bounded before the DAG restriction or one task is unbounded. Since the bug is specifically about a single task-level `end_date` incorrectly stopping the whole DAG, it would be valuable to add an assertion for the mixed case from the issue report (e.g. one task with `end_date`, another without) and verify that `dag._time_restriction.latest` remains `None` when the DAG itself has no `end_date`. Without that scenario, the regression described in the issue is not directly exercised.

Updating the backfill expectation from 2 to 3 changes the test semantics significantly: for a backfill run at `DEFAULT_DATE`, a task whose `end_date` is `DEFAULT_DATE - 1 day` would normally be out of range and should not get a TI. If the production fix is intended to stop task-level `end_date` from truncating the whole DAG, this test should also assert that only the unaffected tasks are created, rather than expecting all three tasks for backfill. Otherwise the test may encode behavior that ignores `end_date` filtering entirely.

Using `timezone.utcnow()` here makes the test time-dependent and can introduce flakiness, especially if `create_task_instance` or related assertions compare `execution_date`/logical date against a separately-evaluated `utcnow()`. Since this is a unit test, it would be safer to derive both values from a single fixed timestamp (or capture `now = timezone.utcnow()` once and reuse it) so the test remains deterministic while still avoiding the old `DEFAULT_DATE` issue.

This change replaces a fixed test date with a dynamic current time, which can make the test non-deterministic. If the goal is only to ensure `end_date` is after the task instance's date, consider capturing a single `now` value and using it consistently throughout the test, or using a fixed aware datetime, to avoid subtle timing-related failures.

Switching to `timezone.utcnow()` here may hide regressions behind wall-clock behavior and make the test harder to reproduce. A deterministic timestamp (or a single shared `now` captured once) would keep the test stable while still ensuring `end_date` is in the future relative to the task instance.

Passing `self.extra_args` through to `Object.load()` can break existing callers because `extra_args` on `S3Hook` is also used for transfer/download options, and not all of those keys are valid for `HeadObject`. For example, options like `ServerSideEncryption` or `StorageClass` are accepted by upload/copy APIs but will cause parameter validation errors here. It would be safer to filter `self.extra_args` to only the subset supported by `head_object`/`load()` (e.g. SSE-C, RequestPayer, ExpectedBucketOwner, VersionId, etc.) before forwarding them.

Converting any aware datetime to UTC here fixes the reported bug, but it also silently broadens the trigger's accepted input contract. Previously this constructor rejected non-Pendulum/UTC tzinfo values; now it will accept any aware datetime that `convert_to_utc` can handle. Please add or update tests to cover at least: naive datetimes still raising, non-UTC aware datetimes being normalized correctly, and existing UTC/Pendulum inputs preserving behavior. That will help guard against regressions in trigger serialization/deserialization and async sensor behavior.

This assertion hard-codes the expected UTC wall-clock time (`01:00`) and only passes if `DEFAULT_TIMEZONE` is a fixed UTC+8 offset. The issue description mentions DAG-specific timezones (e.g. `Europe/Berlin`), so this test should derive the expected value from the DAG's timezone instead of relying on a global default. Otherwise the test can give false confidence and won't catch regressions for non-UTC+8 DAGs or DST-aware zones.

Adding `QUEUED` to the event buffer changes the contract of `BaseExecutor`: scheduler code consuming `get_event_buffer()` has historically handled terminal states (and in some cases `RUNNING`) from executors. Before introducing this helper, please verify all event-buffer consumers correctly process `TaskInstanceState.QUEUED` and won't regress by re-queuing or overwriting state unexpectedly during task adoption.

This lookup assumes every described ECS task ARN maps back to exactly one `TaskInstance` in `tis`, but `__describe_tasks()` can return a subset and may also omit/reshape entries for invalid or no-longer-running ARNs. Using `[...][0]` here will raise `IndexError` if the response contains an unexpected task or if the ARN matching fails, which would abort adoption for the whole batch. Consider building a dict keyed by `external_executor_id` and handling missing matches defensively.

Only the `tasks` portion of `describe_tasks` is processed here. ECS also returns a `failures` list for ARNs that could not be described (for example, tasks that have already stopped or no longer exist). Those TIs will silently fall through to `not_adopted_tis`, but without any logging/metrics it's hard to distinguish expected non-adoption from API issues. It would be helpful to inspect and log `failures` so operators can understand why adoption did not happen.

For newly launched tasks, `add_task()` is called with `attempt_number`, but adoption uses `ti.prev_attempted_tries`. Can you confirm these values have the same semantics? If `prev_attempted_tries` is off by one relative to the executor's internal attempt counter, adopted tasks may be tracked with a different retry/attempt number than tasks started by this executor instance.

This assertion is a bit too weak for the adoption behavior being added. `assert_called_once()` only verifies that `describe_tasks` was invoked, but not that it was called with the expected task ARNs (and ideally only the non-`None` `external_executor_id`s). If the implementation accidentally queried the wrong IDs or included `None`, this test would still pass. Consider asserting the exact call arguments to make the test validate the adoption contract more precisely.

This callback is invoked with the pre-update `dag_run` instance. Since `set_dag_run_state_to_success(..., commit=True, session=session)` persists the state change separately, listeners may observe stale in-memory fields (for example `state`, timestamps, or end date) unless the object is refreshed first. Consider reloading/refreshing `dag_run` from the session before emitting the event so listener implementations receive the final persisted state.

Same concern here: the listener is called with the existing `dag_run` object immediately after `set_dag_run_state_to_failed(...)`, but that helper may update the row without mutating this in-memory instance. Please refresh or re-query `dag_run` before notifying listeners to avoid emitting a failure event with stale run metadata.

This test only asserts the listener's final `state` list, which can miss regressions where the endpoint emits the wrong callback sequence or emits duplicate events. Since the issue is specifically about notifying listeners on manual/API state changes, it would be safer to also assert the exact callback behavior per transition (for example, that `queued` does not trigger success/failure callbacks, and that `success`/`failed` trigger exactly one corresponding event).

Switching the top-level branch from `AIRFLOW_V_2_10_PLUS` to `AIRFLOW_V_3_0_PLUS` changes which listener signatures are used for all 2.10.x test runs. That makes sense if 3.0 introduced the no-`session`/new hook API, but it also means this test helper now has two nearly identical class definitions that can drift over time. Since the only differences appear to be hook signatures, consider factoring the shared state/setup into a common base or adding a brief comment here explaining that 3.0 uses different listener method signatures. That will make future compatibility updates less error-prone.

`dag_id` looks like a required association for a backfill run per the issue description, but this column is created as nullable. Allowing NULL here would permit orphaned backfill records that cannot be tied to any DAG. Consider making this `nullable=False` (and likely adding a foreign key/index consistent with other DAG-linked tables if that is the intended model).

`is_paused` is modeled as nullable, which introduces a third state (`NULL`) in addition to paused/unpaused. For a scheduler control flag this usually leads to ambiguous behavior and extra null-handling in application code. If there isn't a meaningful tri-state here, make it non-null with an explicit server/default value.

This mapping table is intended to associate a backfill with DAG runs, but `dag_run_id` is nullable and there is no foreign key constraint to `dag_run.id`. That allows rows that don't actually reference a DAG run and weakens referential integrity. Unless placeholder rows are intentional, this should be `nullable=False` with a foreign key.

The unique constraint name is prefixed with `ix_`, which is typically reserved for indexes and can be confusing when inspecting schema or migration output. It would be clearer to use a `uq_`-style name (or let Alembic generate one via naming conventions) for this unique constraint.

This mapping table currently stores `backfill_id` as a plain integer without a foreign key. That means the database can't enforce referential integrity, and orphaned `BackfillDagRun` rows could be created if a backfill is deleted or inserted incorrectly. Since this model is explicitly a join/mapping table, it would be safer to declare a `ForeignKey` to `backfill.id` (and likely one for `dag_run_id` as well if it references `dag_run.id`).

Allowing `dag_run_id` to be nullable conflicts with the unique constraint below: most databases permit multiple `(backfill_id, NULL)` rows, so this won't actually prevent duplicate placeholder entries for the same backfill. If the intent is to track 'not created' cases, consider a separate status/reason column and keep `dag_run_id` non-null once a mapping exists, or adjust the constraint/indexing strategy to enforce the intended uniqueness.

This job is gated by `inputs.run-task-sdk-tests`, but that input is not declared under `workflow_call.inputs` in this workflow. As written, callers cannot pass it through the reusable workflow interface, and the condition may fail validation or always evaluate unexpectedly. Please add a `run-task-sdk-tests` input definition (likely a string/boolean, consistent with the other workflows) or remove this condition if the job should always run.

The matrix syntax here looks incorrect for GitHub Actions. `matrix.python-version` should be assigned an array value, but wrapping the expression in quotes turns it into a string. Typically this should be `python-version: ${{ fromJSON(inputs.python-versions) }}` so the matrix expands properly across versions.

This step is conditioned on `matrix.run-tests`, but the matrix defined above only contains `python-version`. Since `run-tests` is never populated, this condition will never be true (or may evaluate unexpectedly), so the actual test step won't run. Either add `run-tests` to the matrix/include entries or remove the condition.

Inside the matrix job, this environment variable is set from `inputs.default-python-version` rather than the current `matrix.python-version`. That means every matrix leg will prepare/run with the same Python version, defeating the purpose of the matrix. This should likely use `${{ matrix.python-version }}`.

This creates `./task_sdk/src/airflow/__init__.py`, but the comment and directory setup above indicate the placeholder is needed for the `airflow.sdk` package. If the goal is to make the editable install include the `airflow/sdk` tree, the file should likely be created under `./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/__init__.py` instead. As written, this turns `airflow` into a regular package in `task_sdk`, which can interfere with the intended namespace-package layout and may mask packaging issues.

This creates `./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/` but the placeholder file is written to `./task_sdk/src/airflow/__init__.py`. If the goal is to ensure the editable install picks up the `airflow.sdk` package, the sentinel file should likely be created under `airflow/sdk` instead; otherwise we may still end up without any file inside the actual package directory being installed.

The image target still points to `output_release-management_prepare-airflow-package.svg`, which doesn't match the new `prepare-task-sdk-package` command. This will send readers to the wrong asset/page. Please update the target to the Task SDK-specific image (or the correct raw URL) so the link and embedded image stay consistent.

This line references `clip-path="url(#breeze-release-management-line-41)"`, but the diff only adds clipPath definitions up to `breeze-release-management-line-40`. That leaves the closing line pointing at an undefined clip path, which can break rendering in some SVG viewers. Please add the missing `line-41` clipPath definition or reuse an existing valid one.

This generated SVG embeds font URLs from cdnjs. For repository-hosted documentation, external asset dependencies can break offline viewing, introduce CSP/privacy issues, and make docs rendering depend on a third-party service. If possible, generate the SVG without remote `@font-face` sources (or use a checked-in/local asset) so the documentation artifact is self-contained and reproducible.

This copies artifacts to a hard-coded `./dist` path instead of the shared `DIST_DIR` used elsewhere in this command. That makes the destination depend on the current working directory and can diverge from the rest of the release-management flow. Consider using `str(DIST_DIR)` here for consistency and to avoid copying files to the wrong location when the command is invoked from a different directory.

This new command is added to the release-management command groups, but I don't see any corresponding entry in this hunk for the actual command implementation or registration. If `prepare-task-sdk-package` is not wired up elsewhere, Breeze will expose it in help/completion but fail at runtime. Please make sure the command name here exactly matches a registered release-management subcommand.

This hard-codes the test discovery path to `task_sdk/tests`, but the issue/PR description says the new distribution should be `airflow-sdk` and live as a top-level folder within the Airflow package. If the actual project layout ends up using a different directory name (for example `airflow_sdk` to match Python package naming conventions), Breeze will silently discover no test packages. Consider deriving this from the canonical project/package path or at least aligning the directory name here with the final package layout.

The new mount path uses `task_sdk`, but the issue/PR description consistently refers to the new package as `airflow-sdk` / Task SDK. If the actual top-level source directory being added is `task_sdk`, this is fine; otherwise Breeze will mount a non-existent host path and the container won't see the new project. Please double-check that this matches the real repository directory name and any other Breeze/CI references.

The path matcher here is narrower than the rest of the new Task SDK wiring: it only includes `task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/*.py`, while the PR description says this project also adds packaging files (for example `pyproject.toml`) and likely other non-Python sources. With the current regex, changes to those files won't trigger `TASK_SDK_FILES` and `run_task_sdk_tests`, so CI can miss relevant validation for the new package. Consider matching the whole `task_sdk/` project (or at least include packaging/config files) instead of only Python sources under `src/airflow/sdk`.

Adding the new workspace package as a dev dependency at the repo root means it will be installed in all development environments, but this PR description says the package is only a skeleton for now. If `task_sdk` is not yet import-safe or has incomplete metadata/dependencies, this can break unrelated local setup and CI jobs. Consider whether this should be added only to the specific environments/jobs that need it until the package is functional.

This bind mount points to `../../../task_sdk`, but the issue/PR describe the new project as `airflow-sdk` and the package is expected to live as a top-level namespace within Airflow. Can we confirm the directory name here matches the actual project layout used elsewhere (build config, CI, Breeze, packaging)? If the source tree ends up being named differently, this local compose setup will silently diverge from CI/package behavior and make local development confusing.

Adding `-e ./task_sdk` here means the Kubernetes CI image will install the new package, but this file is still pinned to the Python 3.9 constraints set. Please verify that `task_sdk` is actually compatible with those constraints and with editable installs in this environment; otherwise Kubernetes CI may start failing even though the package is only a skeleton. If the package is not yet required by these jobs, consider deferring this addition until the CI image needs it.

This creates `./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/`, but the placeholder file is written to `./task_sdk/src/airflow/__init__.py` instead of inside the `sdk` package. If the goal is to ensure the editable install picks up the new package contents, the touched file should likely be `./task_sdk/src/airflow/sdk/__init__.py` to match the directory you just created and the package being installed.

The package name here doesn't match the issue/PR naming (`airflow-sdk` / `task-sdk`). If this distribution is intended to be the new SDK package for DAG-definition-level code, please confirm the final published name now, because changing it later is a breaking packaging change and can also affect CI/release automation, docs, and dependency references.

Packaging `src/airflow` from this project will produce a distribution that installs into the same top-level package as core Airflow. That can work for a namespace package, but only if the package layout is explicitly namespace-safe (e.g. no conflicting `airflow/__init__.py` semantics) and coordinated with the main package build. Otherwise this wheel can overwrite or conflict with the existing `airflow` package contents. Please verify the namespace packaging strategy here before publishing.

This placeholder API makes `airflow.sdk` export a `hello()` symbol, which is likely to become part of the package's public surface once published. Since this PR is only creating the skeleton package, consider avoiding a toy function in `__init__.py` and leaving the module empty (or only defining package metadata like `__all__`/`__version__`) to prevent accidental public API commitments and cleanup work in follow-up PRs.

`py.typed` is a PEP 561 marker file and should generally be an empty data file. Adding the ASF license header and comment text changes it from a pure marker into a non-empty text file, which can confuse tooling expectations and is inconsistent with typical packaging conventions. Consider keeping `py.typed` empty and relying on repository/package-level licensing instead.

Directly mutating `config.inicfg` can be brittle because it relies on pytest internals and may fail if the key is not present or if plugin initialization order changes. Since this test package is intended to be a standalone skeleton, consider setting this through a supported pytest option/marker mechanism or at least guarding the assignment (for example via `setdefault`) to avoid unexpected `KeyError`/compatibility issues.

This import path doesn't seem to match the issue/packaging goal. The issue describes creating an `airflow-sdk` distribution as a top-level namespace package within Airflow, but this test imports `airflow.sdk`. If the intended public package is actually `airflow_sdk` (or another top-level package), this test will lock in the wrong API surface and may pass/fail for the wrong reason. Please align the import here with the final package namespace exposed by the new distribution.

Adding `end_date` here changes the wire format for `TaskState`. Please make sure the supervisor-side deserialization/handling is updated to tolerate both older payloads without this field and newer ones with it, otherwise mixed-version task/supervisor processes could fail or silently ignore the timestamp.

This timeout check currently only applies when `_terminal_state == SUCCESS`, but the PR description says the overtime should apply to all terminal states, not just success. As written, tasks ending in other terminal states (for example `FAILED`/`SKIPPED`) will never trigger this cleanup path. Consider either broadening the condition to all relevant terminal states or renaming the feature/field to make the success-only behavior explicit.

Falling back to `now` when `_task_end_datetime` is missing makes `_overtime` evaluate to `0`, which silently disables overtime enforcement if the task process never sends `TaskState`. That could leave auxiliary/listener processes running indefinitely. It would be safer to guard explicitly for a missing end date and either log/terminate based on another signal or fail fast so this state mismatch is visible.

This new `SKIPPED` path only sets the state, while the success path also includes an `end_date`. Since the PR description mentions sending `end_date` from the task process to the supervisor, omitting it here can leave terminal skipped tasks without a completion timestamp and make overtime/state handling inconsistent across terminal states. Consider populating `end_date=datetime.now(tz=timezone.utc)` here as well (and similarly for other terminal states if applicable).

This expectation appears to conflict with the PR description: the new overtime behavior is said to apply to *all terminal states*, not just success. Keeping `SKIPPED` as a non-kill case may lock in the old `LocalTaskJob` semantics and let a regression slip through if the supervisor is intended to terminate overdue tasks after any terminal state. Please align this parametrized case (and the test name/log assertion) with the new behavior, or clarify in code/docs that overtime still only applies to `SUCCESS`.

This assertion hard-codes `TerminalTIState.SUCCESS`, but the PR description says the supervisor overtime/state reporting now applies to all terminal states. As written, this test only verifies the success path and won't catch regressions for failure/skip/etc. Consider either broadening this test to assert the emitted `TaskState` for multiple terminal outcomes or adding companion tests for the other terminal states so the new behavior is fully covered.

This change removes the explicit pre-check for an existing asset-triggered DagRun with the same `(dag_id, run_after)`, so the scheduler will now always attempt to create a new run for every entry in `asset_triggered_dag_info`. If the scheduler loop sees the same queued asset state again before cleanup/advancement, this can reintroduce duplicate-creation attempts and either hit a uniqueness constraint or create duplicate runs depending on DB guarantees. Please preserve the existence check (or handle `IntegrityError` and advance state safely) before calling `create_dagrun`.

The Calendar API expects RFC3339 timestamp strings for `timeMax`, `timeMin`, and `updatedMin`, but this method forwards raw `datetime` objects directly. Unlike some client libraries, `googleapiclient` does not reliably serialize Python `datetime` instances here, so this can fail at runtime or produce invalid requests. Please convert these values to RFC3339 strings (for example via `datetime.isoformat()` with timezone handling) before passing them to `.list()`.

Using `response["items"]` assumes the API always returns that key. For empty results or some error/edge cases, this may be absent and raise `KeyError`. Consider using `response.get("items", [])` to make pagination more robust.

The default value here should match the API's accepted enum values. Google Calendar `sendUpdates` expects values like `"all"`, `"none"`, or `"externalOnly"`; using the string `'false'` is not a documented value and may lead to request validation errors. Please change the default to `'none'` (or `None` and omit the parameter when unset).

`_create_dag_runs()` no longer returns the created objects, but the method body still just ends after `session.add_all(dag_runs)`. This changes the helper's contract from "build and return test data" to "persist as a side effect", which is inconsistent with the similarly named helpers elsewhere and makes the function implicitly return `None`. Please either add `return dag_runs` here (and in the batch variant below) or rename the helper to make the side effect explicit.

Same issue as the pagination helper above: this helper now persists records as a side effect but does not return the created `dag_runs`, so callers get `None`. Returning `dag_runs` would preserve the previous helper behavior and avoid surprising future test code.

Calling `process.exit(1)` here will terminate the process immediately and can bypass pending stdio writes or any cleanup scheduled in `finally`/`beforeExit` handlers. Since the reported bug is that the process can hang after an error, a safer pattern would be to set `process.exitCode = 1` and then explicitly stop any resources keeping the event loop alive (or rethrow to the top-level and let the process exit naturally once handles are closed). If you do keep `process.exit(1)`, it would be good to document why abrupt termination is required and verify that error output is always flushed.

This test setup injects `const tslib = require('tslib');`, but the synthetic file is loaded in isolation and may not have a real `tslib` module available in all test environments. Since `getConstructorParameters()` only needs the AST shape and not actual runtime execution, consider stubbing `tslib` within the test file contents (or using an inline helper consistently) to avoid introducing an external module dependency into what is otherwise a self-contained parser test.

This helper now supports several `__spread` forms, but it only covers the legacy `__spread` helper. TypeScript versions around this area also emit `__spreadArray`/`__spreadArrays` for downleveled spreads, so the regression test matrix may still miss real-world ES2015->ES5 output depending on the TS version used by package authors. Consider extending `mode` and the generated fixtures to include those helper variants as well, otherwise ngcc could still fail for packages compiled with a different tslib/TS combination.

The generated UMD wrapper in the `inlined_with_suffix` branch appears malformed: `factory(exports))` has an extra closing parenthesis compared to the other branches. Since this helper is used to build synthetic test files, the typo can make the fixture invalid JS and cause the test to fail for the wrong reason or hide parser-related issues. Consider aligning this wrapper with the `inlined` variant.

The new overload still returns `T | null` for any `InjectOptions`, even when `optional` is omitted. That means a call like `inject(Foo, {})` or `inject(Foo, {self: true})` will still be typed as nullable, which doesn't fully deliver the issue/PR goal of narrowing nullability when TypeScript can prove optional injection is not requested. Consider adding a non-null overload for `InjectOptions & { optional?: false | undefined }` (or equivalent) and reserving `T | null` for `optional: true`.

This overload only narrows to `T` for `optional?: false`, but it doesn't cover the common case where callers pass an object literal without `optional` at all (for example `{self: true}`). Such calls will match the broader `InjectOptions` overload below and still return `T | null`, which doesn't fully address the typing issue described in the bug. Consider adding a dedicated overload for options objects where `optional` is absent, e.g. `InjectOptions & {optional?: undefined}` (or a complementary non-optional type), so non-optional object-based calls also narrow to `T`.

Using `timezone.includes('/')` to detect IANA zones looks too narrow. Many valid IANA identifiers do contain `/`, but there are also valid names without it (for example `UTC`, `GMT`, `CET`, `Factory`, `Etc/GMT+1` mixes patterns, and aliases can vary). This heuristic can misclassify inputs and make behavior depend on naming shape rather than actual support. Consider feature-detecting by attempting to construct `Intl.DateTimeFormat` with the provided `timeZone` and falling back only if that throws, or otherwise using a more robust validator.

This `Intl.DateTimeFormat(...).formatToParts(...)` call can throw a `RangeError` for unsupported/invalid time zone names, and the subsequent non-null assertion assumes a `timeZoneName` part is always present. Right now an invalid `timezone` would fail with an unhandled exception here instead of falling back to the existing offset-based behavior. Please wrap this in a guarded path (try/catch and null check) so invalid or unsupported IANA names degrade predictably.

The new docs mention IANA names, but the rest of this parameter description still says "continental US timezone abbreviation", which can be misleading because many abbreviations are ambiguous and the new preferred path is IANA IDs. Consider tightening this JSDoc to clearly distinguish supported legacy abbreviations from recommended IANA names, and make sure the same wording is updated consistently in `formatDate`, `DATE_PIPE_DEFAULT_OPTIONS`, and any public API docs so the behavior is documented uniformly.

This test only covers successful formatting for a few IANA zones, but it doesn't exercise the failure/compatibility path for unsupported or invalid zone IDs. Since support here relies on `Intl` time zone handling and can vary by runtime/ICU data, it would be useful to add a companion assertion for an invalid IANA name (and possibly skip/guard if a zone is not supported) so the behavior is explicit and the test suite is less environment-sensitive.

This test is asserting exact `short`-format strings, which are locale- and ICU-data-sensitive. Since the implementation relies on `Intl` behavior for IANA zones, these expectations can become flaky across environments (different Node/ICU/browser versions may vary in punctuation, zero-padding, or even pattern selection). Consider forcing a known locale in the test setup or using a more stable format/assertion strategy so the test only verifies the timezone conversion behavior.

Registering a new anonymous `abort` listener for every event task fixes the shared-signal bug, but it also means we no longer remove that listener when the event listener is removed manually before the signal aborts. The previous implementation removed the task from `signal.tasks` during cancellation; with this change, the `AbortSignal` will retain a closure over `task` until it aborts, which can become a leak for long-lived signals. Consider storing the abort callback on the task/options and unregistering it in the cancel path.

This unconditionally instantiates `AbortController`, but the previous implementation explicitly handled environments where it is unavailable. In runtimes without `AbortController` support, `fetch()` will now throw immediately even when the caller did not pass a `signal`. Please preserve the old feature-detection/fallback behavior and only create a controller when the API exists (or when cancellation support is actually needed).

Using `{}` as the default here changes behavior for calls like `fetch(url, someNonObjectInit)` by forcing `args[1]` to become an object later. The previous code preserved `null`/missing init values. Consider only cloning/replacing `args[1]` when you actually need to inject a signal, so the wrapper stays as close as possible to native `fetch` argument handling.

This test creates and appends `button1` to `document.body` but never removes it. Since this suite mutates the real DOM, leaving the element attached can leak state into later tests and make failures order-dependent. Please clean up `button1` at the end of the spec (or use a `try/finally`).

The changelog fragment appears to include literal line-number prefixes (`1:` / `2:`) as part of the YAML content. That makes the fragment malformed for Ansible's changelog tooling, which expects top-level keys like `bugfixes:` without indentation or numeric prefixes. Please remove the embedded line numbers so the file is valid YAML.

This hard-codes the list of supported task keywords in the module docs, which is easy to let drift from the actual validation logic in `TaskInclude` over time. Since this PR changes runtime behavior for `notify`/`register`, a stale list here could become misleading quickly. Consider either referencing the canonical source of valid attributes or documenting only the unsupported keywords being fixed here instead of enumerating the full allowed set.

This validation computes `diff` against all keys in `ds`, which also includes internal/common task fields like `action` and potentially parser-added metadata. Since `VALID_IMPORT_KEYWORDS` does not include the import-specific args from `VALID_ARGS` (`file`, `_raw_params`, `apply`), valid `import_tasks` forms may now be flagged as invalid. Consider validating only user-supplied task keywords, or building the allowed set from `VALID_IMPORT_KEYWORDS.union(self.VALID_ARGS)` to avoid rejecting legitimate `import_tasks` syntax.

Using `elif` here means `always_child_state` is only cleared when `rescue_child_state` is absent. In block execution, `rescue` and `always` are distinct child states, and stale failure flags in `always_child_state` would survive whenever a rescue state exists. This looks inconsistent with the intent of recursively clearing all nested error state; consider making this a separate `if` so both child states are traversed.

This inline comment looks like a temporary note from preparing the patch rather than a maintainable code comment. It doesn't explain the underlying bug or why `now` must be initialized before zip creation. Please either remove it or replace it with a brief explanation of the actual reason (for example, that the timestamp is reused for zip entry metadata and must be captured before any potentially invalid date conversion/path).

Using the PEP 604 union operator inside a string literal can break on Python versions that still parse annotations without `|` support. Since this project has historically supported older Python versions in some branches, it would be safer to use `t.Optional["FinalQueue"]` (or enable postponed evaluation consistently) to avoid syntax-compatibility issues while still providing the type hint.

There appears to be a typo in the example column names: `neight_int_ip` doesn't match the earlier variable naming pattern and likely should be `neigh_int_ip` (or another consistent spelling). Since this is documentation, a misspelled fact name can easily mislead users copying the example verbatim.

The changelog fragment appears to include line-number text (`1:`, `2:`, `3:`) as part of the file contents. This will make the YAML invalid for antsibull/changelog tooling. Please remove the prefixed numbers so the file starts with plain `---` followed by `bugfixes:`.

The validation message now always lists `/` and `\\` as invalid characters, but this branch is compiled only on Windows and the actual check no longer rejects `/`. That means users can be told a character is invalid even when it is accepted, which is especially confusing now that path input is intentionally supported. Please keep the displayed `invalid_chars` list in sync with the characters actually rejected here.

The comment is misleading here. HPACK string literals are defined as raw octets, and `HPackEncoder` is not specifically encoding this value as Latin1; it is just emitting the byte sequence for the provided string. This matters because the test is intended to validate Kestrel's request-header decoding behavior, not the encoder's character-set choice. Consider rewording the comment to describe the actual byte on the wire (for example, that this test sends a header value containing byte `0xA3`) so the test remains accurate if the encoder implementation changes.

The new solution entry appears to have a typo in both the project name and the referenced `.csproj` path: `Comopnents` instead of `Components`. If the actual project file uses the correct spelling, this will break solution loading/builds; if the file was also created with the typo, it will introduce an inconsistent package/project name that should be corrected before merging.

The XML docs for `NoCache` are too generic and currently duplicate `Default`/`ForceCache`. In the browser Fetch API, `no-cache` still allows using a cached response but requires revalidation with the server before reuse. Since this is a public enum in a ref assembly, the summary should describe that behavior precisely to avoid misleading consumers.

The documentation for `ForceCache` appears inaccurate/incomplete. `force-cache` is not just 'looks for a matching request in its HTTP cache'; it prefers the cache and may return a stale cached response without contacting the network. Because this API is exposing browser semantics directly, the enum docs should match the Fetch spec terminology more closely.

Could we document or enforce the `same-origin` restriction more explicitly here? `only-if-cached` is only valid with request mode `same-origin`, and if the rest of this API doesn't expose request mode yet, consumers can easily select an enum value that the browser will reject at runtime. At minimum, the XML docs should call out the runtime failure behavior and the dependency on request mode.

The enum member name here doesn't match the browser Fetch API value (`no-cors`). Since this type appears to model browser request modes directly, `NoCors` can be confusing and may drift from the underlying string representation unless there is explicit mapping elsewhere. Consider documenting the exact mapping or aligning the naming/serialization behavior so consumers don't have to guess how this value is translated.

The XML doc here references a `streamingEnabled` parameter, but `EnableStreamingResponse` doesn't take any parameters. This will produce incorrect API docs and suggests the method shape may have changed without updating the comments. Please remove or update this `<param>` entry to match the actual signature.

Setting `Private="true"` on this `ProjectReference` will copy the referenced project's output into the build output, but this reference already has `ReferenceOutputAssembly="false"`, which means it is not being consumed as a normal assembly reference. If the goal here is only to bring in packaging/build metadata for the ref-assembly package, copying the implementation output may be unnecessary and could accidentally ship an extra assembly or create conflicts with the runtime-provided handler. Can we confirm whether this should instead remain non-private, or use a more targeted packaging mechanism?

Changing the assembly name is a breaking change for any existing consumers that compile against or load `WebAssembly.Net.Http` by identity. Since this package is described as a stop-gap ref assembly, can we confirm the implementation/runtime assembly will use the exact same identity (`System.Net.Http.WebAssemblyHttpHandler`), or add compatibility guidance/type forwarding if not? Otherwise consumers may hit binding/reference mismatches once the runtime package lands.

The previous ref assembly exposed `WasmHttpMessageHandler` members for configuring fetch behavior (`Cache`, `DefaultCredentials`, `Mode`, `StreamingEnabled`, etc.), but this replacement only leaves a parameterless `WebAssemblyHttpHandler` constructor. If this package is intended to resemble the current runtime surface until the runtime package is available, removing those members here creates a significant API mismatch and will break existing consumers at compile time. Consider either preserving the existing surface for compatibility or adding the new options-based API in this ref assembly before removing the old members.

The package now ships `System.Net.Http.WebAssemblyHttpHandler.dll`, but the nuspec/package name is still `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Comopnents.WebAssembly.HttpHandler`. That mismatch can be confusing for consumers and may also break any existing packaging/build logic that still expects `WebAssembly.Net.Http.dll`. Please verify the assembly rename is reflected consistently across the project (csproj/assembly name, ref assembly generation, and any pack targets) and consider aligning the package naming/metadata as well.

This switches from `GetFromJsonAsync` to a raw `SendAsync`/`ReadFromJsonAsync` flow, but it no longer validates the HTTP status code first. If the request fails, `ReadFromJsonAsync` may throw a less clear deserialization error or return unexpected results. Consider calling `response.EnsureSuccessStatusCode()` before reading the content to preserve the previous helper's clearer failure behavior.

This changes the previous global `DefaultCredentials` behavior into a per-`HttpClient` handler, but the new client is created without preserving the app's base address until later. More importantly, this only configures the single DI-registered `HttpClient`; any other `HttpClient` instances created elsewhere in the app/tests will no longer automatically include credentials like they did before. If the test relies on all browser HTTP traffic carrying cookies, consider whether this should be applied more centrally or document that only the injected singleton client is covered.

Adding `Microsoft.Graph` as a latest package reference can make template output non-deterministic over time, since templates will pick up whatever the newest Graph SDK version is during dependency updates. Given that Graph has had notable breaking API changes between major versions, it would be safer to pin the version explicitly (or document/verify the expected major version elsewhere) so the generated templates don't silently drift and break.

Using a hard-coded preview version here can make the shared templates/build fragile, especially if the package is updated or removed upstream. Since this repo typically centralizes package versions for reproducibility, please confirm that `0.2.0-preview` is the intended pinned version for all consuming templates and consider whether a stable release (or a comment explaining why a preview dependency is required) would be safer.

Marking the controller type as `[Obsolete]` will surface warnings for any internal framework references to this class as well, which can create noise during builds if this assembly still instantiates or routes to it internally. If the goal is to steer app developers away from this package while keeping the existing implementation functional, consider whether the deprecation should instead be applied at the public package/API entry points (or use `DiagnosticId`/`UrlFormat` for a more targeted message) rather than on this internal controller type.

Marking this internal type as `[Obsolete]` is useful for signaling deprecation, but because the class is internal it won't surface to most consumers. If the goal is to guide app developers away from this package, consider also adding the deprecation signal on the public entry points/package surface (or package metadata) so callers actually see the warning. Otherwise this attribute mainly affects maintainers inside this assembly.

The replacement guidance on this overload looks incorrect. `AddAzureAD(...)` configures the interactive web app flow, but the obsolete message points callers to `AddMicrosoftWebApiAuthentication`, which is intended for APIs/bearer auth. This will mislead MVC/Razor Pages users migrating from this method. Consider updating the message to the corresponding Microsoft.Identity.Web web app API instead.

Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is a good start, but because this class is `internal` the warning will only surface inside this assembly. If the goal is to steer template/app developers away from this package in favor of Microsoft.Identity.Web, this change alone may not provide any guidance to external consumers. Consider also obsoleting the public entry points/options that lead to this implementation, or adding package-level/docs guidance, so the migration path is visible where users actually interact with the API.

Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is a good start, but this only produces a compiler warning. Since this package is being superseded by Microsoft.Identity.Web, consider whether the individual public members in this class should also be annotated (or whether the message should include `error: false` explicitly) so consumers get consistent guidance regardless of how they reference the API. As written, callers using existing constants may not get as clear a migration signal in all tooling scenarios.

Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is appropriate for steering users to Microsoft.Identity.Web, but without `error: false` this remains only an informational warning by default. If the intent is a soft migration path, consider using the overload `Obsolete("...", error: false)` explicitly for clarity and to avoid future ambiguity. Also, since this is a public API, please confirm the deprecation message and removal timeline are aligned with the package's support policy and any API compatibility expectations.

Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is a good signal, but because this class is `internal` the warning will only surface inside this assembly and won't help consumers of the package migrate. If the intent is to deprecate the public feature area, consider also annotating the public entry points/types that expose AzureAD.UI so downstream apps get actionable warnings during compilation.

Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is a good signal, but because this class is `internal` the warning will only surface within this assembly and won't help consumers of the package migrate. If the intent is to deprecate the package/API for app developers, consider also annotating the public entry points that reference `AzureADOptions`/AzureAD UI so callers actually see the guidance to move to Microsoft.Identity.Web.

Suppressing `CS0618` at the project level will hide all obsolete-API usage in this test assembly, including any unrelated regressions introduced later. Since the comment indicates this package is being deprecated, it would be safer to scope the suppression to the specific test files or calls that intentionally exercise obsolete APIs, or at least document which obsolete members are expected here.

Marking the controller type as `[Obsolete]` will surface warnings for any internal framework usage of `AccountController` as well, not just for app developers. Since this is an MVC controller discovered by routing rather than a typical API surface, the attribute may not provide much value to consumers and could create noisy warnings inside this assembly or tests. Please verify that this is the intended deprecation point, or consider moving the deprecation notice to the public package/API surface that developers actually reference (for example the options/extensions/types they use directly).

Marking this internal type as `[Obsolete]` is fine for source consumers, but because the class is `internal` it won't surface much guidance unless the assembly also exposes the message through public entry points or docs. More importantly, if the intent is to discourage any remaining in-assembly usage, consider specifying `error: false` explicitly and ensuring all internal call sites are updated/suppressed intentionally so the warning behavior is predictable.

Marking this internal type as `[Obsolete]` is a good signal, but because the class is `internal` the warning will only surface inside this assembly and won't help consumers migrate. If the intent is to guide app developers away from this package, consider also obsoleting the public entry points/types that reference this implementation (or the package-level APIs) so the deprecation is visible to downstream users.

Marking this internal type as `[Obsolete]` is useful, but because the class is `internal` the warning will only surface inside this assembly. If the goal is to guide app developers away from this package and toward `Microsoft.Identity.Web`, this attribute alone won't reach most consumers. Consider also obsoleting the public entry points/APIs that instantiate or expose this behavior, or adding package/template-level guidance, so the migration message is visible to downstream users.

The replacement guidance on this overload looks incorrect. `AddAzureADB2C(...)` configures the interactive web app flow, but the obsolete message points callers to `AddMicrosoftWebApiAuthentication`, which is intended for web APIs/bearer token scenarios. That will mislead consumers migrating from the MVC/Razor Pages B2C setup. Please update this message to the corresponding Microsoft.Identity.Web web app API (for example `AddMicrosoftIdentityWebAppAuthentication`, or the B2C-specific equivalent if that's the intended migration path).

Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is a good start, but this only produces a compile-time warning for direct references. If the intent is to steer consumers away from the entire AzureADB2C UI package, consider whether the individual public members/extensions in this package also need matching obsoletion so callers get guidance at the actual API usage sites, not just when referencing this type.

Marking the type as `[Obsolete]` is a good migration signal, but this message will only surface as a warning by default. Since the PR is replacing these templates with Microsoft.Identity.Web, consider whether the deprecation should be stronger or more actionable here—for example, including a concrete migration path/API name in the message, and ensuring related public entry points in this package are also marked consistently so consumers don't miss the transition.

Marking the type with `[Obsolete]` is a good start, but because this class is `internal` the warning will only surface inside this assembly and won't help consumers of the package migrate. If the intent is to steer app developers away from this package/template path, consider also obsoleting the public entry points/options/extensions that reference this implementation, or documenting the migration elsewhere, since this attribute alone has limited external effect.

Suppressing `CS0618` at the project level will hide all obsolete-API usage in this test project, including any unrelated regressions introduced later. Since the comment says the package itself will be deprecated, it would be safer to scope the suppression to the specific test files or references that intentionally exercise obsolete APIs, or at least document which obsolete members are expected here.

Suppressing CS0618 at the project level will hide all obsolete-API usage in this test project, which can mask unrelated regressions and make the eventual migration harder. Since the comment says the test "will be deprecated" rather than is already removed, consider scoping the suppression to the specific obsolete call sites or test files instead of adding a blanket `NoWarn` for the entire project.

Adding `Microsoft.Graph` only when `GenerateGraph` is true looks incomplete unless the template also guarantees that `GenerateGraph` can only be enabled together with `IndividualB2CAuth` or `OrganizationalAuth`. In a plain/no-auth template this would introduce a Graph dependency without the corresponding `Microsoft.Identity.Web` packages or auth configuration. Consider either constraining this condition to the auth-enabled cases as well, or validating that `GenerateGraph` is never set independently.

This adds `Microsoft.Graph` whenever `GenerateGraph` is true, but the previous template logic also encoded directory/Graph access intent via `WebProject_DirectoryAccessLevelKey`/`OrgReadAccess`, which was removed above. If `GenerateGraph` is not guaranteed to stay in sync with the auth/scaffolding options, the template can end up referencing Graph without the corresponding code/config, or omit it when Graph calls are generated. Please verify that `GenerateGraph` is derived from the same user choice that previously drove `OrgReadAccess`, or gate this package on the same condition to avoid mismatched template output.

This adds `Microsoft.Graph` whenever `GenerateGraph` is true, but the old `OrgReadAccess`-based properties that appear to drive Graph/read-directory behavior were removed above. If other template logic still keys off `OrgReadAccess`, this can leave the project in an inconsistent state where Graph support is partially enabled or no longer configurable. Please verify that all remaining template conditionals and generated code were updated to use `GenerateGraph` consistently, or preserve the previous property mapping for backward compatibility.

This template is still wiring up the obsolete `AzureADDefaults`/`AddAzureAD` flow and only suppresses the warning locally. Given the PR goal is to replace the organization auth logic with Microsoft.Identity.Web, this should be migrated to the newer `AddMicrosoftIdentityWebApp`/`AddMicrosoftIdentityWebApi` pattern instead of baking obsolete APIs into newly generated projects.

The warning suppression here is scoped only to the method call line, but the obsolete symbol is also referenced in the argument expression (`AzureADDefaults.OpenIdScheme`). Depending on compiler behavior, this can still leave CS0618 warnings in generated projects. If this code must remain, consider wrapping the entire obsolete usage block consistently; ideally, replace it with the non-obsolete Microsoft.Identity.Web configuration.

This continues to generate projects using the obsolete Azure AD B2C extension and suppresses the warning rather than updating the template. Since this PR is intended to incorporate Microsoft.Identity.Web templates, please switch this path to the corresponding Microsoft.Identity.Web B2C setup instead of relying on `AddAzureADB2C`.

This template change suppresses CS0618 around `AddAzureADB2CBearer`, but the PR goal is to move templates to Microsoft.Identity.Web. Keeping the obsolete AzureAD/AzureADB2C extensions here means newly generated apps will still be scaffolded with deprecated auth APIs, and the warning suppression can hide that mismatch. Consider replacing this with the Microsoft.Identity.Web equivalent instead of suppressing the warning.

Setting `shortName` to an empty string is risky in template host metadata. Other parameters here either provide a real short name or omit the property entirely, and some hosts may treat an empty value as invalid or generate odd help output. If these options should not have a short alias, it's safer to remove the `shortName` field for them.

Using an empty string for `shortName` can cause template parameter metadata issues depending on the host implementation. Consider omitting `shortName` instead of setting it to `""` if no short option is intended.
This parameter also uses an empty `shortName`. To avoid potential parsing/help-text inconsistencies across template hosts, prefer removing the `shortName` property when there is no short alias.

The `GenerateApi` computed condition infers API generation by comparing `CalledApiUrl`/`CalledApiScopes` against the Graph defaults. This means a user can set `--calls-microsoft-graph true` and still get `GenerateApi == false` if they leave the defaults unchanged, even though the app is intended to call an API. It also couples behavior to specific default string values, which is brittle if those defaults change later. Consider basing this on an explicit user-facing switch (or at least excluding the Graph case directly) rather than sentinel default comparisons.

This Razor Pages template is rendering `ViewData["ApiResult"]`, but Razor Pages typically populates page data from the `PageModel` rather than a controller-style `ViewData` assignment. Unless the corresponding `IndexModel` is updated to set this key, the generated template will show an empty result. Consider binding this through a strongly typed page model property (for example, `Model.ApiResult`) or ensure the generated `Index.cshtml.cs` sets `ViewData["ApiResult"]` in all `GenerateApiOrGraph` cases.

The `AuthorizeForScopes` attribute is emitted whenever `GenerateApiOrGraph` is true, but its `ScopeKeySection` is hard-coded to `CalledApi:CalledApiScopes`. That matches the downstream web API case, but the Graph template typically stores scopes under a different configuration section (for example `DownstreamApi` or `MicrosoftGraph`). In the `GenerateGraph` branch this can cause incremental consent to look up the wrong config key and fail at runtime. Consider making the section conditional per template variant instead of sharing the same value for both API and Graph.

Switching the B2C links to the `MicrosoftIdentity` area looks correct for the new package, but this line still points to an `EditProfile` action. In Microsoft.Identity.Web, B2C user-flow endpoints are not always exposed with the same controller/action surface as the old `AzureADB2C.UI` package, so this template could generate a broken link unless the corresponding area/action is guaranteed to exist. Please verify that `MicrosoftIdentity/Account/EditProfile` is actually provided by the new templates/package, or update this to the supported route/mechanism for invoking the profile-edit user flow.

Using `DefaultRequestHeaders.Add` on the shared `HttpClient` will accumulate `Authorization` headers across calls, and can throw on subsequent requests. Since this service acquires a potentially different user token per request, set the header on a per-request `HttpRequestMessage` (or assign `DefaultRequestHeaders.Authorization`) instead of adding a new default header each time.

Registering `ITokenAcquisition` here can conflict with the authentication setup in the template, which already adds token acquisition as part of the Microsoft.Identity.Web auth registration. Calling `AddTokenAcquisition(true)` again from this helper makes the extension less composable and can lead to duplicate/overlapping registrations. Consider assuming `ITokenAcquisition` is already registered by the auth pipeline, or at least guard/document this dependency instead of re-registering it here.

This uses `GetService<ITokenAcquisition>()`, which can return `null`, but the result is passed directly into `TokenAcquisitionCredentialProvider`. If the auth services are not registered correctly, this will fail later with a less actionable error. Prefer `GetRequiredService<ITokenAcquisition>()` so misconfiguration is caught immediately with a clear exception.

There's a typo in the local variable name: `tokenAquisitionService` should be `tokenAcquisitionService`. Since this is a project template, the generated code becomes sample code for users, so it's worth fixing the spelling.

Using `Headers.Add("Authorization", ...)` can append a second Authorization header if this provider is invoked more than once for the same request, and it also bypasses the strongly-typed header API. Consider assigning `request.Headers.Authorization = new AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", await _tokenAcquisition.GetAccessTokenForUserAsync(_initialScopes));` instead.

These fields are mutable and use the default private-field style, but they are only assigned in the constructor. Marking them `private readonly` would better communicate intent and prevent accidental reassignment in this template code.

The empty `AddMvcOptions(options => {})` call appears to be a no-op. Keeping it in the template adds noise and may confuse users into thinking some MVC customization is required here. Unless a later step injects options into this lambda, consider removing it and chaining `AddMicrosoftIdentityUI()` directly from `AddRazorPages()`.

The default scope here looks inconsistent with the surrounding guidance. The comments above describe `CalledApiScopes` as the scopes for the Web API being called, but `user.read` is a Microsoft Graph scope, not a custom Web API scope. Since `CalledApiUrl` is parameterized as `[WebApiUrl]`, this default can mislead users into configuring a custom API with a Graph scope. Consider using a placeholder/example that matches a custom API scope (for example `api://<app-id>/access_as_user`) or making the sample conditional when Graph is the target.

Using an empty string for `shortName` is risky here. In template host config, omitting `shortName` is typically safer than setting it to `""`, because some hosts/parsers may treat an empty short option as an invalid alias or generate confusing CLI metadata. Consider removing the `shortName` property entirely for options that intentionally have no short form.

Same concern as above: an empty `shortName` can be interpreted inconsistently by template tooling. If this parameter should not have a short alias, it's better to omit `shortName` rather than set it to an empty string.
This option also uses an empty `shortName`. To avoid potential CLI/template host issues and keep the metadata consistent, consider removing `shortName` when no short switch is intended.

The `GenerateApi` computed symbol is inferred from whether `CalledApiUrl`/`CalledApiScopes` differ from the Graph defaults.
