That means a user can set `--CallsMicrosoftGraph true` and also override the Graph URL or scopes, which will make both `GenerateApi` and `GenerateGraph` true and include both service paths. If these options are intended to be mutually exclusive, consider folding `!CallsMicrosoftGraph` into this expression or otherwise validating the combination to avoid generating conflicting template content.

The `AuthorizeForScopes` attribute here is still pointing at `CalledApi:CalledApiScopes`, but in the Graph path this action uses `GraphServiceClient` rather than `IDownstreamWebApi`. Unless the generated configuration intentionally reuses the same section name for Graph scopes, this will either be misleading or fail to request the correct consent. Please align the scope section with the Graph configuration used by the template.

Using `DefaultRequestHeaders.Add` here will mutate the shared `HttpClient` instance for all future requests from this typed client. If `CallWebApi` is invoked more than once, you'll either accumulate duplicate `Authorization` headers or hit an exception when adding the same header again. Prefer creating an `HttpRequestMessage` per call and setting `request.Headers.Authorization` on that request instead.

Calling `AddTokenAcquisition(true)` inside this helper can conflict with the app's existing Microsoft.Identity.Web setup and may register token acquisition services twice. Since the templates already configure authentication elsewhere, this extension should usually assume `ITokenAcquisition` is already registered (or at least guard against duplicate registration) rather than re-adding it here.

This uses `GetService<ITokenAcquisition>()`, which can return `null` and defer the failure until `TokenAcquisitionCredentialProvider` is constructed or used. Since this dependency is required for `GraphServiceClient`, prefer `GetRequiredService<ITokenAcquisition>()` so misconfiguration fails fast with a clearer error.

There's a typo in the local variable name: `tokenAquisitionService` should be `tokenAcquisitionService`. Since this is template code, it's worth fixing to avoid propagating the misspelling into generated apps.

Using `Headers.Add("Authorization", ...)` can throw if this provider is invoked more than once for the same `HttpRequestMessage`, because the `Authorization` header is expected to be singular. It would be safer and clearer to assign `request.Headers.Authorization = new AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", token)` after retrieving the token.
These fields are only set in the constructor and never reassigned, so they should be marked `readonly` to better express intent and avoid accidental mutation in this template code.

This Graph registration is reading scopes and URL from the `CalledApi` section, which is the same configuration used for generic downstream APIs. In the Microsoft.Identity.Web templates, Microsoft Graph is typically configured from a dedicated `DownstreamApi`/`MicrosoftGraph` section (or equivalent) because Graph's base URL and scopes differ from a custom API. Reusing `CalledApi:*` here can generate a template that points Graph at the wrong endpoint or uses the wrong scopes when both options are present.

Displaying `@ViewData["ApiResult"]` unconditionally inside the `GenerateApiOrGraph` template branch can render an empty placeholder when the controller doesn't populate this value or when the downstream API call fails. Consider guarding this with a null/empty check (or showing a fallback/error message) so generated apps don't present a blank `Api result` section by default.

Switching from `IOptionsMonitor<AzureADB2COptions>` to `IOptions<MicrosoftIdentityOptions>` drops the ability to resolve options by authentication scheme. In the previous version, `Get(AzureADB2CDefaults.AuthenticationScheme)` ensured the B2C-specific named options were used. With `Value`, this will only read the default registration, which can be incorrect if the template configures named options or multiple auth schemes. Consider keeping a monitor/snapshot and resolving the options for the B2C scheme explicitly.

The sample value for `CalledApiScopes` looks inconsistent with the surrounding guidance. The comments above describe this setting as the scope for a Web API to call (for example `api://.../access_as_user` or `<App ID URI>/.default`), but the placeholder value `user.read` is a Microsoft Graph scope. Since this block is named `CalledApi`, using `user.read` here may mislead template users into configuring a custom API call with a Graph scope. Consider either renaming this section to make the Graph scenario explicit, or changing the sample scope to a custom API example that matches the comment.

Adding `CallsMicrosoftGraph` here without any corresponding visibility/constraint metadata means the CLI will expose `--calls-graph` independently of `--called-api-url` and `--called-api-scopes`. If the generated template logic treats Graph calls as mutually exclusive with custom downstream API settings, users can end up with conflicting combinations of options. Consider adding the appropriate symbol constraints or conditional handling in the template config so these parameters can't be supplied in invalid combinations.

`GenerateApi` is inferred by comparing `CalledApiUrl`/`CalledApiScopes` against hard-coded default values, which makes the behavior surprising: a user can pass `--CallsMicrosoftGraph true` and still get `GenerateApi=true` if they also customize scopes/url, causing both API and Graph assets to be included. It also means changing the defaults later silently changes generation behavior. Consider basing this on an explicit user choice (or at least making the conditions mutually exclusive with `GenerateGraph`) instead of string comparisons to defaults.

`CallWebApi()` is awaited here, but the result is never used. In the generated template this can trigger an unnecessary downstream API call on every request and may also fail the endpoint if the downstream API isn't configured correctly, even though the response body doesn't depend on it. Consider either using `downstreamApiResult` in the action result or removing the call from the default template.

Using `DefaultRequestHeaders.Add` here will mutate the shared `HttpClient` instance for all future requests from this typed client. Since `CallWebApi` can be invoked multiple times, this can lead to duplicate `Authorization` headers or stale tokens being sent. Prefer creating an `HttpRequestMessage` per call and setting `request.Headers.Authorization` on that request instead of modifying `DefaultRequestHeaders`.

Calling `AddTokenAcquisition(true)` here can conflict with the authentication setup already performed by the template startup code. Since this extension is intended to add Graph support, re-registering token acquisition in a lower-level helper makes the service wiring less predictable and can lead to duplicate/overlapping registrations. Consider assuming `ITokenAcquisition` has already been added by the auth configuration, or at least guard/document this dependency instead of registering it again here.

There is a typo in the local variable name (`tokenAquisitionService`), and more importantly `GetService<ITokenAcquisition>()` can return `null`, which would only fail later when constructing the credential provider. Since this extension requires token acquisition to be registered, `GetRequiredService<ITokenAcquisition>()` would make the dependency explicit and fail fast with a clearer error.

This template is under `WebApi-CSharp`, but `GetAccessTokenForUserAsync` requires a signed-in user context. In a Web API template, requests are often app-only or represent downstream API calls without an interactive user, so this can fail or be misleading for generated projects. Consider whether this provider should use app token acquisition instead, or make it explicit that this class is only valid for delegated/user-authenticated scenarios.

This Web API template is configuring `.AddMicrosoftWebAppCallsWebApi(...)`, which is intended for web apps acquiring tokens to call downstream APIs on behalf of a signed-in user. In a pure API template, there is no interactive user sign-in, so this setup is likely the wrong abstraction and may generate a non-working sample for `GenerateApiOrGraph`. If the goal is to support downstream API calls from the API, consider using the API-specific token acquisition pattern (for example via `EnableTokenAcquisitionToCallDownstreamApi`) or otherwise align this with the Microsoft.Identity.Web guidance for protected web APIs.

The Graph registration is reading scopes and URL from `CalledApi:*`, but Microsoft Graph typically uses its own configuration section (for example `DownstreamApi`/`MicrosoftGraph`) and a fixed Graph base URL. Reusing `CalledApi` here can make the generated template configuration misleading or incorrect, especially when both Graph and a custom downstream API are involved. It would be safer to bind Graph from a dedicated config section that matches the generated appsettings entries.

There appears to be an extra closing brace in the commented template block here. The `AzureAd` object is already closed on the previous line, and this additional `//  },` would produce mismatched braces when the template directives are expanded for `OrganizationalAuth`. Please verify the generated `appsettings.json` for those auth options and remove this duplicate closing brace if it's unintended.

Using a random GUID suffix here makes the test name non-deterministic, which can make failures harder to reproduce and can leave behind uniquely named project artifacts on reruns. If the goal is to avoid collisions between theory cases, consider deriving the project name from `auth`/`args` (sanitized) or using a helper that guarantees uniqueness while preserving readability.

Using `Guid.NewGuid().ToString().Substring(0, 10).ToLower()` introduces culture-sensitive casing in a test-generated project name. While GUIDs are typically hex, `ToLower()` without an explicit culture can still trigger analyzer warnings and is generally discouraged in test infrastructure. Consider `ToLowerInvariant()` instead to keep the name generation deterministic across locales.

Appending a random `Guid` suffix here makes the test project name nondeterministic, which can make failures harder to reproduce and can also bypass any intended reuse/caching behavior in `GetOrCreateProject`. If uniqueness is required to avoid collisions between theory cases, consider using a deterministic suffix derived from the test parameters (`auth`/`args`) instead of randomness.

`IHttpResetFeature.Reset(int errorCode)` accepts any `int`, but this implementation immediately casts it to `Http3ErrorCode` for both logging and abort behavior. That means invalid or non-HTTP/3 values will still be treated as a valid enum member and may produce undefined/reset behavior on the wire. Consider validating that `errorCode` is a defined HTTP/3 application error code (or at least within the allowed range) before calling `ApplicationAbort`, and fail fast with a clearer exception if it isn't.

Calling `ResetHttp3Features()` from `OnReset()` is important for cleanup, but this hook can be reached from multiple reset/abort paths. Please make sure `ResetHttp3Features()` is idempotent and safe to invoke after `ApplicationAbort(...)`/`Abort(...)` has already started tearing down the stream; otherwise feature state could be reset twice or race with abort handling.
There’s a typo in the test name (`WithExeption500`). Renaming it to `ResponseTrailers_WithException500_Cleared` would improve readability and make test discovery/searching easier.

This test only verifies that the stream ends, but it doesn’t assert the expected 500 response or that trailers were actually suppressed after the exception. Since the test name says trailers are cleared on exception, consider asserting the status code on `responseHeaders` and/or explicitly verifying that no trailing HEADERS frame is received.

The response headers are read here but never validated. Adding at least a basic assertion (for example, `:status`) would make the test more robust and help distinguish a valid response-with-trailers from an unexpected extra HEADERS frame sequence.

Clearing `_decodedHeaders` here avoids leaking values between successive `ExpectHeadersAsync()` calls, but it also means the method returns the same mutable dictionary instance every time. Any caller that keeps a reference to a previous result will observe it being emptied/overwritten on the next decode. Consider returning a copy (or allocating a fresh dictionary per decode) so previously returned header sets remain stable and tests don't become order-dependent.

Resetting `_state` after decoding a multi-segment `ReadOnlySequence` is important, but doing it only in this overload can leave the decoder in a non-initial state when callers use the `ReadOnlySpan<byte>` overload directly. Since `Decode(segment.Span, handler)` appears to advance parser state incrementally, consider ensuring the state is reset in a shared/finally path or in the span-based entry point as well so the decoder is consistently reusable regardless of which overload is called.

This allocates a new `JsonSerializerOptions` instance on every serialization call. Since `ClickAsync` serializes both the descriptor and event args, this adds avoidable per-event overhead and can offset some of the allocation savings from switching to UTF-8 bytes. Consider caching a static readonly options instance (or a generated `JsonTypeInfo`/context if available) and reusing it here.

Throwing on any unrecognized JSON property makes this reader much stricter than typical event payload deserialization and can break forward/backward compatibility if the browser-side payload adds extra fields. Since these readers are being introduced primarily as a size/perf optimization, it would be safer to ignore unknown properties unless strict validation is explicitly required across all WebEventData readers.

Throwing on any unrecognized JSON property makes this reader much stricter than the framework's previous deserialization behavior, which typically ignored extra fields. That can become a compatibility issue if browsers/framework code add new event payload members over time, because older servers/clients would start failing to parse otherwise valid events. Unless strict validation is required here, consider ignoring unknown properties (or gating this behind a debug/validation mode) to preserve forward compatibility.

Throwing on any unrecognized `dataTransfer` property makes this reader much less forward-compatible than the previous `MouseEventArgsReader.Read(...)` pattern. Browser event payloads can gain extra fields over time, and rejecting them here would break deserialization for otherwise valid events. Unless strict schema validation is intentional across all event readers, it would be safer to ignore unknown properties (or gate this behind a debug/validation mode) to preserve compatibility with newer clients/browsers.

This introduces a hard failure for any additional fields on `DataTransferItem`. Since these payloads originate from browser-side JSON, being strict here can turn harmless schema additions into runtime breaks. Consider ignoring unknown properties instead, unless the rest of the WebEventData readers are intentionally enforcing a closed schema for all nested objects.

Throwing on any unrecognized JSON property makes this reader much less forward-compatible than the previous behavior. Browser event payloads can gain extra fields over time, and rejecting them here could break older servers/clients that would otherwise safely ignore unknown data. Unless strict schema validation is required for security reasons, consider skipping unknown properties instead of failing deserialization.

Throwing on any unrecognized JSON property makes this reader much less forward-compatible than the previous behavior. Browser event payloads can gain extra fields over time, and older servers/clients should generally ignore unknown properties rather than fail deserialization. Unless strict schema validation is required here, consider skipping unrecognized properties instead of throwing.

Throwing on any unrecognized JSON property makes this reader much less forward-compatible than the previous behavior. Browser event payloads may gain extra fields over time, or different clients may send superset payloads, and this would now fail deserialization for otherwise valid events. Unless strict schema validation is required across all event readers, it would be safer to ignore unknown properties and only read the fields this type understands.

Throwing on any unrecognized JSON property is a behavioral change from the previous implementation, which silently ignored extras. That makes the reader much less forward-compatible: adding a new field on the JS side or receiving payloads from a newer client could now break existing servers with a `JsonException`. Unless strict schema validation is intentional across all event readers, consider preserving the old behavior and ignoring unknown properties here.

Throwing on any unrecognized JSON property makes this reader much less forward-compatible than the previous behavior. Browser event payloads can gain extra fields over time, and rejecting them here could break clients/server interop for otherwise valid events. Unless strict schema validation is required for security reasons, it would be safer to ignore unknown properties and only read the fields this type understands.

Throwing on any unrecognized top-level property makes the event payload contract very brittle. Browser event payloads can gain extra fields over time, and older servers should generally ignore unknown JSON members for forward/backward compatibility. With this change, adding a new client-side touch field would start failing deserialization entirely instead of just skipping it. Consider ignoring unknown properties here unless strict validation is explicitly required.

This has the same compatibility concern in the nested touch point reader: rejecting unknown members means any future extension to the touch point payload will break existing apps at runtime. Since these readers are replacing the more permissive serializer path, it would be safer to skip unrecognized properties here as well unless the protocol is intentionally locked down.

Throwing on any unrecognized top-level property makes this reader much stricter than the previous behavior and can break forward/backward compatibility if the client starts sending additional metadata fields. Since these payloads come from browser event JSON, it may be safer to ignore unknown properties unless strict validation is explicitly required.

This has the same compatibility concern in the nested `EventFieldInfo` reader: rejecting unknown properties means any future extension of the payload shape will start failing deserialization instead of being safely ignored. Please confirm that the protocol is intentionally closed, or consider skipping unknown fields here.

Using `!=` here is fine for a flags enum, but this change also alters behavior on the first options callback: `_fields` starts at its default value, so if `loggerOptions.LoggingFields` is non-default, the processor will increment `_fileNumber` before any file has been created. That can cause the very first log file for a process to start at the next suffix instead of the base file name. Consider guarding this with an initialization flag (or only rotating after a file has already been opened) so we only create a new file when the fields change relative to a previously active configuration.

Using `DateTimeOffset.Now` directly makes this test time-dependent and can still be flaky around date boundaries or under slow CI runs. Since the production behavior is based on the current date, consider injecting/faking time in `FileLoggerProcessor` (or at least capturing the date from the same source the processor uses) so the expected filenames are deterministic and the test doesn't need the `Hour == 23` escape hatch.

This mutates the same `W3CLoggerOptions` instance that was already handed to the monitor. That verifies the current implementation, but it doesn't model how `IOptionsMonitor` typically delivers changes in production, where a new options snapshot is provided. Consider updating the test helper to publish a new `W3CLoggerOptions` instance here so the test covers the real options-change path and avoids coupling to in-place mutation semantics.

`InvokeChanged()` will throw a `NullReferenceException` if no callback has been registered via `OnChange()` yet. Since this is a test helper, it would be safer to use a null-conditional invocation (or guard clause) so tests don't fail for the wrong reason when `InvokeChanged()` is called before subscription.

This test rename and the updated assertions indicate a behavioral change: inferred body parameters are no longer represented in `ApiDescription.ParameterDescriptions`. Given the issue/PR is about exposing request-body required/optional metadata via `Accepts`/`Consumes`, removing these assertions also drops coverage for body parameter type/source/requiredness entirely. If the body is now expected to be surfaced somewhere else (for example via request body metadata rather than parameter descriptions), it would be good to add explicit assertions for that new location so this test suite still validates the request-body contract instead of only asserting absence.

This overload makes `entityTag` non-nullable, but the proposed API and existing file-result patterns allow callers to omit the ETag. As written, consumers can't pass only `lastModified` without manufacturing an `EntityTagHeaderValue`, and this also diverges from the `Results.Stream(..., EntityTagHeaderValue? entityTag = null, ...)` shape described in the issue. Consider changing this and the overload below to accept `EntityTagHeaderValue?`.

Same concern here: `entityTag` is required even though the new streaming API is intended to support optional conditional request metadata. Making this parameter nullable would better match the issue's proposed API and avoid forcing callers to provide an ETag when they only want to set `lastModified` or `fileDownloadName`.

This writes directly to `Response.Body`, which bypasses the `IHttpResponseBodyFeature` pipeline. In ASP.NET Core, file/streaming executors typically go through the response body feature so wrappers such as compression, buffering, and test/server-specific body implementations are honored. Consider invoking the callback against `context.HttpContext.Features.Get<IHttpResponseBodyFeature>()?.Stream` (or otherwise using the body feature abstraction) instead of `Response.Body` directly.

This API file now exposes `PushFileStreamResult` and its executor as public surface, but the issue/proposed API is specifically about adding a `Results.Stream(Func<Stream, Task>)` overload in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http`. If this MVC type is intentionally public, that's fine, but it looks like the implementation may be leaking an internal plumbing type into the public contract instead of only exposing the higher-level API. Please verify that `PushFileStreamResultExecutor` is meant to be publicly supported and not just an implementation detail.

Consider making this callback immutable after construction (for example, `public Func<Stream, Task> StreamWriterCallback { get; }`). `FileResult` instances are typically configured up front and then executed, and allowing the delegate to be reassigned later makes it possible to set it to `null` after the constructor guard has run, which could lead to a runtime failure in the executor.

This assertion only verifies that nothing was written to the response body, but it doesn't prove the `streamWriterCallback` itself was skipped for `HEAD` requests. An implementation could still invoke the callback and write to a different stream or perform expensive side effects while this test still passes. Consider tracking whether the callback was executed (for example with a boolean flag) and asserting that it remains false.

Conditioning `System.Diagnostics.EventLog.Messages` on `TargetOsName == 'win'` fixes the Linux case, but it may still be incorrect for cross-targeting or cross-OS test execution because the expected file set should match the runtime under test, not necessarily the build target. If these tests can run on a different OS than `$(TargetOsName)`, consider keying this off the actual shared framework artifact location / runtime being validated (or the same property used to resolve `DOTNET_ROOT` / test assets) to avoid false positives or negatives in CI.

This broadens the lookup from a fixed `win-x64` package to any `*-x64` runtime package, but `Directory.GetFiles(...).FirstOrDefault()` makes the test nondeterministic if more than one matching package exists in the folder (for example, if multiple RIDs are present in CI artifacts). That could cause the test to open the wrong package and either fail intermittently or validate the wrong runtime list. Consider deriving the expected RID from the current test environment / build metadata and matching a single exact package name, or at least asserting that exactly one file matches before selecting it.

This condition keys off the current test host OS, but the issue/PR is about validating build artifacts that may not match the host platform. In Helix or cross-targeted test runs, we can execute on one OS while inspecting artifacts produced for another, so `RuntimeInformation.IsOSPlatform(...)` may remove `System.Diagnostics.EventLog.Messages` incorrectly. It would be safer to derive the expected assembly list from the artifact layout / runtime identifier under test (e.g., the shared framework path or RID) rather than the machine running the test.

Switching `ConfigureWebDefaultsCore` to `UseKestrelSlim` changes the behavior of this helper from the full-featured default Kestrel setup to the trimmed/pay-for-play variant. Since this method name still reads like the common/default path, callers may unexpectedly lose HTTPS/QUIC support unless they explicitly opt back in. If this method is used outside the new slim-builder flow, consider either keeping the existing `UseKestrel` behavior here or renaming/splitting the helper so the behavioral change is explicit.

Switching `ASPNETCORE_URLS` to an HTTP-only value is useful for the new slim scenarios, but this helper still unconditionally sets `ASPNETCORE_ENVIRONMENT=Development` and passes `DevCert` into `AspNetProcess`. In Development, templates may still try to configure HTTPS/redirection or rely on the dev cert path, so `noHttps: true` can become a partial opt-out rather than a true no-TLS test setup. Consider also disabling the dev-certificate-related setup (and possibly using a non-Development environment) when `noHttps` is requested so these tests exercise the intended pay-for-play behavior more reliably.

Similar to the built-project path, this only removes the HTTPS URL binding. If the published app still receives `DevCert`/HTTPS-related configuration elsewhere, tests using `noHttps` may not accurately validate the trimmed 'no TLS support' scenario. It would be safer to make the entire launch configuration consistently HTTP-only when this flag is set.

This change now disables HTTPS unconditionally for this test path, but the comment says it only applies to the "minimal/slim/core scenario". If `ApiTemplateCore` is also used by variants that still include HTTPS support, this can mask regressions by never validating HTTPS address registration for them. Consider gating `noHttps: true` on the specific template/options under test, or updating the test naming/scope so it’s clear this helper only covers the no-TLS scenario.

This message hard-codes the concrete API name `UseHttpsConfiguration()`. Since the PR description already notes the API/name is still in flux, embedding it directly in a shared resource string risks the error text becoming stale if the public surface is renamed before release. Consider either deferring the exact method name to the call site or using a more generic message that can be kept in sync more easily.

Setting `_isInitialized = true` before the service is fully initialized can leave the instance in a permanently broken state if `new TlsConfigurationLoader(...)` or any later assignment throws. Subsequent calls will skip initialization and then hit null-forgiven fields like `_tlsConfigurationLoader!`. Consider only flipping `_isInitialized` after all initialization steps succeed, or resetting it in a `catch`/`finally` path.

Minor doc issue: `implementations off all other methods` looks like a typo and should likely be `implementations of all other methods`. Since this is a new internal abstraction that explains the opt-in TLS behavior, tightening the wording will make the contract clearer.

This struct stores reference-type fields and is passed around as a nullable value (`CertificateAndConfig?`). Consider making the fields/properties nullable or validating constructor arguments explicitly. As written, `default(CertificateAndConfig)` is still representable and would contain null references despite the non-nullable annotations, which weakens the null-safety of this API.

The return value from `useHttps` is ignored here. Since the delegate is typed as `Func<ListenOptions, ListenOptions>`, callers may reasonably expect it to return a replacement/wrapped `ListenOptions` (similar to how `UseHttps()` has historically been used). If the intent is side effects only, this should be an `Action<ListenOptions>`; otherwise assign the result back to `options` before binding so any transformed instance is actually used.

Consider validating `httpsConfigurationService` before assigning it. Both constructors now require this dependency, but unlike `transportFactories`, it isn't null-checked here. If DI registration is missing or a test constructs `KestrelServerImpl` manually, this will fail later in less obvious places (for example when creating `TransportManager` or during bind) instead of throwing immediately with a clear argument error.

The constructor no longer validates `options`, `configuration`, or `httpsConfigurationService` for null, but the rest of this type still dereferences all three immediately (`new ConfigurationReader(configuration)`, `_httpsConfigurationService.IsInitialized`, etc.). If any caller passes null, this changes the failure mode from a clear `ArgumentNullException` at construction time to a later `NullReferenceException`. Please keep the guard clauses here unless nullability is enforced elsewhere.

Throwing `NotImplementedException` here is risky because this type is being passed as a real `IHttpsConfigurationService` implementation. If any existing or future call path reaches this method, the failure will look like an internal bug rather than an unsupported configuration scenario. Since the PR is explicitly trying to provide helpful bind-time guidance when HTTPS configuration isn't enabled, consider throwing a more specific exception with an actionable message (or implementing the method by delegating to the existing worker/service) so behavior stays consistent with the new pay-for-play model.

Same concern here: `NotImplementedException` makes this concrete service fragile if SNI-backed HTTPS configuration is ever exercised through this constructor path. Given the PR's goal of graceful opt-in/opt-out behavior, it would be better to either delegate to the normal HTTPS configuration logic or throw a targeted exception that explains how to enable the missing HTTPS support.

Using `NotImplementedException` for `LoadDefaultCertificate` can produce an unexpected runtime crash if certificate loading is triggered indirectly (for example by configuration-based HTTPS setup). Since this service reports `IsInitialized => true`, callers have no signal that parts of the contract are intentionally unavailable. Consider either fully implementing the interface for this fallback service or failing with a descriptive exception that matches the new `UseHttpsConfiguration` guidance.

Relying on `Debug.Assert` here means release builds won't enforce the invariant. If `ApplyDefaultCertificate` is reached before HTTPS configuration is initialized, production code can still proceed and potentially select the development certificate incorrectly, which is exactly what the comment warns about. Consider turning this into a runtime guard (for example, throwing the same helpful exception used elsewhere when HTTPS configuration hasn't been enabled) instead of an assert-only check.

Calling `EnableHttpsConfiguration()` here changes the behavior of `UseHttps(Action<HttpsConnectionAdapterOptions>)` from a pure endpoint configuration API into one that also enables global HTTPS configuration support. That seems intentional for the slim-builder scenario, but it also means any code path that invokes this overload indirectly will now pull in the feature and potentially reduce the trimming benefit. Please verify that all implicit/default HTTPS paths were removed or updated consistently, otherwise this can become an accidental opt-in that undermines the pay-for-play goal.

Loading the development certificate with `new X509Certificate2(...)` can trigger SYSLIB0057 on newer target frameworks and also bypasses the newer loader APIs. Since this file is being introduced as part of the TLS pay-for-play refactor, it would be better to use `X509CertificateLoader` (or the existing certificate-loading abstraction) here as well to avoid adding a fresh use of the legacy constructor.

This test now depends on `IHttpsConfigurationService` being registered, but the added mock doesn't verify the new pay-for-play behavior introduced by this PR. Since the goal is to fail with a helpful error when HTTPS configuration isn't enabled, it would be useful to add a companion test that omits this registration and asserts the expected failure path. Otherwise this test may just mask a missing-service regression without covering the intended behavior.

This helper now creates and initializes a concrete `HttpsConfigurationService` instead of using the `IHttpsConfigurationService` already registered on `options.ApplicationServices`. That can make tests diverge from production DI behavior and bypass any mocked/disabled implementation the test intentionally configured. Consider resolving `IHttpsConfigurationService` from `ApplicationServices` when available, and only falling back to `new HttpsConfigurationService()` if nothing is registered.

Adding `UseKestrelSlim` as a new public API here makes the current placeholder name part of the shipped surface. The PR description explicitly says `name TBD`, so if the naming is still under discussion, this should not be committed to `PublicAPI.Unshipped.txt` yet or it will effectively lock in the API shape/name. Please confirm the final naming before recording it in the public API baseline.

Registering `HttpsConfigurationService.IInitializer` with `AddSingleton` means every call to `UseHttpsConfiguration()` adds another descriptor. Since extension methods can be composed or invoked multiple times by libraries, this can lead to duplicate registrations and repeated initialization side effects. Consider using `TryAddSingleton` (or otherwise making the registration idempotent) so the API is safe to call more than once.

Using `host.Run` in a test assertion can block indefinitely if the expected failure doesn't occur, since `Run` starts the host and waits for shutdown. To keep the test deterministic, prefer asserting on `StartAsync()` instead, which exercises bind-time validation without introducing a long-running call path.

This test also uses `host.Run`, which is risky in unit/integration tests because it blocks until shutdown. If the exception is not thrown for any reason, the test will hang rather than fail quickly. `StartAsync()` should be enough to validate the bind failure here.

Same concern here: asserting on `host.Run` makes the test depend on a blocking API. Since the behavior under test is startup/bind validation, using `await Assert.ThrowsAsync<InvalidOperationException>(() => host.StartAsync())` would be safer and more precise.

This assertion should avoid `host.Run` for the same reason as the other cases: it can hang the test if startup unexpectedly succeeds. Consider switching to `StartAsync()`/`ThrowsAsync` so the test only covers the bind-time failure path.

This test setup now registers the concrete HTTPS configuration services directly, which means these tests no longer exercise the new pay-for-play default behavior where HTTPS configuration is disabled until explicitly enabled. That can mask regressions in the intended failure path. Consider keeping the default test container aligned with production slim/default builder behavior and only adding these registrations in tests that specifically need HTTPS configuration enabled.

This test helper now unconditionally registers the real `HttpsConfigurationService`, which changes the behavior of all `TestServer`-based tests from the new slim/default production behavior described in this PR. That can mask failures around missing HTTPS configuration and reduce coverage of the pay-for-play path. Consider making this registration explicit/opt-in in the helper, or at least mirroring the same enable/disable semantics used by the production host setup so tests exercise the intended bind-time failure behavior.

This test helper now manually registers `IHttpsConfigurationService` and its initializer, which mirrors framework wiring. That makes the test more brittle to future DI changes in Kestrel startup: if the production registration graph changes, these tests can start failing for setup reasons unrelated to the behavior under test. If possible, consider using the same extension/helper that production uses to register HTTPS configuration services, or centralize this test-only service setup in a shared helper to avoid duplicating internal registration details.

Using a bare `Mock.Of<IHostEnvironment>()` here can make the test brittle if `ApplyDefaultCertificate` or related code starts reading environment properties such as `ApplicationName`, `EnvironmentName`, or `ContentRootPath`. Since this helper is intended to satisfy DI for many HTTPS tests, consider providing a concrete/stable host environment (or at least setting the commonly used properties on the mock) so the test doesn't start failing with null/default values for reasons unrelated to HTTPS behavior.

This test host now unconditionally registers the HTTPS configuration service and initializer, which may mask the new pay-for-play behavior this PR is trying to validate. If `TestServer` is used by scenarios intended to behave like the slim builder, these registrations will make HTTPS configuration available by default and could prevent tests from catching the expected bind-time failure/helpful error path. Consider making this registration explicit/conditional in the test setup so tests can opt into or out of HTTPS configuration support.

`Microsoft.CSharp.RuntimeBinder` is added here but doesn't appear to be used anywhere in this hunk or the surrounding test additions. Unused references in test projects can hide accidental dependencies and may be problematic for trimming-focused changes. Consider removing this using if it's not needed.

Using `host.Run` here makes the assertion depend on the synchronous run path and can obscure whether the failure is happening during bind/startup versus later host lifetime behavior. Since the success path uses `StartAsync`, it would be more precise and consistent to assert on `StartAsync`/`Start` throwing the expected `InvalidOperationException`.

This test also asserts via `host.Run`, which couples the expected failure to the full run loop instead of the bind/start phase being validated by this scenario. Consider asserting on `StartAsync`/`Start` instead so the test clearly verifies startup failure and matches the successful branch above.

This new property is intended to carry a server-side control value used during sign-in/refresh, but unlike the proposed API it isn't marked with `[JsonIgnore]`. `AuthenticationProperties` is commonly serialized, and without ignoring this property we risk leaking the refresh token anywhere the properties object is logged or JSON-serialized. Please add `[JsonIgnore]` here to keep the token out of serialized payloads.

This creates a `ClaimsIdentity` with only the authentication type set. The comment says sign-in will fail without an identity name, but no name claim is being added here, so the refresh flow is relying on handler-specific behavior to recover from an otherwise invalid principal. If `BearerTokenHandler` still requires a name/`IsAuthenticated` principal before it processes `AuthenticationProperties.RefreshToken`, this endpoint will always challenge. Please either construct a principal that satisfies the handler's minimum requirements or add a guard/test proving the handler can refresh from this placeholder principal.

Assigning `OnSigningIn` here unconditionally means any existing handler configured earlier on `BearerTokenOptions.Events.OnSigningIn` will be replaced, and if `configureOptions` also sets `OnSigningIn`, this identity logic is silently lost. Since the refresh flow depends on security-stamp validation and principal reloading, consider composing the callbacks instead of overwriting them so both identity's handler and user-provided handlers run.

The XML doc here still points callers to `BearerTokenExtensions.AddBearerToken(...)`, but the issue/PR description introduces `AddIdentityBearerToken<TUser>()` specifically so Identity can hook `OnSigningIn` for security stamp validation and principal reloading during refresh. Referring users to the generic bearer-token registration here is likely to produce a working setup that silently skips the Identity-specific refresh behavior. Consider updating the guidance to recommend `AddIdentityBearerToken<TUser>()` when bearer auth is used with Identity.
Minor doc typo: `ans services` should be `and services`.

Throwing `NotImplementedException` here is risky because this composite scheme is being registered as the default authentication scheme for the API endpoints setup. Any code path that signs in via the default scheme (or signs out from it) will now fail with a 500 instead of forwarding to the appropriate underlying handler. This should either forward sign-in/sign-out to the application or bearer scheme explicitly, or use a more appropriate exception if these operations are intentionally unsupported and guaranteed unreachable.

Same concern as `HandleSignInAsync`: leaving sign-out unimplemented on a registered composite auth handler can break logout flows with a runtime 500 if the default scheme is used. Consider forwarding sign-out to the application cookie scheme (or another intended target) instead of throwing.

This change removes `IdentityApiEndpointsIdentityBuilderExtensions` from the public API listing and replaces it with a different type name. Since the issue/PR description frames these as API renames/moves, please confirm the old extension class is either intentionally removed as a breaking change or preserved as a forwarding/shim API. If the old type still exists for compatibility, it should remain in the public API file; if it was removed intentionally, that should be called out explicitly because consumers may already be using the previous extension type from preview builds.

This test appears to read the access token from the login response instead of the refresh response. That means it never verifies that `/identity/refresh` actually returns a usable new bearer token, and the test could still pass even if refresh produced an invalid or missing `access_token`. Consider reading `access_token` from `refreshContent` here.

This looks like a copy/paste bug: `accessToken` is being assigned from `refresh_token`. As written, the later authorization check is using a refresh token as a bearer token, so the test isn't validating the intended behavior. It should likely read `access_token` instead.

Renaming the virtual event method from `MessageReceived(...)` to `MessageReceivedAsync(...)` is a source-breaking change for anyone subclassing `BearerTokenEvents` and overriding the old method. Since this type is public API, consider keeping the original virtual method as a forwarding shim (or otherwise preserving compatibility) and adding the `Async`-suffixed method alongside it.

The refresh token is generated from the original `user` variable, not from `signingInContext.Principal`. That means any principal changes made in `OnSigningIn` during a refresh (for example, reloading claims from the store or validating/replacing the principal) will affect the new access token but not the new refresh token. This can leave the two tokens out of sync and break the intended identity refresh flow. Consider creating the refresh ticket from `signingInContext.Principal` instead.

Renaming `BearerTokenProtector` to `TokenProtector` is a public API breaking change. Since this PR description frames the work as adding refresh-token support, it would be safer to preserve the existing property (for source/binary compatibility) and either alias it to the new name or mark the old member obsolete. Otherwise existing apps configuring `BearerTokenProtector` will silently stop compiling/upgrading cleanly.

Removing `BearerTokenOptions.BearerTokenProtector` from the public API list looks like a breaking change for anyone already using the preview API. If the implementation is intentionally renaming this member to `TokenProtector`, we should either keep the old property as an obsolete alias for compatibility or explicitly call out and justify the breaking change before shipping.
Minor XML doc typo: "written the to" should be "written to the". Since this is public API documentation, it's worth fixing before shipping to avoid carrying the typo into generated docs.

Removing `RefreshTokenKey` and the `RefreshToken` property from `AuthenticationProperties` appears to conflict with the issue/PR design, which explicitly adds `AuthenticationProperties.RefreshToken` for the `/refresh` flow. If this removal is intentional because the value is being stored elsewhere, the surrounding API/docs/examples need to be updated accordingly; otherwise this will break the proposed `HttpContext.SignIn(..., properties, ...)` refresh scenario and any code expecting to pass the refresh token via `AuthenticationProperties`.

Removing the `AuthenticationProperties.RefreshToken` entries from the unshipped API file looks inconsistent with the issue/PR scope, which explicitly adds this new public API. If `RefreshToken` still exists in the source, these lines should remain here so the API baseline matches the implementation; otherwise this will either hide an intended public API change or cause API validation/baseline drift.

This null-check throws `ArgumentException` and would turn a server misconfiguration into a 500 from the endpoint. Since `/refresh` is part of the public auth flow, it would be better to either ensure `RefreshTokenProtector` is always configured when the bearer scheme is registered, or fail with a more specific startup/configuration error rather than at request time.

Can `refreshTicket.Principal` ever be null for an invalid or malformed token? If so, `ValidateSecurityStampAsync(refreshTicket.Principal)` may throw before we reach the challenge path. It may be safer to explicitly guard `refreshTicket?.Principal` here and treat a missing principal the same as an invalid/expired refresh token.

By signing in with only the recreated principal, this endpoint no longer passes the incoming refresh token through `AuthenticationProperties`. That means any `BearerTokenOptions.OnSigningIn` customization that relies on `Properties.RefreshToken` to detect refresh flows (as described in the API proposal) won't run as expected for `/identity/refresh`. Consider preserving the refresh token in `AuthenticationProperties` when calling `SignIn` so downstream bearer-token events can distinguish refresh from initial login.

This change removes the identity-specific `OnSigningIn` wiring that previously validated the security stamp and rebuilt the principal during refresh. Based on the issue/PR description, `AddIdentityBearerToken<TUser>()` is supposed to be the API that preserves that behavior when using bearer tokens without cookies. As written, any caller-provided `configureOptions` is passed straight through to `AddBearerToken(...)`, so refresh requests will no longer get identity integration unless something else reattaches it. Please make sure `HandleSigningIn<TUser>` is still registered here (and composed with any user-supplied `OnSigningIn` callback rather than overwritten), otherwise `/refresh` can bypass the intended security-stamp validation and principal reload.

Registering `TimeProvider` in DI changes the test setup from configuring the bearer handler directly to relying on the handler resolving `TimeProvider` from services. That only works if `AddIdentityBearerToken`/`BearerTokenHandler` now consistently reads `TimeProvider` from DI. If any path still uses `options.TimeProvider`, this test could stop controlling token expiry and become flaky. Consider keeping an assertion or companion test that verifies the configured `TimeProvider` is actually honored during refresh/access token expiration.

This appears to reference the old `BearerTokenProtector` property name, but the proposed API and surrounding changes rename it to `TokenProtector`. If `BearerTokenOptions` has already been updated, this won't compile; if not, it leaves the implementation inconsistent with the new public API. Please assign `options.TokenProtector` here instead so the configure options match the renamed contract.

Throwing `NotImplementedException` from the parameterless `Configure` overload is risky because the options pipeline can invoke this method when configuring all instances. That would turn normal service resolution into a runtime failure. If unnamed configuration isn't supported, consider making this a no-op or delegating to `Configure(Options.DefaultName, options)` instead.

Removing `OnSigningIn`/`SigningInAsync` from `BearerTokenEvents` looks inconsistent with the issue/PR design, which explicitly relies on `BearerTokenOptions.OnSigningIn` to validate the security stamp and rebuild the principal during refresh. If this callback is being moved elsewhere, the corresponding API and all call sites/docs need to be updated in the same change; otherwise this breaks the intended refresh-token extensibility and likely introduces a source-compatibility regression for preview consumers.

This line now uses `Options.BearerTokenProtector` directly, but the previous implementation had a built-in fallback protector created from data protection. If `BearerTokenProtector` isn't initialized elsewhere, this will throw at sign-in time and break the default `AddBearerToken` flow. Please either preserve the lazy fallback here or ensure `BearerTokenProtector` is guaranteed non-null during options setup.

Similar to the access token path, this assumes `RefreshTokenProtector` is always configured. Since refresh support is being added as a default capability, a missing protector here would cause a null-reference during login/refresh. Consider adding the same kind of fallback/default initialization that previously existed for `TokenProtector`.

The refresh-token validation and `Events.SigningInAsync(...)` hook were removed from `HandleSignInAsync`, but the issue/PR description explicitly calls out `OnSigningIn` as the mechanism Identity uses to validate the security stamp and reload the principal during `/refresh`. Without invoking that callback here, refresh requests won't be able to swap the principal or reject invalid refresh tokens as designed. Please restore the refresh-token handling and `SigningInContext` event invocation before generating the response.

Changing this from a nullable auto-property to a non-nullable property that throws on get is a behavioral break for existing callers. Previously, consumers could inspect `TokenProtector` for `null` to determine whether the default protector should be created later; now any such access will throw before post-configuration runs. If the intent is still 'optional and framework-populated when not provided' as the XML docs say, consider keeping the property nullable (or exposing a separate internal accessor) so reading the option before initialization remains safe.

Same concern here: the docs describe this as optional and defaulted by the framework, but the getter now throws if accessed before configuration has populated it. That can surprise option validators, diagnostics, or app code that reads options during startup. Consider preserving nullable semantics publicly and only enforcing non-null at the point the handler actually needs to use the protector.

Changing this event ID from 10 to 1 is a breaking change for log consumers that filter, alert, or correlate on `EventId`. Since this PR is adding refresh-token support rather than redefining existing logging contracts, it would be safer to preserve the existing ID for `AuthenticationSchemeSignedIn` and assign a new unique ID to any new/changed log events instead of renumbering existing ones.

This hunk removes the newly proposed public API surface for `BearerTokenEvents.OnSigningIn`/`SigningInAsync` and `SigningInContext`, even though the issue/PR description depends on that callback for Identity security-stamp validation and principal reloading during refresh. If these members were intentionally renamed or moved, the corresponding replacement APIs should be listed here; otherwise this looks like an accidental API regression that would break the documented refresh-token extensibility scenario.

Updating the baseline from `System.Composition/*1.0.31*` to `*7.0.0*` is a significant version jump in the source-build allowlist. Can we confirm this is the exact version introduced by the interceptor-related dependency changes and that no additional Roslyn/System.Composition entries are needed? If the dependency can float to a different patch, this strict pattern may also cause source-build churn.

This updates `MicrosoftCodeAnalysisVersion_LatestVS` to a prerelease Roslyn build (`4.7.0-3.23314.3`). Since this property is explicitly described as the version used to represent the latest VS-compatible toolset, pointing it at a prerelease package may break assumptions in source-build or environments expecting a stable VS-aligned version. Please confirm that all consumers of `$(MicrosoftCodeAnalysisVersion_LatestVS)` can handle prerelease versions, or consider keeping this property on the stable VS version and using separate properties for the interceptor-specific compiler packages.

This change removes the trailing period from the diagnostic title. If this is intentional to standardize analyzer titles/messages, please make sure the corresponding baselines/tests that assert exact diagnostic text are updated as well, and consider applying the same convention consistently across all analyzer titles/messages in this resource file to avoid mixed punctuation styles.

Removing the final period changes the exact user-facing diagnostic message. Since analyzer tests often compare message text verbatim, this may cause unrelated test churn unless all affected expectations are updated. Also worth confirming whether message punctuation is being normalized intentionally across the analyzer resources, because this file will otherwise end up with inconsistent formatting.

Marking the entire test class as quarantined will suppress all tests in this file, including any unrelated scenarios, which can reduce coverage and make regressions harder to catch. If only specific cases are flaky due to #49126, consider applying `QuarantinedTest` at the individual test level instead so the rest of the suite continues to run normally.

Marking the entire test class as `[QuarantinedTest]` will skip all completion-provider coverage in this file, which can easily mask unrelated regressions. If only specific scenarios are flaky due to the interceptor/compiler changes, consider quarantining the individual test methods instead, or add a TODO/removal condition so this doesn't become a permanent blanket disable.

The generated interceptor method name is derived only from `HttpMethod`, `LineNumber`, and `CharacterNumber`. That can still collide when a single source location produces multiple valid endpoints (for example, if the same invocation is seen in multiple generator passes/partials or if locations are reused across generated trees). Since all interceptor definitions are emitted into the same generated type, a duplicate name here would break compilation. Consider including a more stable unique component (for example a hash of the file path or endpoint signature) in the emitted method name.

Defining `InterceptsLocationAttribute` unconditionally in `System.Runtime.CompilerServices` can cause duplicate type definitions if multiple generators in the same compilation emit the same helper, or if the BCL later provides this attribute. Consider guarding emission behind a symbol/type-existence check or generating it in a way that avoids collisions across generated files.

This suppresses IL2026/IL3050 whenever the endpoint model has no RDG diagnostics, but it doesn't verify that an interceptor was actually generated for this specific invocation. If code generation is skipped later in the pipeline (for example due to emit-time constraints, feature gating, or generator failures outside `Endpoint.Diagnostics`), we could end up hiding real linker/AOT warnings for the framework `Map` call. Consider tying the suppression to the same condition used to emit the interceptor, or otherwise proving that generation succeeded for this location before reporting the suppression.

This cast chain assumes every intercepted invocation is a simple member-access call like `endpoints.MapGet(...)`. That can break for conditional access, generic-name/member-binding forms, or any syntax shape where `operation.Syntax` is still an `InvocationExpressionSyntax` but `Expression` is not a `MemberAccessExpressionSyntax`, causing an `InvalidCastException` during generation. Since `InterceptsLocation` is very sensitive to exact source coordinates, it would be safer to derive the column from the actual invoked expression/token (or at least guard these casts and report a diagnostic when the syntax shape is unsupported).

Switching equality to `SymbolEqualityComparer.IncludeNullability` changes endpoint parameter identity so that `string` and `string?` are now treated as different signatures. That seems reasonable for the new delegate-casting/interceptor flow, but it also means any caches/deduplication keyed by `EndpointParameter` may stop coalescing handlers that previously shared generated code. Please verify the downstream grouping logic intentionally wants nullability to participate here, otherwise this could increase duplicate generation or break reuse assumptions.

We're now tracking `HasDefaultValue`, but this type's equality/hash code still ignore both `HasDefaultValue` and `DefaultValue`. If generated signatures or delegate casts depend on defaulted parameters being distinguishable, two parameters with the same type/name/ordinal but different default-value semantics could still compare equal and be merged incorrectly. Consider whether these new fields should participate in equality as well.

This new validation only accepts invocations where the route-handler parameter type is exactly `System.Delegate`. That matches the new interceptor-generated overloads, but it will reject existing framework overloads and any intermediate states where the handler parameter is still a concrete delegate type (for example `Func<...>`). If this helper is used before interception has been resolved, it could cause the generator to skip valid endpoint mappings entirely. Consider either documenting that this method must only run on already-intercepted invocations, or broadening the check to also recognize delegate-typed symbols derived from `System.MulticastDelegate`/`System.Delegate` as appropriate.

Appending `= {p.DefaultValue}` directly into the emitted lambda signature looks risky for source generation. `DefaultValue` may not already be valid C# source for all parameter kinds (e.g., strings needing escaping/quoting, chars, enums, `default`, `null`, or culture-sensitive formatting for numerics). Since this string is now used as generated code rather than just a delegate type name, it would be safer to route default-value emission through a dedicated formatter that produces valid C# literals.

This generated baseline now emits `InterceptsLocationAttribute` as a `file`-scoped type. That matches the issue description, but it also changes the expected accessibility/shape from the previously documented `internal sealed class`. If the generator is intentionally relying on C# 11 `file` types here, it would be good to make sure the test matrix only runs on language versions/toolsets that support `file`, otherwise these baselines will fail to compile on older compilers.

The attribute is no longer indented with the surrounding members, unlike the other generated methods. Since this is a baseline file, even whitespace drift can hide real generator regressions and make snapshots noisy. Consider fixing the emitter so the attribute is aligned consistently with the method declaration.

The issue description specifies emitting `InterceptsLocationAttribute` as an `internal sealed class`, but this baseline now generates it as `file sealed class`. If the generator intentionally changed visibility, the issue/spec and all baselines should be updated consistently; otherwise this may indicate the generated output no longer matches the intended contract for the temporary attribute definition.

Formatting looks off here: the attribute is not indented like the surrounding members, unlike the previous interceptor method above. Since this is a snapshot for generated code, inconsistent indentation can make baseline diffs noisy and may point to a codegen formatting bug.

Defining `InterceptsLocationAttribute` as `file` scoped inside the `System.Runtime.CompilerServices` namespace may not match the intended shape from the issue description, which calls for an `internal sealed class`. A `file` type is only visible within this generated file, so if any generated code is split across files or if tests/assertions expect the documented accessibility, this could cause inconsistencies. Please confirm that `file` is required here and that all interceptor usages remain in the same file.

This replaces the previous `SourceKey` metadata with a `GeneratedCodeAttribute`. If any runtime behavior, diagnostics, or tests still rely on source-location metadata being attached to the endpoint builder, this is a functional change rather than a pure refactor. It would be worth verifying that endpoint metadata consumers no longer depend on the old source key information before removing it from generated output.

The issue description calls for emitting `InterceptsLocationAttribute` as an `internal sealed class`, but this baseline now uses `file sealed class`. If the generator intentionally changed visibility, it would be good to confirm this matches the compiler's lookup requirements and update the design/docs accordingly; otherwise the baseline may be masking a mismatch between the intended generated API and the actual output.

Defining `InterceptsLocationAttribute` as `file` scoped inside `System.Runtime.CompilerServices` may not match the intended generated shape from the issue description, which calls for an `internal sealed` type in that namespace. A `file` type is only visible within this generated file, so if any generated code is split across files or if tests compare against the documented contract, this could become a compatibility/maintainability mismatch. Please confirm the generator and baselines consistently use the same accessibility model.

This metadata addition appears to replace the previous `SourceKey` metadata with `GeneratedCodeAttribute`. If any runtime behavior, diagnostics, or tests still rely on source-location metadata being attached to the endpoint builder, this is a functional change rather than a pure refactor. It would be worth verifying that removing the source key metadata is intentional and that no downstream code depends on it.

Defining `InterceptsLocationAttribute` as a `file`-scoped type inside the `System.Runtime.CompilerServices` namespace is likely to make the generated baseline invalid C#. `file` types can only be referenced from the same source file, but the interceptor method using `[InterceptsLocation(...)]` is emitted later under a different namespace block. Since the issue description calls for an `internal sealed class InterceptsLocationAttribute`, this should probably remain `internal` rather than `file` to ensure the attribute is visible to the generated interceptor methods.

This generated metadata no longer adds the `SourceKey` entry that was previously emitted for the endpoint. If any tests or runtime behaviors still rely on source-location metadata being present on `EndpointBuilder.Metadata`, this baseline change could mask a regression. Please confirm that replacing it with only `GeneratedCodeAttribute` is intentional everywhere and that source-location-based assertions have been updated accordingly.

Defining `InterceptsLocationAttribute` as a `file`-scoped type inside the `System.Runtime.CompilerServices` namespace may prevent the generated interceptor method in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.Generated` from resolving `[InterceptsLocation(...)]`, because `file` types are only visible within the declaring file. If the real generator emits the attribute into a separate generated file, the interceptor methods won't be able to reference it. Please make sure the attribute is emitted into the same generated file as the interceptor methods, or relax the accessibility so the attribute is visible where it's used.

Since this file is a checked-in baseline, whitespace changes can create noisy diffs and make generated output harder to compare. It would be good to keep the emitted indentation consistent with the rest of the generated members.

This test name doesn't appear to cover the regression described in the issue. The bug is about multiple `Map*` calls on the same source line colliding, but this test only verifies identical interceptors coming from different files. Consider adding a test that places two `MapGet` calls on the same line in a single source file and asserts generation/execution succeeds, otherwise the original edge case may still regress unnoticed.

Using a shared `ParseOptions` with `LanguageVersion.Preview` and the `InterceptorsPreview` feature flag changes the language mode for every test built on this base class, not just the interceptor-specific scenarios. That can mask regressions or make unrelated tests depend on preview syntax/features. Consider scoping these parse options to only the tests that require interceptors, or at least documenting why all derived tests must run under preview mode.

Filtering out `GeneratedCodeAttribute` here changes the test from excluding a very specific implementation detail (`SourceKey`) to excluding a broad, standard attribute type. If the endpoint metadata can legitimately include user- or framework-supplied `GeneratedCodeAttribute` instances, this may hide regressions by dropping real metadata from the assertion. Consider filtering the exact generated marker introduced by this change (or asserting on the expected generated metadata separately) rather than excluding all `GeneratedCodeAttribute` values.

Setting `IsTrimmable` on the sample changes its publish/runtime characteristics beyond the interceptor fix described in this PR. Unless this sample is specifically being used to validate trimming compatibility, this can introduce trim-analysis warnings or make the sample fail under trimming for reasons unrelated to the issue being fixed. Consider keeping this out of the sample project or adding the corresponding trim-safe annotations/tests if trimming support is intentional.

Adding `InterceptorsPreview` here enables a preview compiler feature for this project. Since this is a library project, that can have broader build/packaging implications and may require corresponding SDK/version guards or repo-wide configuration to avoid breaking consumers/build legs that don't support the feature. Please confirm this project actually needs the preview flag directly (rather than inheriting it centrally), and consider documenting or conditioning it on the appropriate SDK/version property.

Iterating a `Dictionary` here makes the output order dependent on hash/insertion behavior rather than explicitly defined. For incremental generators, unstable ordering can cause unnecessary churn or nondeterministic output between runs. If downstream code depends on a consistent sequence, consider preserving the original encounter order explicitly or sorting the grouped results before returning them.

This new helper no longer accepts a `TextSpan` and now normalizes `tree.FilePath` directly, which changes behavior compared to the previous implementation that used `GetMappedLineSpan(span)` to honor `#line` mappings. Since the issue here is about distinguishing multiple `Map*` calls on the same source line, dropping span-based mapped path resolution could regress scenarios where generated/source-mapped files rely on the logical mapped path for interceptor identity. Please verify that all call sites either intentionally want the physical syntax tree path, or preserve source-mapped path handling elsewhere.

This special-case only excludes `NavigationException` itself. If the redirect signal is ever wrapped (for example as an `InnerException` or inside an `AggregateException` from async flows), the error boundary path would still treat it as a normal error and could continue to block navigation. If the framework expects navigation-related exceptions to always bypass error boundaries, consider whether this check should also unwrap common wrapper exceptions or centralize that logic in `HandleException` so the behavior is consistent regardless of how the exception is surfaced.

This test currently only covers the happy-path redirect and browser history behavior. Since the bug is specifically about `ErrorBoundary` handling a `NavigationException` as an error, it would be stronger to also assert that no error UI is rendered after the click (for example, by checking that the error boundary fallback content is absent). Otherwise the test could still pass if the redirect happens after briefly entering an error state that this regression is meant to prevent.

This null guard fixes the reported `NullReferenceException`, but the PR description indicates there are no tests included. Since this method is reached from the `Form` getter, it would be good to add a regression test covering `new FormFeature(IFormCollection)` and verifying that accessing `Form` returns the provided collection without touching `_request`-dependent antiforgery logic. That will help prevent future refactors from reintroducing this edge case.

This helper is now checking `[Required]` on action parameters/request-body parameters, but the issue and PR title are specifically about schema properties marked with `[Required]`. `ApiParameterDescription.ParameterDescriptor` won't cover POCO/record members inside the generated schema, so this change alone doesn't make those properties appear in the schema's `required` list. Please make sure the property-level schema generation path also inspects `RequiredAttribute`; otherwise this fix will only affect operation parameters and request bodies, not the reported bug.

This replaces the configured `TypeInfoResolver` with `jsonOptions.Value.SerializerOptions.TypeInfoResolver?.WithAddedModifier(...)`. If the original resolver is `null`, the modifier won't run at all, so `[Required]` still won't be reflected for apps relying on the default reflection-based contract resolver. Consider falling back to `new DefaultJsonTypeInfoResolver().WithAddedModifier(...)` (or otherwise preserving default behavior) when no resolver is configured.

This test only covers top-level parameters, but the issue and PR are about required handling on generated schemas/properties. Since `[Required]` on parameters may already be interpreted differently from `[Required]` on object members, this can leave the main regression untested. Consider adding a companion test that verifies a complex type with a `[Required]` property is emitted with that property in the schema's `required` list, so the scenario from the issue is explicitly covered.

This assertion depends on the iteration order of `schema.Required`, which is not guaranteed by the OpenAPI model and can make the test flaky if the required-property collection is emitted in a different order. Consider asserting set membership instead (for example, checking count plus `Contains("title")` and `Contains("completed")`) so the test validates the behavior without coupling to ordering.

This assertion assumes a deterministic ordering of `schema.Required`. In OpenAPI, `required` is effectively a set, and the generator may not guarantee insertion order across implementations or future refactors. Using an order-independent assertion (for example, asserting the count and checking membership) would make the test less brittle while still validating the intended behavior.

Applying `parameterDescription.DefaultValue` here only covers API parameters. The issue description is specifically about schema properties annotated with `[DefaultValue]`, and this method is in the parameter-processing path (`ApplyParameterInfo`). Unless there is a separate property/schema-generation hook elsewhere in the PR, this change won't populate `default` for object properties in generated component schemas. Please verify the default is also applied when generating property schemas from model metadata/attributes, not just for parameters.

Using `LastOrDefault()` here makes the selected `DefaultValueAttribute` depend on reflection enumeration order, which is not guaranteed and can vary across runtimes. If multiple attributes are present, this could produce nondeterministic OpenAPI output. Consider either using `GetCustomAttribute<DefaultValueAttribute>()`/`SingleOrDefault()` to enforce a single attribute, or explicitly documenting and validating the precedence rule.

This assertion expects a `float` default value to be emitted as `OpenApiInteger`. That seems inconsistent with OpenAPI numeric typing and could mask a serialization bug if non-integral float defaults are later added. Consider asserting `OpenApiFloat`/`OpenApiDouble` (or adding a case like `3.5f`) so the test verifies the numeric type as well as the value.

The test name doesn't match what it's actually validating. This case now asserts a `DefaultValue`-driven schema default (`property.Value.Default`) in addition to validation attributes, so the current name is misleading and makes it harder to understand the regression being covered for issue #56215. Consider renaming the test to mention `DefaultValueAttribute` explicitly, or splitting the default-value assertion into a dedicated test.

The test name still refers to `ValidationAttributes`, but this PR is specifically adding support for `DefaultValueAttribute`. Since the assertions now also verify `property.Value.Default`, the current name is misleading and makes it harder to understand what behavior regressed if this test fails. Consider renaming it to explicitly mention default values (or both validation and default-value attributes if that's the intent).

This now throws on any non-`--` token passed to `parseGivenArgs`, which is a behavior change from the previous `parse()` implementation that explicitly filtered out legitimate residue (for example build targets) before calling `parseArg`. If any caller accidentally passes mixed args instead of `getSkippedArgs()`, target parsing will now fail with an options syntax error. Consider either documenting/enforcing that `parseGivenArgs` only accepts skipped Starlark-option candidates, or preserving the old filtering here to make the method safer for reuse and tests.

Exposing `getSkippedArgs()` as a mutable `List` directly from `impl` can leak internal parser state to callers if `impl.getSkippedArgs()` isn't already immutable/defensively copied. Since the rest of this API tends to return immutable snapshots (for example `getAliases()` and `getResidue()`), consider returning `ImmutableList.copyOf(impl.getSkippedArgs())` here as well to preserve encapsulation and avoid accidental external mutation.

This returns the mutable `List<String>` stored inside the parser state. Exposing that directly makes it possible for callers to accidentally mutate internal option values after parsing, which can lead to hard-to-debug ordering/canonicalization issues. Consider returning an immutable copy/view instead (for example `ImmutableList.copyOf(...)`).

This changes the contract of `parseStarlarkOptions` for malformed single-dash Starlark flags from "leave it in residue" to "throw immediately". That may be correct with the new skipped-args handling, but it also broadens behavior beyond the ordering bug this PR is fixing. If this is intentional, it would be good to add or reference coverage showing that non-Starlark/target-like residue is still preserved where expected, so we don't accidentally regress callers that relied on invalid args remaining in `getResidue()`.

Using `options.split(" ")` here is brittle for test inputs because it produces empty tokens for repeated spaces and doesn't handle quoted arguments. Since this helper is now feeding args directly into `parseGivenArgs`, a malformed split could change parsing behavior independently of the feature under test. Consider using a whitespace-aware splitter (for example `Splitter.onPattern("\\s+").omitEmptyStrings()`) or passing a pre-tokenized list into this helper.

This test uses `--//f:color=green`, but the custom `color` build setting defined above doesn't declare any allowed values, so the override value isn't actually constrained by the rule. Since the bug report and fix are specifically about preserving override semantics for string flags with meaningful values, it would be stronger to define/validate the accepted values (for example via a `string_flag`-style implementation or explicit validation) so the test can't accidentally pass with an arbitrary string that wouldn't be valid in the real-world scenario.

The generated HTML link here looks malformed: the `href` value is missing the closing quote before the `>` (`href="../globals/module.html#use_extension>`). This will likely break the rendered Starlark docs for `use_extension`. Please close the attribute value, e.g. `href="../globals/module.html#use_extension"`.

Using `(Set<?>) raw` here is stricter than the existing list/map adapters and may fail if Gson materializes the delegate as another `Collection` implementation rather than a `Set`. Since JSON arrays don't preserve set semantics, deserialization is typically backed by a generic collection type. Consider accepting `Collection<?>` here (and in the copy function) and constructing the `LinkedHashSet` from that to avoid a potential `ClassCastException` during deserialization.

The Starlark API docs here are still placeholder text (`"foo"`).
