Since this introduces a new public method on `module_ctx`, leaving placeholder documentation will make the generated Starlark reference unclear and can block users from understanding the required semantics of `root_module_direct_deps` and `root_module_direct_dev_deps`. Please replace these with real descriptions before merging.

This parameter doc is also still a placeholder. Because the behavior of automatic `use_repo` fixups depends on the exact meaning of this list, the generated API docs should explain what repository names are expected here, whether they must be direct repos only, and how `None` is interpreted.

Same issue here: the parameter documentation is placeholder text. This should describe how `root_module_direct_dev_deps` differs from `root_module_direct_deps`, especially since callers need to know when a repo belongs in the dev-dependency set versus the regular dependency set.

This warning message embeds raw ANSI escape sequences for color/bold formatting. Bazel event handlers are not guaranteed to write to an ANSI-capable terminal, so these codes may show up literally in logs, IDEs, or machine-consumed output. It would be safer to avoid terminal control codes here, or gate them behind an output-capability check before adding styling.

Deriving `ModuleKey` by parsing `getLocation().file()` is fairly brittle: `Location` is primarily diagnostic metadata, and its file path format may not always be a canonical `<moduleKey>/MODULE.bazel` string (for example, synthetic locations, alternate path separators, or future changes in how module files are labeled). Since this method is now part of the model API, it would be safer to store the `ModuleKey` explicitly on `ModuleExtensionUsage` when the object is created instead of reconstructing it from the source location.

This records `exportedName` as a dev import, but the `imports` map is keyed by `localRepoName`. If the same exported repo is imported under different local aliases with different `dev_dependency` values, tracking only the exported name loses that distinction and can mark all aliases as dev-only. Please confirm the downstream consumers of `setDevImports(...)` expect exported names rather than local repo names; otherwise this should likely store `localRepoName` instead.

Using `indexOf('@')` makes the parser accept strings with additional `@` characters in the version segment (for example `foo@1.0@bar`), which `toString()` would never produce. That can lead to confusing `Version.parse(...)` errors and makes `fromString` less strict than the serialized format. Consider validating that there is exactly one separator (e.g. compare `indexOf('@')` and `lastIndexOf('@')`) and throw a dedicated parse error when the format is invalid.

This error message is misleading because the failure here is the absence of the `@` separator, not a version parsing problem. A more precise message that includes the input string would make debugging easier, especially since actual version parse failures are handled later by `Version.parse(...)`.

This evaluates the returned metadata for validation/side effects, but the result is discarded. If `ModuleExtensionMetadata.evaluate(...)` computes normalized metadata or fixup information needed later in extension resolution, dropping the return value here could make the new `extension_metadata` API effectively a no-op beyond validation. Please confirm whether `evaluate` is intentionally side-effect-only; if not, the evaluated metadata should likely be captured and propagated in the resulting SkyValue.

This assertion hard-codes ANSI escape sequences in the expected warning text. That makes the test brittle if event formatting changes (for example, if color output is disabled in tests or the warning text is later compared in a non-terminal context). It would be safer to assert on the semantic content of the message and either strip ANSI codes before comparison or check the buildozer command block separately.

This paragraph now describes the fallback as checking visibility "with respect to the `cc_library` target", but the issue/PR description says the fallback is checked relative to the target's package / rule usage. Referring to a target here is a bit imprecise for visibility semantics and could confuse readers into thinking visibility is evaluated against an individual target rather than the consuming package. Consider rewording this to explicitly say the package of the rule instance (or consuming target).

This unconditionally emits a summary for every completed command once the module is registered, even when remote execution/cache isn't actually enabled or when `actionContextProvider` failed to initialize. In those cases `stats` will still exist but the output may be misleading (for example, a zeroed summary on local-only builds). Consider guarding this with the same remote-enabled condition used to set up the remote components, or only registering/subscribing when remote is active.

This increments the remote cache hit counter before `downloadRemoteResults(...)` succeeds. If the download later throws `CacheNotFoundException`, the action falls through to execution, but it has already been counted as a cache hit, which will skew the reported stats. Consider moving `stats.addRemoteCacheHit()` to after a successful download, or otherwise only recording the hit once the cached result is actually usable.
This method name doesn't follow Java naming conventions. Since this is a public API on a new class, `summary()` would be more idiomatic and consistent with the rest of the codebase.

If there are no remote actions (`remote == 0`), this will print `x/0 remote actions cached`, which is confusing and can happen when remote cache/execution is enabled but everything ran locally. Consider handling the zero-remote case explicitly so the summary remains meaningful.

The summary only reports cache hits among remote actions, but the issue asks for cache hit/miss statistics. Since `remote` here is `remote_exec + remote_cache_hit`, the number of misses is effectively `remote_exec`; consider surfacing that explicitly in the output so users can see both hits and misses.

These tests now pass `null` for the new constructor parameter everywhere, which means this suite doesn't exercise the new stats-reporting behavior introduced by the feature. Since the PR is specifically about printing remote cache statistics, it would be valuable to add at least one test here that injects a non-null stats collector / reporter and verifies the expected counters or output are updated.

This introduces a second `test_remote_stats` function with the same name as the one defined above. In shell, the later definition overrides the earlier one, so the first test case will never run. Please rename this fallback-specific test (for example, `test_remote_stats_with_fallback`) so both scenarios are exercised.

There appears to be an accidental `1:` prefix inserted before the shebang. That would break the script header and likely make the test file invalid/executable in the wrong way. Please remove this stray text so the file starts with `#!/bin/bash`.

This now calls `setExecutionInfo` unconditionally, which changes the previous behavior where execution info was only set when `execution_requirements` was explicitly provided. If `getFilteredExecutionInfo(...)` returns only the propagated tag-based requirements, this is fine, but if it can return an empty map for actions that previously had no execution info, please confirm that `setExecutionInfo(emptyMap)` is semantically equivalent to not calling it at all for all action types/builders. Otherwise this could introduce subtle behavior changes beyond the intended tag propagation.

Merging the user-provided `execution_requirements` with `putAll` means explicit action-level settings override propagated target tags. For the `no-*` tags introduced by this change, that allows a rule implementation to silently re-enable remote execution/sandboxing by passing a conflicting value, which seems at odds with the issue goal that target tags like `no-remote` should always take effect. Please clarify and enforce the intended precedence here (for example, reject conflicting keys or make propagated tags win).

This assertion hard-codes `"local"` as the propagated execution info, but the change described in the PR is specifically about propagating a limited set of target tags like `no-remote`, `no-cache`, `no-remote-cache`, `no-remote-exec`, and `no-sandbox`. Using `local` here makes the test intent unclear and may not actually validate the new behavior. Consider updating the fixture/setup and assertion to use one of the newly supported propagated tags so the test directly covers the feature introduced by this change.

This test uses `supports-worker`, but the tag added in `testFilteredExecutionInfoFromTags` is `supports-workers` (plural). Since the PR description says only a specific set of tags should be propagated (`no-remote`, `no-cache`, `no-remote-cache`, `no-remote-exec`, `no-sandbox`), this assertion is confusing and may be validating an unintended alias or typo rather than the intended filtering behavior. Consider aligning the spelling with the canonical execution requirement or replacing it with one of the explicitly supported tags to make the test unambiguous.

The test function name includes non-ASCII characters (`шт_кгду`). Shell test discovery and tooling in this repo typically assume simple ASCII identifiers, and unusual characters here can make the test harder to invoke/debug across environments. Consider renaming this to a descriptive ASCII-only name, e.g. `test_tags_propagated_to_run_with_exec_info`.

`ctx.actions.run_shell` expects `command` to be a string, but this test passes a list. That makes the test invalid or at least not representative of the intended API usage. Please change this to a shell command string (for example, `command = "touch %s" % ctx.outputs.out.path`) so the test actually exercises tag propagation instead of failing on action construction semantics.

Including `volumetric` in the sort key changes the global ordering of clusterable lights. If any downstream code assumes the previous packing/grouping order (for example, offsets/counts for non-volumetric point/spot lights, shadowed lights, or stable GPU buffer layout), this can introduce subtle mismatches unless the corresponding consumers are updated in lockstep. Please double-check that all code reading the clustered light lists now understands this extra partitioning.

Prioritizing volumetric lights only after `is_spot_light` and `shadows_enabled` means a non-volumetric shadow-casting point light can still be kept ahead of a volumetric spotlight when the clusterable object limit is hit. Since this PR's goal is to make volumetric fog work for point/spot lights, that ordering may still evict relevant volumetric lights under pressure. Please confirm this priority is intentional, or consider moving `is_volumetric_light` earlier in the sort key so volumetric lights are preserved first.

The `VOLUMETRIC` flag is only set when `light.shadows_enabled && light.volumetric`, but the new counting logic above reserves slots for point lights when `(light.volumetric || light.shadows_enabled)`. That mismatch means a non-shadowing volumetric point/spot light can be counted toward the volumetric budget yet never receive the `VOLUMETRIC` flag here, which looks inconsistent with the PR goal of supporting volumetric fog for point/spot lights independently of shadowing. Please verify whether volumetric participation is intended to require shadows; if not, this condition should likely be based on `light.volumetric` alone (with matching count logic).

This uses `view_start_pos.z` to choose the clustered-light slice for the entire fog march, but the ray then samples many positions (`P_world`) at different depths. That means point/spot volumetric contribution is only gathered from the cluster containing the ray origin, so lights in later slices along the ray can be missed entirely. Consider recomputing the cluster index per step from the current sample position/depth, or otherwise iterating the relevant clusters intersected by the ray segment.

The spotlight is inserted with `VolumetricLight`, but unlike the point light it doesn't set an explicit `range`. Relying on the default here can make the example less predictable and may fail to clearly demonstrate volumetric fog for spot lights if the default attenuation distance is too small or changes in the future. Consider setting `range` explicitly to match the scene scale.

Pushing an empty `Vec` here changes the meaning of the conflict payload: downstream code can no longer distinguish between "all accesses conflict" and "there were only ignored component-specific conflicts". In the previous branch, an empty list meant "do not report", but here it now means "report a conflict with no component ids". If any diagnostics/UI logic assumes the vector contains concrete `ComponentId`s, this may produce confusing ambiguity messages or make `ignored_ambiguities` ineffective for `AccessConflict::All`. Consider propagating `AccessConflict` through `conflicting_systems` instead of collapsing `All` to `Vec::new()`.

Using the full panic string here makes the test quite brittle: any harmless change to the error formatting, fully-qualified type path, or help text/URL will fail the test even if the conflict detection still works correctly. Since this test is validating that `Query<EntityMut>` now triggers the aliasing panic, it would be more robust to assert on a stable substring (for example `expected = "error[B0001]"` or a shorter fragment mentioning `Query<...EntityMut...>`), or to capture the panic and check only the essential parts.

This line takes a reference to a temporary `String` created by `format!`, which is then used outside the match arm. That pattern is brittle and can easily run afoul of lifetime rules; it also makes `accesses` switch between `&str` and `&String`-coerced-to-`&str`. It would be safer and clearer to make `accesses` an owned `String` in both branches, e.g. `String::new()` for `All` and `format!(...)` for `Individual(...)`, then interpolate that owned value into the panic message.

Inserting `'state` at a fixed index assumes the first generic parameter is always the world lifetime. That holds for the happy path, but if the input struct has additional lifetimes or a different generic ordering, this can generate invalid generics or silently bind the wrong parameter. It would be safer to locate the actual world lifetime parameter explicitly (or prepend both synthetic lifetimes) instead of hard-coding index `1`.

This replaces the *first* generic parameter with `'fetch`, which only works if the first parameter is guaranteed to be the world lifetime. The derive currently only checks that some lifetime exists, not that it is first or uniquely the world lifetime. For structs with multiple lifetimes, this can rewrite the wrong one and produce incorrect generated code. Consider identifying and rewriting the specific world lifetime parameter instead of `first_mut()`.

Picking the first generic parameter as the world lifetime is brittle. A user can legally declare multiple lifetimes or place type params before the relevant lifetime, and this derive will either reject valid input later or generate incorrect code. Since the feature is documented around a named `'w` lifetime, it would be better to validate the expected lifetime shape explicitly (for example, exactly one lifetime for non-filter queries) and emit a targeted error if the input doesn't match.

The public derive name here looks inconsistent with the PR/issue context, which describes a `WorldQuery` derive macro. Exposing `#[proc_macro_derive(Fetch)]` risks leaking an internal implementation detail into the public API and may not match the intended user-facing syntax (`#[derive(WorldQuery)]`). If this is meant to be the end-user macro, consider renaming it here (and similarly for `FilterFetch`) or adding a clear compatibility/export layer so the documented derive name is actually available.

The derive macro name in these docs looks inconsistent with the PR/API naming. This section says `derive@super::Fetch` / `#[derive(Fetch)]`, but the feature being introduced is `WorldQuery` derive, and the PR examples use `#[derive(WorldQuery)]`. If `Fetch` is only an internal/legacy alias, these docs will be confusing for users and may become stale quickly. Consider updating the documentation to reference the public derive users are expected to write, or explicitly document the alias if both are intentionally supported.

The derive macro referenced here appears to be inconsistent with the PR description and examples, which introduce `#[derive(WorldQuery)]` plus `#[world_query(filter)]` for custom filters. Pointing users to `derive@super::FilterFetch` may be incorrect or at least confusing if `FilterFetch` is not the intended public derive entry point. Consider updating this documentation to reference the actual supported derive path for filters.

This doc comment again suggests deriving `FilterFetch` directly, but the surrounding PR context indicates filters are defined via `#[derive(WorldQuery)]` with `#[world_query(filter)]`. If direct `FilterFetch` derive is not actually exposed or recommended, this example will mislead users and may fail to compile as written.

The example uses `#[derive(FilterFetch)]`, which seems out of sync with the new API described in the PR (`#[derive(WorldQuery)]` + `#[world_query(filter)]`). Please verify this example against the actual exported macros; otherwise the docs will direct users toward a non-existent or non-public derive.

The example and comments still refer to `Fetch` / `FilterFetch` derive macros, but this PR is introducing `WorldQuery` derive support. Using the old names here is likely to confuse users and may become incorrect if those derives are not part of the public API anymore. Please update the example, derives, and surrounding documentation to consistently use `WorldQuery` (and the corresponding filter form/attributes) so the example matches the feature added by this PR.

Using `::std::boxed::Box` here makes the generated code depend on `std`, while the rest of this helper prefers `core` where possible. If `bevy_reflect_derive` is ever used in a `no_std` context, this will reintroduce hygiene/compatibility issues. Consider using `::alloc::boxed::Box` instead (with the appropriate crate availability assumptions) to keep the expansion as portable as possible.

Using `TypeInfo::Value(ValueInfo::new::<Self>())` for `dyn Reflect` may be misleading now that `get_type_info()` is expected to match the static `Typed::type_info()` result. A `dyn Reflect` value can wrap a struct/tuple/list/etc., but this static implementation always reports the trait object itself as a `Value`, which does not describe the underlying reflected type. If this impl is only needed to satisfy trait bounds, consider documenting that `Typed for dyn Reflect` is metadata about the trait object type rather than the erased value, or avoid exposing it in places where callers may confuse it with the concrete type info.

Using `TypeInfoCell::non_generic()` inside the tuple macro looks problematic because this `Typed` impl is generic over `($($name,)*)`. A non-generic cell would be shared across all monomorphizations of the same impl, so the first instantiated tuple type could cache `TypeInfo` that is then incorrectly reused for other element types. This should likely use `TypeInfoCell::generic()` so each concrete tuple type gets distinct cached metadata.

The `?Sized` bound conflicts with the use of `TypeId::of::<T>()` below in the generic branch, because `TypeId::of` requires `T: 'static + ?Sized` and this method currently doesn't express the `'static` requirement. As written, this can make the signature misleading or fail for some callers. Consider tightening the bound to `T: Any` (which already implies `'static`) or otherwise adding the explicit `'static` requirement here.

This adds the `AssetChanges<A>` resource's archetype component to the query's archetype access, but `archetype_id` is captured once in `init_state` from the resource storage at that moment. Resource archetype/component IDs can change when the resource archetype is rebuilt (for example after resource insertion/removal patterns), which would leave this cached ID stale and make access tracking incorrect. It would be safer to avoid caching the resource archetype component ID here, or recompute it from the current world/resource storage when needed instead of storing it in `AssetChangedState`.

Using `ticks.this_run()` here means the recorded asset change tick reflects when `asset_events` runs, not when the asset was actually mutated. If an asset is modified after a system with `AssetChanged<A>` has already run in the same schedule, this can make the change appear as if it happened *after* that system's `last_run`, causing it to be reported again on the next frame even though the modification happened before the system's previous execution. Please double-check the scheduling/tick semantics here and document or guard against this potential off-by-one-frame behavior.

Re-exporting `AssetChanged` and `AssetOrHandleChanged` from the prelude as `#[doc(hidden)]` makes these new public query filters effectively available to users while hiding them from generated docs. Since the PR changelog presents these as user-facing additions, this can make discovery and documentation inconsistent. If these types are intended to be public API, consider removing `#[doc(hidden)]` (or documenting why they should stay hidden).

This assertion is duplicated with the one immediately above (`StructLabel { a: 0, b: 0 }.intern() != EnumLabel::Struct { a: 0, b: 0 }.intern()`). Keeping the same check twice doesn't add coverage and makes the test noisier. Consider replacing this with a missing case or removing the duplicate.

These new tests only cover the happy path for `Eq`/`Hash`-compatible labels. Given the PR's goal is to restore support for value-carrying labels, it would be useful to add at least one assertion that the interned value can actually be used in schedule APIs (for example, inserting/running/removing a schedule keyed by `TupleLabel(…)` or `StructLabel { … }`). Right now this verifies pointer/equality behavior in isolation, but not that the derived labels work end-to-end with the scheduling machinery.

This assertion is duplicated a few lines later with the exact same operands. Duplicating the same `assert_ne!` doesn't add coverage and makes the test noisier; consider replacing one of them with a missing case or removing it.

This `assert_ne!` is also a duplicate of the earlier `StructSet` vs `EnumSet::Struct` check. It would be better to remove the duplicate or use the slot for a distinct behavior that isn't currently exercised.

Switching from `log::warn!`/`log::debug!` to the `tracing` macros changes which logging backend receives these messages. If the rest of this module or crate still relies on the `log` facade, this can make these diagnostics disappear unless a tracing subscriber is configured. Please confirm the crate consistently uses `tracing` here, or consider keeping the existing facade for compatibility.

The wording here is a bit misleading: `SRes` values in `RenderCommand::render` are still dereferenceable, but deref coercion often won't produce the expected type because of the `'static` wrapper/lifetime mismatch. Saying they are not "automatically dereferenced" can read as if `Deref` is unavailable entirely. Consider clarifying that method calls and coercions may require an explicit `into_inner()` to obtain `&T` due to lifetime limitations, rather than implying deref never happens.

This warning is a bit vague for API docs. Since the function is specifically `push_child_unchecked`, it would be clearer to document the concrete invariant being violated here: calling this with the same `child` more than once can insert duplicate entries into `parent`'s `Children`, which may break assumptions in hierarchy traversal/removal code. Consider explicitly stating that duplicates are allowed by this helper and that callers must ensure the child is not already present.

Reducing `MAX_DIRECTIONAL_LIGHTS` to `1` for all WebGL builds fixes the Adreno crash, but it also changes rendering behavior globally on every WebGL device, including GPUs that were reported to work correctly (e.g. Mali). This looks like a broad workaround for a vendor-specific shader/compiler issue. If possible, gate this on the actual backend/adapter at runtime or document the intentional regression clearly, because scenes using multiple directional lights on WebGL will now silently lose support.

This ignores the error from `url.Parse`, which means an invalid `u` will silently produce a `Remote` with a nil or partially parsed URL. Since this constructor is likely to be used for user-provided `--repo`/URL input, it would be safer to return `(*Remote, error)` and let callers handle malformed values explicitly.

Returning `remotes[0]` changes the previous selection behavior from preferring named remotes (`upstream`, `github`, `origin`) to whatever order the resolver happens to return. If `remoteResolver.Resolver()` preserves git config order, commands may now pick a different base remote than before in multi-remote repos, which could be a behavioral regression. Consider keeping the explicit name-based preference here, or document/guarantee that the resolver already sorts remotes with the same priority.

If `getConfig()` fails, this error is returned but not cached in `remotesError`, unlike the earlier `readRemotes()` and empty-remotes cases. That means subsequent resolver calls will keep retrying config loading instead of behaving consistently with the other failure paths. Consider assigning `remotesError = err` before returning here.

This assertion only checks the count, but the behavior being added is host-aware remote resolution. To make the test more robust, consider also asserting which remotes were excluded (for example that `origin` on `github.com` is filtered out) and, if relevant, that all returned remotes belong to the configured host. That would better protect the intended GHE-selection behavior from regressions.

This error text is clearer, but it hard-codes a very specific remediation (`clear it`) and assumes the caller is trying to save auth data. `CheckWriteable` is a generic config-layer method, so this message may be surfaced in contexts other than `gh auth login`, and on Windows `clear it` is also a bit shell-specific. Consider wording that is both action-oriented and context-neutral, e.g. telling the user to unset the environment variable before retrying, while preserving which variable caused the conflict.

If `limit` is 0 or negative, this loop behaves incorrectly: with `limit == 0` it still makes one API request because `len(workflows) < limit` is false only after entering setup, and with `limit < 0` `perPage` becomes invalid for the API. It would be safer to validate `limit` up front and either return an empty slice for `limit <= 0` or clamp `perPage` to a positive value.

The `contents` API returns base64 with line breaks in the `content` field. Decoding `result.Content` directly can fail with `illegal base64 data` for valid responses. Please normalize the payload first (for example by stripping `\n`/whitespace, or by using the `encoding` field from the response and handling `base64` explicitly) before calling `DecodeString`.

This prompt format still includes the workflow ID and full path (`name (ID: ..., path: ...)`), which conflicts with the issue's goal of showing a simpler, consistent disambiguation like `Foo Flow (foo.yml)`. Since `--web` already uses `filepath.Base(workflow.Path)` above, consider using the basename here as well and omitting the ID unless there's a strong reason to expose it in interactive prompts.

The shared test setup above this `t.Run` mutates `tt.opts` and initializes the prompt stubber before the subtest starts. This makes the loop more fragile and can lead to cross-test interference if these cases are ever run in parallel or if setup starts depending on subtest-local state. Consider moving the `httpmock`, `IO`, `BaseRepo`, and `prompt.InitAskStubber()` setup inside the `t.Run` body so each subtest is fully isolated.

This helper is exported, but the name is very generic and doesn't indicate that it returns a `glamour.TermRendererOption` specific to our markdown rendering. Since this package already exposes a small API surface, a more specific name (for example `WithNoIndentation` or `WithoutMarkdownIndentation`) would make call sites clearer and reduce the chance of future naming collisions.

These test-only mutator methods are now part of the production `AuthConfig` contract, which leaks testing concerns into the domain interface and forces every real implementation to expose behavior that shouldn't exist outside tests. Since the goal of this change is to improve `go generate` ergonomics, it would be cleaner to keep `AuthConfig` focused on runtime behavior and move these setters behind a separate test-only interface or helper/mock type.

This changes the editor cancellation behavior from the issue's acceptance criteria. The editor flow is supposed to allow canceling creation via an empty message, but here an empty first line is treated as an error (`title can't be blank`). If `TitledEditSurvey` returns empty title/body when the user clears the buffer, this will surface as a validation failure instead of a graceful cancel. Consider handling the fully-empty submission as a user cancel path, consistent with the editor-based issue create experience.

In editor mode this unconditionally overwrites any existing body state with the template body. That can discard body content populated earlier from `--body`, `--body-file`, `--fill`, recovery state, or other prefilled sources. It would be safer to only apply the template body when `state.Body` is still empty, similar to how the title is guarded just above.

This test only covers the happy path where `prefer_editor_prompt` enables editor mode, but it doesn't verify precedence when the user also provides explicit content flags like `--title`, `--body`, `--fill`, or `--fill-first`. Since editor mode changes how title/body are sourced, it would be useful to add a case asserting that explicit CLI input disables or conflicts with config-driven editor mode as intended. Otherwise a regression here could silently change command behavior without failing tests.

This description appears inaccurate for local installs. The issue report shows that `gh extension install .` creates a symlink for the extension directory itself (e.g. `~/.local/share/gh/extensions/gh-workflow-stats -> <repo>`), and execution then looks for `<repo>/gh-workflow-stats`. Saying the CLI manages it as a symlink "pointing to an executable file" could mislead users about what is actually linked. Consider rewording this to explain that the installed extension is a symlink to the local repository, and that `gh` expects to find an executable named after the repository in that repository root.

Raising `TimeoutError` here doesn't actually exercise the new behavior described in the PR. This test only verifies that the POST returns without crashing when the service handler raises, but it does not prove that a long-running service continues in the background or that the API returns after the 10s timeout window. Since the bug is about shielding service execution from client disconnect / long execution, consider making the listener `async`, blocking on an event or sleep, and asserting the HTTP response completes while the service still finishes afterward.

This changes the behavior for all WeatherFlow sensor descriptions, not just the ones that can legitimately report `None` during low-voltage conditions. If any entity using this helper expects `raw_data_conv_fn` to always run (for example to normalize/derive a value or handle other falsey inputs), returning early here may mask issues globally. Consider limiting the `None` handling to the affected sensors or documenting/asserting that every `raw_data_conv_fn` is safe to skip when the raw value is `None`.

Using the protected `_async_refresh()` here bypasses the normal config-entry first refresh flow and can also swallow non-HTTP failures that still leave `last_update_success` false. Since the goal is to avoid setup failure only when rain is unsupported for a location, it would be safer to keep the public refresh path and handle the specific unsupported-rain case in `_async_update_data_rain()` (or catch the library-specific exception there) rather than calling a private coordinator API.

This mock appears to register the EUR/SE3 request for the same date (`2024-11-05`) as the main SEK request, but the PR changes behavior to load adjacent days as well. If the integration now fetches previous/next-day data for all configured sensors/currencies, this fixture may be incomplete and could miss additional requests (for example EUR on 2024-11-04/2024-11-06), making the tests less representative of the new behavior or causing brittle failures when request patterns change.

This retry path no longer reinstalls the failure patch before the second `async_configure` call. In this test we first simulate a connection error, assert the form error, and then expect a successful reconfigure. However, after switching from the old method patching to the shared client/http fixtures, the success of this second call now depends on whatever state those fixtures happen to be left in after the first failed request. That makes the test less explicit and potentially flaky if the mocked error persists across calls. Consider resetting/redefining the mock response for the second configure step so the recovery path is guaranteed by the test itself.

The fixture was changed from `get_data` to `get_client`, but this test never uses `get_client` directly. If the fixture is only needed for side effects/setup, that coupling is not obvious here and makes the test harder to understand. Consider either using the fixture explicitly in the assertions/patch setup, or renaming it to `_get_client` (or similar) to make the intentional unused dependency clear.

This final recovery assertion no longer verifies that a fresh fetch actually happened after the previous error cases. In the old version the test asserted the mocked fetch was called again before checking the updated state. Since this PR changes the coordinator to load multiple days, it would be safer to also assert that the client was invoked here (with an expected call count/pattern), otherwise the state could pass by reusing cached data and the recovery path would remain untested.

This assertion no longer matches the test name or the bug scenario being covered. `test_sensor_no_previous_price` is now frozen at `2024-11-06T00:00:00+01:00` and expects a valid previous price, so it no longer verifies the midnight edge case where the previous price used to be unavailable. As written, both tests now assert essentially the same neighboring-hour values. Consider either renaming this test to reflect the new behavior, or better, keep/add a dedicated assertion for the original boundary condition (e.g. previous price at local midnight) so the regression described in the issue remains explicitly covered.

Using `if device.current_tariff` will treat a valid tariff value of `0` as missing and return `None`. If the upstream API can ever report `0` (or another falsy value), this sensor will incorrectly become unavailable. Consider checking explicitly for `None` instead, e.g. `str(device.current_tariff) if device.current_tariff is not None else None`.

The new entity name is very generic. Since this integration already exposes multiple electricity-related sensors, `Tariff` may be ambiguous in the UI and translations. Consider naming it something more specific like `Active tariff` or `Current tariff` to match the issue/PR description and make the entity clearer for users.

This fixture does not contain valid JSON: the file includes line-number prefixes (`1:`, `2:`, etc.) and the top-level object key is unquoted. If the tests load this via a JSON parser, they will fail or not exercise the intended code path. Please store the raw payload as valid JSON only, e.g. `{ "tr": 1, ... }`.

Adding `camera` here means camera entities will now be set up through the config-entry forwarding path. Please verify that the Ring camera platform has been migrated to support config entries (`async_setup_entry`/`async_unload_entry`) and no longer relies solely on YAML setup, otherwise camera entities may stop loading or be initialized twice during migration.

This setup path now silently skips cameras without an active Ring Protect subscription, whereas the previous implementation logged an error and created a persistent notification listing the affected devices. That changes user-visible behavior and may make it much harder to understand why some Ring cameras never appear after migration to config entries. Consider preserving at least a log warning or notification for unsubscribed cameras.

Using `SOURCE_USER` here changes the semantics of how this flow is initiated: this setup path is still triggered from YAML import, not an actual user action. Overloading the source just to suppress the discovery notification can break config-flow logic that branches on `SOURCE_USER` vs `SOURCE_IMPORT` (e.g. duplicate checks, abort reasons, migration/import handling). A safer fix would be to keep the real source and suppress the notification another way, or update the flow to treat import differently without creating a discovery prompt.

This concatenates the static and connected attribute lists on every property access. Since `device_state_attributes` can be called frequently, it would be better to iterate the two lists separately or define a module-level combined constant once to avoid repeated list allocation.

`config` is accepted by the base constructor now, but it is never assigned to `self.config` here. That leaves `SamsungTVLegacyBridge._get_remote()` calling `self.config.copy()` on `None`, which will break the first time a legacy remote is created. Please persist the constructor argument in the base class (or in every subclass consistently).

This unconditionally creates a remote via `_get_remote()` just to close it. If no connection exists yet, `close_remote()` can end up opening a new socket and then closing it, and it may also raise if setup/auth fails. Consider guarding on `self._remote is not None` and closing that instance directly instead.

This treats any successful HTTP response as `STATE_ON`, including non-200 responses from an unexpected service on that port. It would be safer to validate the response status (and possibly the expected Samsung API payload) before reporting the TV as on.

There appears to be a typo in the method name here: `acces_denied` is missing the second `s` from `access`. Since this looks like a callback intended to be invoked by the bridge layer, a misspelling can easily lead to the callback never being called unless the same typo is duplicated elsewhere. Renaming it to `access_denied` would make the intent clearer and reduce the risk of integration bugs.

The previous implementation explicitly handled connection/auth exceptions during state refresh and mapped them to `STATE_ON`/`STATE_OFF` appropriately. This new direct call assumes `SamsungTVBridge.get_state()` fully encapsulates all of that behavior. If the bridge lets exceptions propagate, `update()` can now fail and break entity polling instead of degrading to `STATE_OFF` or triggering reauth. Please verify that `get_state()` preserves the old exception handling semantics, or keep a defensive `try/except` here.

This removes all local retry/error handling around key transmission and delegates it entirely to the bridge. In the old code, transient websocket/broken-pipe failures triggered a reconnect attempt and auth-related errors were swallowed/logged so service calls didn't raise unexpectedly. If `SamsungTVBridge.send_key()` doesn't mirror that behavior, command delivery may regress for flaky connections. Consider documenting or enforcing the same retry/exception contract at this call site.

Using `update_query(self.extra_authorize_data)` allows subclasses to override core OAuth parameters generated just above (for example `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, `state`, or `response_type`) because `update_query` replaces existing keys. That makes the base flow easier to misuse and could break callback validation or weaken security guarantees. Consider merging in the opposite direction or explicitly filtering/validating `extra_authorize_data` so only provider-specific, non-core parameters can be added.

The result of `async_forward_entry_unload()` is ignored here. If one of the platform unloads fails, we still delete the coordinator data and return `True`, which can leave the config entry in a partially unloaded state. Consider collecting the results from `asyncio.gather(...)`, returning `all(results)`, and only cleaning up `hass.data[DOMAIN][entry.entry_id]` when every platform unload succeeded.

This now returns the raw attribute value directly, but several of the configured binary sensors are keyed by a specific `on_value` (for example the burner states and the OpenTherm error code). In the previous implementation those sensors compared the measurement against the configured trigger value before producing a boolean. With the current logic, any non-zero/non-empty value will be treated as `True`, which changes the semantics for sensors like `thermostat_info_burner_info_2` and `thermostat_info_ot_communication_error_0`. Please preserve the `on_value` comparison in the entity metadata and apply it here before handling inversion.

This still uses `device_state_attributes`, which has been deprecated in Home Assistant in favor of `extra_state_attributes`. Keeping the old property can break attribute exposure on newer core versions and is inconsistent with the rest of the rewritten integration. Please rename this property to `extra_state_attributes`.

There is a typo in the comment (`HAVC` -> `HVAC`). More importantly, leaving `set_hvac_mode` as a no-op means the entity advertises `hvac_modes` but ignores user requests to change them. Since only `HVAC_MODE_HEAT` is supported, consider implementing a guard that rejects unsupported modes explicitly (or making this method async and handling `HEAT`/`OFF` if the backend supports it) so service calls don't silently succeed without effect.

This logs the full `user_input` at error level, which is noisy and may expose user-selected agreement details in logs during a normal config flow. It also looks like leftover debugging since no error occurred here. Please remove this log line, or replace it with a targeted debug log that does not dump the full input.

`power_meter_reading_low` is mapped to the same measurement as tariff 1 (`meter_high`). That looks like a copy/paste bug and would make both entities report the same value. Should this be `meter_low` instead?

This assumes `CONF_WEBHOOK_ID` is always present in `entry.data`, but `unregister_webhook()` can also be called on shutdown or during partial setup/failed registration paths. If the webhook ID was never stored, this will raise a `KeyError` while Home Assistant is stopping. Consider guarding this with `.get(CONF_WEBHOOK_ID)` and only calling `webhook_unregister` when an ID exists.

Directly mutating `self.coordinator.last_update_success` is brittle here. `DataUpdateCoordinator` treats this as internal state tied to refresh cycles, and setting it manually from a service/entity call can leave the coordinator in an inconsistent availability state until the next refresh. Consider exposing availability through the integration's own state handling or triggering a proper coordinator refresh/failure path instead of writing this attribute directly.

Pinning the new dependency to `toonapi==0.1.0` looks risky for such a large rewrite, especially since this PR depends on new OAuth/webhook/push behavior. If any follow-up fixes land in the library, Home Assistant will be stuck on the initial release. Please double-check that `0.1.0` is the intended published version and that it already contains all functionality used by this integration, or bump to the exact release that matches the code here.

The `via_device` target here points to `(DOMAIN, agreement_id, "meter_adapter")`, but this file does not define any device with that identifier. In Home Assistant, `via_device` should reference an existing device identifier tuple, otherwise the device graph/parent-child relationship won't resolve correctly. Please either add a corresponding meter adapter device entity or point this to an identifier that is actually created (for example the display/root device if that's intended).

Same concern as above: this `via_device` references `(DOMAIN, agreement_id, "meter_adapter")`, but there is no matching device definition in this module. If no entity creates that device, Home Assistant can't attach this device under its parent. Consider referencing an existing identifier or introducing the missing meter adapter device.

This POST response is never closed. In Home Assistant we generally use `async with session.post(...) as resp:` so the connection is released back to the pool even when `raise_for_status()` or JSON parsing fails. Leaving it as a bare awaited request can leak connections under repeated token refresh/auth flows.

Using a set literal here makes entity creation order non-deterministic. That can lead to unstable ordering in the entity registry/UI and make tests harder to reason about. Consider using a list/tuple instead, like the solar and boiler sections below.
This is also iterating over a set, so the order of created gas sensors is not guaranteed. For consistency and deterministic entity ordering, this should be a list/tuple rather than a set.

The return type here is annotated as `Optional[str]`, but sensor states in this integration appear to include numeric values as well. Narrowing the type to `str` is misleading and may hide type issues; consider using a broader type (for example `StateType`/`Optional[StateType]`) that matches Home Assistant sensor state expectations.

Turning the holiday mode switch off currently sets the active state to `ACTIVE_STATE_AWAY` while enabling the program. That looks inconsistent with the switch semantics: disabling holiday mode should likely restore the normal thermostat state/program rather than explicitly putting Toon into away mode. As written, users may end up in away mode after turning holiday mode off. Please verify the API contract here and use the appropriate active state for returning to normal operation.

This test requirements change swaps `toonapilib` for a completely different package name, but the pinned version is `0.1.0`, which looks like an initial release for a newly introduced dependency. Given this PR rewrites the integration around the new library, it would be safer to pin to a version that includes the exact API surface and auth fixes used by the integration/tests, or at least confirm that `requirements_all.txt`/`manifest.json` are aligned to the same package and version. Otherwise CI may pass/fail inconsistently or future installs may break if the integration code expects behavior added after `0.1.0`.

This assertion hard-codes the rendered agreement label (`"1 - None None, None"`), which looks derived from optional address/name fields being `None`. That makes the test brittle to harmless formatting changes in the config flow and also normalizes a user-facing string containing `None`. Consider constructing the `Agreement` with realistic display fields or asserting/selecting by a stable identifier instead of the formatted label.

Using `> 1` assumes the ping utility's exit-code semantics are consistent across all supported platforms. That may be true for the Linux `ping` invocation shown in the issue, but other implementations can use different non-zero codes for packet loss/unreachable vs actual execution errors. Since this integration already has platform-specific command construction, it would be safer to either document and gate this behavior per platform or inspect stderr/output to distinguish expected unreachable results from real command failures. Otherwise we risk suppressing legitimate errors on some environments.

These pytest tests no longer set up the `zone` component / `zone.home` and `zone.work` entities before calling `async_setup_component`, unlike the previous `setUp()` which did this for every test. Since many assertions depend on those zones existing, this can make the tests fail or become order-dependent depending on shared fixture state. Please add a fixture (or call `config_zones(hass)` before setup) so each test has the same baseline as the old `unittest` version.

This test mutates the module-level `sh.EXECUTE_LIMIT` and only restores it at the very end. If any assertion above fails, the global timeout will leak into later tests and make the suite order-dependent/flaky. Please wrap this in a `try/finally` or use `patch.object(sh, "EXECUTE_LIMIT", 0.02)` as a context manager so the original value is always restored.

Patching `DemoLight.async_turn_on` at the class level affects all demo light entities in this test, including `light.kitchen_lights`. That means both service calls can block on `turn_on_wait`, which makes the test behavior broader than the comment suggests and more sensitive to implementation details. If the intent is to simulate a single slow entity, consider patching only the `light.ceiling_lights` entity instance instead of the whole class.

Switching this to `while self._tasks` fixes the immediate leak, but it also changes the synchronization semantics: any task that appends a new entry to `self._tasks` while this loop is running will now be awaited as part of setup, potentially extending setup indefinitely if entities keep scheduling more additions. If that is intentional, it would be good to document it here and add a regression test covering both the GC case and the possibility of chained `async_add_entities()` calls during setup.

Using only the thermostat identifier here changes the humidifier entity's unique ID from the previous `...-humidifier` format to the same base identifier used by the parent thermostat/device. Even if entity domains differ, changing an existing `unique_id` can break entity registry continuity and user customizations on upgrade. Consider preserving the old suffix or otherwise handling migration explicitly.

This module-level string is not a valid docstring because it appears after an import, so it becomes a no-op string literal. If this is intended as a section comment, prefer a `# Common pytest fixtures` comment instead; if it should document the module, move it to the top of the file before imports.

This fixture assumes `data.ecobee` already exists on a plain `Mock`, which can make the test setup less explicit and easier to break if attribute access changes. Consider assigning `data.ecobee = mock.Mock()` first, then stubbing `get_thermostat` on that nested mock so the fixture structure is clearer and more intentional.

Re-exporting imported fixtures via `__all__` is unusual in pytest test modules and may not actually be needed for fixture discovery here. Since the fixtures are already imported into the module namespace, pytest can use them without `__all__`. Adding this can also be misleading because `__all__` only affects `from module import *`, not fixture registration. Consider removing this unless there is a specific import pattern elsewhere that depends on it.

This module-level `__all__` looks unnecessary here and can interfere with pytest fixture discovery/import behavior. Since this is a test module rather than a package API surface, exporting only these names doesn't add value and may make the fixtures less obvious to maintainers. Consider removing it unless there's a specific import pattern depending on it.

This triple-quoted string is being used as a standalone expression, not a comment, so it has no testing value and is a bit misleading. More importantly, the previous test covered invalid modes with `pytest.raises(ValueError)`, but that assertion was dropped in this rewrite. Please replace these string literals with normal comments and restore a negative test for unsupported modes so behavior remains covered.

Passing humidity as a string weakens this test because the service/API path previously exercised an integer value. If `set_humidity` is expected to accept numeric humidity, this should use an `int` (for example `40`) and assert the ecobee client is called with an integer as well; otherwise the test may mask a type regression.

This adds a new per-entity cache in `hass.data`, but unlike `WARN_DIP` it is never cleared. Since entity IDs can be dynamic (especially for MQTT/discovery-based sensors), this set can grow indefinitely over the lifetime of the instance. Consider either expiring entries after the warning decision is made, scoping this to the current statistics run, or reusing a structure that gets reset periodically.

This assertion checks `caplog.text` after the second statistics run, but the log buffer still contains messages from the first run unless it is explicitly cleared. That makes the test vulnerable to false positives if the warning is emitted earlier than intended. Consider calling `caplog.clear()` before the second `do_adhoc_statistics(...)` (or asserting on newly added records only) so this verifies the warning is first emitted on the second dip, not just present at some point in the test.

Calling `Discover.discover()` unconditionally during `async_setup` can make startup fail or stall if network discovery raises a `SmartDeviceException` or times out. Since discovery here is only used to seed config flows/migrations, it would be safer to catch discovery errors and continue setup with an empty device list so the integration doesn't become unavailable at startup due to transient network issues.

Using `Discover.discover_single(entry.data[CONF_HOST])` for every entry setup re-discovers the device by host instead of reusing the device object already collected in `DISCOVERED_DEVICES` or instantiating directly from the config entry data. This adds extra network I/O during setup and may make setup less reliable for the exact connectivity issue this PR is trying to address. Can we reuse the previously discovered device when available, and only fall back to a direct lookup if needed?

Parsing the selected device by splitting the display string on spaces is brittle. `device.alias` is user-controlled and may contain arbitrary spaces or even end with a MAC-like token, which can cause us to extract the wrong key and fail to look up `self._discovered_devices[mac]`. It would be safer to use a stable identifier as the form value (for example the formatted MAC itself) and keep the human-readable label separate.

Consider making this an immutable tuple instead of a list. Since `PLATFORMS` is a module-level constant and now annotated as `Final`, using a tuple better matches the intent and avoids accidental mutation elsewhere in the integration.

`emeter_readings.total` can be `None` on some Kasa devices / firmware combinations even when `has_emeter` is true. Passing that through directly may leave the energy entity unavailable or break assumptions in consumers expecting a float. Consider normalizing missing realtime fields (especially `total`) the same way `emeter_today` is handled, or guarding against partial emeter payloads before building the coordinator data.

Accessing `self.device.hw_info["sw_ver"]` directly can raise a `KeyError` if a device/firmware does not expose that field. Since this integration is being broadened to more device types via `python-kasa`, it would be safer to use `.get("sw_ver")` (or another library-provided version attribute if available) so `device_info` generation doesn't fail and make the entity unavailable.

This broadens setup from bulbs to `is_light_strip` and `is_dimmer`, but the entity class still exposes bulb-specific capabilities/properties (for example color temperature / HS color accessors later in the class). If a dimmer or light strip does not implement the same API surface as `SmartBulb`, this can lead to attribute errors or incorrect supported features. Consider either narrowing this check to devices guaranteed to support the full interface used by `TPLinkSmartBulb`, or gating the entity properties/features based on the actual device capabilities.

Scheduling cleanup after a fixed 60-second delay seems racy: config-entry flows started above are asynchronous and device discovery/network I/O can easily take longer than that. If migration hasn't finished yet, `async_cleanup_legacy_entry` may run before entities are reassigned, leaving the legacy entry behind until a later manual cleanup. Consider tying cleanup to completion of the spawned migration tasks (or retrying until no legacy entities remain) instead of using a hardcoded timeout.

This `cast(float, ...)` only satisfies the type checker and does not protect against `None` or other unexpected values at runtime. Since availability can fluctuate for these devices and the setup-time filter only checks the initial coordinator payload, a later refresh could still produce a missing/`None` emeter value and cause the entity update to fail. Consider keeping the return type as `float | None` and handling absent values defensively here (for example via `.get(...)`) instead of forcing a float.

Using `{host}` in the flow title may produce unstable or less user-friendly entry titles if the device IP changes (common with DHCP). Since this PR moves to per-device config entries, consider whether the title should rely on a stable identifier or omit the host to avoid config entries appearing to 'rename' when network addressing changes.

Removing the explicit HS210 workaround here may reintroduce the delayed state update issue mentioned in the deleted `_async_device_workarounds()` comment (`#55190`). If `async_refresh_after` does not include an equivalent model-specific delay before refreshing, `async_turn_on/off` can refresh too early and leave the entity in a stale state right after a toggle. Please confirm that this decorator or the new coordinator logic fully covers that behavior, or preserve the workaround for affected models.

The wording here says discovery will be used when the host is empty, but this flow now appears to support a separate `pick_device` step after discovery. That can be confusing to users because it implies automatic setup rather than a selection step. Consider clarifying the description to mention that leaving the host empty will search for devices and let the user choose one.

This mock is using `MagicMock(auto_spec=SmartBulb)`, but `unittest.mock` expects `spec`/`spec_set` or `create_autospec` for autospeccing. As written, `auto_spec` is just treated like an arbitrary attribute configuration, so the mock won't actually enforce the `SmartBulb` interface. The same pattern appears in the plug/strip helpers below. Consider switching to `MagicMock(spec=SmartBulb)` or `create_autospec(SmartBulb, instance=True)` to make these tests catch API mismatches against `python-kasa`.

The `device` parameter is ignored here, so `_patch_discovery(device=...)` will always return `_mocked_bulb()` instead of the caller-provided device. That makes this helper misleading and can hide test coverage gaps for plugs/strips or device-specific behavior. Consider returning `device if device else _mocked_bulb()` to match `_patch_single_discovery`.

This change removes the previous re-export of `mock_light_profiles` from `tests.components.light.conftest`. If any TP-Link light tests still rely on that fixture implicitly, they will start failing with a missing fixture error. Please either keep that import here or confirm all affected tests were updated to import/provide the fixture another way.

This assertion relies on `new_entry` being updated in place after the import flow aborts. In Home Assistant config entry updates often replace the stored entry object rather than mutating the previously returned instance, so this can make the test brittle or give a false signal depending on implementation details. Consider reloading/fetching the config entry from `hass.config_entries.async_entries(DOMAIN)` and asserting the stored entry's `data[CONF_HOST]` instead.

This assertion only checks that the entry ends up in `SETUP_RETRY`, but it doesn't verify the behavior that matters for the reported regression: that a temporarily unavailable device is retried and can later recover without requiring a manual integration reload. Consider extending this test to simulate the device becoming available on a subsequent setup/reload and assert the entry transitions to `LOADED`.

Setting `bulb.color_temp = None` here makes this test rely on an implicit fallback to HS mode, but the assertions below still expect `color_temp` support (`ATTR_SUPPORTED_COLOR_MODES` includes `"color_temp"` and min/max mireds are asserted). That combination is a bit inconsistent and could mask regressions in capability detection. Consider keeping a valid color temperature value when the device is supposed to support color temp, or explicitly disabling `is_variable_color_temp` if this test is meant to cover an HS-only bulb.

This assertion looks inconsistent with the expectations just above. `ignored_entity_reg` and `garbage_entity_reg` are explicitly asserted to remain on `config_entry`, so `er.async_entries_for_config_entry(entity_reg, config_entry)` should not be empty here. As written, the test would either fail or mask the intended behavior. Consider asserting the specific remaining entity IDs/unique IDs instead of `== []`.

These string literals are missing commas, so Python concatenates them into a single list element. As written, this only checks one combined entity_id instead of verifying that each sensor entity is absent, which weakens the test and can let regressions slip through. Please add commas between each entry.

Replacing `plug.update` after setup may not affect the object actually being polled by the entity if the integration wraps or copies the device reference during initialization. This makes the unavailable-state assertion somewhat implementation-dependent. Consider patching the update method before setup, or patching the exact object/method used by the coordinator/entity, so this test reliably verifies the regression around devices becoming unavailable.

Using `entity_description.unique_id or entity_description.key` fixes the collision for derived sensors, but it also changes the unique ID format for any existing entities that start setting `unique_id`. In Home Assistant, that means users can lose entity registry customizations unless a migration is added. Please confirm whether any existing descriptions will now resolve to a different identifier and, if so, add an entity registry migration path.

This debug log will print the full `entities` list, which typically falls back to each entity object's `repr`. That tends to be noisy and not very actionable in production logs, and may also trigger expensive stringification during setup. If the goal is to help diagnose missing sensors, consider logging a concise summary instead (for example, the number of entities and their `entity_description.key`/`unique_id` values).

Adding the new model to `MODELS_VACUUM` alone may not be sufficient to fix the reported setup failure. The issue description shows a `KeyError` during `async_setup_entry`, which suggests the config entry/device data path is breaking elsewhere rather than model recognition itself. Please verify that `roborock.vacuum.s4` is also handled consistently in any model-specific setup/migration logic and add a regression test covering config-entry setup for this model so we don't mask the original bug.

This appears to invert the fix described in the PR. Based on the issue/description, `device.temperature` reflects the configured/derived target sensor value (and can include offsets or an external sensor reading), while `device.actual_temperature` is the thermostat's own measured temperature. Returning `device.temperature` whenever a temperature sensor exists will still report the wrong current temperature for thermostats paired with a DECT 440. Please verify the semantics from the underlying library and consider using `actual_temperature` for the climate entity's `current_temperature` instead.

Setting `has_temperature_sensor = True` on the shared climate mock changes the default capabilities of every thermostat test that uses this fixture. That broadens the mock beyond the bug being fixed and can mask regressions in code paths that should handle thermostats without a temperature sensor. Consider keeping the default as `False` and enabling it only in the specific test case(s) that need to exercise `current_temperature` from a sensor-capable thermostat.

This assertion appears to encode the old, incorrect behavior described in the PR. For the automatic-offset case, the issue states that `device.temperature` reflects the external sensor/offset-adjusted value while `device.actual_temperature` is the thermostat's own measured value, and the code change is intended to use the correct source for `current_temperature`. If the fix is in `climate.py`, this test should assert the expected corrected value here; otherwise it may either fail against the new implementation or validate the wrong behavior.

This early return changes behavior for recursive calls, but it does so silently. Since the issue is a user-facing deadlock, it would be helpful to also emit a warning/error log here explaining that a recursive call was blocked and why. Otherwise users may just see the script stop working without enough context to diagnose the configuration problem.

The new `failed_recursion` execution state appears to be introduced here, but there is no surrounding context showing that this value is handled anywhere else (trace/UI/tests). If this state is not already recognized by the existing script execution reporting, it may be ignored or surface inconsistently. Please verify all consumers of `script_execution_set()` understand this new status, or reuse an existing failure state.

This assertion only verifies that the script eventually reaches `test.script`, but it doesn't check the behavior that actually regressed: the recursive self-call should not leave the script entity stuck/running or unable to be invoked again. As written, an implementation that skips the recursive call but still continues would pass. Consider extending the test to assert the script is no longer `on` after completion and/or that a second invocation succeeds, so the deadlock/frozen-state bug is covered more directly.

This change drops the previous UTC normalization and now exposes the raw `sent` value directly. That alters the attribute format/semantics compared to the old behavior (`_to_utc(...).isoformat()`), and could lead to inconsistent timestamps across entities or break consumers/tests that expect UTC-normalized values. If the goal is only to keep the attribute present while the sensor is off, it would be safer to preserve the existing conversion and only fall back to an empty value when the source field is missing.

Same concern here: `start` used to be converted to UTC before being exposed, but this now returns the raw upstream string. That is a behavioral change beyond adding more attributes, and may introduce timezone inconsistencies. Consider keeping the old `_to_utc` conversion and only using a blank/default value when `raw_warn.start` is absent.

This also changes `expires` from a UTC-normalized ISO timestamp (or `None`) to a raw string (or empty string). Besides the timezone regression, switching from `None` to `""` changes the attribute type, which can be surprising for automations/templates. Please consider preserving the previous normalized format and type contract while still ensuring the attribute exists when no warning is active.

This now indexes `data[ATTR_DESCRIPTION]` directly, but the issue description mentions exposing these attributes even when the binary sensor is `off`. In that state, `data` may be empty or may not contain all of these keys, which would raise a `KeyError` and prevent attributes from being exposed at all. Consider using `.get(...)` with a default empty string/`None` for the newly added attributes (and likely the existing ones too) so the entity always reports a stable attribute set.

This assertion now expects an empty string for `ATTR_START` when the source value is missing, but the issue description specifically calls out preserving attributes while the sensor is `off` so the UI can discover them. Returning `""` here also changes the previous `None` semantics for missing timestamps and may be inconsistent with the other `off`-state assertions in this file, which still expect `None`. Please verify this is intentional in the implementation and, if so, consider adding a dedicated test/comment that documents why missing datetime fields use empty strings while inactive warnings use `None`.

This test now hard-codes a localized timestamp with a `+01:00` offset. That makes the expectation dependent on timezone conversion behavior and the test environment/configuration, whereas the previous UTC value was stable. If the implementation intentionally converts to local time, it would be safer to assert against a parsed datetime or explicitly set/freeze the Home Assistant timezone in the test to avoid brittle failures.

Using a static identifier `(DOMAIN, DOMAIN)` means every SABnzbd config entry will be attached to the same device in the registry. If a user has multiple SABnzbd instances configured, all sensors will be merged under one device. Consider including a per-config-entry identifier (for example `config_entry.entry_id` or another instance-specific value) in `identifiers`, and align the sensor `unique_id` with that as well.

Setting the unique ID to only `description.key` is not instance-specific, so sensors from multiple SABnzbd integrations would collide (`status`, `speed`, etc. would all share the same unique IDs). Please prefix/suffix this with a stable identifier for the configured instance, such as the config entry ID or host.

This annotation is inconsistent with the assigned value: `None` is not a `HafasClient`. More importantly, if `entry.data[CONF_PROFILE]` contains an unsupported value, `client` remains `None` and gets stored in `hass.data`, which will likely fail later in the sensor platform with a less clear error. Consider validating the profile here and either raising `ConfigEntryNotReady`/`ValueError` or using an exhaustive mapping so setup cannot succeed with a `None` client.

This creates the config entry from `user_input`, which means the normalized/validated station values returned by `validate_input()` are discarded. Since `validate_input()` resolves the entered names to `start_station.name` / `destination_station.name`, using `info` here would avoid storing ambiguous or misspelled user input and keep the saved config consistent with the validated stations.

Catching `ValueError` here and logging it as an "Unexpected exception" is misleading, because `ValueError` is used intentionally above for station validation failures. Consider logging this at debug/info level without a traceback, or handling a more specific custom exception so expected validation errors don't look like internal failures in the logs.

This manifest declares a `homekit` matcher for a cloud API integration that doesn't appear to discover devices via HomeKit. Including `homekit: {}` here can cause the integration to be treated as supporting HomeKit discovery when it does not. Please remove this key unless there is actual HomeKit discovery support implemented elsewhere.

This slicing looks incorrect: `", ".join(...)` already produces a properly separated string, and `[:-2]` will remove the last two characters of the final product name (or return an empty/shortened string for a single product). It would be safer to drop the slice entirely and just use the join result.

The `only_direct` label is phrased as a question, while Home Assistant translation strings for config flow fields are typically short field labels rather than full questions. Using a noun/adjective label like `Direct connections only` will better match the rest of the form and keep translations consistent.

The translation string is user-facing and should avoid punctuation that may not match the style used elsewhere in config flows. Consider changing this to a plain label like `Only direct connections` for consistency and easier reuse across locales.

Please verify that `pyhafas==0.3.0` is the exact version declared in `homeassistant/components/hafas/manifest.json`. `requirements_all.txt` is generated from manifests, so any mismatch will cause CI/hassfest or dependency generation to drift and make future updates harder to track.

Adding `pyhafas` here is correct for test environments, but this file is generated from integration manifests. Please make sure the dependency is also declared in `homeassistant/components/hafas/manifest.json` and that `script.gen_requirements_all` / `hassfest` were run, otherwise this change can drift from the source of truth and be overwritten later.

This line appears to include an accidental `1:` prefix and leading indentation, which would make the file invalid Python syntax. It should just be the module docstring, e.g. `"""Tests for the HaFAS integration."""`.

The docstring looks copied from another integration (`Abode`) and doesn't match this helper. Please update it to describe the HaFAS test setup helper, otherwise it will be confusing when maintaining these tests.

This test builds `requests.Response` objects manually and assigns to the private `_content` attribute just to feed `HafasResponse`. That makes the fixture tightly coupled to `requests` internals and can break if `HafasResponse` or `requests` starts relying on other response fields (status code, encoding, headers, etc.). Consider parsing the fixture JSON through a helper/factory provided by `pyhafas`, or at least initialize the response more completely so the test doesn't depend on a partially constructed `Response` object.

Importing `Profile` from `homeassistant.components.hafas.config_flow` makes this test helper depend on the config flow module, which is a relatively heavy import path and can create brittle test coupling. If `Profile` is also used by runtime code outside the flow, consider moving it to a shared module like `const.py` so both the integration and tests can import it without pulling in config-flow-specific code.

This fixture does not appear to be valid JSON as committed: the file starts with `1:{` and every line is prefixed with line-number text (`2:`, `3:`, ...). If the tests load this via `json.loads`/`json.load`, parsing will fail immediately. Please store the raw response body without embedded line numbers so the fixture is valid JSON.

This fixture appears to include line-number prefixes (`1:`, `2:`, etc.) as part of the file content. If committed as-is, it won't be valid JSON and any test code that loads it with a JSON parser will fail. Please store the raw API response without embedded line numbers.

This fixture appears to include line-number prefixes (`1:`, `2:`, etc.) as part of the file content, which makes it invalid JSON and tightly couples the test data to the diff view rather than the actual API payload. Please store the raw response body only, otherwise any test that loads this as JSON will fail or require custom parsing.

This test bypasses the actual user step by passing `data` directly to `async_init`, so it never verifies that the config flow first shows a form or that the schema/validation of the user step works. For a config flow test, it's usually better to call `async_init(..., context={"source": SOURCE_USER})` without data, assert `FlowResultType.FORM`, and then submit the form with `async_configure`. Otherwise regressions in the initial step can slip through unnoticed.

This assertion hard-codes the string representation of a duration (`"1:12:00"`). If the integration exposes `timedelta`-like values and formatting changes slightly (for example due to localization, serialization, or a future refactor), this test will fail even though the underlying value is still correct. Consider asserting against the actual typed value if available, or normalizing/parsing the attribute before comparison to make the test less brittle.

Similar to the `time` assertion above, this test depends on the exact string formatting of the delay value. Since durations are often represented as `timedelta` objects internally and serialized differently across implementations, asserting the formatted string can make the test unnecessarily fragile. It would be safer to compare a normalized duration value instead.

This assertion also relies on a specific string formatting for a duration attribute. To avoid brittle tests, consider validating the semantic value of the delay (for example, zero duration) rather than its exact string representation.

This assumes `self._sync_status["slave"]` is always present when `is_master` is true. If the API omits the key or returns `None`, `nodes` becomes `[None]` and `slave.get(...)` will raise. Please guard against a missing/empty slave node before iterating so `group_members` doesn't fail during transient sync states.

Building the AddSlave request from `entity.ip` can break for players that haven't completed `force_update_sync_status()` yet, because `ip` is initialized to `None` and only populated from `@id` later. The previous implementation used `host`, which is available from construction time. Consider using `host` here or validating that `ip` is set before sending the command.

When the current player is the master, passing `self` into `remove_slaves()` generates `/RemoveSlave?slave=<master-ip>&port=<master-port>`. That removes a slave, not the coordinator, so this likely won't ungroup the master correctly and may be rejected by the device. If Home Assistant expects `async_unjoin_player()` to dissolve the whole group for a coordinator, this should probably remove all current slaves (or use a dedicated API call if Bluesound provides one) instead of trying to remove the master from itself.

This looks like a stray debug statement left in the implementation. It logs an unexplained `"5 %s"` warning during normal grouping flows and will add noise to user logs. Please remove it or replace it with a meaningful debug-level message if it's still needed.

Removing the `join`/`unjoin` service descriptions is consistent with the breaking-change note, but this also drops the only in-integration service metadata users and tooling rely on for discovery. Please make sure the integration docs and release notes explicitly point users to `media_player.join` / `media_player.unjoin`, otherwise this will look like functionality was removed rather than migrated.

This default implementation now silently returns `false` for all non-`BaseSelectBinder` binders, which is fine as a fallback, but it also means alias qualification failures are indistinguishable from "no alias support here".
