Given this PR changes name resolution behavior for chained calls on select-list aliases, it would be safer to document in the header or enforce via override that only `BaseSelectBinder` participates, otherwise future binders may accidentally miss alias qualification and regress this feature without any signal.

This negative test looks malformed rather than intentionally invalid for alias ordering: the previous select item is missing a trailing comma, and there is also a trailing comma before `FROM`. That means the failure could be caused by SQL syntax errors instead of the binder rejecting a forward alias reference. To make this test actually validate the issue, add the comma after `only_alphabet.lower() as lower` and remove the comma after `as only_alphabet`, then assert the expected binder error message if possible.

This assumes `RemoteUrls` is non-null and contains at least one valid URL. In the new manifest format, malformed or partially populated entries would now throw here with `IndexOutOfRangeException` or `UriFormatException`, which makes filename generation brittle. Consider guarding against `null`/empty arrays and invalid URL strings, and fall back to `"update.bin"` when no usable URL is available.

This compatibility check now only rejects manifests with a newer `MinimumCompatibleVersion`, but the previous implementation also rejected entries when the package updater format didn't exactly match the supported version. With the current logic, older or otherwise different `PackageUpdaterVersion` values will still be treated as compatible and `Packages` may be consumed even if their structure/semantics differ. If the updater format is not guaranteed to be backward-compatible, consider validating `PackageUpdaterVersion` here as well (or documenting why only a minimum-version check is sufficient).

After `JSONSignature.VerifyAtLeastOne(fs, verifyOps)` the stream position is likely no longer at the beginning of the JSON payload. `JsonSerializer.Deserialize<UpdateInfo>(fs)` will then read from the current position and can fail or return null depending on how `VerifyAtLeastOne` consumes the stream. Please reset `fs.Position = 0` (or deserialize from a fresh stream) before deserializing.

Same concern as above: verification is performed on `fs` immediately before deserialization, so the stream may be left at EOF or another offset. Consider rewinding the stream before calling `JsonSerializer.Deserialize`, otherwise reading installed manifests can become flaky.

`System.Text.Json` is stricter than Newtonsoft.Json and returns `null` for some inputs instead of creating an object. Since `remoteManifest` is dereferenced immediately below, this should be null-checked or defaulted (for example `?? throw ...` / `?? new UpdateInfo(...)`) to avoid a `NullReferenceException` on malformed or incomplete source data.

This recursive call can overflow the stack if the queue contains many empty streams in a row. Since `Read` may legitimately return 0 for each exhausted stream, it would be safer to replace the recursion with a loop that keeps dequeuing/disposing empty streams until it either reads data or runs out of streams.

This dictionary indexer can throw `KeyNotFoundException` before the explicit null/invalid-operation check below is reached. If `verifier.Algorithm` comes from untrusted manifest data or a caller passes an unsupported algorithm without a custom `VerifyMethod`, verification will fail with an unexpected exception instead of a controlled error. Consider using `TryGetValue` here (similar to the signing path) and throwing `InvalidOperationException` when no built-in verifier exists.

This test appears to verify the wrong stream. `source` is initialized with the malformed payload, but `Verify` is called on `target`, which is still empty. That means the assertions only prove that verifying an empty stream returns no valid signatures, not that malformed signed JSON is handled without throwing. Consider passing `source` here (and rewinding if needed) so the test actually exercises the invalid inputs listed above.

This hard-codes a `:` separator between key file paths, which is not portable in a VS Code launch config. On Windows, `:` is part of the drive prefix (for example `C:\...`), so parsing `UPDATER_KEYFILE` by colon can become ambiguous or break entirely. If the application expects multiple key files, consider switching to a platform-safe delimiter (for example `;` or the OS path-list separator) or passing the values as separate arguments/env vars.

Encoding the filename with `HttpUtility.UrlEncode` can produce `+` for spaces, which is correct for form encoding but not always for URL path segments. Since this value is being substituted into package URLs as a path/file component, consider using `Uri.EscapeDataString(Path.GetFileName(x.CreatedFile))` instead to avoid generating URLs that some servers/clients may interpret incorrectly.

Same concern here: `HttpUtility.UrlEncode` is intended for query/form encoding and may encode spaces as `+`. Because `${FILENAME}` appears to be inserted into a manifest URL/path, `Uri.EscapeDataString("latest-v2.manifest")` would be safer and more standards-compliant for path segments.

Renaming the environment variable from `UPDATE_FROM_V1_URL` to `UPDATE_FROM_INCOMPATIBLE_URL` is a breaking configuration change for existing build environments. Unless all CI/deployment configs are updated in lockstep, this will silently fall back to the default URL. Consider supporting the old key as a fallback for backward compatibility, or at least logging when only the legacy key is present.

`--log-console` currently only adds a custom `ILogDestination`, but `Log.StartScope(LogHandler, null)` is still initialized unconditionally just above. If this method can be called more than once in the process lifetime (for example during reconnect/restart flows introduced by this PR), each call will append another console destination and duplicate every log line. Consider guarding against repeated registration or making the console destination idempotent.

Using `innerHTML` here replaces the previous DOM-node append/remove flow with raw HTML injection. Since `exportToSvg()` now receives `files`, and image support introduces file-backed content that may include user-provided SVGs, this broadens the XSS surface if any serialized SVG content is not fully sanitized. It would be safer to keep appending the created `SVGSVGElement` node directly (or otherwise sanitize before assigning to `innerHTML`).

This new `maxWidth` calculation no longer uses `viewportX`/`offsetLeft`, but it also drops the left canvas offset from the available-width computation. If the canvas is not flush with the window (e.g. sidebar open, non-zero `offsetLeft`), `appState.width` is only the canvas width while `zoom.translation.x` is in viewport coordinates, so mixing these spaces can still produce incorrect widths. Consider keeping the calculation in a single coordinate space—either derive from `viewportX` consistently, or subtract `appState.offsetLeft` here as well—to avoid regressions when the canvas is shifted within the page.

Using `pattern: 'idle'` is looser than the intended API and will also validate strings that merely contain `idle` (for example `"idle-now"`). Since the PR description defines a single supported string value, this should be constrained to an exact match, e.g. `enum: ['idle']` or `pattern: '^idle$'`, to avoid accepting invalid configuration values.

The updated description for `true` no longer explains that this behavior is specifically about keep-alive/persistent connections and that the goal is to avoid waiting for them to time out during shutdown. That context was present before and is important to distinguish `forceCloseConnections: true` from normal `server.close()` behavior. Consider restoring a brief note that this applies to persistent/keep-alive connections and causes shutdown to proceed immediately rather than waiting for those sockets to expire.

The type change matches the new `'idle'` mode, but the PR description also changes the default behavior to `'idle'` when supported. Since this option is part of the public API, please make sure the surrounding JSDoc/docs for `FastifyServerOptions` are updated here as well to explain the new semantic difference between `false`, `true`, and `'idle'`, otherwise TypeScript users will see the new union type without any guidance on the runtime behavior or version constraints.

The generated regex is too permissive here: `new RegExp("idle", "u")` will accept any string containing `idle` (for example `"idlefoo"` or `"fooidlebar"`), while the PR description says the option should only allow the exact string value `"idle"`. Please anchor the pattern (e.g. `^idle$`) or use an enum/const in the source schema so invalid values are rejected.

The error message hard-codes `closeIdleConnections`, but this option is only meaningful for Node versions that expose the newer server-closing APIs. It would be helpful to mention the runtime requirement (for example, that this needs a Node.js version supporting `server.closeIdleConnections()`) so users can immediately understand whether this is a configuration mistake or an unsupported platform.

Setting `server.closeAllConnections = null` only disables the `closeAllConnections` branch, but this test is named `custom` and appears to be exercising the legacy Fastify fallback path. If the implementation now prefers `closeIdleConnections` when available, a Node version that exposes `closeIdleConnections` will still take the native path here, so this test may not actually cover the intended fallback behavior. Consider also removing/stubbing `closeIdleConnections` on the custom server to make the scenario deterministic across supported Node versions.

This custom `serverFactory` stub only exposes `on()`, which is enough to trigger the constructor path here, but it doesn't resemble the minimum shape of a real server very closely. That makes the test brittle if Fastify starts touching other server methods/properties during initialization for unrelated reasons. Consider stubbing the smallest realistic server surface used elsewhere in tests (for example `on`, `close`, and any other commonly accessed members) so this test stays focused on validating the `closeIdleConnections` capability check rather than failing due to an incomplete mock.

Removing `forceCloseConnections` from the expected `initialConfig` weakens coverage for the behavior changed in this PR. Since the default is now conditional (`"idle"` when `closeIdleConnections` is available, otherwise `false`), the test should assert that value explicitly rather than dropping the field entirely; otherwise regressions in the exposed default config could slip through unnoticed.

Same concern here: this expectation no longer verifies `forceCloseConnections` at all. Given the PR changes both semantics and defaults for this option, it would be better to assert the environment-dependent expected value (or branch the assertion based on API availability) instead of omitting the property from `initialConfig` checks.

This new error entry is missing the issue/PR cross-reference that the surrounding error docs usually include, while `FST_ERR_LOG_INVALID_LOGGER_INSTANCE` still has one. For consistency and easier traceability, consider adding a `See ([#4624](https://github.com/fastify/fastify/issues/4624))` line here as well.

Removing `boolean` from `loggerInstance` matches the new API intent, but this is a breaking type change for consumers. If runtime validation still accepts `false`/`true` here anywhere in the codepath, the typings will become stricter than the implementation. Please double-check that the runtime rejects booleans for `loggerInstance` consistently, or document this explicitly in the migration notes/tests so TS and JS behavior stay aligned.

The wording here is a bit misleading for the new API. With `loggerInstance` now being a valid top-level option, this message could be read as saying Fastify only accepts a config object for logging in general. Consider making the error explicitly about the `logger` field, e.g. `The logger option only accepts a configuration object. Use loggerInstance to pass an existing logger.` This will make migration clearer and reduce confusion when users hit this breaking change.

Throwing here for any value that satisfies `validateLogger(options.logger)` changes the previous deprecation path into an immediate hard failure. Given the issue description says `{ logger: <logger_instance> }` should be deprecated, not removed outright, this looks like it may break existing users earlier than intended unless this is explicitly part of the major-version contract and all docs/tests have been updated accordingly. If the intent is still deprecation, consider emitting the deprecation warning and continuing to support the instance for now, or gate this behavior behind the documented breaking-change version.

Removing `FSTDEP015` here looks risky unless all remaining references to this warning code have also been deleted. If any code path still emits `FSTDEP015` during the deprecation period, `warning.emit()` will fail because the warning is no longer registered. Since the issue/PR description explicitly mentions deprecating `{ logger: <logger_instance> }`, please double-check that this deprecation has actually been fully removed everywhere, or keep the warning definition until the deprecated path is gone.

This test can pass even if `Fastify({ logger })` stops throwing, because there is no assertion after the `try/catch` to fail when no exception is raised. Since `t.plan(2)` only guarantees two assertions if the `catch` runs, a non-throwing path may leave the test hanging or produce a less clear failure. Consider using `t.throws(() => Fastify({ logger }), { code: 'FST_ERR_LOG_INVALID_LOGGER_CONFIG' })` or adding an explicit failure in the `try` block.

This version check is a bit too broad for the API change you're working around. `Psych` 3.1/3.2 still supports the legacy positional arguments, while the breaking change happens in v4.x. Switching all `>= 3.1` runtimes to keyword args may unnecessarily rely on behavior that wasn't introduced consistently across all older Rubies/Psych combinations. Consider checking for `Psych >= 4` (or feature-detecting the `safe_load` signature) and keeping the positional fallback for earlier versions to reduce compatibility risk.

This condition treats any item that `!canBeSentToBin` as a permanent deletion and shows the warning, but `HasRecycleBin(source.FirstOrDefault()?.Path)` only checks the first selected item's path. For mixed selections (e.g. some items on a local drive and some on a network location), the dialog behavior can be wrong depending on which item happens to be first. Consider determining recyclability for the full selection, or at least ensuring all selected items share the same delete semantics before basing the confirmation policy on the first item.

Increasing the shared details-row size constant from 28 to 32 affects every details layout, not just the search results scenario described in this PR. Since the PR notes this is to improve thumbnail sizing in details view, can we confirm this doesn't unintentionally change row density/app layout across the entire app? If the goal is only to fix thumbnail rendering, a more targeted thumbnail-size adjustment may be safer than changing the global item height constant.

This also changes the global column view item size from 28 to 32, but the PR description only mentions improving thumbnails in details view. Is column view intended to be affected as well? If not, this may introduce an unrelated UI regression by reducing information density in column view.

Switching from `ThumbnailOptions.UseCurrentScale` to `ThumbnailOptions.ResizeThumbnail` changes behavior beyond the search-refresh work in this PR: it may return a thumbnail resized to the requested dimensions but no longer explicitly respects the current display scale/DPI. That can make thumbnails appear softer on high-DPI displays compared to the previous behavior. If the goal is to improve quality for details view, consider combining flags if supported here, or at least verify this path on different scale factors so we don't regress thumbnail sharpness.

This has the same behavioral change as the file-thumbnail path above: replacing `UseCurrentScale` with `ResizeThumbnail` may improve sizing consistency, but it can also stop honoring the current DPI scale and lead to blurrier folder thumbnails on high-resolution displays. Please confirm this is intentional and test both list/details views across scale factors, or preserve the previous scale-aware option if image sharpness matters here.

This subscribes to `context.PropertyChanged` but the action never unsubscribes. Since `context` is resolved from the IoC container and may outlive the action instance, this can keep `CloseSelectedTabAction` alive and cause duplicate notifications over time. Consider implementing a disposal/unload pattern and detaching the handler when the action is no longer used.

`IsExecutable` guards against `CurrentTabItem` being null, but `ExecuteAsync` can still be invoked after state changes and before the command source re-evaluates that property. Passing `context.CurrentTabItem` directly here risks a null argument or closing the wrong tab if the selection changes concurrently. It would be safer to snapshot and validate the current tab before calling `CloseTab`.

This now looks up a new resource key (`"NextTab"`), but the PR description doesn't mention adding/renaming localization resources. If that key isn't present in the resw files, the UI will show the raw key instead of a label. Please verify the corresponding localized string was added or keep the existing key until resources are updated.

The label resource key was renamed from `SwapTabLeft` to `PreviousTab`, but this will only work if the new localization entry exists in all resource files. If that key hasn't been added yet, the UI may show the raw key name or a missing string. Please verify the resource rename is included everywhere this action is localized.

Replacing `SwapTabLeft` with `PreviousTab` changes the public command surface of `CommandManager`. If any existing callers, keybinding registrations, or XAML bindings still reference `SwapTabLeft`, this will break at runtime or leave the old shortcut path unused. Please verify that all references to the old command code/property were updated consistently, or consider keeping a compatibility alias if both concepts are still needed.

Adding `CloseSelectedTab` here is only one part of wiring the command. Since this PR also introduces `Ctrl+W` / `Ctrl+F4`, please make sure the new command code is registered everywhere the command system expects it (enum definition, keyboard accelerator mapping, command palette/context menu exposure, and any can-execute/state handling). Otherwise the property will exist but the shortcut may still not invoke it correctly.

Renaming `SwapTabLeft`/`SwapTabRight` to `PreviousTab`/`NextTab` changes the public `ICommandManager` contract. If any existing callers still use the old members, this will be a breaking change at compile time and may also leave the old drag/reorder semantics without a replacement. Please verify all implementations/usages are updated consistently, or consider keeping separate commands if "swap/reorder" and "navigate" are distinct behaviors.

This change removes the existing `SwapTabRight`/`SwapTabLeft` resource keys and replaces them with new keys. If any XAML, command metadata, or localization files still reference the old names, those lookups will fail at runtime and the strings will fall back or disappear. Please verify all usages were updated and consider whether keeping the old keys for compatibility would be safer.

The new wording is inconsistent with the existing command style in this file: these entries read like descriptions (`Moves to...`) while the removed values were imperative action labels (`Swap tab...`). If these strings are shown in menus/tooltips/shortcut settings, users will likely expect concise command names such as `Previous tab` / `Next tab` / `Close current tab` rather than sentence descriptions.

Removing the `Ctrl+F4` / `Ctrl+W` accelerators from `Page.KeyboardAccelerators` changes existing close-tab behavior, but this hunk doesn't show an equivalent replacement in the same scope. If these shortcuts are now handled elsewhere, it would be good to confirm they still work when focus is inside child controls (address bar, search box, etc.), since moving accelerators off the page can change routing and regress tab closing.

Setting `keyReleased` to `false` for every executed command changes the previous behavior for all shortcuts, not just tab switching. The old logic only kept the key in a 'pressed' state for `SwapTabLeft/SwapTabRight` so holding `Ctrl+Tab` could continue cycling, while other commands were gated until key release. With this change, any executable command may now be repeatedly triggered while the key is held due to key repeat. If the intent is to fix tab switching, this should likely remain scoped to the tab-swap commands (or be handled explicitly per command) rather than applied globally.

Returning a formatted label when there is no selection changes the previous behavior from `string.Empty` to a visible action text. That can make the command appear enabled/labeled even though `SelectedItems.Count == 0`, which is especially risky now that actions are surfaced in the new remapping UI. If the intent is only to provide a stable display name for settings, consider separating the settings/display label from the context-sensitive runtime label instead of changing the empty-selection case here.

Commenting out `MediaHotKey` here removes the back-media key binding from this action entirely, which looks like a functional regression rather than a temporary hide. The PR description only mentions hiding mouse-button actions, not disabling media/browser keys. If the intent is to exclude this binding from the remapping UI, it would be safer to keep the action-level hotkey definition and filter it at the presentation/remapping layer instead of deleting it from the action implementation.

Commenting out `MediaHotKey` leaves the action without its media-key binding and introduces dead code. If the intent is to temporarily hide or disable non-remappable bindings, it's better to remove the property entirely or gate it behind a feature flag / explicit capability check so the behavior is intentional and discoverable. As written, this risks silently regressing support for dedicated forward keys (`Keys.GoForward`) without any explanation in code.

Parsing now depends on localized display strings (`LocalizedKeys` / `LocalizedModifiers`) instead of enum names. That makes persisted hotkey values culture-dependent: a mapping saved as `Ctrl+A` in one language may fail to parse after the app language changes, and non-localized code paths that still pass enum-style strings will also stop working. Consider keeping `Parse` based on the stable `Code` representation (enum names) and using localized labels only for UI display.

Changing `parseSeparators` from `[',', ';', ' ', '\t']` to only whitespace means persisted or user-entered shortcut lists that still use commas/semicolons (which the previous implementation accepted, and `Code` historically emitted) will no longer parse correctly. That creates a backward-compatibility risk for existing JSON settings and any manually edited values. Consider keeping `,` and `;` as accepted separators in `Parse`, even if new serialization now uses spaces.

Adding `DefaultHotKeys` to the interface is useful for reset-to-default behavior, but this is a breaking change for every existing `IRichCommand` implementation. Please make sure all implementers initialize this collection independently from `HotKeys`; if both properties reference the same mutable `HotKeyCollection`, user edits could accidentally mutate the defaults and break the reset-all feature.

This creates a WinRT wrapper from the raw ABI pointer returned by `GetForWindow`, but the pointer is never released. `FromAbi` does not take ownership of the COM reference, so this can leak a reference each time `ShareItem` is invoked. Please release `result` after creating `manager` (for example via `Marshal.Release(result)` in a `try/finally`), or use a helper that transfers ownership safely.

We're subscribing to `DataRequested` on every share invocation, but there is no corresponding unsubscription. Since `DataTransferManager` is tied to the window, repeated calls can accumulate handlers and cause duplicate share payload generation or retain this object longer than intended. Consider unsubscribing after the request is handled, or using a one-shot handler pattern.

This GUID is being introduced as a mutable public field, but it represents a fixed COM interface identifier. Consider making it `private` unless it is intentionally part of the public API, and prefer `static readonly Guid ... = new("A5CAEE9B-8708-49D1-8D36-67D25A8DA00C");` (or `public static ref readonly Guid` if you want to avoid copies) for readability and to reduce the chance of transcription errors in the split-argument form.

This new check deletes `collaboration` rows whose `user_id` no longer exists, but the PR description says missing collaborators should be handled by ghosting rather than removing them. Deleting the row here may silently drop repository permissions/history and makes the doctor command behavior inconsistent with the runtime fix. Could we either convert these rows to the ghost user during autofix, or at least limit this check to reporting-only unless there is a dedicated migration/fixer for that transformation?

Setting the header unconditionally here can override a `Content-Type` that may already have been set by earlier logic or middleware. Since this helper is intended as a fallback for unstable MIME detection, it would be safer to only apply it when `w.Header().Get("Content-Type")` is empty, so we don't accidentally replace an explicitly chosen type.

Using `#` in the target name is risky in a Makefile because `#` starts a comment during parsing. Even though it's escaped here, this pattern can be brittle and may not behave consistently across make implementations or when invoked from the shell. Consider using a safer separator for the pattern target (for example `test-sqlite-migration-%`) and translating it inside the recipe if needed.

There's a typo in this expected fixture value: `CHAGNGE` looks like it should be `CHANGE`. Even if this is only test data, misspellings in fixtures make the intent harder to understand and can accidentally lock in incorrect expectations if the source fixture uses the correct spelling.

This fixture appears to include copied line-number prefixes (`1:`, `2:`, etc.) as part of the YAML content. In this form, the first entry key becomes `1:-` instead of a list item starting with `-`, which is likely invalid for the fixture loader and may prevent the migration test from parsing the file correctly. Please remove the embedded line numbers so the file is valid YAML.

There’s a typo in this sentinel value (`CHAGNGE` -> `CHANGE`). Even though it’s just fixture data, misspellings in expected values make the test intent harder to read and can be confusing when diagnosing failures.

The larger column size fixes the MySQL migration failure, but this migration struct now diverges from the main `webauthn_credential` model definition. If the runtime model still uses the default string mapping (typically `VARCHAR(255)`), a later sync/migration could try to shrink the column again or leave schema definitions inconsistent across code paths. Please make sure the corresponding persistent model is updated to the same explicit size as well.
Minor typo in the comment: `CredentalID` should be `CredentialID`. Since this explains the sizing rationale for a migration workaround, it's worth keeping the comment precise.

This lookup appears to query `webauthn_credential` by the old U2F registration's `id` (`x.ID(reg.ID)` / `id = ?`) rather than by the stable relationship to the user/credential data. Since the migration that created `webauthn_credential` would have generated new autoincrement IDs, there is no guarantee those IDs match `u2f_registration.id`. In that case `has` will often be false and the oversized `credential_id` values will never be repaired. Consider matching the migrated credential using fields that are preserved across the migration (for example `user_id` plus the original credential identifier/name), instead of assuming identical primary keys across tables.

This test only verifies the schema after running `increaseCredentialIDTo410`, but it never inserts a credential ID longer than 255 characters or exercises the failing U2F->WebAuthn migration path described in the issue. As written, it can pass even if the original regression still exists. Consider seeding a `u2f_registration` row whose encoded credential ID is >255 chars and asserting that the migration succeeds and preserves the full value.

Enabling `AllowFastForwardOnly` unconditionally for repositories created from examples changes the default PR capabilities for all new repos, regardless of instance-level configuration. If this merge style is intended to be configurable like the other defaults, this should probably be derived from `setting.Repository.PullRequest` (or whatever global/default policy is used elsewhere) instead of hardcoding `true`, otherwise example-based repos may expose a merge method that admins expected to keep disabled.

The new merge style is described in the issue/PR as `fast-forward-only`, but this label is just `Fast-forward`. That wording is ambiguous with the existing `Rebase then fast-forward` option and may confuse users in the UI. Consider making the string explicit, e.g. `Fast-forward only`, to clearly distinguish the new behavior.

Enabling `AllowFastForwardOnly` by default for repositories that don't yet have a `PullRequestsConfig` changes existing behavior for API edits unrelated to merge styles. This means an older repo with no stored PR config will suddenly get fast-forward-only enabled the first time `updateRepoUnits` initializes defaults, even if the instance-level default is different or the feature wasn't previously intended to be on. Consider deriving this from the same default source as the other merge settings (e.g. settings/default config) rather than hard-coding `true` here.

This fallback ordering makes `fast-forward-only` lower priority than squash, so when both are enabled the UI will preselect squash even if the branch is actually fast-forwardable. Since `--ff-only` is only valid in a subset of cases, defaulting to it unconditionally may also be wrong unless later code revalidates against the current PR state. Consider choosing the default merge style based on mergeability/fast-forwardability rather than only the repo config flags, or at least place this behind a capability check so the selected default is always executable.

Using `git merge --ff-only` here can still create a merge commit if the current branch has diverged and Git decides a fast-forward is not possible, so this implementation relies entirely on Git's failure behavior. Please make sure the caller/UI/API path distinguishes this expected `not fast-forward` case from a generic merge failure and surfaces a clear message to users; otherwise this new merge style may look broken rather than intentionally blocked.

The `allowed` check here only verifies the repository setting and `CommitsBehind == 0`, but a fast-forward merge also requires the PR branch to be mergeable without conflicts and to satisfy any other mergeability constraints already enforced elsewhere. As written, the UI can present `fast-forward-only` as available even when the backend will reject it. Consider reusing the same mergeability predicate used for the other merge actions (for example, conflict / blocked-state checks) and then adding the `CommitsBehind == 0` condition on top.

This adds `fast-forward-only` to the default merge style dropdown unconditionally, but the available default styles should usually stay consistent with the enabled merge methods above. As written, an admin can select `fast-forward-only` as the default even when `pulls_allow_fast_forward_only` is unchecked, which can leave the repo config in an inconsistent state. Consider gating this `<option>` (and the corresponding menu/default-text entries below) on `AllowFastForwardOnly`, similar to how the backend validation should enforce that the default style is one of the allowed styles.

The new `allow_fast_forward_only_merge` field is added to the `Repository` schema without a description, while the corresponding field in `EditRepoOption` includes one. This makes the generated API docs inconsistent and less discoverable for clients consuming the repository response. Consider adding a matching description here as well.

Skipping empty `CustomAvatarRelativePath()` avoids the reported directory-copy failure, but it may also silently skip repositories whose avatar path is missing/invalid in the DB. Since this command is a migration tool, it would be safer to at least log when a repo/user is skipped so operators can detect incomplete migrations instead of getting a false sense of success.

This only handles `discard > math.MaxInt32` once before falling through to `int(discard)`. If `discard` is larger than `2*math.MaxInt32`, the remaining value is still greater than `math.MaxInt32`, and the cast on line 340 can overflow on 32-bit platforms. The previous implementation looped until the remaining value fit. Consider changing this to a `for discard > math.MaxInt32` loop to preserve the old safety guarantees.

Good catch adding a discard on the non-`commit` path, but this assumes the payload is always followed by exactly one extra byte. That matches `cat-file --batch`'s usual `<contents>\n` framing, yet if the stream is already desynchronized or the header size is malformed, `DiscardFull(batchReader, size+1)` can itself consume bytes from the next record and hide the original problem. Consider documenting this framing assumption here or reusing a helper that discards a full batch entry consistently across all call sites so the behavior stays correct and centralized.

Discarding `size+1` bytes here assumes the object payload is always followed by a single delimiter byte. That matches the current `git cat-file --batch` format, but this code path is in a generic parser loop and the assumption is subtle. Please consider documenting why the extra byte is required (or centralizing this into a helper) so future changes don't accidentally switch this back to `size` and reintroduce stream desynchronization bugs.

Switching from `rd.Discard(int(size))` to `DiscardFull(rd, size+1)` looks correct for draining the full `cat-file` payload plus its trailing delimiter, but this changes the previous `int(size)` conversion to a wider arithmetic path. Please make sure `size+1` cannot overflow the parameter type expected by `DiscardFull` (or add an explicit guard/cast here), especially since object sizes come from git output and may be larger than `int` on some platforms.

Using `assert.True(t, IsErrNotExist(err))` makes failures harder to diagnose because it only reports a boolean mismatch. Since this test is specifically validating the error type/regression, consider using a more descriptive assertion such as `assert.ErrorIs` (if the error is wrapped) or `assert.Truef` with the actual error included, so unexpected failures show the returned error value.

If `X-Forwarded-Proto` is present but `X-Forwarded-Host` is missing, this now falls back to `req.Host`, which in a reverse-proxy setup is often the internal upstream address (for example `gitea:3000`). That recreates the broken AppURL/token URL behavior described in the issue. To avoid generating externally invalid URLs, consider requiring both forwarded scheme and forwarded host before guessing, and otherwise fall back to `setting.AppURL`.

This assertion now expects `MakeAbsoluteURL` to ignore both the request host and `X-Forwarded-Host`, but the issue/PR context is specifically about clarifying reverse-proxy behavior and preserving correct externally visible URLs. By hard-coding the configured `AppURL` here, the test no longer protects against regressions where proxied requests should resolve to the forwarded host/proto. If the implementation intentionally changed, it would be good to add separate coverage documenting when request headers are ignored vs honored; otherwise this expectation may be masking the original reverse-proxy/container-registry bug rather than validating the intended behavior.

This keeps the legacy `github.com/russross/blackfriday` v1 module in `go.mod` while also adding `github.com/russross/blackfriday/v2`. If the codebase is being migrated to v2, retaining v1 here can leave an unnecessary duplicate dependency in the module graph unless some transitive import still requires it directly. Please verify whether v1 is still needed; if not, remove it to avoid carrying both major versions.

This adds both `github.com/russross/blackfriday v1.5.2` and `github.com/russross/blackfriday/v2 v2.0.1` to the module graph. Since the issue/PR is specifically migrating to v2, please verify whether any remaining imports still require the v1 module. Keeping both versions is valid in Go modules, but it can indicate an incomplete migration and leaves two APIs in use at once.

This call still uses the old v1 `HTMLRenderer` signature. In blackfriday/v2, `HTMLRenderer` expects an `HTMLRendererParameters` struct rather than `(flags, title, css)`, so this won't compile as written. Please switch to `blackfriday.NewHTMLRenderer(blackfriday.HTMLRendererParameters{Flags: blackfridayHTMLFlags})` (or the equivalent constructor used elsewhere in the migration).

Converting the parsed output with `[]byte(body.String())` introduces an extra string allocation compared to the old API, which returned `[]byte` directly. Since `Parse` already returns a `*bytes.Buffer`, consider returning `body.Bytes()` (or a copied slice if you need ownership isolation) to avoid the unnecessary conversion and reduce allocations on larger markdown inputs.

This import path looks inconsistent with the rest of the updated README, which now says v2 should be installed/imported via `gopkg.in/russross/blackfriday.v2`. Changing the example to `github.com/russross/blackfriday` can mislead users and may pull the wrong major version. Consider keeping the example on the explicit v2 import path for correctness.

This changes title blocks from a dedicated `TitleBlock` renderer callback to a synthetic level-1 `Heading` node. That’s a behavioral change, not just an API migration: downstream code that distinguishes title blocks from headings may now render them differently or lose metadata. If v2 has no native title-block node, consider preserving the original semantics more explicitly (e.g. a dedicated node type or a compatibility path in the renderer) rather than overloading `Heading`.

`endsWithBlankLine` is currently stubbed to always return false, but `finalizeList` relies on it to determine whether a list is tight or loose. As written, lists that should be loose will likely be emitted as tight, which is a visible parsing regression. This TODO should probably be implemented before switching list finalization over to this logic.

This ignores the return value and error from `w.Write`. In this vendored helper that means short writes or I/O failures are silently dropped, which can produce truncated/invalid output with no signal to the caller. If the v2 API allows it, consider propagating write errors (or at least documenting why they are intentionally ignored) so renderer failures aren't hidden.

This now emits `" />` unconditionally when closing an image tag. That changes behavior for non-XHTML output, where the previous renderer used `>` unless `UseXHTML` was enabled. As written, HTML mode will still produce self-closing XHTML-style ``, which is inconsistent with the rest of this renderer (for example `Hardbreak`/`HorizontalRule` still branch on `UseXHTML`). Consider using `r.closeTag` or branching on `r.Flags&UseXHTML` here to preserve the configured output format.

The language info is interpolated directly into the `class` attribute without escaping. If the fenced code info string contains quotes or other special characters, this can break the HTML attribute or allow injection. The old code escaped the language value before writing it. Please escape `info[:endOfLang]` before formatting the attribute.

This indexes `link[0]` without checking `len(link) > 0`. An empty destination can occur for malformed or edge-case links/images and would panic here. The previous implementation had the same assumption, but since this helper is now reused more broadly (`addAbsPrefix`, `appendLinkAttrs`, `needSkipLink`), it would be safer to guard empty slices up front and return `false`.

This change drops the previous parenthesis-balancing logic when scanning inline link destinations. As written, an inline link like `[x](http://example.com/foo(bar))` will now stop at the first `)` and parse the URL incorrectly. Since the PR is intended as an API migration, this is a behavioral regression from the old implementation. Please preserve nested-paren handling here or delegate to the upstream v2 parser logic.

Using `i += bytes.IndexByte(data[i:], '>')` is unsafe here. When `>` is not found, `IndexByte` returns `-1`, which makes `i` smaller rather than negative in many cases, so the subsequent `if i < 0` check won't reliably detect failure and can return an incorrect tag length. Capture the result in a separate variable and check for `-1` before adding it to `i`.

This stub changes behavior compared to the old code path, which called `normalizeURI` before rendering links. Returning the input unchanged means URI normalization/sanitization is effectively disabled for all links created through this parser. If this is temporary for the migration, it should be implemented before merging or the old normalization behavior should be preserved.

This dereferences `parser.renderer` unconditionally, but `New()` no longer guarantees a default renderer unless callers go through `Run()` or explicitly pass `WithRenderer(...)`. Any direct use of `New(...).Parse(...)` followed by rendering through this path will panic if no renderer was configured. Consider either assigning a default renderer in `New()` or guarding here with a nil check and a clearer failure mode.

`String()` indexes directly into `nodeTypeNames` without any bounds check. If a `NodeType` with an unexpected value is ever constructed, this will panic during formatting/logging, which makes debugging harder. Consider guarding against invalid values and returning a fallback like `fmt.Sprintf("NodeType(%d)", t)` instead.

This dereferences `sibling.Parent` unconditionally when `sibling.Prev == nil`. If `n` is the root node (so `n.Parent == nil`), inserting before it will panic here after `sibling.Parent = n.Parent`. Either reject insertion before a root node explicitly or guard this case before updating `FirstChild`.

The return value from `w.Write` is ignored here (and also below on line 450/455). Since `Process` now writes to an arbitrary `io.Writer`, short writes or write errors can be silently dropped, which is a behavioral regression compared to writing into a `bytes.Buffer`. Consider changing `Process` to return an `error` and propagate write failures from all `Write` calls.

This vendored CI config uses Bash process substitution (`<(...)`), but Travis runs `script` commands with `/bin/sh` unless a Bash shell is explicitly requested. On environments where `/bin/sh` is not Bash, this line will fail to parse. Since this file is vendored and not used by Gitea's own CI, consider dropping it entirely from `vendor/`, or rewrite the check to avoid Bash-specific syntax if you intend to keep it.

This assigns `block.Tight = true`, but the rest of the function updates `block.ListData.Tight` instead (`block.ListData.Tight = false` and `finalizeList` also mutates `block.ListData.Tight`). That leaves two different tightness fields out of sync, and the initial value on `ListData.Tight` is never set here. If downstream rendering reads `ListData.Tight`, lists may be treated as loose by default. Consider initializing `block.ListData.Tight = true` here as well, or using a single source of truth for list tightness.

These `io.Writer.Write` calls ignore the returned error. In this vendored copy that may be upstream behavior, but it means write failures are silently dropped and rendering can continue with truncated output. If possible, consider propagating the error from `escapeHTML`/`escLink` (or at least documenting why it is intentionally ignored) so callers can handle short writes and writer failures correctly.

This vendored `go.mod` line appears to include a prefixed line-number string (`1:`) and leading spaces, which makes the module declaration invalid. The file content should be exactly `module github.com/russross/blackfriday/v2`; otherwise vendoring/module resolution may fail when tooling reads this file.

This hard-codes the XHTML-style self-closing image tag even though the renderer now supports both HTML and XHTML via `UseXHTML`/`closeTag`. As written, `` will be emitted even in HTML mode, which is inconsistent with how `
` and `
` are handled elsewhere in this file. Consider using `r.closeTag` here so image output respects the configured renderer mode.

This is currently a stub, but `link()` now routes normal link destinations through `normalizeURI()`. Returning the input unchanged means URI normalization/sanitization is effectively disabled for all parsed links, which can change behavior from the previous version and may leave unsafe or malformed destinations untouched. If v2 expects this helper to canonicalize or percent-encode URIs, we should either implement that logic here or avoid introducing the function until the behavior is defined.

`String()` indexes directly into `nodeTypeNames` without any bounds check. If a `NodeType` with an unexpected value is ever constructed, this will panic during formatting/logging, which makes debugging harder. Consider guarding the index and returning a fallback like `fmt.Sprintf("NodeType(%d)", t)` for unknown values.

This dereferences `sibling.Parent` unconditionally when `sibling.Prev == nil`. If `n.Parent` is nil (for example, calling `InsertBefore` on the root node), `sibling.Parent` will also be nil and this will panic. It would be safer to either reject insertion before a root node explicitly or guard this case before updating `FirstChild`.

The condition here appears inverted relative to the feature name and the behavior below. When `SmartypantsFractions` is **not** set, this branch enables only the hard-coded `1/2`, `1/4`, and `3/4` replacements via `smartNumber`; when it **is** set, the `else` branch enables the generic fraction handler for all digits. If the flag is meant to enable fraction processing, this should likely be `if flags&SmartypantsFractions != 0` instead, otherwise fraction handling will be active even when the option is disabled.

This vendored module list now includes both `github.com/russross/blackfriday` and `github.com/russross/blackfriday/v2`. Since the PR description says the migration is to v2, keeping the legacy module here may indicate a partial upgrade or an indirect dependency still pulling in the old path. Please verify whether the codebase still intentionally depends on both import paths; otherwise this can leave two incompatible Blackfriday APIs vendored at once and make the migration incomplete/confusing.

This writes debug output directly to stdout during HTML rendering. In production this will pollute logs/output for every footnote backlink rendered and can become a performance/noise issue. Please remove this `fmt.Fprintf` (or replace it with gated debug logging if it is still needed).

This appears to be leftover debugging code. Rendering markdown should not emit to stdout, otherwise normal page renders will generate unexpected console/log output. Please remove this line or use the project's logger behind a debug flag.

Ignoring the error from `xurls.StrictMatchingScheme` makes package initialization silently fall back to a nil regexp if the pattern generation ever fails, which would later panic at call sites. Even if this currently never errors for a constant scheme, it's safer to handle it explicitly during init (or panic with context) so failures are caught deterministically.

This regex only accepts lowercase TLDs (`[a-z]{2,6}`), so `www.Example.COM` or other uppercase/mixed-case domains won't be linkified even though DNS names are case-insensitive. Since this parser is replacing existing markdown behavior, that could be a subtle regression. Consider making the TLD part case-insensitive, e.g. `[A-Za-z]{2,6}` or using a case-insensitive regexp.

Moving the compiled regex into `common.LinkRegex` makes this package-level mutable state shared across packages. Since `CustomLinkURLSchemes` updates it at runtime, this can introduce initialization-order or data-race issues if other code in `modules/markup/common` starts reading it before this function is called or concurrently. Consider keeping the regex encapsulated behind an accessor/setter in `common` (or initializing it there) so the ownership and synchronization are explicit.

The checked/unchecked rendering appears to be inverted here. When `n.IsChecked` is true, this branch emits an unchecked `<input>` and omits the `checked` class, while the `else` branch adds `checked` for unchecked items. This will display task list state incorrectly. Please swap the two branches or adjust the emitted attributes/classes so `IsChecked == true` produces a checked checkbox.

Initializing `converter` only once means its configuration is frozen based on the first call to `RenderRaw`. In particular, `setting.Markdown.EnableHardLineBreak` is read during that first invocation and any later config reload or test that mutates this setting will be ignored. Consider either rebuilding the renderer when settings change, or moving the hard-wrap behavior into per-render options instead of global singleton initialization.

Reusing a package-level `parser.Parser` here may not be safe if `StripMarkdownBytes` is called concurrently. Goldmark parsers can keep parsing state and are typically created per parse/document, while the old implementation instantiated a new parser on each call. Please verify the parser is concurrency-safe; otherwise store the immutable `goldmark.Markdown` configuration and call `Parser().Parse(...)` per invocation, or create a new parser each time.

`html.WithUnsafe()` looks unnecessary for stripping plain text and link extraction, and it changes parsing/rendering behavior around raw HTML. Since this code never uses the HTML renderer output, enabling unsafe HTML here may cause `StripMarkdownBytes` to include or preserve content that the previous stripper ignored. Consider removing this option unless there's a specific stripping-related reason to keep it.

Returning `*bytes.Buffer` here introduces an API that can panic if callers assume sanitization always succeeds. `bluemonday.Policy.SanitizeReader` returns `nil` on read/copy errors, unlike `Sanitize`/`SanitizeBytes` which always return a value. Consider either returning `(*bytes.Buffer, error)` and propagating failures explicitly, or documenting/handling the `nil` case before exposing this helper.

Removing this test case drops coverage for a useful negative case: a leading `#123` at the start of a line that should be treated as a title/header-like construct rather than an issue reference. Since this PR changes markdown parsing behavior, that distinction is exactly the kind of subtle regression we should keep pinned down. Unless the intended behavior has explicitly changed, please keep this test (or replace it with an equivalent assertion documenting the new expected behavior).

This vendored `Makefile` target performs networked `go get` installs and mutates files under `./fuzz`, which is risky to carry inside `vendor/`. Vendored code is typically expected to be inert source-only dependencies; shipping upstream maintenance targets here can confuse contributors and may be picked up accidentally by tooling. Consider excluding upstream build/test helper files like this `Makefile` from the vendored copy unless Gitea actually intends to invoke and maintain them.

`childCount` is incremented before checking whether `v1` actually belongs to `self`, and before the `v1 == nil` path delegates to `AppendChild` (which increments again). This can leave the tree with an incorrect child count: appending via `InsertBefore(..., nil, ...)` adds 2, and passing a non-child `v1` adds 1 without inserting anything. Consider moving the increment into the successful insertion paths only.

The language parser only stops at a literal space, so fenced code info strings separated by tabs (or other ASCII whitespace) will be treated as part of the language name. That can change syntax-highlighting behavior compared to CommonMark-style whitespace handling. Consider stopping on any whitespace character here, e.g. via `unicode.IsSpace` or explicit checks for `' '` and `'\t'`.

This branch appears to clear the wrong flag. `SetSoftLineBreak(false)` should unset `textSoftLineBreak`, but it currently clears `textHardLineBreak` instead. As written, a soft line break can remain set after being disabled, and toggling soft breaks may unexpectedly affect hard breaks.

`NewTableRow` accepts an `alignments` argument, and `TableRow` has a corresponding `Alignments` field, but the constructor currently ignores the parameter and returns an empty struct. This looks like a functional bug: any row-level alignment metadata passed by the parser will be lost. Consider initializing the field here, e.g. `return &TableRow{Alignments: alignments}`.

Minor typo in the doc comment: `impelemtns` should be `implements`. Since this is vendored code that may still be exposed in generated documentation or searched by contributors, it's worth correcting for clarity.

The comparator passed to `SortChildren` never returns `0`, even when two footnotes have the same `Index`. That violates the usual comparator contract and can lead to unstable or incorrect ordering if duplicate indexes ever occur. Consider handling equality explicitly, e.g. return `0` when the indexes match.

This nil check is dead code: `m` was just assigned on the previous line, so `m == nil` can never be true here. More importantly, the slice bounds depend on `at := bytes.IndexByte(line, '@')`; if `FindEmailIndex` ever returns a value without an `@`, `m[2]` becomes `-1` and `line[m[2]:m[3]]` will panic. It would be safer to validate `at >= 0` explicitly before building/slicing with `m`.

This extension only registers the inline parser and HTML renderer, but the parser relies on delimiter processing (`pc.PushDelimiter(node)`) to turn `~~...~~` into an `ast.Strikethrough` node. Without also registering the delimiter processor with the parser, the delimiters may remain as plain text and the renderer for `ast.KindStrikethrough` will never be invoked. Please verify that `Extend` also wires in the delimiter processor expected by this parser.

This index check can panic on lines that contain only whitespace. `pos` is advanced past leading spaces and `limit` is reduced by trailing spaces, so for an all-space line `limit` becomes 0 while `len(line) > 0` is still true, and `line[limit-1]` dereferences `line[-1]`. Please guard on the trimmed bounds instead (for example `if pos < limit && line[limit-1] == '|'`) before indexing.

The parser is registered with priority `0`, but the comment above says it must take precedence over `parser.LinkParser`. In goldmark, parser ordering is priority-sensitive, so using `0` may not actually guarantee this parser runs before the link parser across versions/configurations. Please verify the link parser's priority and choose an explicit higher-precedence value (or document why `0` is safe here), otherwise task list markers like `[x]` can be parsed as links instead of checkboxes.

Using priority `9999` makes this inline parser run after almost everything else, which can prevent typographic substitutions from happening in common cases where other parsers consume the same trigger characters first. In particular, `<` is also used by autolink/raw HTML parsing, so `<<` may never reach this parser. Please verify the intended ordering against the other goldmark inline parsers/extensions and consider a lower priority or documenting why this must run last.

This vendored `go.sum` file is being added as an empty file. In a vendored dependency, `go.sum` is typically not needed at all, and if it is intentionally included it should contain the module checksums generated by `go mod tidy`/`go mod vendor`. An empty tracked file can be misleading and may indicate the vendoring step was incomplete. Consider removing this file from `vendor/` or regenerating vendor metadata so only necessary files are committed.

This inserted `endif()` closes the surrounding `if(gRPC_BUILD_TESTS)` block before immediately opening another one. That changes the structure of the generated CMake and can accidentally terminate the previous test section earlier than intended. Unless there is a specific need to split the block here, it would be safer to keep `mock_stream_test` inside the existing `if(gRPC_BUILD_TESTS)` section and avoid introducing an extra close/open pair.

This test only verifies that `MockClientAsyncReaderWriter` is instantiable, but the regression described in the issue is specifically about missing mocked virtual methods (`StartCall` and the `Write(..., WriteOptions, void*)` overload). A stronger test would exercise those APIs directly, e.g. by setting `EXPECT_CALL` on them or at least taking their addresses / invoking them, so future changes that accidentally drop one of these methods are caught more explicitly.

The new pattern makes whitespace between `}` and `)` optional, which fixes the reported case, but `\s` also matches newlines. That means this regex will now accept `}\n);` as a valid module terminator, potentially matching across lines in ways the build step didn't before. If the intent is only to allow style-guide spacing on the same line, consider restricting this to horizontal whitespace (e.g. `[ \t]*`) and making the quantifier non-lazy unnecessary (`*` is enough). The same concern applies to the similar regex updated below.

This change drops the `getAttribute` fallback and only copies truthy DOM properties from `node`. That can break `jQuery.globalEval`'s documented/internal pattern of passing a plain object to fake script attributes (especially `nonce`), since such objects may not expose reflected DOM properties the same way a real `<script>` element does. It also reintroduces the original `nonce` handling concern noted in the removed comment: in some environments the property may not preserve the value reliably once connected, while `setAttribute` was intentionally used on the new script element. Please confirm that all supported browsers expose `nonce` correctly via the property and that object-based callers still work, or keep a safe attribute fallback for this case.

This now calls `elem.getRootNode( composed )` unconditionally in the default implementation. The later fallback only checks `documentElement.getRootNode`, which assumes every `elem` passed here also implements `getRootNode`. That may not hold for non-Element DOM nodes or mixed-document environments, and would turn a previous boolean check into a runtime error. Consider guarding on `elem.getRootNode` at the call site (or checking `elem.nodeType`/feature support per node) before invoking it.

Dropping the `getAttribute("nonce")` fallback may break CSP nonce propagation in environments where the `nonce` IDL property is intentionally hidden or normalized differently from the content attribute. Since this code is used to preserve the nonce when re-evaluating external scripts, relying on `node.nonce` alone could cause scripts to execute without the original nonce and fail under strict CSP. Consider keeping the attribute fallback unless we've verified all supported browsers expose the nonce reliably via the property.

This changes the `:scope` decision from a feature check to a browser sniff. That assumes every non-IE browser we support has correct `:scope` behavior in `querySelectorAll`, which is a stronger condition than the previous `support.scope` probe. If any supported environment (including embedded/older WebViews) lacks `:scope` support or has partial behavior, selectors here will start failing instead of falling back to the ID hack. Consider keeping a capability check, or at least combining it with `!isIE` rather than replacing it entirely.

Changing `rbuggyQSA` from an array built via runtime feature detection to a hard-coded IE-only regexp also changes its type when `isIE` is false: it now becomes `false` instead of the previous falsy `0`/empty-length result after `&&`. If downstream code assumes `rbuggyQSA` is either a `RegExp` or a consistently falsy value this is probably fine, but it's worth double-checking all call sites since this is a behavioral change in module shape in addition to dropping Edge support.

This changes the guard from a feature check to a browser sniff. That makes the test less robust: any non-IE environment in the test matrix that also lacks `Array.prototype.flat` would now execute this branch and fail for the same reason the old code avoided. Since the behavior under test depends on `Array#flat` availability rather than IE specifically, it would be safer to keep the feature detection (or combine it with `!QUnit.isIE` only if there's a separate IE-specific reason).

This UA check is now broader than before: `/\bsafari\b/i` also matches Chromium-based Edge user agents because they include `Safari/...`. Previously Edge was explicitly excluded. If the special-case here is intended only for Safari/Blink behavior, this change may start applying the workaround to new Edge as well, which could mask regressions or make the expectation less precise. Consider excluding `Edg/` (and possibly other Chromium-based browsers if needed) or replacing the UA sniff with a feature/behavior check tied to the actual condition under test.

This gating changed from a feature check (`document.body.getRootNode`) to a browser check (`!QUnit.isIE`). That makes the test run in any non-IE environment even if Shadow DOM/root-node support is missing or partial, which weakens the test's portability and can cause false failures in non-browser or custom test environments. Consider keeping the capability check as part of the condition, e.g. `QUnit.jQuerySelectors && document.body.getRootNode && !QUnit.isIE`.

The updated comment says this branch catches Edge as well, but `/chrome/i` won't match Chromium-based Edge user agents, which use `Edg/` rather than `Chrome` as the identifying token for browser detection. If these tests are expected to run in the new Edge, this branch may not select `expectedMap.chrome` there and could leave `expected` unset. Consider either updating the detection to explicitly include `Edg/` or adjusting the comment if Edge is intentionally excluded from this test mapping.

This now assumes every `DataAdapter` implements `get_jax_iterator()`. Given the previous implementation fell back to `super()._get_iterator(...)`, this removes the safety net and will fail hard if any adapter is missing the new method or returns a non-iterator. Please make sure all adapters provide this API consistently (or keep a fallback / explicit error), and add coverage for the main adapter types so this backend-specific path doesn't regress.

This now unconditionally calls `get_torch_dataloader()`, which changes behavior for every Torch backend input, not just `TorchDataLoaderAdapter`. The previous implementation fell back to the base iterator for adapters that only expose numpy/generic iteration and also applied device prefetching for those paths. Unless every `DataAdapter` is guaranteed to implement `get_torch_dataloader()` and return a true iterator (not just a `DataLoader`), this can either break non-DataLoader inputs or regress performance by dropping the prefetch path. Consider keeping an adapter/type-aware fallback here (or wrapping with `iter(...)` if the adapter returns a `DataLoader`) and adding coverage for non-`DataLoader` torch inputs.

Using `self.array[0]` to determine dataset length is fragile because `self._inputs` can be an arbitrarily nested structure, not necessarily an indexable sequence whose first element is a batchable tensor. In those cases this can either fail or report the wrong length. It would be safer to derive the length from `self._num_samples` from the enclosing adapter, or from a flattened first leaf via `tree.flatten(self.array)[0]`.

`get_torch_dataloader()` returns a `DataLoader`, not an iterator. Iterating over it in a `for` loop works, but this test still won't exercise the regression from the issue, which happens when code calls `next(...)` directly after selecting the backend-specific iterator for `steps_per_epoch`. Please add coverage that explicitly does `iter(adapter.get_torch_dataloader())` / `next(...)` (or goes through the epoch iterator path) so we verify the returned object is consumable in the same way as the training loop.

The new backend hook is named `get_torch_dataloader()`, but the issue being fixed is specifically that `EpochIterator` needs an *iterator* when `steps_per_epoch` is used. Returning a `DataLoader` here is easy to misuse because `DataLoader` is iterable, not an iterator, and calling `next(...)` on it will fail unless the caller wraps it in `iter(...)`. To make the contract unambiguous and avoid repeating the original bug in other call sites, consider either renaming this API to something like `get_torch_iterator()` or updating the docstring to explicitly state that callers must do `iter(...)` before consuming it with `next()`.

Returning a `DataLoader` here may reintroduce the original `steps_per_epoch` failure path, because `EpochIterator` advances `_current_iterator` with `next(...)` and a `DataLoader` is iterable but not itself an iterator. Unless every torch caller now wraps this in `iter(...)`, this should return an actual iterator (or the torch epoch iterator should explicitly call `iter(...)`) to avoid `'DataLoader' object is not an iterator` when `steps_per_epoch` is set.

This now delegates directly to `data_adapter_utils.get_numpy_iterator(self.generator)`, but unlike the previous implementation it no longer obviously guarantees that the peeked first batch is yielded back to the caller. Since `__init__` consumes one element via `peek_and_restore()`, please make sure `self.generator` still includes `self._first_batch` (or explicitly prepend it here), otherwise generator-backed training may silently skip the first batch.

Given the issue this PR is fixing, please verify that `get_torch_dataloader()` returns an actual iterator-compatible object for the torch epoch loop when `steps_per_epoch` is set. A `torch.utils.data.DataLoader` is iterable but not itself an iterator, so if the torch trainer calls `next(...)` on the result directly, this will reproduce the original crash unless the backend wraps it with `iter(...)`.

This test exercises `get_torch_dataloader()` by iterating over the returned object directly, but the reported regression is specifically that `fit(..., steps_per_epoch=...)` fails because the torch path expects an actual iterator (`next(...)` is called) rather than just an iterable/DataLoader. As written, this can still pass even if `TorchEpochIterator` is broken. Please add a backend-level test that calls `iter(...)`/`next(...)` on the torch object or, better, a trainer/epoch-iterator test covering `steps_per_epoch` with a `DataLoader` so the original bug is caught.

This now returns a backend-converted iterable rather than a native `torch.utils.data.DataLoader`. That matches the new API name used by the backend iterator path, but the method name is misleading and can easily reintroduce the original bug if callers assume they can do `iter(...)`/`len(...)`/inspect DataLoader-specific attributes. If the contract is intentionally "native torch iterator" rather than an actual DataLoader, consider renaming this method (or documenting the contract clearly) to avoid confusion and future misuse.

For the torch path, `get_torch_dataloader()` returns a `DataLoader`, not an iterator. Iterating over it in a `for` loop is fine here, but this test still won't exercise the regression from the issue, which happens when Keras calls `next()` directly under `steps_per_epoch`. Please add coverage that explicitly uses `iter(adapter.get_torch_dataloader())` or, better, a trainer/epoch-iterator test with `steps_per_epoch` so we verify the returned object is consumable in that code path.

Returning a Torch `DataLoader` here may reintroduce the original `steps_per_epoch` failure if the torch epoch iterator expects an actual iterator and calls `next(...)` directly. To make this adapter contract consistent with the PR goal of creating a native iterator per backend, consider returning an iterator (`iter(...)`) here or ensure all torch-side consumers wrap the result with `iter()` before use. A regression test covering `fit(..., steps_per_epoch=...)` with a TF dataset on the torch backend would help catch this.

Using `get_torch_dataloader()` directly in `enumerate(it)` doesn't exercise the regression described in the issue: the crash happens when code expects an *iterator* and calls `next(...)` on the returned object while `steps_per_epoch` is set. A `DataLoader` is iterable but not itself an iterator, so this test would still pass even if the bug remains. Please add coverage that explicitly checks iterator semantics here (e.g. `it = iter(adapter.get_torch_dataloader())` or, better, a trainer/epoch-iterator level test with `steps_per_epoch`) so the original failure is caught.

This test only verifies that `get_torch_dataloader()` is iterable via `for ... in`, but the reported regression was specifically that `fit(..., steps_per_epoch=...)` ends up calling `next()` on the returned object and crashes because a `DataLoader` is not itself an iterator. It would be good to add an assertion here that the torch path returns a native iterator (e.g. `it = iter(adapter.get_torch_dataloader())` / `next(it)`), or better, a higher-level training test covering `steps_per_epoch`, so this bug is actually caught.

This regression test only verifies the default iteration path for a `DataLoader`, but the reported bug specifically occurs when `steps_per_epoch` is set. Without exercising `EpochIterator(..., steps_per_epoch=...)` here, this test may still pass even if the `DataLoader`/iterator handling remains broken in the bounded-steps path. Please add coverage for a torch `DataLoader` with `steps_per_epoch` to ensure we catch the original crash (`DataLoader` not being an iterator).

This now delegates sizing to the buying power model, which is a good fix for margin-aware quantity calculation, but it also changes the semantics of `Percent(...)`: the old implementation produced an absolute target quantity for the requested portfolio weight, while `GetMaximumOrderQuantityForTargetValue` may return the maximum order size needed to reach that target under the model's constraints. Please verify that this API returns a final target quantity rather than a delta/order quantity in all buying power model implementations; otherwise `PortfolioTarget` could end up holding an order quantity instead of the intended target holdings quantity.

Setting `UniverseSettings.DataNormalizationMode` here is good, but this regression algorithm never actually asserts that the selected securities are using `Raw` normalization. As written, the test could still pass even if this setting is ignored, because the behavior and expected statistics don't explicitly validate the normalization mode. Consider adding a runtime check in `OnSecuritiesChanged` (or similar) that each added security has `security.DataNormalizationMode == DataNormalizationMode.Raw`, and fail the algorithm if not.

This new algorithm file is being added as `<None>`, while most Python algorithm files in this project are included as `<Content>`. If this file is intended to be discovered/copied like the other regression algorithms, using `<None>` may prevent it from being packaged or handled consistently by the build/test infrastructure. Please confirm whether this should be `<Content Include="RawPricesUniverseRegressionAlgorithm.py" />` instead.

Python files in this repo generally avoid C#-style statement terminators. The trailing `;` is unnecessary here and makes the example less idiomatic Python. Consider removing it to keep the regression algorithm consistent with the rest of the Python codebase.

Using the hard-coded string literal `"BTC.Bitcoin"` here makes the regression test depend on the exact symbol-formatting convention introduced by this PR. Since the rest of the change adds type-aware symbol creation and a backward-compatibility shim in `SymbolCache`, it would be safer to place orders using the `Symbol` returned by `AddData<Bitcoin>(...)` (or store it in a field) instead of duplicating the serialization format in the algorithm. That keeps the test focused on behavior and avoids another break if the custom symbol naming scheme changes again.

This change hard-codes the new fully-qualified custom data symbol into the Python regression algorithm, but the PR description explicitly calls out a backwards-compatibility shim via `SymbolCache.GetSymbol("BTC")` for unambiguous custom symbols. Updating this sample to only use `"CHRIS/CME_SP1.QuandlFuture"` means we no longer exercise that compatibility path in Python, which is the area called out as problematic for string-based indexing. Consider adding at least one assertion/access using the legacy ticker form (`"CHRIS/CME_SP1"`) so this regression test verifies both the new unique symbol format and the intended fallback behavior.

Registering the alias in `SymbolCache` before `CreateSecurity(...)` means the cache is mutated even if subscription/security creation fails later in this method. That can leave a stale `alias -> symbol` entry behind and affect subsequent lookups. Consider moving the cache update until after the security/config are successfully created, or wrapping the later steps in a try/catch and rolling back the cache entry on failure.

Passing `typeof(T)` when creating the constituent symbols changes the identity of every symbol produced by the selector, not just the universe symbol. That makes sense for `SecurityType.Base`, but this overload is generic over any `securityType`. If callers use this API with non-`Base` types, the extra type argument is ignored today by `Symbol.Create`, which creates an implicit dependency on that implementation detail. Consider guarding this with `securityType == SecurityType.Base` (or documenting/enforcing that this overload is only intended for custom/base data) so future changes to `Symbol.Create` don't accidentally alter non-base universe behavior.

Passing `null` for the custom data type here preserves the old non-disambiguated `SecurityType.Base` SID format, which can still collide if a user-defined universe contains multiple custom data types with the same ticker. The PR description notes that `Symbol.Create`/base symbol generation now accepts a `Type` specifically to maintain uniqueness for custom universes, so this call site should likely forward the actual custom data type instead of `null`.

Including `SecurityType.Base` here changes the market-hours lookup key from `symbol.Value` to `symbol.ID.Symbol`. For custom data symbols, `ID.Symbol` now contains the type suffix (for example `BTC.Bitcoin`), but the market-hours database entries for base/custom data are typically keyed by the market/ticker pair rather than this internal SID symbol. That means this can break `GetEntry`/lookup behavior for custom data unless the database is also populated with the new suffixed key format everywhere. Please verify the database key format for `SecurityType.Base` and consider keeping `symbol.Value` for market-hours lookups if the type suffix is only meant for SID uniqueness.

Using `dataType.Name` for namespacing can still produce collisions when two custom data types from different namespaces or assemblies share the same class name. Since the issue here is symbol uniqueness by custom data type, consider using a more stable discriminator (for example `FullName`, or another explicitly defined type identifier) instead of just the simple type name.

Passing `baseDataType` straight into `SecurityIdentifier.GenerateBase(...)` changes the identity of all `SecurityType.Base` symbols created through this overload, but the parameter is optional and not validated. If a caller creates a base symbol without supplying `baseDataType`, this will still fall back to the old non-disambiguated SID and can reintroduce collisions depending on the call site. Consider either enforcing `baseDataType != null` for `SecurityType.Base` here or documenting/guarding the fallback explicitly so new callers don't accidentally bypass the fix.

This fallback scans the entire `Symbols` dictionary on every cache miss (`Where(...).ToList()`), which can become expensive as the symbol cache grows and may impact hot paths that rely on string-to-symbol resolution. Since this is intended as a backwards-compatibility shim for custom data, consider narrowing the search to `SecurityType.Base` entries or maintaining a dedicated alias map for bare custom tickers to avoid an O(n) full-text search on misses.

This test verifies that the two `Security` instances remain distinct when accessed by `Symbol`, but it doesn't exercise the backward-compatibility behavior described in the PR (`SymbolCache.GetSymbol("BTC")` resolving a unique custom symbol when only one exists). Since the bug fix also changes symbol cache lookup semantics, it would be useful to add assertions around string-based lookup for both the unique and ambiguous cases to catch regressions there as well.

This test depends on `LiveTradingFeaturesAlgorithm.Bitcoin` from the algorithm project just to obtain a custom data type. Pulling a test in `Tests/Common` across assembly boundaries to `QuantConnect.Algorithm.CSharp` makes the unit test more brittle and increases coupling unnecessarily. Consider defining a small local `BaseData` test stub in this test file (or a shared test helper) instead, so the test only validates `GenerateBase` behavior without depending on an unrelated algorithm type/name remaining stable.

Passing `null` for the custom data type here means this test is still exercising the legacy/non-disambiguated `SecurityIdentifier` path, even though the bug fix is specifically about type-qualified base symbols. That weakens coverage for the new behavior and could let regressions slip through in `SecurityService` when handling custom data created via `AddData<T>`. Consider generating the symbol with the actual custom data type (for example `typeof(LiveTradingFeaturesAlgorithm.Bitcoin)`) and, if needed, adding a separate test for the backward-compatible `null` path.
