The previous code had the same issue, but this refactor is a good opportunity to propagate a proper error instead so the task can fail cleanly and the caller gets structured failure information.

This `unwrap()` can also panic on malformed or unreadable batch content. Since this method now returns a `Result`, it would be better to map this into an application error and return it, rather than aborting processing unexpectedly.

Using `split_once("asc(")` will match `asc(` anywhere in the string, not just at the start. That means malformed values like `fooasc(price)` would still be accepted and parsed as `price`. Since this helper is meant to parse the v1 ranking rule format, it would be safer to validate the full shape explicitly (e.g. `strip_prefix("asc(")` followed by `strip_suffix(")")`) so only exact `asc(field)` rules are accepted.

Same concern here: `split_once("desc(")` accepts `desc(` in the middle of the string, so invalid inputs such as `xxdesc(price)` would parse successfully. Consider using `strip_prefix("desc(")` and `strip_suffix(")")` to ensure the parser only accepts the exact legacy `desc(field)` syntax.

This mapping looks suspicious: `"invalid_request"` is being converted to `Code::InvalidRankingRule`. In the v2 dump format, `invalid_request` is a broad error code and narrowing it to a ranking-rule-specific variant can change the semantics of restored failed tasks. If there is a generic `InvalidRequest`/`BadRequest` equivalent in `meilisearch_error::Code`, it would be safer to map to that instead.

Failing on any unrecognized legacy error code makes dump compatibility brittle: older/newer dumps containing an unmapped code will abort restoration entirely. Since this module is specifically for backward compatibility, consider mapping unknown values to a generic error code (for example `Code::Internal`/`BadRequest`) while preserving the original string, instead of bailing here.

This drops the original `content_uuid` from the legacy dump and replaces it with `Uuid::default()`. If any later processing relies on `content_uuid` to locate the stored document payload, restored document-addition tasks from v3 dumps will no longer be functionally complete. Can we preserve `content_uuid` here instead of discarding it?

Using `unwrap()` while restoring dump data can panic on malformed or unexpected legacy input and abort the whole import. Since this is compatibility code, it would be safer to propagate a structured error or map invalid legacy `uid` values to a recoverable failure instead of crashing.

Making `sender` public weakens the actor abstraction and lets external code bypass the `DumpActorHandle` API by sending arbitrary `DumpMsg`s directly. That makes it harder to preserve invariants and evolve the message protocol safely. If this is only needed for construction elsewhere, prefer keeping the field private and exposing a constructor/factory or a narrowly scoped accessor instead.

Adding `v4` here is fine, but this change also removes the local `compat` helpers that parse legacy `asc(...)` / `desc(...)` ranking rules. If any of the existing dump loaders (`v1`, `v2`, or `v3`) still depend on those helpers for backward-compatibility when restoring older dumps, this will break compilation or legacy dump loading. Please make sure those functions were moved to another shared module and all older loaders still use them, or keep them here until support for old dump formats is fully removed.

This changes a previously working v1 dump loader into an unconditional panic. If v1 dumps are still part of the supported upgrade path, this will break restore at runtime instead of returning a structured error. Even if support is intentionally dropped temporarily, `unreachable!` is misleading here because the code path is reachable; please return an explicit `anyhow::bail!(...)`/domain error or keep the old implementation until the new task-store migration is ready.

The broad `#[allow(dead_code, unreachable_code, unused_variables)]` on `load_dump` is masking the fact that the function body is currently stubbed out. This makes it easier to accidentally ship a broken loader without compiler feedback. Prefer removing the blanket allow and either implementing the migration or returning a normal error from the function.

Using `unreachable!("Updates could never be aborted.")` here makes dump restoration brittle: if a v2 dump contains an `Aborted` status for any reason (older experimental builds, corrupted dumps, or future compatibility mismatches), the process will panic instead of returning a recoverable error. Since this code is part of a migration path, it would be safer to either map `Aborted` explicitly to a v3 representation or return an `anyhow` error with context rather than crashing.

Using `enumerate()` here reassigns task IDs based on the order of entries in `updates/data.jsonl`, which can change the original identifiers from the V3 dump. Since task UIDs are now part of the persisted task resource and may be referenced externally, this migration should preserve the original update ID if `v3::UpdateEntry` contains it, or at least derive the next IDs from dump data rather than the iteration index. Otherwise restored dumps can expose different task IDs than before.

This call ignores the result of `copy_user_id`, which means failures during analytics restoration are silently swallowed. If preserving telemetry state is important for dump restoration, consider propagating or at least logging the error so operators can diagnose why the user id was not restored.

This changes the behavior for V1 dumps from attempting a migration to unconditionally rejecting them. If dropping V1 import support is intentional, it should be reflected in the PR/compatibility notes and ideally covered by a dedicated test, because this is a breaking change for existing dump users. Otherwise, we should keep the previous loader path or add a compatibility migration here instead of bailing out.

Marking this test as ignored leaves the dump path effectively unverified after a substantial refactor (new task store/update file store flow and V4 metadata). Since the PR description still lists integration testing as TODO, this risks shipping regressions in dump creation. Please either adapt the test to the new behavior or add equivalent coverage before merging.

Ignoring the error-path test removes coverage for failure handling in dump generation, which is especially important now that the implementation depends on `register_job`, a oneshot response, and `task_store.dump`. It would be better to update the mocks/assertions for the new flow rather than disabling the test.

Converting `UpdateFileStoreError` through `internal_error!` will map every file-store failure to `Code::Internal`. If any of those errors can be caused by user input or request constraints (e.g. invalid upload state, size/IO conditions that should surface differently), this loses useful error semantics. Please double-check whether `UpdateFileStoreError` needs its own enum variant and `error_code()` mapping instead of being folded into a generic internal error.

The error message still says `Missing update database size`, but this builder field and the surrounding code were renamed from update store to task store. Keeping the old wording will be confusing when this path is hit and makes debugging/configuration harder. Consider updating the message to `Missing task database size` (or similar) for consistency.

This offset calculation looks suspicious: `offset` is already a task id in the public API, but here it is shifted again by `creation_task_id`. That will skip valid tasks for indexes whose creation task id is non-zero (e.g. asking for tasks after task 50 on an index created at task 100 becomes 150). Since you already filter by `index_uid`, the extra addition likely produces incorrect pagination.

Conditioning the `started_at`/`finished_at` import on a terminal status can leave those date indexes empty for tasks that were already started when the dump was created but are restored as `Enqueued`/`Processing`. Since the bug is about date filters after dump import, this may still break `startedAt` queries for in-progress tasks if dumps can contain them. Can we confirm the dump format only includes terminal tasks here, or otherwise index any non-`None` timestamps from the dump regardless of status?

This query string repeats the `limit` parameter (`uids=0&limit=1&limit=1`). Even if the handler currently tolerates duplicate params, this makes the test less precise and can hide bugs in query parsing. It would be better to keep a single `limit=1` here, consistent with the other test cases.

The pagination metadata here looks inconsistent with the response shape: when `from` is `1` and `limit` is `1`, returning `next: 0` is surprising and may hide an off-by-one/import regression in task pagination after dump restore. Since this PR is specifically about task filtering/querying after import, it would be safer to assert the expected pagination contract explicitly (for example `next: null`/omitted when there is no next page, or the next cursor value if one exists) so the snapshot doesn't lock in potentially incorrect behavior.

Adding `uos.umount()` here changes the esp8266 API surface, but this hunk doesn't show any corresponding guard for builds where the underlying VFS object may not implement `umount`. Since `vfs_proxy_call()` dispatches dynamically, calling this on configurations without a matching method could turn into a runtime `AttributeError`/unsupported-operation path. Please make sure this export is conditioned consistently with the actual VFS support on esp8266, or document/handle the failure mode explicitly.

Removing `STATIC` makes this symbol globally visible, which fixes the immediate access issue but also broadens the linkage of an internal helper in a shared extmod file. That increases the risk of name collisions and tightens coupling between ports/modules. If this function only needs to be called from another file, consider exposing it via a dedicated declaration in a private header and keeping the implementation scoped as narrowly as possible, or adding a port-specific wrapper instead of exporting the raw helper directly.

Exposing `fatfs_umount` in the public header broadens the API surface for all ports, but the PR description notes this was done by removing `STATIC` just to support `uos` proxying. If this helper is only needed internally by the VFS/uos implementation, consider keeping it private or introducing a narrower wrapper instead. Making it globally visible increases coupling and the risk of unintended cross-module use.

This directly dereferences `((fs_user_mount_t *)vfs_in)->readblocks[1]` without any validation. If `umount()` is called on an uninitialised/partially constructed VFS object, or after a previous unmount, this can pass a null/invalid pointer into `fatfs_umount()` and recreate the kind of crash this issue is trying to fix. Please guard against a missing block device and consider clearing the stored pointer/state after a successful unmount so repeated calls fail cleanly instead of invoking undefined behaviour.

Removing the default assignment for `IAMSTATIC` changes the shell environment for the rest of this script. If any later logic or sourced helper scripts still read `IAMSTATIC` to decide whether to produce static binaries, this will silently flip behavior from `true` to unset. Given the PR description says cross/static build logic is being removed, it would be safer to confirm there are no remaining references (including in sourced scripts or Dockerfile stages), or replace this with an explicit cleanup/comment so the intent is clear.

Removing the `IAmStatic` linker flag changes the generated version metadata. If any runtime logic, tests, packaging scripts, or downstream consumers still read `dockerversion.IAmStatic`, this will silently fall back to the variable's zero/default value instead of the previous explicit `true`. Please confirm that all references to `IAmStatic` were removed or intentionally updated in the same change, otherwise this can alter reported build characteristics for existing build paths.

Removing `export IAMSTATIC='false'` changes the build environment for this script, but the rest of the file still appears to rely on the dynamic-build path being selected implicitly. If any downstream logic in `hack/make/.binary` or related scripts still keys off `IAMSTATIC` to decide linker flags, tags, or output layout, this can silently regress the daemon build back to static behavior. Please confirm that all consumers of `IAMSTATIC` were removed in this PR, or keep setting it here until that cleanup is complete.

Removing `IAMSTATIC='false'` here may change the behavior of downstream build logic that still keys off this flag to distinguish dynamic vs static builds. Even though this script already clears `LDFLAGS_STATIC_DOCKER` and strips `netgo`/`osusergo`, any remaining checks of `IAMSTATIC` in sourced make helpers could now fall back to their default path and accidentally re-enable static-specific behavior. Please verify that `IAMSTATIC` is no longer consumed anywhere in the dynbinary path, or keep setting it explicitly to avoid regressions.

Treating `errcode.ErrorCodeDenied` as `not found` looks risky. `DENIED` is commonly returned for authentication/authorization failures, so this helper may trigger the new tag-based fallback for permission problems instead of only for the non-conformant "manifest unknown on digest pull" case described in the PR. That can mask the real error and add an unnecessary extra request. Consider limiting this to actual not-found codes (`ManifestUnknown`, `NameUnknown`) or gating `Denied` more narrowly based on registry-specific behavior.

This wraps `err` unconditionally, which means a successful fallback (`ms.Get` returns `nil`) will turn into a non-nil error with the message only. As written, the subsequent `if err != nil` will always fail after the fallback path, even when the manifest was fetched successfully. Please only wrap when `err != nil`, e.g. `if err != nil { err = errors.Wrap(err, ...) }`.

This check rejects any `RUN` flag in the classic builder, which matches the current goal, but it also changes behavior for flags that may already be supported or become supported later without updating this guard. Consider validating only the specific unsupported BuildKit-only flags here (for example `mount`, `network`, `security`) or centralizing the unsupported-flag list, so this doesn't become a blanket blocker that can drift from actual classic-builder capabilities.

Including `"any-flag"` here weakens the test against the behavior described in the issue/PR. Unsupported-but-known flags should require BuildKit, but truly unknown flags are supposed to remain parse errors. If `dispatch()` ever starts distinguishing those cases correctly, this test will fail for the wrong reason. Consider limiting this table to known BuildKit-only RUN flags (for example `mount`, `network`, `security`) and covering unknown flags in a separate parser-level test.

This emits `create`/`tag` events unconditionally for every BuildKit build result, but BuildKit may also return an existing image from cache rather than creating a new one. In that case, `ActionCreate` would be misleading and could generate false-positive notifications. Can we gate this on whether a new image was actually produced, or otherwise align the event semantics with what BuildKit guarantees here?

This callback currently emits an `image create` event unconditionally for every BuildKit export. That can produce duplicate notifications for tagged builds, because those already go through the image service/tagging path and emit image events there. Since the issue is specifically about untagged images, consider guarding this so we only log `create` when the export did not also result in a tag/reference being created, or otherwise deduplicate at the caller.

Emitting the `create` event before `unpackImage` completes can produce a false-positive notification: subscribers may observe an image as "created" even though the build later fails during unpack and the image is not actually usable. Since the issue is about notifying after a successful build, consider moving this event emission until after `unpackImage` succeeds (or otherwise aligning it with the point where the image is fully committed).

This emits the `image create` event before `SetBuiltLocally(id)` succeeds. If `SetBuiltLocally` fails, `CommitImage` still returns an error even though subscribers have already observed a successful create event, which can leave the event stream inconsistent with the API result. Consider moving the event emission until after all post-create bookkeeping succeeds, or otherwise ensure the event is only published once the commit operation is fully successful.

Appending `DOCKER_BUILDKIT` to `cmd.Env` can leave multiple entries for the same variable if the current process environment already contains `DOCKER_BUILDKIT`. Depending on how the child process resolves duplicates, the earlier value may win and make this helper ineffective or flaky in CI. Consider replacing any existing `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=` entry instead of always appending a new one.

Using `before.Format(time.RFC3339)` truncates the timestamp to whole seconds, which can make this test flaky by including older `image create` events from the same second. Since the assertion only checks for the presence of `"image create"`, a pre-existing event could satisfy the test even if the build under test emitted nothing. Consider filtering with a more precise timestamp (for example RFC3339Nano if supported) and/or asserting on the built image ID/name to tie the event to this build.

This subscribes to the full event stream without any filters, so the test can pass or fail based on unrelated daemon activity (especially in shared/parallel CI). It would be safer to scope `Events` with filters such as `type=image` and ideally a `since` timestamp captured just before the build, so we're only observing events produced by this test run.

The error return from `apiClient.Events` is ignored here. If event subscription fails, the test will just wait for the timeout and report a misleading assertion failure. Please assert on the error so setup failures are surfaced directly.

Matching only on `type=image` + `action=create` is fairly broad and can pick up image-create events from other concurrent operations. Since this test is intended to verify the event emitted for the image built here, consider extracting the built image ID from the build output and asserting that `evt.Actor.ID` (or another identifying attribute) matches it.

Using `path.Join` here is a little risky because this test is only skipped on Windows daemon targets, but it can still be compiled/run from a Windows client environment where `t.TempDir()` returns OS-native paths. For filesystem paths, `filepath.Join` is the safer choice and avoids mixing slash semantics across platforms.

The validation error message here is now misleading for the new feature set: this path can also reject valid HTTP proxy configurations, but the log only mentions `socks5[h]://...` syntax. Since `aclk_verify_proxy()` accepts both SOCKS5 and HTTP proxies, this should mention both supported formats (or reuse the same wording as the `http_proxy` env validation) to avoid sending users in the wrong direction when `[agent_cloud_link].proxy` is set to `http://HOST:PORT`.

This returns success (`0`) after logging an invalid port, which means callers may continue with a partially parsed/invalid URL. That looks especially risky now that proxy support increases the number of connection paths depending on correct host/port parsing. Consider returning a non-zero error here and cleaning up any allocated output buffers before exiting.

Closing `developer_log` here can invalidate the `FILE *` before it is passed to `clean_kprobe_events()` in the child path if any future refactoring moves code around, and more importantly the current reordering means the child now closes all descriptors twice: once before `setsid()` and again at the end. Since `fclose(developer_log)` already closes its underlying fd, the second `for (i=getdtablesize(); i>=0; --i) close(i);` can hit an already-closed descriptor and makes the shutdown flow harder to reason about. Consider consolidating descriptor cleanup into a single place and keeping ownership of `developer_log` explicit.

Guarding `release()` with `refCnt() > 0` avoids an `IllegalReferenceCountException`, but it also silently hides double-cleanup / ownership bugs. Since `cleanAllHttpData()` is the factory's final cleanup path, skipping already-freed entries can leave stale `HttpData` objects in the tracking map without surfacing the underlying lifecycle issue. If this is intentional, consider at least documenting why double-release is expected here or removing released entries earlier so the factory doesn't need to special-case invalid ref counts.

This calculation looks wrong in the delimiter-found path. `lastDataPosition` was reset to `0` just above, so `leftBytes` becomes the full remaining writable range and the subsequent compaction is effectively a no-op. I think this should use `startDelimiter` (the number of bytes consumed before the delimiter) when shifting/truncating the buffer, otherwise the consumed bytes are not actually discarded and the memory-saving behavior may not work as intended.

The test name has an inconsistent capitalization (`BIg`), which makes it harder to read and search for. Consider renaming it to something like `testBigFileUploadDecoder` for clarity and consistency with the rest of the test suite.

Using a 1,000,000-byte chunk size here makes the test allocate and process ~100MB twice, which can make the unit test suite slow and memory-sensitive in CI. Since this test is validating streaming behavior and boundary handling rather than throughput, it would be safer to reduce the payload size while still keeping it large enough to exercise the incremental buffering logic.

This assertion relies on a hard-coded 10MB threshold, which is fairly implementation-specific and may become brittle if allocator behavior or internal buffering changes. If the intent is to verify that disk mode does not retain the full upload in memory, consider asserting against a bound derived from the test input (for example, significantly less than `fileSize`) or checking the relative difference between disk and memory modes instead.

Caching only `frameLengthInt` changes the behavior of the later validation path: on subsequent invocations where the frame length was already cached, the local `frameLength` remains `0`, so if `initialBytesToStrip > frameLengthInt` this call will report the wrong frame length to `failOnFrameLengthLessThanInitialBytesToStrip(...)`. More importantly, this introduces split state for a partially decoded frame without resetting it on all exceptional/discard paths, which can leave the decoder out of sync. If you want to cache across invocations, the full frame metadata should be persisted/reset consistently, not just the truncated int length.

Catching `Exception` here is very broad and can hide real bugs in the seekbar preview positioning logic. Since this method runs on every user-driven progress change, swallowing unexpected exceptions and only centering the preview may make failures hard to diagnose and could mask regressions. Consider narrowing this to the specific exceptions you expect (for example, null/layout state issues) and guarding the risky accesses (`getThumb()`, widths, layout params) explicitly before doing the calculation.

This calls `getBitmapAt(progress)` synchronously from `onProgressChanged`, which is invoked very frequently while the user drags the seekbar. If bitmap extraction/cropping/resizing is non-trivial, doing it on the UI thread here can cause jank during scrubbing. It would be safer to ensure this path is fully cached/lightweight, or move the expensive work off the main thread and only post the final image update back to the view.

Recycling `srcBitmap` here is risky because ownership of the bitmap is unclear. `optPreviewThumbnail` is passed in from the caller, so this helper may end up recycling a bitmap that is still cached, reused for later seek positions, or even the same instance returned elsewhere. That can lead to `Canvas: trying to use a recycled bitmap` crashes on subsequent accesses. Consider letting the component that created/caches the bitmap manage its lifecycle, or only recycle the newly created scaled bitmap when replacing it, not the caller-owned source bitmap.

Creating a new `ExecutorService` on every `resetFrom()` call can leave previously started background work running even after a newer reset supersedes it. Since `shutdown()` does not cancel the submitted task, rapid video/metadata changes may still download and decode thumbnails for stale requests, wasting threads, network, and memory. Consider keeping a single executor for this holder (or cancelling the previous `Future`) so outdated jobs can be interrupted instead of only ignored at commit time.

This eagerly downloads every sprite sheet in the selected frameset during reset. For long videos, that can mean fetching and retaining a large number of bitmaps up front, which is risky given the PR's own concern about bitmap memory usage. Since `getBitmapAt()` only needs a subset at any moment, it may be safer to load pages lazily or cache only the currently needed/nearby pages instead of preloading all URLs.

Each call to `getBitmapAt()` will execute this supplier and create a brand new cropped `Bitmap`. During seek scrubbing this can allocate many short-lived bitmaps and put significant pressure on the GC / heap. Consider caching the cropped result per position (or reusing a smaller working bitmap) so repeated requests for the same frame don't keep reallocating.

Access to `currentUpdateRequestIdentifier` is not synchronized or volatile here, while it is written from `resetFrom()` and read from worker threads. Without a happens-before relationship, a background task may observe a stale identifier and continue processing an outdated request. Marking this field `volatile` (or synchronizing reads/writes consistently) would make the cancellation check reliable.

Finding the nearest frame by streaming over the entire key set on every lookup is O(n) per seek event. Since this method is likely called repeatedly while the user drags the seek bar, this can become unnecessarily expensive for videos with many preview frames. Because positions are generated at fixed intervals, you could compute the nearest slot directly from `durationPerFrame` (or use an ordered structure / floor-ceiling lookup) instead of scanning all keys each time.

This builds the exception message without a separator, so callers will get messages like `Failed to download imageIO_ERROR`. Consider adding a delimiter or including the URI as well (e.g. `"Failed to download image: " + getFailReason().getType() + " (" + imageUri + ")"`) to make debugging failures easier.

This only hides the controls for the fullscreen SPACE-key path. The issue description and PR summary mention external resume via media buttons/bluetooth as well, so fixing it here alone leaves other resume entry points inconsistent. Consider centralizing the "hide controls when playback transitions to playing" behavior in the playback state change handler (or another common play/resume path) instead of duplicating it per input source.

Calling `hideControls(0, 0)` unconditionally here may hide the UI even when `play()` does not actually transition the player into a playing state (e.g. buffering, playback failure, or if this callback is invoked while the player is not ready/visible). The PR description mentions doing this only when the player is currently playing, so it would be safer to guard this with a playback-state check or trigger it from the actual `onIsPlayingChanged`/equivalent state callback instead of the media-session command handler.

Removing `f32` from the list is potentially misleading unless support was also removed in code. The PR description says the available types are limited, but this doc change should stay aligned with the actual accepted values for the env var. If `f32` is still a valid cache type in the backend or inherited from llama.cpp, users may lose a supported option and the docs will become inaccurate. Please either keep `f32` documented (perhaps marked as not recommended) or explicitly note that Ollama does not support it.

This changes the KV size calculation from pure integer math to `float64` and then casts back to `uint64`. For large context sizes/model dimensions, that can introduce rounding error in the scheduler's memory estimate, and it's unnecessary here because the supported cache types are all simple rational multipliers (`f16=2`, `q8_0=1`, `q4_0=0.5`). It would be safer to keep this in integer arithmetic (e.g. compute a base element count and apply a numerator/denominator pair) so the estimate stays exact.

This refactor removed the previous `checkFlashAttentionSupport` gating from the size estimate. That means `estimateKvCacheSize` will now calculate reduced memory for `q8_0`/`q4_0` even on models/runners where quantized KV cache isn't actually supported and the runtime may fall back to `f16`. Since the PR description says the scheduler uses these estimates, this can under-estimate required memory and lead to incorrect placement/OOM decisions. Consider normalizing `cacheType` here based on actual support (or ensure callers always pass a validated/effective cache type rather than the requested one).

This change removes the previous coverage for cache-type selection and flash-attention gating, leaving only a unit test for `kvCacheBytesPerElement`. Given the feature being added, that creates a regression in functional test coverage: we no longer verify that invalid/unsupported KV cache types fall back correctly, that embedding models reject quantized KV, or that hardware/flash-attention constraints are enforced. Please keep this new low-level test, but consider restoring the higher-level behavior tests (or replacing them with updated equivalents) so the end-to-end cache-type selection logic remains protected.

This now gates KV cache quantization on flash-attention support, which looks stricter than the feature itself. The PR description says KV cache quantization should be independently configurable, but with this logic `kvct` stays empty unless flash attention is enabled *and* supported, so memory estimation will fall back to the default path even on backends/models that support quantized KV without FA. That can under-estimate the feature's availability and produce incorrect scheduler decisions. Consider checking KV-cache-type support independently of flash attention here.

This change removes the normalization step (`kVCacheQuantization`) before calling `estimateKvCacheSize`, so the test no longer verifies that unsupported/requested cache types are coerced to the actual runtime type. Given the issue/PR description says the scheduler should account for quantized KV estimates, this can hide mismatches between estimation and the real cache type selection logic. Consider either keeping the pre-normalization here or adding a separate test that asserts `estimateKvCacheSize(kVCacheQuantization(...), ...)` matches runtime behavior, especially for unsupported/unknown types and model-specific restrictions.

The embedding test setup no longer switches `arch` to `bert`, but the comments still describe embedding-specific behavior. If `createTestModel("llama", "mean")` is not treated the same as an embedding model by the production logic, these cases may stop exercising the intended path and could give false confidence. Please confirm that `poolingType = "mean"` alone is sufficient, or restore the architecture change so the test matches a real embedding model configuration.

This now appends `--kv-cache-type` whenever the model supports the requested type, but the PR description and llama.cpp behavior indicate KV cache quantization is only valid when flash attention is enabled. If `envconfig.KvCacheType()` is set to a quantized value while `fa` is false, we silently ignore it; if `fa` is true but the env var is empty/default, we may still pass an unsupported/invalid value depending on `SupportsKVCacheType("")`. Consider explicitly gating non-default KV cache types on flash attention and warning when a requested KV cache type cannot be applied because flash attention is disabled, so users don't get a silent no-op.

This returns `true` for an empty `GpuInfoList`, which can incorrectly report flash-attention support when no GPU is present. Since the method name implies actual device capability, consider explicitly handling `len(l) == 0` and returning `false` (or renaming the method to reflect the current `all GPUs support it` semantics).

This says K/V cache quantization can be used `when Flash Attention is enabled`, but the wording is ambiguous about whether Flash Attention is merely beneficial or actually required. If quantized KV cache only works with Flash Attention in the implementation, it would help to state that explicitly here (for example, "requires Flash Attention"). Otherwise users may set `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE` alone and be confused when it has no effect or falls back silently.

This introduces a free-form string env var, but the help text already implies a constrained set of supported values. It would be safer to validate and normalize this at the config boundary (for example: lowercase + allow only `f16`, `q8_0`, `q4_0`) so invalid values fail early with a clear error instead of propagating deeper into runner/scheduler logic and potentially causing mismatched memory estimates or backend errors.

This sets `type_v` from the same user-provided KV cache type as `type_k`, but the issue/PR context and llama.cpp support are primarily about quantizing the **key** cache. Quantizing V as well can have a larger quality impact and removes the ability to keep the current safer default for values. If the intended feature is key-cache quantization, consider leaving `type_v` at `GGML_TYPE_F16` or exposing K and V as separate options so users can opt into V quantization explicitly.

The new flag is passed through as a raw string, but this hunk doesn't show any validation or normalization before it reaches `llama.NewContextParams`. Since the PR description only supports a limited set of KV cache types (`f16`, `q8_0`, `q4_0`), it would be safer to validate here (and ideally normalize case) so invalid values fail fast with a clear CLI error instead of depending on lower layers or silently falling back.

This fallback only forces `f16` when flash attention is unavailable, except for `f32`. That means an invalid explicit value (for example `"bogus"`) will also be silently converted to `f16` before reaching the `switch`, so the warning in the `default` branch never fires in the non-flash-attention path. Consider validating/normalizing `cacheType` before this fallback, or reusing `kVCacheQuantization` here, so unsupported values are handled consistently and still logged.

This test sets up `tt.gpus`, but the value is never passed into `kVCacheQuantization` or otherwise injected into the code under test. As written, cases that differ only by GPU capabilities (`cuda` driver 6 vs 8, metal, rocm, multi-GPU, etc.) won't actually validate the intended behavior and may give false confidence. Consider wiring the GPU list into the function under test (or mocking the GPU discovery path it uses) so these table entries exercise real hardware-dependent logic.

When `numGPULayers < 0`, this test does not clear `OLLAMA_NUM_GPU_LAYERS`. Since `t.Setenv` only applies in branches where the value is non-negative, a previously set process env could leak into these cases and make them order/environment dependent. It would be safer to always set the variable explicitly for every test case (for example, to `"-1"` or `""`).

This division can panic if `ggml.KV().BlockCount()` is 0. The existing code already tolerated missing layer metadata (`blk.0` warning above), so it would be safer to guard this path as well and either skip the per-layer KV adjustment or fall back to a conservative estimate when block count is unavailable.

This test is intended to verify the fallback behavior when flash attention is disabled, but it only sets `OLLAMA_FLASH_ATTENTION=false` and never clears `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE`. Since `kVCacheQuantization` appears to consult environment configuration elsewhere in this file, a previously-set `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE` could override `tt.cacheType` and make these cases order-dependent or flaky. Consider explicitly resetting `OLLAMA_KV_CACHE_TYPE` inside this test (or avoiding env-dependent resolution here) so the test remains isolated.

This creates a new empty `discover.GpuInfoList`, so `gpus.FlashAttentionSupported()` will always evaluate against no devices instead of the actual GPUs passed into `NewLlamaServer`. As a result, flash attention (and the new KV cache quantization path gated behind it) can be incorrectly disabled even on supported hardware. Consider passing the real `gpus` list into `checkFlashAttentionSupport` rather than constructing an empty one here.

Switching the default indices from `Enumerable.Range(0, frameCount)` to `TilesCount` fixes the tileset-image case, but it also changes behavior for templates that omit `Frames` when `TilesCount` is smaller than the available frame count. In that case we now silently ignore trailing frames instead of validating that the image layout matches the sprite data. If `TilesCount` can be unset / zero or inconsistent with the actual image, this may mask bad definitions. Consider clarifying the intended precedence here and adding a guard or fallback when `TilesCount <= 0` (or when it exceeds `frameCount`) so invalid templates still fail deterministically.

Switching these validation failures from `InvalidOperationException` to `YamlException` improves classification, but the new messages no longer include the YAML node location (`info.Nodes[0].Location`) that the previous code embedded. That location is especially useful for linting and for diagnosing invalid sequence definitions in large rulesets. If `YamlException` here is not automatically associated with the current node elsewhere, consider preserving the location in the exception message or constructing the exception with node context so the error still points to the offending YAML entry.

When a terrain template references a missing frame, this only logs the problem but does not mark the command as failed. That means the utility can still exit successfully even though it found invalid tile sprite references, which seems inconsistent with the issue being fixed and with the sequence checks below that set `failed = true`. Consider setting `failed = true` here as well.

Missing template image files are reported here, but they also don't currently cause the command to fail. Since the purpose of this utility is to lint missing sprites, it would be safer to set `failed = true` in this callback so missing tileset images produce a non-zero exit code just like missing sequence sprites do.

Disabling the dropdown when only one valid owner exists prevents changing the value, but it doesn't ensure the actor is actually assigned to that valid owner. If an existing map already contains an invalid owner for this actor, the UI will now be locked in an invalid state and the underlying crash condition can still persist. Consider normalizing `selectedOwner`/`actor.Owner` to the sole valid owner here (or when loading the actor) instead of only disabling the control.

Removing `Range<T>` from this file can be a breaking change if any code in the solution still references `OpenRA.Graphics.Range<T>`. Since this PR description mentions deleting unused types, please double-check there are no remaining references (including reflection/serialization or external consumers) before removing it, or consider moving it to a more appropriate shared location instead of deleting it outright.

Disabling nullable immediately after `IGlobalModData` means only this single interface is nullable-aware while the rest of `Manifest.cs` remains in the old context. That can create inconsistent nullability contracts within the same file, especially if the surrounding types interact with this interface. If the intent is to migrate this file progressively, consider enabling nullable for the whole file or at least for the related declarations in this area so annotations and warnings are applied consistently.

The null-forgiving cast here relies on the constructor invariant that `item1OrArray` is initialized whenever `item2OrSentinel == Sentinel`. That isn't obvious at the use site, and because `Values` is a struct it can also be default-constructed, which would violate that invariant and turn this into a runtime NRE. Consider making the backing fields non-nullable with explicit initialization, or guarding against the default struct state before dereferencing `item1OrArray`.

This change preserves behavior, but `GetConstructor(Type.EmptyTypes)` can still return `null` when the type has no public parameterless constructor. With nullable reference types enabled, this line is still a potential NRE site because `Invoke` is called unconditionally. Consider routing this through `GetCtor(type)` or adding an explicit null check with a clearer exception so failures are easier to diagnose.

Using a no-op delegate here changes the ordering semantics if `DelayedAction.CompareTo` uses the `Action` field as a tie-breaker after `desiredTime`. Previously `null` acted as a sentinel value that was guaranteed not to collide with a real callback; now any queued `() => { }` (or another delegate considered equal) at the same timestamp could affect the insertion point and which actions are selected as pending. It would be safer to keep a dedicated sentinel that cannot overlap with real queue entries, or refactor the search to compare only on time.

With `#nullable enable`, the constructor parameter is now treated as non-null at compile time, but this runtime guard was also protecting callers from other nullable-oblivious code paths (e.g. older code, reflection, or external consumers) and ensuring a clear `ArgumentNullException` instead of a later `NullReferenceException` on `stream.CanRead`. Consider keeping an explicit null check here, or annotate/guard in a way that preserves the previous exception behavior.

Removing the explicit null check changes the exception behavior for `null` inputs: `stream == null` will now surface as a `NullReferenceException` on `stream.CanSeek` instead of an argument-related exception. Even with nullable reference types enabled, callers from non-nullable-aware code can still pass `null`, so it would be better to keep an explicit guard (e.g. `ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(stream)` or the project's equivalent helper) to preserve a clearer contract and failure mode.

Using `GetFieldUnforgiving(f)` changes the failure mode here from a possible fallback/null path to an exception if the field name cannot be resolved. Since `f` appears to come from parsed YAML/input, this makes `InvalidValueAction` less robust and could turn a recoverable config error into a hard failure. Consider preserving the previous tolerant behavior here (e.g. check for a missing field and log/return a safer default) unless the field name is guaranteed to always exist.

Disabling nullable analysis immediately after `ISoundLoader` means the related `ISoundFormat` interface remains oblivious to nullability, even though it is part of the same sound-loading contract. That weakens the benefit of annotating `TryParseSound`, because implementations/consumers of `ISoundFormat` in this file won't participate in the same analysis. Consider keeping `#nullable enable` for the whole file unless there is a specific warning here that still needs to be addressed.

Changing `text` to nullable alters the public contract of this extension method, but the comment above still says it writes a length-prefixed string without documenting how `null` is encoded. Since this is a serialization helper, callers need to know whether `null` is supported and what wire format it produces; otherwise this can introduce subtle compatibility issues with existing readers/writers. Please either keep the parameter non-nullable or update the method/docs to make the null encoding explicit and ensure the corresponding read path handles it consistently.

Changing `Add` from `object` to `string` is a behavioral breaking change for existing callers that may currently pass numbers, booleans, enums, or other values and rely on `ToString()` here. If that API narrowing is intentional, it would be good to verify all call sites; otherwise consider keeping an overload that accepts `object` (or a generic/value overload) and converts safely, while still annotating nullability explicitly.

Defining `System.Diagnostics.CodeAnalysis.MemberNotNullAttribute` unconditionally can conflict on target frameworks/runtimes where this attribute is already publicly available. Since the comment mentions this is only a Mono workaround, please consider guarding this file with a framework/feature conditional (or `#if !NET5_0_OR_GREATER` / equivalent project symbol) so we don't end up with duplicate type definitions on other builds.

Changing `byPlayer` to nullable on `IDefaultVisibility` broadens the contract for every implementation and caller. Please verify that all implementers of this interface have been updated to accept `Player?` and correctly handle the null case; otherwise this can create nullability mismatches or hide a behavioral change behind the annotation update.

This nullable change affects the full `IVisibilityModifier` contract. It would be good to confirm that all implementations and call sites are intentionally prepared for `byPlayer == null`, since this is not just a warning fix but a semantic API change that may require explicit null handling in implementers.

Returning `Activity?` changes the interface contract and may ripple into consumers that previously assumed a non-null `Activity`. Please make sure all callers are updated to handle a null return explicitly, and consider documenting what `null` means here so implementations stay consistent.

`Path.GetDirectoryName(string?)` returns `null` not only when `path` is `null` or a root directory, but also when the input does not contain any directory information (e.g. just a file name like `"foo.txt"`). Throwing `ArgumentException` here will therefore break valid existing call sites that may reasonably pass file names. Consider either renaming/documenting this helper to reflect the stricter contract, or handling the no-directory case separately instead of treating it as an argument error.

Adding an alias to the `OpenRA.Game` project reference changes how that assembly can be consumed from this project: once an alias is specified, the default global reference is no longer guaranteed unless every consuming file is updated appropriately. Since this PR is about nullable annotations and the surrounding diff doesn't show any corresponding `extern alias OpenRAGame;` usage, this introduces a cross-project build risk and an unrelated behavioral change. If the alias is only needed for a small number of files, consider adding it only where required and verify the project still compiles without breaking existing unqualified references.

Changing the `Count[0] == n` case from an early return to an exception alters the behavior of `Huffman` construction in a potentially incompatible way. In the original implementation this appears to represent a valid 'empty code set' state that the caller may handle later, whereas this now treats the same input as corrupt. Please verify against the blast format/spec and existing callers that an empty tree is truly invalid here; otherwise this could reject inputs that were previously accepted.

This field is now annotated as nullable even though it is initialized to a non-null default. Unless the engine intentionally supports disabling engine credits by setting this to null in mod data, this weakens the contract unnecessarily and forces downstream null checks. Consider keeping `EngineCreditsFile` as non-null and only marking fields nullable where `null` is a valid configuration value.

Disabling nullable immediately after the interface limits the benefit of this migration and can create inconsistent nullability contracts within the same file. If `ExternalConditionInfo` and related types in this file are also low-dependency, consider keeping `#nullable enable` for the whole file (or at least documenting why only the interface is opted in) so consumers don't have to reason about mixed nullable contexts here.

Disabling nullable immediately after the interface limits the benefit of this migration to just `IWallConnectorInfo`, while the rest of the file remains unchecked. Since this PR is progressively enabling nullable annotations on types/files, consider keeping `#nullable enable` for the remainder of the file (or at least for `WithWallSpriteBodyInfo` and related types) so any nullability issues in the implementation are surfaced too.

Allowing `resourceType` to be nullable here changes the public contract of `IResourceLayerInfo`, but the companion method `TryGetResourceIndex` on the same interface was also updated to accept `string?`. If `null` is intended to be a valid input for these lookup-style methods, it would be worth documenting the expected behavior explicitly (e.g. always return `false` and set the out value to default) so implementers handle it consistently. Otherwise, keeping the parameter non-nullable would better preserve the previous API semantics.

Converting `VersionCheckProtocol` with the default `ToString()` can be culture-sensitive for some value types and may also change the wire format if this isn't already a string. Since this value is sent as part of a web request, it would be safer to serialize it explicitly using an invariant format (or keep the original type if `HttpQueryBuilder` can handle it) so the request payload remains stable across locales and future type changes.

Disabling nullable immediately after `IMock` leaves the rest of the test types in this file outside nullable analysis, including `MockTraitInfo.Create`, which still returns `null` from a non-nullable `object` return type. If the goal is to progressively annotate this file, it would be better to keep `#nullable enable` for the whole file (or at least annotate `Create` as returning `object?` if the base signature allows it) so this test code doesn't silently bypass the nullability checks introduced by the PR.

This now unconditionally queues a `DockActivity` whenever the minelayer is out of ammo, but the issue being fixed is specifically that rearming should work even when the unit does **not** have `Repairable`. Can `DockActivity` operate correctly for a `Rearmable` actor without any repair-related trait or target preselection? If it still depends on shared docking/repair infrastructure, this could preserve the original regression where the rearm order/cursor never appears for non-repairable ground units. It would be worth adding/confirming a test case for a minelayer with `Rearmable` but no `Repairable` trait.

This overload is named `CountActorsWithTrait<T>`, but the actual filter now also depends on the actor implementing `IDockable` and matching the provided `dockType`. That makes the method semantics quite different from the existing `actorName` overload and can be misleading to callers/readers. Consider renaming this to something more explicit (e.g. `CountDockableActorsWithTrait<T>` or `CountActorsWithTraitAndDockType<T>`) to avoid confusion and accidental misuse.

This now unconditionally queues a `DockActivity` through `rearmable.DockManager`, but the reported issue is that rearming should not depend on unrelated repair/docking traits being present. Can we confirm that `DockManager` is always initialized for every `Rearmable` aircraft/unit and that it can resolve a valid rearm dock independently of `Repairable`? Otherwise this change may preserve or reintroduce the bug where the rearm flow silently fails when no repair-capable dock is available.

This looks like leftover debug logging. Writing to `Console` from an activity constructor will spam output for every docking attempt and bypass the engine's normal logging/debug facilities. Please remove this or gate it behind an appropriate debug/logger mechanism before merging.

This changes the proximity check from the configured `CloseEnough` distance to exact cell equality, which is a significant behavioral regression for docking. It can break cases where units are supposed to dock/rearm/repair from adjacent cells or from within a radius, and it seems directly related to the issue about ground units not being able to rearm unless they also satisfy other docking assumptions. Can we preserve the distance-based check here instead of requiring `self.Location == dock.Location`?

We're always issuing a generic `MoveTo(dock.Location, ...)` here, even for aircraft, while the aircraft-specific landing path is commented out and only runs after `isCloseEnough` becomes true. Combined with the exact-cell check above, this risks making aircraft and other non-cell-sharing dockables fail to ever reach a valid docking state. Should aircraft still use their dedicated landing/offset approach here, or should the move target account for `AircraftOffset`/dock-specific approach rules?

This hard-codes `RepairableInfo` when determining the actor below, which reintroduces the exact coupling described in the issue: rearming/docking behavior now depends on the host also being repairable. Since this refactor is meant to unify docking across repair/rearm/harvest, the occupancy check should use the new docking abstraction (`Dock`, `DockManager`, or a dock-related trait/interface) instead of `RepairableInfo`.

Looks like leftover debug logging. Writing directly to `Console` from an activity will spam output in normal gameplay and isn't consistent with the engine logging patterns. Please remove this or replace it with a gated/debug logger if it's still needed.
This appears to be temporary debug output. It should be removed before merge, otherwise every dock sequence completion will print to stdout.
Another direct `Console.WriteLine` in hot gameplay code. This will generate noisy logs on every cancel and should be removed or converted to proper debug logging.

The condition here looks inverted. In the `Dock` state you undock when `IsCanceling || !CanStillDock()`, but in `Loop` this uses `!IsCanceling || !CanStillDock()`, which means the loop continues even when the activity is canceling as long as the dock is still valid. That seems inconsistent with the surrounding state machine and may prevent cancellation from taking effect promptly.

This changes the stuck-harvester check from the refinery delivery cell to the dock actor's base `Location`. In the old code the comparison used `Location + DeliveryOffset`, which matters for structures whose docking/delivery point is not on the actor origin. Using `lastproc.Location` here can prevent the unblock logic from triggering when the harvester is actually parked on the dock cell, leaving it stuck after delivery. Can this use the docking cell/offset exposed by the new docking system instead of the raw actor location?

Similar to the unblock logic above, this now seeds the resource search from `LinkedDock.Location` instead of the actual docking/delivery position. Previously `GetSearchFromProcLocation()` used the refinery `DeliveryOffset`, so harvesters resumed searching from the cell they really returned to. Falling back to the structure origin may skew the search center for docks with non-zero offsets and regress harvester behavior around larger or asymmetrical buildings. Consider using the dock's resolved docking cell here.

This changes the callback contract from the actor to `DockManager`, but `TraitOrDefault<DockManager>()` can be `null`. If any `INotifyHarvesterAction` implementation assumes a non-null argument, cancelling harvest on actors without a `DockManager` will now fail or silently skip important cleanup. Please verify all implementations were updated for the new parameter semantics, or guard/fallback here to preserve previous behavior.

`dockAnimPlayed` is set to `true` unconditionally, even when `wda == null` and no docking animation was actually started. That makes the undock path behave as if a dock animation completed, which contradicts the guard in `OnStateUndock()` and can trigger undock notifications/behavior for units that never visually docked. Consider only setting this flag when a docking animation is present (or rename/split the flag so it reflects "entered dock state" vs "animation played").

This uses `Trait<WithVoxelUnloadBody>()`, which will throw if the actor doesn't define that trait. Since `VoxelDockSequence` is a new generic docking activity, it would be safer to either validate this requirement at construction time with a clearer error message or use `TraitOrDefault` and guard the voxel-specific behavior. Otherwise a misconfigured dockable unit will fail at runtime in a way that's hard to diagnose.

This now only checks for `Repairable`, but the issue being fixed is that some ground units need to dock for rearming without having a `Repairable` trait. With this change, those units will still fail the cursor/order generation path because `TraitOrDefault<Repairable>()` returns null. If docking is meant to be unified across repair/rearm/resupply, this lookup should probably go through a shared dockable interface/base trait (or also consider `Rearmable`) instead of hard-coding `Repairable` here.

This now unconditionally queues `DockActivity` using `dockManager` and `rearmable`, but both are initialized via `TraitOrDefault`. For aircraft that have `AircraftInfo` but do not carry one or both docking traits, this can turn a previously valid script call into a null-driven failure or a no-op with unclear behavior. Since this PR is specifically addressing docking/rearmability edge cases, it would be safer to either guard here with a clear script exception/message when docking is unsupported, or keep the old behavior for non-dockable aircraft.

Changing the script API from `Actor destination = null` to `Dock destination = null` is a breaking change for existing scripts that pass a host actor/building to `ReturnToBase`. If script compatibility matters, consider keeping the `Actor`-based overload and resolving its `Dock` internally, or at least documenting this migration explicitly.

This now assumes every actor exposing `HarvesterProperties` also has a `Harvester` trait, and will throw at runtime if a script calls `FindResources()` on an actor without it. Since this PR is refactoring docking/harvesting behavior, can we either keep the previous actor-based constructor if available, or guard this with a trait check / clearer error so scripts fail predictably?

This path now unconditionally queues `DockActivity(dockManager, rearmable)` when `IdleBehavior == ReturnToBase` and no dock is linked, but `dockManager` is optional (`TraitOrDefault`) and may be null for aircraft definitions that haven't been migrated yet. That would either create a null-driven activity or silently change behavior compared to the previous `ReturnToBase` flow. Please guard this with a null check (or ensure `DockManager` is mandatory for all `ReturnToBase` aircraft) before queuing the activity.

The trailing `// error` looks like a temporary debugging note left in production code. It doesn't explain the intended behavior and suggests this branch may still be known-broken. Please remove it or replace it with a proper comment/tests if there is a real edge case here.

This changes the stop-order exemption from `Resupply`/`ReturnToBase` to only `DockSequence`. Given the issue here is that rearming should no longer depend on `Repairable`, please double-check that every rearm/docking flow now keeps `DockSequence` as the current top-level activity. If any path still uses a child activity (or another wrapper) while docking, `AttackBase.OnStopOrder` will now cancel it and the rearm cursor/behavior may regress. A brief comment here explaining that all former resupply/return-to-base cases are now unified under `DockSequence` would also make this safer to maintain.

This check became much broader after the refactor: `DockSequence` now appears to be the shared docking activity for harvesters, repair, rearm, and other docking flows. `IsRearming` will therefore likely return true for units that are merely docking for non-rearm reasons, which can change bot squad behavior. Can we narrow this to actual rearm-capable dockables (e.g. inspect the actor's dockable trait/state or the dock type) instead of matching the generic activity type?

This changes the building count from summing explicit `RearmActors` to counting by `DockType`, which matches the new docking model, but it may broaden the result set. If multiple unrelated building types share the same dock type, the bot could overestimate available reload capacity and stop producing air units too early. Please verify that `DockType` is unique enough for rearming here, or add an additional filter so we only count structures that can actually service this actor.

Typo in the public API: `IsUncoccupied` should be renamed to `IsUnoccupied` (or `IsOccupied` with inverted logic). Since this is a new trait introduced by the refactor, fixing the spelling now will avoid propagating the typo into call sites and mod code.

This method only checks occupancy/alliance/liveness, but it never validates that the dock actually supports the requesting unit's dock type (`Info.Type` / `MyDockType`). Given the issue and PR goal of decoupling rearm/repair/harvest docking, omitting the dock-type compatibility check here risks allowing unrelated dockables to reserve or target the wrong dock.

This allocates a temporary list every time occupancy is refreshed. Since `RefreshOccupancy()` is called from hot paths (`Occupancy`, `CanDock`, `Reserve`, etc.), this may create avoidable GC pressure. Consider iterating backwards over `reservedDockables` and removing dead entries in-place instead.

This unconditional `false` for `TransformOnDock` looks like it reintroduces the issue described in the PR/issue context: deploy/return-to-base docking becomes unavailable unless some other non-transform dockable path exists. Since `CanIssueDeployOrder` depends on `DockingPossible(ReturnToBaseDockType)`, transform-on-dock units will never get a deploy/rearm order even if a valid dock exists. Consider checking the configured dock types against the available `IDockable`s here instead of hard-blocking all transform-on-dock cases.

By dropping `IIssueOrder`/`IOrderVoice` here, this trait no longer contributes its own `Move`/`Enter` targeters or voice responses. `ResolveOrder` still accepts `Move`, but there is nothing in this class now that exposes how those orders are issued, and the old `Enter` handling was removed entirely. Can you confirm that another trait now provides both the order targeters/cursors and voice for transformed aircraft? Otherwise this looks like a behavior regression where transformed aircraft can no longer show the enter/rearm cursor or receive player-issued docking orders.

This condition no longer preserves the `ReturnToBase` parent activity that the old code explicitly exempted. Since the previous behavior avoided cancelling both `Resupply` and its `ReturnToBase` wrapper, narrowing this to only `DockSequence` may cause stop orders from unrelated traits to interrupt an in-progress docking/return flow. Please verify that `ReturnToBase` has been fully removed or is now always represented by `DockSequence`; otherwise this change could reintroduce the rearm/docking interruption issues this refactor is trying to solve.

Using `dock.Location` here changes the transport target from the refinery's delivery cell (`refineryActor.Location + iao.DeliveryOffset`) to the dock actor's base location. For harvesters/refineries, the delivery offset is often the actual interaction point, so this may regress carried harvesters being dropped at the wrong cell or failing to reach the dock correctly. If `Dock.Location` is intended to represent the usable dock cell, please verify that it already includes the equivalent of `DeliveryOffset`; otherwise this should likely use the dock's interaction offset rather than the raw actor location.

This callback assumes `RequireForceMoveCondition` is always non-null, but the field defaults to `null` and the observer is only registered conditionally. If `GetVariableObservers()` is never invoked before `DockingPossible(...)` is used, `requireForceMove` will stay at its default `false`, which can make the force-move gate ineffective until the first variable update. Consider initializing `requireForceMove` from `Info.RequireForceMoveCondition` during construction/trait creation so the initial docking cursor/validation state matches the configured condition immediately.

This loop can become non-terminating when modifiers keep `value` above `storageLimit` even after `count` reaches 0 (e.g. positive flat/rounding effects, or a negative `storageLimit` edge). The previous refinery-based `AcceptResources` path delegated this logic elsewhere, but now harvester unloading owns the fit-to-storage calculation. Please add a `count > 0` guard and handle the zero-count case explicitly before calling `GiveResources`/returning.

This changes resource acceptance notifications from the docking structure to every owned actor with `INotifyResourceAccepted`. That is a significant behavior change from the old `IAcceptResources.AcceptResources(...)` flow, where the accepting building controlled side effects. It may break traits that expect notifications only from the actual refinery/dock actor. Consider routing the notification through the current dock/acceptor actor instead of broadcasting to all owned actors.

This changes the production gate from the old `Reservable.IsReserved(self)` check to a `Dock`-specific occupancy check, which means production is now blocked only when a `Dock` trait exists and reports occupied. That can unintentionally change behavior for producers that previously relied on `Reservable` but do not define `Dock`, because `TraitOrDefault<Dock>()?.IsUncoccupied() ?? true` will evaluate to `true` and allow production. If the intent is to preserve the old reservation semantics during the docking refactor, we should either keep compatibility with `Reservable` here or ensure every affected producer now always has a `Dock` trait.

This now allows any actor with `Rearmable` to dock as long as it is missing ammo, but the reported issue was specifically that ground units could not rearm unless they also had `Repairable`. Can you verify that the new `Dockable`/`DockManager` path still exposes a rearm order/cursor for non-repairable ground units based solely on `DockType = rearm`? If order discovery still depends on repair-related traits elsewhere, this change alone won't fully address the bug.

Changing from explicit `RearmActors` to a shared `DockType` is a significant behavioral shift. Please make sure there is validation or migration coverage for mods that previously relied on actor-specific rearm targets, otherwise units may unexpectedly be able to dock at any structure exposing the same dock type.

This implementation now listens to the generic docking callback, but `WithHarvestAnimation` appears to only care about harvester-specific behavior. By replacing `MovingToRefinery(Actor self, Actor refineryActor)` with `MovingToDock(DockManager dockable, Dock dock)`, this trait may start receiving notifications for any dockable action routed through `DockManager` (repair/rearm as well as harvesting). If this trait is attached to actors that can use multiple dock types, consider filtering on the dock/action type before treating it as a harvest-related transition, otherwise the animation state could be driven by unrelated docking operations.

This refactor changes the callback from a harvester-specific event to the generic docking event, but `WithHarvestOverlay` now no longer knows whether the dock movement is actually a refinery/resource-return action. Since this trait is harvest-specific, please verify that implementing `INotifyDockableAction.MovingToDock` here doesn't cause the overlay state to react to unrelated docking flows (e.g. repair/rearm) for actors that may share the dockable infrastructure. If needed, guard on the dock type or originating trait to preserve the previous behavior.

This method was switched from `INotifyDockResupply.BeforeResupply(Actor, ResupplyType)` to `INotifyDock.Docked(DockManager, Dock)`, but the implementation still references `types`. In the new signature there is no `ResupplyType` parameter, so this either won't compile or no longer has enough information to determine whether the dock interaction is actually a repair. Since the issue/PR is about separating docking behaviors, this overlay should avoid assuming every dock event implies repair. Please derive the repair state from the new dock API (e.g. dock type/capabilities) or preserve equivalent resupply-type information in the notification.

Same concern here: after migrating to `INotifyDock.DockTick(DockManager, Dock)`, this code still depends on `types.HasFlag(ResupplyType.Repair)`. Beyond the compile issue, using a generic dock tick for repair overlay updates risks showing repair visuals for non-repair docking actions (e.g. rearm/refuel) unless the new API exposes the dock purpose. Please update this logic to use the new docking metadata instead of the removed `ResupplyType` argument.

This state is exposed as a mutable public field, but the trait itself doesn't implement any docking-related interface or event hook here, so it's not obvious who is responsible for keeping `Docked` in sync with the new docking system. Given the issue this PR is addressing, a render trait that depends on external ad-hoc mutation is easy to miss during the refactor and can leave the unload body stuck in the wrong state. Consider wiring this to an explicit docking/condition signal or at least encapsulating the state behind a method/property updated by the dock manager.

This now only looks for an active `RepairsUnits` trait after docking has already started, and returns `true` immediately if none is found. Given the issue this PR is addressing, that means a unit with `Repairable` can still dock at a repair/rearm structure that has no active repair capability, but the dock will complete without doing anything. If `Rearmable` relies on the same docking flow, this can still block rearming behind the presence of `RepairsUnits`. Consider validating repair capability as part of dock eligibility/targeting instead of silently succeeding here, or otherwise ensure rearm-only docks remain usable when no `RepairsUnits` trait is present.

This refactor removes the start/finish repair notification fields from `RepairsUnitsInfo`, but the issue being fixed is that rearming should no longer depend on the `Repairable` trait. If any UI/cursor/order logic or YAML definitions still rely on `RepairsUnits` for repair feedback, this becomes a breaking schema/API change and may silently drop user-facing notifications for repair docks. Please confirm these fields were intentionally migrated to the new docking traits/manager and that existing mod YAML is updated or compatibility-handled.

Changing `INotifyDock` from parameterless `Docked/Undocked` callbacks to `Docked/DockTick/Undocked(DockManager, Dock)` is a breaking interface change. Since this PR is intended to fix rearming without `Repairable`, please double-check that all existing implementers that previously relied on `INotifyRearm` or `INotifyDockResupply` have been migrated to receive per-tick resupply/rearm notifications through this new interface; otherwise docking may succeed but the actual rearm/resupply behavior will silently stop.

`INotifyDockable` now mirrors `INotifyDock` exactly, including the new `DockTick` callback. Having two interfaces with identical signatures makes it hard to understand which side of the docking relationship should implement which contract, and increases the risk of wiring the wrong notifications. If these are meant for different participants (host vs dockable), consider documenting that distinction clearly or using more specific names/method shapes.

`MovingToDock` no longer passes the owning `Actor self`, unlike the old harvester-specific callbacks. That may be fine when callers can always reach `dockable.Self`, but it is still a source-compatible break for existing implementations and can make generic notification handlers less convenient. Please verify all consumers have been updated and that no code depended on receiving the actor directly.

Switching from `Harvester` to `IDockable` broadens this hotkey from “cycle harvesters” to every dockable unit (e.g. repairable/rearmable units). That changes the user-facing behavior of a harvester-specific hotkey and may also surface the issue from #16403 in reverse by selecting unrelated dockable units. If the intent is still to cycle only harvesters, this should remain filtered to `Harvester` (or additionally filter `IDockable` instances by harvester capability).

This changes the semantics of the harvester count: the previous code filtered by `a.Actor.Owner == player`, but the new version counts every alive harvester in the world. Since this widget is rendering stats for a specific `player`, this will show the global harvester total for every row unless `IsAliveAndInWorld` already includes an owner check (which would be surprising). Please keep the player filter here, e.g. combine `a.Actor.Owner == player` with `a.Trait.IsAliveAndInWorld`.

Switching from `Refinery` to the generic `Dock` trait broadens the match set significantly. `HarvesterInsurance` appears to need a structure that can actually accept harvester deliveries, but this query will now return any owned actor with a `Dock` plus `FreeActorWithDeliveryInfo`, even if its dock type is only for repair/rearm/aircraft. That could cause the insurance logic to activate from an incompatible structure. Consider filtering by the relevant dock type/capability instead of just the presence of `Dock`.

Adding `DockManager` here addresses the new docking architecture, but `Repairable` no longer declares any dock target in this actor after `RepairActors: fix` was removed. Unless `Repairable` now defaults to the correct dock type, this can break the original repair/rearm routing for ground vehicles or make it depend on implicit defaults. Given the issue about rearming without `Repairable`, it would be safer to set the intended `DockType` explicitly here (e.g. the dock type provided by `fix`) so the unit's docking behavior remains unambiguous.

This looks like a typo in the new `Dock` trait: `Ofset` is likely meant to be `Offset` (matching the other dock definitions in this hunk, e.g. `PROC` and `FIX`). If left as-is, the helipad dock position may be ignored and aircraft docking/rearming could fail or use a default location.

Adding `TickRate: 15` changes the harvester's docking/harvest update cadence, but this hunk doesn't show any corresponding balance or behavior adjustments. Given the docking refactor and the reported regression around rearm/repair availability, this risks introducing a gameplay change for harvesters that is unrelated to the issue being fixed. If this value is only needed to preserve previous behavior under the new docking system, it would help to document that here or verify that the default tick rate already matches the old cadence.

Adding `DockManager` here addresses the new docking architecture, but this change also removes the explicit `RepairActors: repair_pad` restriction from `Repairable`. Given the reported regression that rearming/servicing ground units depends on repairability, can you confirm this unit still only docks at the intended structure(s)? Without an equivalent dock-type/config mapping, this may broaden or break valid dock targets compared to the previous behavior.

The issue here is that ground rearming now depends on the presence of a matching `Dock` definition, not just the old repair/rearm traits. This repair pad is configured with `Type: repair` only, so units that need to rearm from this structure may still fail to show the rearm cursor unless the dock type also matches their rearm docking requirements. Please verify whether this structure is intended to support rearming as well as repairing, and if so add the appropriate dock type(s) or separate dock entries so rearmable ground units can discover it.

Setting a fixed `TickRate: 20` here changes how often the harvester's docking/search logic updates, but this hunk doesn't show any corresponding balance or behavior adjustments. Given this PR is refactoring docking globally, hard-coding a slower polling interval on the harvester risks introducing delayed docking/resource return behavior that differs from other dockable units or from previous defaults. Please confirm this value is intentional for D2K specifically and not masking a regression from the refactor; if it is required, a short comment or consistency check against other harvester definitions would help.

This adds `DockManager` for the plane, but the issue being fixed is that rearming should not depend on `Repairable`. Can you confirm that `ReturnToBaseDockType: rearmPlane` is sufficient for cursor/target validation and that no `Repairable`/repair dock path is still consulted anywhere in the new docking flow? If any code still checks repairability when resolving return-to-base or dock eligibility, this config change alone won't address the regression for non-repairable units.

Adding `DockManager` here fixes the new docking flow, but `Repairable` is now left without any explicit dock target configuration. In the old setup this unit could only repair at `fix`; after the refactor that restriction may be lost unless `Repairable` defaults to the intended dock type. Please verify that ground vehicles still only consider repair docks equivalent to `fix`, otherwise this could unintentionally broaden valid docking structures.

This plane now gets `DockManager`, but the nearby removal of `WaitDistanceFromResupplyBase` suggests aircraft resupply/repair behavior changed at the same time. Please confirm the new docking manager preserves the previous standoff/waiting behavior around crowded pads, otherwise aircraft may bunch up or block each other while trying to dock.

This looks like a typo: elsewhere in this hunk the dock position key is `Offset`, but here it is written as `Ofset`. If the trait parser only recognizes `Offset`, the ship repair dock on `SPEN` will silently ignore its docking position and may break repair/rearm behavior.

Possible typo: `Ofset` is inconsistent with the `Offset` key used for other `Dock` definitions in this same file. If `Dock` expects `Offset`, this dock location for `SYRD` won't be applied, which could prevent units from docking correctly.

Removing `RearmActors` here changes how these aircraft discover valid rearm pads.
