Given the issue this PR is addressing, please confirm that `Rearmable` now derives its valid docks exclusively from the new `Dock`/`DockManager` flow and that helipads (`gahpad`, `nahpad`) are tagged with the correct dock type. Otherwise these units may lose the rearm cursor or be unable to rearm at all after this refactor.

This actor no longer declares any explicit rearm targets. If the new docking system is intended to infer them globally, it would be worth verifying this with a rules test or at least checking that both GDI and Nod helipads expose compatible `Dock` definitions; otherwise ORCAB may silently stop rearming.

Dropping `RearmActors` from all aircraft is a broad behavior change. Please make sure faction-specific or mod-specific restrictions are still preserved by the new dock typing, since this YAML no longer documents which structures `SCRIN` is allowed to use for rearming.

Since the explicit `gahpad`/`nahpad` whitelist is removed, the correctness of rearming now depends entirely on the pad-side configuration. A follow-up check on the corresponding helipad rules would help ensure APACHE still gets a rearm cursor and doesn't regress in the same way as the ground-unit issue described in the PR.

This `Repairable` block no longer specifies a docking target/type, whereas the old config explicitly limited repairs to `gadept`. Given the issue being fixed, removing the target here risks making ground-unit rearm/repair behavior depend on defaults or become broader than intended. Can we explicitly set the appropriate `DockType` (or equivalent replacement for `RepairActors`) here so these units still only dock at the intended structure and don't regress into 'cannot rearm without Repairable' or 'can dock anywhere' behavior?

Given the issue being fixed, this depot may also need to advertise the `rearm` dock type in addition to `repair`. Ground units that can rearm but are not `Repairable` were previously blocked because rearming depended on repair docking. If any TS ground units are expected to rearm at the depot (e.g. minelayer-style units), leaving this as `repair` only would preserve the bug by preventing the rearm cursor/order from appearing.

Adding `rearm` to this helipad's dock types looks correct for aircraft, but the issue being fixed is specifically that rearming was implicitly gated by `Repairable:` for ground units. Please verify that the new docking path now checks `Dock`/`RepairsUnits` instead of still depending on `Repairable`, otherwise this YAML change alone won't address the regression and may only cover air units.

The old `TiberianSunRefinery` trait also set `DiscardExcessResources: true`, but that behavior is not carried over in this replacement. If `Dock` does not implicitly preserve it, harvesters unloading at a full refinery may now behave differently and potentially regress resource handling. Please confirm this flag is migrated elsewhere or add the equivalent setting here.

Setting `DiscardExcessResources: true` changes harvester delivery semantics: any resources that cannot be deposited at the dock are now silently dropped. Since this PR is primarily about refactoring docking, this looks like a gameplay behavior change rather than a mechanical migration. Can we confirm this is required for TS harvesters under the new `Dock`/`DockManager` flow, and not masking a regression caused by removing `DeliveryBuildings`/`DeliverVoice`? If it is required, it would be good to document why here because this could otherwise cause unexpected resource loss.

Enabling `PROV_CIPHER_FLAG_PAD_IV` changes the generic IV validation to accept shorter nonces, but this also broadens the accepted range for all init paths using this provider. Please double-check that the implementation still rejects invalid lengths such as `0` (if that is not intended) and anything above 12 consistently, and consider adding/pointing to tests that cover the boundary values (`1`, `11`, `12`, `13`) so this flag change doesn't accidentally relax validation more than the issue requires.

This only ever sets `ctx->pad_iv` to 1 and never clears it. If a context is reinitialized via `ossl_cipher_generic_initkey()` with a different cipher/flag set, the previous `pad_iv` state can leak across initializations and incorrectly relax IV validation. It would be safer to assign from the flag directly (e.g. `ctx->pad_iv = (flags & PROV_CIPHER_FLAG_PAD_IV) != 0;`) so the field always reflects the current algorithm configuration.

Adding `pad_iv` as a 1-bit field changes the layout of `struct prov_cipher_ctx_st`, which is shared across provider cipher implementations. Please verify that all initialization/copy paths for this context explicitly set or preserve the new field; otherwise duplicated or partially initialized contexts could observe an indeterminate `pad_iv` value and accept/reject IV lengths inconsistently.

This test only exercises one shortened IV size (8 bytes). Since the fix changes the generic IV-length validation to allow any `ivlen <= 12`, it would be safer to add coverage for the boundary cases as well—especially 11 bytes and possibly 1 byte / 12 bytes—to catch off-by-one regressions in the new provider-side length check and padding logic.

This change removes the existing NULL guard and will dereference `cipher` unconditionally. That is unrelated to the CMAC/mac-size fix described in the PR, and it changes the public API behavior from returning 0 for `NULL` to crashing. Unless there is a strong reason and all callers have been audited, this should keep the defensive check.

This still assumes `macctx->ctx` and the result of `CMAC_CTX_get0_cipher_ctx(macctx->ctx)` are non-NULL. If `cmac_size()` can be reached on a partially constructed or failed context, `EVP_CIPHER_CTX_get0_cipher(cipherctx)` would dereference a NULL `cipherctx`. Consider guarding `macctx`, `macctx->ctx`, and `cipherctx` before calling into the EVP_CIPHER_CTX accessors so this path is robust against all uninitialized/error states, not just the 'cipher not set yet' case.

This conditional skips validation when `size_before_init == 0` but `size_after_init != 0`, which is exactly the case introduced by this fix for CMAC. As written, the test only asserts that not *both* sizes are zero, but it never verifies that `size_after_init` matches `size_val` (or any expected value) when the pre-init size is unavailable. That leaves a gap where a MAC could return an incorrect post-init size and still pass. Consider validating `size_after_init` independently whenever `size_val != 0`, regardless of `size_before_init`.

This prefix now embeds a newline, but `genb64()` also unconditionally appends a newline after any non-empty prefix. For generated cases that use `prefixes[]`, that means the actual input becomes two lines of junk (`"#foo\n"` plus an extra blank line), which changes the shape of the test data compared to the documented variants and the previous tests. If the intent is exactly two junk lines here, consider keeping prefixes/suffixes newline-free everywhere and letting `genb64()` be the single place that inserts line breaks, or document that this corner-case test intentionally bypasses that convention.

This suppresses warnings whenever all `LoaderExceptions` are `BadImageFormatException`, but the original issue shows a top-level `BadImageFormatException` can also be thrown directly (not wrapped in `ReflectionTypeLoadException`). In that case we still log a warning below, so the noisy scenario this change is trying to address may remain. Consider handling top-level `BadImageFormatException` similarly, or centralizing the suppression logic so both wrapped and unwrapped cases are treated consistently.

We're enumerating `LoaderExceptions` multiple times here (`Count(...)` and then `Count()` again). Since this is an array on `ReflectionTypeLoadException`, it would be clearer and cheaper to use `All(ex => ex is BadImageFormatException)` or cache the collection length/count once. That also makes the intent of the condition easier to read.

Changing `RejectMessage` from `private` to `public` broadens the surface area of `ClientMessageCenter` for what appears to be an internal error-handling helper. If this is only needed by other code in the same assembly, consider keeping it non-public (for example `internal`) to avoid exposing a low-level API more widely than necessary.

Releasing the body/header buffers before attempting to turn a failed response into an error response can be risky. In the `Response` branch below, the same `msg` instance is reused and re-sent via `MsgCenter.SendMessage(msg)`. If `ReleaseBodyAndHeaderBuffers()` clears transport/header state needed for re-serialization or sending, this could lead to malformed retries or another failure path. Consider moving buffer release into the terminal failure paths only (reject/drop), or ensure this method only releases the old payload and not metadata required for the retry.

Sending `Response.ExceptionResponse(deserializationException)` directly here can reintroduce the original problem if the deserialization exception itself is not serializable. Since this path is specifically handling serialization/deserialization failures, it would be safer to wrap it in a known-serializable runtime exception (or otherwise sanitize the payload) before sending the response.

Wrapping the serialization failure in `Response.ExceptionResponse(exc)` can re-enter the same serialization path with the original exception object. Since the issue here is specifically that some exceptions are not serializable, this risks another serialization failure and relies on the retry guard to eventually drop the message. Consider converting `exc` to a known-serializable exception or a plain error payload/string before assigning it to `BodyObject`, so the caller reliably receives an error instead of a second failure.

Replacing the original response payload with `Response.ExceptionResponse(exc)` can itself fail serialization when `exc` is not serializable—the exact issue this PR is addressing. Since this branch only retries once (`retryCount < 1`), a second failure will just drop the message and the caller can still time out. Consider wrapping `exc` in a known-serializable fallback exception/message here (or converting to a generic serialization-failed response) before re-sending so the error path is guaranteed to be deliverable.

There appears to be a typo in this method name: `GetFromOtheSilo` is missing the `r` in `Other`. Since this is a public grain interface used by tests/codegen, the typo will propagate into implementations and test calls, making the API harder to read and potentially causing confusion when searching for related methods like `SendToOtherSilo`.

There appears to be a typo in this method name: `GetFromOtheSilo` is missing the `r` in `Other`. Since this is part of a test grain API, the typo can easily propagate into interfaces/tests and make the intent harder to follow. Consider renaming it to `GetFromOtherSilo` for consistency with `GetGrainOnOtherSilo` and `SendToOtherSilo`.

There appears to be a typo in the invoked method name: `GetFromOtheSilo()` is likely intended to be `GetFromOtherSilo()`. If the interface was also updated with this spelling, it would be worth correcting there too for readability and discoverability; otherwise this test may not compile.

This records the wrong object for reference tracking. `placeholderReferenceId` was created for the deserialized `Stack<T>`, but here the session is updated with `array` instead of `result`. That will break object identity/reference preservation for stacks and can also leave the placeholder unresolved for the actual return value. Please record `result` here.

Using `SequenceEqual` here can miss the regression described in the issue. `Stack<T>` enumerates from top to bottom, so if both serialization and deserialization reverse the internal order, `left.SequenceEqual(right)` can still pass even though `Pop()` would return a different value than before serialization. Consider asserting stack semantics explicitly (for example by comparing the sequence of successive `Pop()` results on copies, or by constructing a known stack and verifying the first pop after round-trip matches the original top element).

These new playground projects are being added directly to the main solution. Since the issue being fixed is about `TestingHost`/`TestClusterOptions`, can we confirm that shipping these sample apps in `Orleans.sln` is intentional? Adding playground projects here increases solution load/build surface for all contributors and CI, so if they are only for demos/manual validation it may be better to keep them in a separate solution or ensure they are excluded from default build workflows.

Same concern here: adding the frontend playground project to the root solution means it will participate in normal solution operations. If this app is only intended as a demo for MAR, consider whether it should live in a dedicated sample/playground solution instead of `Orleans.sln` to avoid extra maintenance and build overhead.

When a new silo is started here, the `addresses` array is never refreshed, so all subsequent placement hints still target only the original 4 silos. That means the simulation does not actually exercise placement toward the newly joined silo and can give a misleading picture of rebalancing behavior after scale-out. Consider re-querying `IManagementGrain.GetHosts(onlyActive: true)` and rebuilding `addresses` after `host5` starts.

This guard does not match the message and appears off by one: `siloData.Count > 5` will reject 6 silos, while the exception says the frontend can't support more than 6. If the UI limit is really 6, this should likely be `> 6`; otherwise update the message to avoid confusion.

Hard-coding `http://localhost:5000` can easily conflict with other local services and makes the playground less convenient to run alongside the cluster project. Consider either omitting `applicationUrl` so the SDK assigns an available port, or choosing a port coordinated with the rest of the playground setup/documentation.

This file appears to include line-number prefixes (`1:`, `2:`, etc.) as part of the JSON content. As written, the file is not valid JSON and the ASP.NET configuration loader will fail to parse it. Please remove the embedded line numbers so the file contains only the JSON object.

This file appears to include line-number prefixes (`1:`, `2:`, etc.) as part of the JSON content. If committed as-is, `appsettings.json` will be invalid JSON and the frontend configuration will fail to load. Please remove the prefixed numbers so the file contains only the JSON object.

Assigning `worker.onmessage` inside `updateChart` re-registers the handler on every refresh. Since `fetchData` runs on an interval, this creates unnecessary churn and can also make request/response ordering harder to reason about if the worker is still processing a previous message. Consider registering the message handler once when the worker is created and only calling `worker.postMessage(data)` from `updateChart`.

Hard-coding `http://localhost:5000` makes the playground fragile outside a local dev setup and can also trigger cross-origin issues depending on how the frontend is hosted. If this page is served by the same app, prefer a relative URL (for example, `/api/stats/silos`) or make the API base configurable.

If `data` is empty or all `activations` values are 0, `totalActivations` becomes 0 and this division produces `NaN`/`Infinity`, which can break the visualization logic. Consider guarding for `totalActivations <= 0` and returning an empty matrix (or treating `numCells` as 0) in that case.

This loop can under-fill the matrix because placement is random and capped at 500 attempts per silo. As occupancy rises, collisions become common, so `cellsFilled` may never reach `numCells`, making the rendered distribution inaccurate and nondeterministic. It would be more reliable to precompute/shuffle the available 400 cell positions and assign the first `numCells` positions instead of retrying randomly.

This XML doc references `IActivationRebalancerMonitor.WorkerReportPeriod`, but `IActivationRebalancerMonitor` is described as an internal system target in the PR notes. Exposing an internal type in the public API documentation can cause doc/visibility issues and leaks an implementation detail into the contract. Consider rewording this to avoid referencing the internal interface directly (for example, describe the lag in terms of the monitor reporting interval without a `<see cref>` to the internal type).

Consider making this reporting interval configurable instead of hard-coding it on the interface. The PR description explicitly notes that report freshness can lag behind `ActivationRebalancerOptions.SessionCyclePeriod`, and with a fixed 30s value users cannot tune that tradeoff for tests or deployments with shorter cycle periods. Exposing this via options would also align better with the issue about allowing more realistic environment/statistics behavior in testing.

Consider making this callback asynchronous (for example, returning `ValueTask`) or explicitly documenting that implementations must be non-blocking. Since reports are pushed by the monitor, a synchronous listener API can let a slow or throwing subscriber delay or disrupt report fan-out and any internal reporting loop.

Using `[AlwaysInterleave]` on `GetReport()` means this call can run concurrently with `ResumeRebalancing`/`SuspendRebalancing` and any worker activity. Since the report is described as also waking up the rebalancer, this can expose partially updated state unless the implementation snapshots report data atomically. Please verify the worker state/report generation is concurrency-safe, or consider removing interleaving if strong consistency is expected from `GetReport(force: true)`.
This file is missing a trailing newline. Please add one to match the repository's usual formatting conventions and avoid unnecessary diff noise in future edits.

`DateTime` here is ambiguous unless the producer/consumer contract guarantees UTC. Since this report can cross silo/process boundaries and is primarily diagnostic, using `DateTimeOffset` or explicitly documenting/enforcing UTC would avoid timezone/kind-related inconsistencies when interpreting `TimeStamp`.

Adding a new method to `ISiloControl` changes the system-target contract and can break compatibility with existing silos/clients during rolling upgrades or mixed-version clusters unless the implementation and versioning story are handled explicitly. Please confirm that this interface is only ever used intra-version, or gate this behind the existing versioning/compatibility mechanisms so older silos do not fail when this command is invoked.

The validation here does not match the XML docs above. The docs say `SessionCyclePeriod` "must be greater than 2 x DeploymentLoadPublisherRefreshTime", but this check allows equality (`<` instead of `<=`). Either tighten the validation or update the documentation so consumers do not get conflicting guidance.

The XML docs for this new overload appear to be copied from grain APIs and are misleading in `SystemTarget`: they say "Registers a timer to send regular callbacks to this grain" and "This timer will keep the current grain from being deactivated." System targets are not deactivated like grains, so this documentation should be updated to match the system-target semantics used above to avoid confusion for callers.

The assignability check is reversed. As written, this only succeeds when `TProvider` is a base type/interface of `ILifecycleParticipant<ISiloLifecycle>`, but the intent here is to detect when the provider implements that lifecycle interface. This means lifecycle-participating providers will not be registered. Consider changing this to `typeof(ILifecycleParticipant<ISiloLifecycle>).IsAssignableFrom(typeof(TProvider))`.

Registering `GrainMigratabilityChecker` only as its concrete type can make it unavailable to consumers which request it via an interface or base abstraction. If this type is intended to participate in migration decisions outside of direct concrete-type injection, consider also registering the corresponding service contract (for example via `AddFromExisting<... , GrainMigratabilityChecker>()`) to keep DI usage consistent with the surrounding registrations.

Using `grainType.GetUniformHashCode()` as the cache key can produce collisions, which would cause different grain types to share the same migratability result incorrectly. Since this logic gates whether an activation can be migrated, a false positive/negative here could lead to invalid migration behavior. Consider keying the dictionaries by `GrainType` itself (or another collision-free identifier) instead of the hash.

The heartbeat timeout check appears to be using `WorkerReportPeriod`, but this timer only runs every `TimerPeriod` (`2 * WorkerReportPeriod`). Since the condition is evaluated on the timer tick, the monitor cannot actually react after a single missed report and will only notice on the next timer execution. This makes the effective detection delay larger than the threshold in the log message and comments. Consider comparing against `TimerPeriod` (or reducing the timer interval) so the timeout semantics match the implementation.

This warning hard-codes the assumption that `0` memory usage means `IEnvironmentStatisticsProvider` is missing, but the issue/PR explicitly adds support for using either the test provider or the real provider in `TestingHost`. In test scenarios, `0` can also come from the configured test statistics provider rather than a missing implementation, so this message may be misleading. Consider rewording it to describe the observed condition (`0` memory usage reported`) and mention the provider as only one possible cause.

This appears to compute the *harmonic mean of activation counts*, but the variable is named `meanMemoryUsage` and is later used in formulas as if it were a memory metric. Since `ComputeEntropy` multiplies by `x.MemoryUsage / meanMemoryUsage`, using an activation-count mean here changes the units and likely skews the rebalancing decisions. If the algorithm is intended to be memory-aware, this should probably be derived from `MemoryUsage` instead of `ActivationCount` (or renamed/reworked to match the actual formula).

This method is named and consumed as a memory-usage mean, but it is actually using `ActivationCount` here. That makes the result inconsistent with both the method's caller (`meanMemoryUsage`) and the entropy/ideal-distribution formulas, which use memory terms. Consider switching this to `value.MemoryUsage` (and validating non-zero memory, which you already do earlier) or otherwise renaming the method/variables to reflect that this is intentionally based on activation counts.

The interface name here does not match the one described/used elsewhere in the PR (`IFailedRebalancingSessionBackoffProvider`). As written, this will either fail to compile or register the wrong service type in DI. Please align this implementation with the actual interface name used by `AddActivationRebalancer<TProvider>()` and the registration code.

The decrement happens before the migration call, so when `count == 1` this loop breaks immediately and migrates nothing. More generally, this will migrate at most `count - 1` activations. Consider checking `remainingCount <= 0` before decrementing, or decrementing only after a successful `grainContext.Migrate(...)` call.

This replacement is conditional on an existing `IEnvironmentStatisticsProvider` registration being present. If the test host setup changes or another configurator removes that service earlier, `UseRealEnvironmentStatistics` will silently do nothing and the test cluster will still use the test provider. Since the option is intended to force the real provider, consider removing all existing registrations for `IEnvironmentStatisticsProvider` and then unconditionally adding `EnvironmentStatisticsProvider` (for example via `RemoveAll<IEnvironmentStatisticsProvider>()` + `AddSingleton<...>()`).

Only the first matching registration is removed here. If multiple `IEnvironmentStatisticsProvider` descriptors exist, the container will retain the others and resolution semantics may not consistently switch to the real provider. To make this option deterministic, remove all registrations for `IEnvironmentStatisticsProvider` before adding `EnvironmentStatisticsProvider`.

This adds the same option to the dictionary a second time under a different key. `nameof(UseRealEnvironmentStatistics)` is already written on line 119, and since `nameof(...)` also evaluates to `"UseRealEnvironmentStatistics"`, this assignment is redundant and can mask future changes if the two code paths diverge. Consider removing this extra entry and keeping a single source of truth in the initial dictionary initializer.

This loop is checking `report.Status`, but `report` still contains the initial value fetched at line 17 (`Executing`). Since it is never updated before the condition is evaluated, the loop will be skipped even if the rebalancer has not actually resumed yet. That makes the subsequent assertion rely on timing rather than the actual state transition. Consider fetching a fresh report before the loop or rewriting this as a do/while (or polling helper with timeout) so the condition is evaluated against current state.

This polling loop has the same issue as the earlier one: `report` is not refreshed before the condition is checked, so if the previous value is already `Suspended` the loop will never run and the test can pass without observing the transition caused by `SuspendRebalancing(duration)`. Please initialize `report` from `GetRebalancingReport(true)` before entering the loop, or use a polling helper that always fetches first and includes a timeout.

This loop also depends on a stale `report` value from earlier in the test. If `report.Status` is not `Executing` at this point, the loop is skipped and the test does not actually wait for the indefinite suspension to take effect. It would be safer to fetch the current report before evaluating the condition and to add a timeout to avoid hanging indefinitely if the state never changes.

This test waits exactly one `SessionCyclePeriod` between observations, but the rebalancer depends on asynchronously published environment/load statistics. In practice that can make the test timing-sensitive and flaky, especially now that real environment statistics can be used in `TestingHost`. Consider waiting until a stable condition is observed (or at least adding a buffer/poll loop tied to the publisher refresh/report period) instead of assuming one delay equals one completed rebalance cycle.

This inline data looks identical to the valid-looking case on the previous line (`..., 10, 999`) but the test expects `ValidateConfiguration` to throw for every row. If these values are actually valid, this test will fail or become misleading. Consider either removing this case, changing it to an actually invalid combination, or splitting valid/invalid scenarios into separate tests so the intent is clearer.

Setting `RequestContext` here without restoring/clearing it can leak the placement hint into later grain calls on the same async flow, which makes tests order-dependent and can skew activation placement outside this helper. Please scope this value with a `try/finally` and remove/reset the key after the loop (or set it per-call in an isolated scope).

Using the real `EnvironmentStatisticsProvider` makes this test depend on the host machine's actual memory/process metrics, which can introduce nondeterminism and flakiness in CI. Since the assertions below only verify migration/state preservation and not memory-aware balancing behavior itself, consider keeping the default test provider here or explicitly documenting/isolating why real stats are required for this scenario.

This loop waits a fixed number of session periods and then only checks directional movement, which can make the test flaky: the rebalancer has a configurable due time and may need more or fewer cycles depending on timing and environment statistics. Since this PR specifically enables using the real `EnvironmentStatisticsProvider` in tests, convergence timing can vary even more. Consider polling until a concrete success condition is met (for example, reduced variance or expected report status) with an overall timeout instead of hard-coding `3` cycles.

Applying a custom formatter here fixes `APIMod.Settings` for our own MessagePack usage, but this type is also sent over SignalR, which uses its own resolver pipeline. If `ModSettingsDictionaryFormatter` isn't registered in the resolver used by SignalR on both client and server, this attribute can produce payloads the server can't parse (matching the `streamIds` offset/desync error described in the PR). Please verify this formatter is compatible with SignalR's MessagePack contract or gate it to the persistence path only.

The top-level container is being written as an array with `value.Count`, but each entry serializes two elements (`key` and `value`). The deserializer also assumes an array and reads one key/value pair per item, which means the payload shape is non-standard and easy to misinterpret by other MessagePack consumers (like SignalR). This likely explains the protocol offset issues mentioned in the PR description. Consider serializing this as a proper map (`WriteMapHeader` / `ReadMapHeader`) instead of a flat array so the encoded structure matches a dictionary contract.

The exception parameter name here is misleading: the unsupported value is `kvp.Value`, not `bytes`. Using `nameof(bytes)` will make diagnostics harder when this fails at runtime. It would be clearer to throw with `nameof(value)` or include the actual runtime type in the message.

This should mirror the serializer's container type. If the intent is to encode a dictionary, reading an array header here bakes in a custom wire format that other MessagePack-based components won't understand. Switching both sides to `ReadMapHeader`/`WriteMapHeader` would make the payload self-describing and more compatible with SignalR's MessagePack handling.

Using `MessagePackCode.ToFormatName(bytes[offset])` and matching on string literals like `"float 32"`/`"float 64"` is brittle and allocates unnecessarily. It would be safer to branch on the raw code byte (`MessagePackCode.Float32` / `MessagePackCode.Float64`) so this logic isn't dependent on exact format-name strings.

Overriding `HandleNonPositionalInput`/`HandlePositionalInput` at the base `SongProgress` level changes input behaviour for every implementation, including any variant that may intentionally support seeking or other interaction. Since the comment explicitly mentions that some implementations allow seeking, this default could silently break those cases unless they all remember to override these properties back. Consider applying the non-handling behaviour only to the gameplay variant affected by this bug, or document/enforce the required override in interactive subclasses.

This only starts/stops the looping `sliderslide` sample when `Tracking` changes. If `ShouldPlaySamples` changes while the player is already tracking the slider (for example due to gameplay sample muting/toggling or other runtime sample-state changes), the loop won't be updated and can keep playing incorrectly until tracking changes again. Consider also reacting to `ShouldPlaySamples` changes or re-evaluating this condition during `Update()`.

Using `.loc(axis=axis)[mask.to_numpy()]` changes the selection path from positional `_take(...)` to label-based indexing semantics. That can be risky here because `dropna` builds a boolean mask aligned to the current axis positions, and `_take(mask.nonzero()[0], axis=axis)` preserves that positional behavior regardless of index type. With `.loc`, boolean indexer validation/alignment rules differ and may behave differently for duplicate labels, non-unique indexes, or depending on whether `mask` is converted to a plain ndarray versus kept as an Index/Series-like object. If the goal is just to avoid the categorical-index regression, a positional API such as `.iloc(axis=axis)[mask.to_numpy()]` would better match the previous semantics and reduce the chance of introducing new behavior changes.

Changing `ambiguous` from `"infer"` to `True` fixes the midnight-DST case here, but it also changes the general semantics for *all* ambiguous boundaries by always selecting the DST side. That may be incorrect for some time zones / frequencies and is broader than the issue being fixed. Can we limit this to the specific midnight-ambiguity path (or derive the intended fold from `first`/`last`) and add coverage showing this does not regress other ambiguous resampling cases?

Using `ambiguous=True` here hard-codes the expected labels to the DST interpretation for *all* generated midnights, which is a bit implicit and may not clearly express the bug being fixed. Since this test is specifically about choosing the first midnight on the transition day, it would be more robust/readable to construct the expected index explicitly (or at least assert the 2018-11-04 label directly) so the test documents that the ambiguous day should resolve to `2018-11-04 00:00:00-04:00` rather than relying on `tz_localize(..., ambiguous=True)` semantics.

This changes the return type in the non-split path from a single block (`self`) to a list containing a block, while the existing `convert` branch above also returns a list only when `split_and_operate` was used. `_replace_coerce` callers may rely on the previous scalar-vs-list contract here, so this can introduce subtle type inconsistencies unless all call sites normalize the result. Please verify the expected return type for this branch and, if a list is required, consider making that contract explicit across the whole method.

Using `or convert` here means we now rebuild blocks even when there were no matches in the current block, solely because dtype conversion is enabled globally. That fixes the reported regression for all-object blocks, but it also broadens the behavior for mixed managers: untouched blocks may now be passed through `_extend_blocks`, which can trigger unnecessary block splitting/consolidation and potentially alter dtypes/layout for columns that had no replacements. Can we narrow this to only append `result` when `convert` actually changed the block's dtype/values, rather than whenever `convert` is truthy?

This test only covers the single-row `DataFrame(["a"])` case. The reported regression is specifically about dtype inference when the replacement mapping contains multiple keys and only one matches, so it would be safer to also assert the resulting dtype explicitly (e.g. `result.dtypes[0]`) and/or add a case with `DataFrame(["b"])` and `DataFrame(["a", "b"])` to ensure the fix holds across the scenarios described in GH26632.

This file appears to add a standalone `test()` script with `print` statements and an `if __name__ == "__main__":` block rather than a pytest test. In pandas' test suite, tests should be expressed as `test_*` functions/assertions so they run under CI; otherwise this change won't validate the issue or prevent regressions.

Enumerating a very large hard-coded list of encodings makes this test slow, platform-dependent, and brittle because codec availability/behavior can vary by Python version and OS. It would be better to parametrize a small set of representative encodings that exercise the intended behavior, or skip unsupported codecs explicitly.

Using `print` in a test doesn't assert any expected behavior, so the test can pass even if `read_csv` is wrong. Please replace this with concrete assertions on `test_results` (or, better, assert per-encoding outcomes inside parametrized pytest cases).

The `__main__` entry point is not appropriate in a pandas test module and suggests this is being used as an ad hoc script instead of an automated test. Please remove it and integrate the coverage into pytest so it runs in the normal test suite.

The expected frame currently mixes `2.0` with integer literals, so pandas will infer the column dtypes implicitly. Since this test is meant to guard a regression in `groupby(...).sum()` on MultiIndex columns, it would be safer to make the expected dtype explicit (e.g. all floats, matching the issue report/output) to avoid the test passing or failing due to dtype inference changes rather than the aggregation behavior itself.

Using `is_list_like(ls_elem)` here looks too broad for distinguishing the two return shapes. `Block`/extension-array-backed objects can also be list-like, so this may accidentally iterate into objects that should be treated as a single block. Since the comment says the distinction is specifically `list of lists` vs `list`, it would be safer to check for the concrete container type returned by `replace_list` (e.g. `isinstance(ls_elem, list)`) or otherwise normalize `block_replaced` at the source.

This expected `Series` is missing the dtype index (`0`, `1`) that `DataFrame.dtypes` normally carries. `assert_series_equal` checks the index too, so this can make the test fail for the wrong reason or become brittle if defaults change. Consider constructing the expected value with the same index as `result`, e.g. `pd.Series([np.dtype('float64'), np.dtype('float64')], index=result.index)` or `expected = result.copy()`-style logic for the dtype assertion.

Same issue here: `result.dtypes` is indexed by the frame's column labels, but this expected `Series` uses the default `RangeIndex`. Matching the index explicitly would make the test validate dtype preservation instead of depending on implicit alignment/defaults.

Using `np.array_equal` on `Axes.get_position().get_points()` may be too strict for identifying twinned axes, because subplot positions can differ by tiny floating-point amounts across matplotlib versions/layout engines even when they are effectively the same axes location. That could cause internally shared axes to be misclassified as externally shared and reintroduce label removal in some environments. Consider using a tolerance-based comparison such as `np.allclose(...)` instead.

This creates a 2x4 grid, but the subsequent `add_subplot(231)` / `add_subplot(233)` calls use a 2x3 subplot geometry. Mixing two different subplot layouts in the same figure makes the test setup harder to reason about and can accidentally change axes positions in ways unrelated to the behavior under test. Since `_has_externally_shared_axis` distinguishes twinned axes by comparing positions, it would be safer to keep the subplot geometry consistent with the original `subplots(2, 4)` layout (or build all axes explicitly) so the test only exercises sharing semantics, not layout side effects.

Similar to the x-axis case above, this test initializes a 4x2 grid and then replaces entries with `add_subplot(321)` / `add_subplot(325)`, which are 3x2 subplot specs. Because the implementation relies on axis positions to tell external sharing from twinning, using inconsistent subplot geometries can make the test pass or fail for layout reasons rather than the intended sharing behavior. Consider using matching 4x2 subplot indices here as well.

Only `operator.mod` is disabled here, but the reverse modulo path still maps to `%`. That means `Series.__rmod__`/scalar-left modulo can still go through numexpr and preserve the inconsistent negative-mod semantics this patch is trying to avoid. For consistency, `roperator.rmod` should likely also be disabled.

Removing this comment changes the rationale around `%` dispatch, but the implementation here still routes `mod` through the generic arithmetic path. Given the issue/PR context, this likely re-enables the numexpr fastpath for modulo, which was previously disabled because it could trigger floating-point exceptions. Can we make the behavior explicit in code (e.g. by forcing `use_numexpr=False` for `operator.mod` or documenting the guard in `arith_method`) and add regression coverage for both the >10k-row case and the old FPE scenario? As written, this line alone makes it hard to tell whether the original safety concern has actually been addressed.

This branch treats any boolean `ndarray` as a positional mask without validating that its length matches the number of columns. Unlike the slice/integer cases, a mismatched mask here could silently produce `ilocs` of the wrong size and only fail later in a less obvious place. Consider checking `len(column_indexer) == len(self.obj.columns)` before converting it with `np.arange(...)`, to keep behavior aligned with other boolean indexer validation paths.

This test only covers assigning a 2D ndarray to a top-level MultiIndex column selection, but the reported regression also mentions tuple/slice-based selection on unsorted MultiIndex columns. Since the bug is specifically about behavior depending on lexsort state, it would be safer to add an assertion for `df.loc[:, ('A', slice(None))] = ...` (or otherwise verify the exact selected columns) so the fix is protected against regressions in the more explicit MultiIndex path as well.

Restricting the `_aggregate_frame` path to single-column groupbys changes the dispatch behavior for any multi-column aggregation that passes kwargs. That matches the reported bug, but it also means kwargs are now forwarded into the `GroupByApply` path below, which may alter behavior for existing UDFs that previously received a whole DataFrame via `_aggregate_frame`. Please add/verify tests covering multi-column `DataFrameGroupBy.agg` with kwargs to ensure we still pass each column/Series as intended and don't regress the single-column DataFrame case.

This test only exercises a UDF that returns a scalar via `np.mean`, but the reported bug was specifically triggered by a function like `scipy.stats.circmean` where passing kwargs changed whether `agg` received a Series vs. a DataFrame. As written, this may pass even if the original regression is still present. Consider adding a stronger assertion that the UDF is invoked on each grouped column independently (e.g. by recording the input type/shape), or using a non-vectorized function that would fail if a whole DataFrame is passed.

This regression test only covers the default `sum()` behavior, which can pass even if the transpose path still mishandles empty `DatetimeIndex` metadata in other reduction configurations. Since the reported bug is specifically about preserving the index type through the transposed reduction path, it would be safer to also assert the equivalent explicit form `df.T.sum(axis=0).index` (or otherwise make the axis equivalence explicit) so the test guards the exact code path described in the issue.

This assigns to `self.fget`, but the cdef readonly block above still declares `func, name, __doc__` and does not include `fget`. In a cdef class, undeclared attributes are not allowed, so this will either fail to compile or leave the descriptor state inconsistent. Please update the declared fields to rename `func` to `fget` as well, or keep the existing attribute name and only rename the parameter.

Using `# type: ignore[misc]` here suppresses all mypy issues caused by the decorator and effectively makes `column_looper` untyped, which can hide real signature/return-type regressions in this function. Since the PR is about improving typing around untyped decorators, it would be better to type the decorator result explicitly (e.g. via a typed wrapper/helper or a targeted cast on the decorated function) rather than silencing the entire line.

Using `# type: ignore[misc]` on the `@numba.jit` line suppresses the untyped-decorator error, but it also hides any future typing issues on this decorator usage. Since this pattern is repeated for every numba kernel, it would be better to address it centrally (for example via a typed wrapper/stub for `numba.jit` or a file-level pyright ignore as mentioned in the PR) rather than scattering per-function ignores throughout the module.

This inline ignore duplicates the same suppression used on the other numba-decorated functions in this file. Repeating `type: ignore[misc]` on each kernel makes the typing workaround harder to maintain and easier to forget when adding new functions. Consider consolidating the suppression in one place instead of annotating every decorator individually.

The added comment says the decorator makes the function untyped, but the actual fix here is just to silence mypy locally. If the goal of this PR is to improve typing quality, a local ignore on each decorator is a fairly broad escape hatch; a typed helper around `numba.jit` would preserve the function signatures and avoid normalizing this suppression pattern across the codebase.

Please consider whether this should be a pyright-only suppression rather than a mypy `type: ignore[misc]`. The PR description notes pyright-specific handling for untyped decorators; using a mypy ignore here may conflate tool-specific issues and can become stale if numba typing improves later.

This `type: ignore[union-attr]` suppresses a real typing ambiguity: `categories.dtype` can be either a NumPy `dtype` or an `ExtensionDtype`, but `construct_array_type()` only exists on the latter. Since this branch is specifically guarded by `isinstance(categories, ABCExtensionIndex)`, it would be better to narrow the type explicitly (e.g. via a cast/assert to `ExtensionDtype`) before calling `construct_array_type()` rather than silencing the error. That keeps the code self-documenting and avoids masking future regressions if the guard and the call ever drift apart.

This `type: ignore[return-value]` suppresses a real typing gap without narrowing the runtime type. Since `dta` is a datetime-like array and `view(self.dtype)` is expected to produce a `PeriodArray`, it would be safer to make that explicit with a cast (or by tightening the return type of `view` for this path) rather than silencing the error. Otherwise we risk hiding cases where `view` could actually return an `ndarray`/`ExtensionArray`, which is especially relevant for the typing issues around `IntervalIndex.left/right` mentioned in this PR.

Using `# type: ignore[assignment]` here suppresses the descriptor/property typing mismatch rather than modeling it correctly. Since `AxisProperty` is intended to expose an `Index` when accessed on instances, this broad ignore risks hiding the same root cause behind issues like `IntervalIndex.left/right` being inferred as functions. Consider giving `AxisProperty` a proper descriptor type (e.g. generic `__get__` overloads) or annotating these attributes in a way type checkers understand as properties, instead of silencing the assignment error at each use site.

Same concern as for `index`: this ignore fixes the immediate mypy error but leaves the descriptor untyped, which can continue to confuse static analyzers about attribute-vs-method access. A typed `AxisProperty` descriptor would avoid repeating ignores across classes and better address the underlying typing bug.

Using `# type: ignore[list-item]` here suppresses a real type mismatch rather than documenting the intended contract. The comment above indicates `lev` is `Union[ndarray[Any, Any], Index]`, but `levels` is expected to contain only `Index` objects. Since this code path is related to index construction, it would be safer to normalize `lev` to an `Index` explicitly (or widen the annotated type if `ndarray` is genuinely valid) instead of silencing the error. Otherwise we risk hiding a mismatch that can resurface in downstream typed uses like `IntervalIndex.left/right`.

Using `# type: ignore[arg-type]` here suppresses a real mismatch: `levels_list` is a list of `Index` objects, but `qs` is documented by the error as an `ndarray[float64]`. Since `MultiIndex.from_product` is built from index-like levels, it would be better to normalize `qs` to an `Index` before appending (for example `Index(qs)`) so the runtime structure and the type signature stay aligned. That also avoids hiding future regressions behind a broad ignore.

Using `# type: ignore[misc]` here suppresses all `misc` issues on this line, which is broader than the actual problem and can hide unrelated typing regressions. Since the PR description mentions this is specifically about untyped decorators, it would be safer to either add a targeted ignore for the decorator-related error code (if available in the configured mypy version) or isolate the suppression with a brief note explaining why `numba.jit` cannot currently be typed more precisely.

This broad `# type: ignore[misc]` will silence any future `misc` diagnostics on the decorator line, not just the untyped-decorator complaint. Consider narrowing the suppression to the specific decorator issue or documenting more explicitly why `numba.jit` must remain ignored here, so later typing problems around this wrapper are less likely to be masked.

Using `# type: ignore[misc]` on `@cache_readonly` suppresses the typing problem but also hides the root cause for all similarly decorated properties. Since this PR is about improving decorator typing, it would be better to type `cache_readonly` itself (or provide a typed overload/stub for property-like usage) so attributes like `IntervalIndex.left/right` are inferred as properties rather than functions. Otherwise this ignore may preserve the original issue instead of fixing it.

Casting the result of `cache_readonly(...)` to `bool` is misleading here: at class definition time `cache_readonly` returns a descriptor/property-like object, not a boolean value. This may silence one type checker, but it also erases the descriptor semantics and can cause other checkers to infer the attribute incorrectly (similar to the `IntervalIndex.left/right` issue). It would be safer to cast to the descriptor type returned by `cache_readonly` (or add a typed annotation/stub for `cache_readonly`) rather than to the eventual instance value type.

Using `# type: ignore[misc]` here suppresses the real typing problem rather than fixing it. In this function, `method` is assigned either `attr` (which can be a `property`) or a newly defined callable, so the variable ends up with an inconsistent inferred type. That ambiguity is likely related to the original `IntervalIndex.left/right` typing issue, where accessors can be inferred as functions. Consider splitting the branches so they return directly, or annotate separate variables for the property and callable cases, instead of reusing `method` with incompatible types.

This ignore is another symptom of `method` being typed as `property | callable` across branches. If `method` remains a union that includes `property`, type checkers may continue to lose precision for generated accessors. Refactoring to avoid reusing the same name for both a property and a function would remove the need for this suppression and make the generated API easier for static analyzers to understand.

Using `# type: ignore[union-attr]` here suppresses a real typing mismatch rather than narrowing `self.dtype` to the concrete `IntervalDtype` that `IntervalIndex` is expected to carry. Since this PR is about improving typing around `IntervalIndex`, it would be better to make that invariant explicit (for example via a typed helper/property or a cast/assert to `IntervalDtype`) instead of silencing the error. Otherwise we keep hiding the same issue that likely affects attributes like `left`/`right` as well.

Using `# type: ignore[override]` here suppresses a real signature mismatch between `Index._constructor` and this override, which can leak imprecise typing to callers. Since this property returns the `from_tuples` classmethod rather than a class object, it would be better to align the base annotation (if `_constructor` is intended to be any index-construction callable) or introduce a more precise protocol/type alias for constructor-like callables instead of silencing the override. This is especially relevant given the typing issue in the PR/issue around properties being inferred as functions.

Using `# type: ignore[override]` here suppresses a real API mismatch with `Index._can_hold_na` rather than documenting or fixing it. Since this property is inherited and accessed through `@cache_readonly`, it would be better to align the base-class annotation/signature (or make the override explicitly match the descriptor type expected by type checkers) so subclasses don't need per-site ignores. Otherwise this can mask future behavioral drift between `Index` and `NumericIndex`.

The `# type: ignore[index]` hides that `self.dtype` is typed as `Union[dtype[Any], ExtensionDtype]`, but `_engine_types` only accepts `dtype[Any]` keys. If an `ExtensionDtype` ever reaches this path, this becomes a real runtime `KeyError` risk, not just a typing issue. Consider narrowing with an `isinstance(self.dtype, np.dtype)` check or adjusting `_engine_types`/the class invariant so the type relationship is explicit instead of ignored.

This ignore is very broad for a decorated property and may hide future typing regressions on `_should_fallback_to_positional`. Since this pattern likely appears elsewhere with `@cache_readonly`, it may be better to provide a proper typed stub/annotation for `cache_readonly` (or switch to a plain `@property` if caching is unnecessary) rather than suppressing `misc` at each use site.

Using `# type: ignore[union-attr]` here suppresses a real typing mismatch rather than narrowing it. In this class, `self.dtype` is expected to be a `PeriodDtype`, so it would be better to make that explicit (e.g. via a cast/local variable or a more precise attribute annotation) instead of ignoring the access. Otherwise we keep hiding potential regressions and the original `IntervalIndex.left/right` typing issue remains unaddressed by stronger type information.

This ignore has the same problem as above: it papers over the fact that `self.dtype` is typed too broadly for `PeriodIndex`. Since `_can_partial_date_slice` relies on `resolution` being present, consider narrowing `self.dtype` to `PeriodDtype` once and reusing that, rather than adding another `union-attr` suppression.

Using `# type: ignore[override]` here suppresses a real API mismatch rather than documenting the intended property type. Since this method is decorated with `@cache_readonly`, type checkers already struggle to distinguish it from a method, which is closely related to the `IntervalIndex.left/right` issue described in the PR context. It would be safer to type the decorator (or expose `_constructor` as a properly typed property in the stubs/base class) instead of adding a local ignore, otherwise we risk preserving incorrect callable-vs-attribute inference for cached properties.

This ignore also masks an override incompatibility on a cached attribute. Given the goal of improving typing around decorated properties, suppressing `_data` here may hide the same underlying problem: `@cache_readonly` is not carrying the property return type through inheritance. Can we fix the decorator/base annotation instead of silencing the override locally? That would avoid more false function/member inferences on cached readonly attributes.

This `type: ignore[union-attr]` suppresses a real typing gap without documenting why it is safe here. `self.dtype` can be either `np.dtype` or `ExtensionDtype`, and only the latter is guaranteed to expose `na_value` statically. Since this branch is specifically handling the scalar default replacement based on the block's dtype, it would be better to narrow the type explicitly (or use a helper that abstracts `na_value` retrieval for both numpy and extension dtypes) instead of silencing the union error. Otherwise we risk masking future regressions around dtypes that don't provide the expected missing-value sentinel.

Using `# type: ignore[assignment]` here suppresses a real mismatch rather than preserving the expected type. `new_levels` is inferred/expected as a `FrozenList`, but this branch now assigns a plain `list`, which can hide downstream typing issues and weakens the benefit of enabling stricter checks. Can we construct the same container type as the other branch (or explicitly annotate/cast to the intended type) instead of ignoring the assignment?

Annotating `index` as `Index` while assigning an `AxisProperty` descriptor fixes the local mypy error via `# type: ignore`, but it also hides the descriptor-vs-instance type mismatch from other checkers and can make the class attribute look like a concrete `Index` at definition time. Since this PR is about improving typing around descriptors/properties, it would be better to model this as a property/descriptor in the type surface (or use a checker-specific ignore if unavoidable) rather than broadening the class body with an ignored incompatible assignment.

The new annotation narrows `forbidden` to `list[str] | None`, but this decorator only iterates/compares membership and doesn't appear to require a mutable list specifically. If callers pass tuples or other string collections, this type will be unnecessarily restrictive and may create new typing friction. Consider annotating this as a more general container/sequence type (for example `Collection[str] | None` or `Sequence[str] | None`) to better match the runtime behavior.

Using `# type: ignore[misc]` here suppresses all mypy `misc` errors on the decorator line, which can hide unrelated typing regressions. Since the PR description mentions this is specifically about untyped decorators, it would be better to either type `import_optional_dependency("numba")`/the `jit` attribute more precisely or add a short comment explaining the exact false positive being suppressed and why a narrower fix isn't feasible. As written, this broad ignore makes future type issues on this line harder to detect.

Adding `# type: ignore[misc]` here suppresses the untyped-decorator error, but it also hides the return type of the decorated function from type checkers. Since this PR is addressing typing around untyped decorators, it would be better to type the `numba.jit(...)` decorator (or wrap/cast the decorated function to its original callable signature) rather than introducing a broad ignore at the call site. Otherwise we risk masking real type issues in `online_ewma` and similar functions.

Enabling `disallow_untyped_decorators` globally is a good step, but the PR description explicitly notes there are still known untyped-decorator violations reported by pyright that are not addressed here. Turning this on without either fixing those decorators or documenting/tooling the remaining exceptions risks making type-checking behavior inconsistent across checkers and CI. Please make sure the outstanding sites are handled in this change (or add the necessary scoped ignores/config so the setting is actually enforceable).

Using `np.isscalar(indices)` here will catch Python and NumPy scalars, but it may still let 0-D array-likes such as `np.array(1)` through, which are effectively scalar indexers and can trigger the same downstream behavior. Consider also rejecting 0-dimensional arrays (for example by checking `is_array_like(indices)`/`np.ndim(indices) == 0`) so the new error consistently covers all scalar-like inputs.

The assertion only checks the `Index.take` path, but the reported bug also mentions `DataFrame.take` surfacing the same confusing error. Consider adding a companion test at the NDFrame level (e.g. `Series`/`DataFrame.take(1)`) to ensure the new `TypeError` and message propagate through the public API that users hit most often.

Building a dense boolean `mask` of length `len(self)` fixes the index recalculation bug, but it also changes the complexity of every scalar comparison from operating only on `sp_values` to allocating O(n) memory. For large sparse arrays this can be a significant regression. Can we instead derive a new sparse index from the compared `sp_values` (and only materialize when necessary), or at least document/guard the cases where densifying is unavoidable?

This FIXME is useful context, but it doesn't actually add coverage for the regression described in the issue/PR. Since the bug is specifically about `SparseArray` results retaining stale `sp_index` after comparisons / `isna`, it would be better to add explicit tests here that assert both the values and the resulting sparse indices (e.g. `array.sp_index.to_int_index().indices`) instead of only leaving a comment. Also, `XXX` is a bit too vague for a long-lived note; please replace it with a concrete issue number or actionable TODO.

Marking these new tests as `xfail` weakens the regression coverage for this bug fix. The PR description says the scalar-comparison case is now expected to work, but `test_array`/`test_sparse_array` are being added as permanently expected failures in the main comparison test class. If these cases are intentionally out of scope, it would be better to either omit them from this PR or add a stricter `xfail` (e.g. `strict=True` and ideally a linked GH issue) so an unexpected pass doesn't get silently ignored.

This deprecation note only mentions `Series.astype`, but the underlying behavior and issue discussion appear to involve datetime-like array casting more generally. If the deprecation also affects `Index.astype`/`DatetimeIndex.astype` or other callers that accept `np.datetime64`, the whatsnew entry should name those APIs explicitly to avoid under-documenting the scope of the change.

This branch ignores the `copy` argument and always delegates to `self._ndarray.astype(dtype)`, which may allocate even when `copy=False` was requested. Since this is a deprecation path for `astype(np.datetime64)`, it would be better to preserve the existing `copy` semantics here as well (e.g. pass `copy=copy` if supported, or otherwise document/handle the behavior explicitly) to avoid a surprising API inconsistency during the transition.

The error message here drops the target dtype, which makes it less actionable and also differs from the existing message reported in the issue (`Cannot cast DatetimeArray to dtype datetime64`). Since this branch is specifically handling unitless `np.datetime64`, it would be better to include `dtype` in the message (e.g. `... to dtype {dtype}`) for consistency and easier debugging.

The warning match here is quite brittle because it only checks the first sentence fragment. If the implementation emits a slightly different message for `np.datetime64` vs `'datetime64'`, or includes guidance about the replacement dtype, this test may pass or fail inconsistently. Consider matching a more specific stable substring (for example including `np.datetime64`/`'datetime64'` or the recommended explicit unit) so the test verifies the intended deprecation behavior more precisely.

This change makes the test accept the current NumPy nightly failure by expecting a `TypeError`, but it also bakes a known upstream regression into our test suite. That can mask a pandas bug or leave the test permanently weakened once NumPy fixes the issue. Consider marking this case with `xfail` (scoped to the affected NumPy dev versions and linked to the upstream issue) instead of changing the expected behavior, so we still document that `describe()` is supposed to work for complex dtypes and automatically notice when the upstream regression is resolved.

`Index.get_loc` is a low-level lookup API that should either return an integer/slice/mask for a found label or raise when the label is missing. Swallowing every `KeyError` here based on monotonicity changes semantics far beyond `.loc` slicing and will affect scalar lookups too. Also, `self` is already an `Index`, so `self.index` is not the right attribute here. If this bug is specifically about slice error messaging, the handling should likely live in the slice-bound path (e.g. `slice_locs`/`get_slice_bound`) rather than in `get_loc`.

Returning a `DataFrame` from `Index.get_loc` breaks this method's contract and will likely cause type errors in many callers, since `get_loc` is expected to return a location-like object, not a pandas container. If the goal is to improve the user-facing behavior for `.loc` slicing on non-monotonic indexes, this should be handled at a higher layer with a more informative exception, not by returning an empty frame here.

This new top-level `import pandas as pd` appears to be added only to construct a `DataFrame` in `get_loc`. Importing the top-level package from within pandas internals is generally avoided because it can introduce circular-import/startup issues, and it shouldn't be necessary here if `get_loc` keeps its existing return contract.

Moving plugins into `parcelDependencies` means they will now be resolved relative to the user project instead of this config package. Given the reported regression, please double-check that every plugin moved here is safe to load that way. In particular, any plugin/runtime that still does a plain `require('@babel/core')` internally can break under Yarn 2 workspaces unless `@babel/core` is declared as a peer/dependency and auto-installed into the project as well.

Passing the entire `resolvedOptions` object into `ParcelConfig` changes this constructor contract quite a bit. Given the regression described here, please double-check that `ParcelConfig` now consistently uses the project/root resolution context from `resolvedOptions` when loading plugins and their peer deps (e.g. `@babel/core`), rather than accidentally falling back to the plugin/runtime package location. If the constructor still only needs a few fields, keeping the API explicit would also make it easier to verify we aren't depending on unrelated options implicitly.

Passing the full `ParcelOptions` object into `loadPlugin` changes the serialization boundary for `ParcelConfig`: `serialize()` now includes `options`, which typically contains much more than the previously stored `packageManager`, `fs`, and `shouldAutoInstall` fields and may include non-serializable/runtime-specific values. Since `ParcelConfig` is serialized/deserialized across worker boundaries, this can introduce cloning/IPC issues or unnecessarily large payloads. Consider passing only the specific fields `loadPlugin` needs instead of the entire options object, or ensure `ParcelOptions` is explicitly safe to serialize here.

Requiring every plugin with `path.join(options.projectRoot, 'index')` changes the module resolution base even for plugins that were intentionally resolved from the config package itself. That can break plugins which declare peer deps and expect them to be resolved relative to their own package/config, while only the auto-installed case needs project-root resolution. Consider using the same `resolveFrom` that was passed to `resolve(...)` (or only switching to project root when you actually rewrote `resolveFrom` above) so the require base stays consistent.

`packageManager.resolve` is now called with `configPath` as the second argument, whereas the previous implementation resolved from `path.dirname(configPath)` and restricted extensions to `['.json']`. If `packageManager.resolve` interprets the second parameter as a basedir (as in the other new call sites above), passing the file path instead of its directory can change module resolution behavior, especially under workspace/PnP setups. It also drops the explicit `.json` extension constraint. Please verify this API expects a file path here, or switch to `path.dirname(configPath)` / preserve the extension behavior to avoid resolving the wrong package or failing to find JSON configs.

Passing the full `options` object here changes the `ParcelConfig` constructor contract from individual dependencies to a shared mutable bag. Please double-check that every `new ParcelConfig(...)` callsite was updated consistently and that `ParcelConfig` only relies on the specific fields it needs. If some paths still instantiate it with `(packageManager, inputFS, shouldAutoInstall)`, this will fail at runtime in a way that's hard to trace.

Clearing the entire `parcelConfigCache` on every require-cache invalidation looks broader than necessary and may cause avoidable config reloads across unrelated entries. Since this function is called for a specific `file`, can we invalidate only cache entries that depend on that file (or at least only the matching config path) instead of dropping the whole cache? Otherwise incremental rebuild performance may regress, especially in long-lived workers.

This assertion was weakened from checking the full diagnostic (including the suggestion) to only checking the base "Cannot find module" message. That makes the test less effective at catching regressions in the resolver's user-facing diagnostics. If the suggestion is intentionally no longer emitted, it would help to add an assertion for the new expected behavior or a comment explaining why; otherwise, consider preserving the more specific expectation.

Using a fixed `sleep(100)` to make cache invalidation tests pass is likely to introduce flakiness across environments. If the intent is to ensure the file watcher/cache key sees a different mtime, it would be more robust to explicitly bump the file timestamp (or wait until `stat.mtimeMs` changes) rather than relying on a hard-coded delay that may still be too short on some filesystems/CI machines.

Using `"*"` here makes the fixture non-deterministic and can mask regressions if a different version of `parcel-runtime-nothing` is resolved over time. Since this test is exercising config/plugin resolution behavior, it would be safer to pin the dependency to a specific version (or a tightly scoped range) so the fixture remains stable.

Using `"*"` here makes the integration test non-deterministic because it can resolve to whatever version happens to be available rather than the workspace/local package version this fixture is meant to exercise. Since this PR changes plugin resolution behavior based on declared dependencies, it would be safer to pin this to the expected test package version (or use the workspace protocol if applicable) so the test validates the intended resolution path consistently.

Removing the `realpathSync` normalization here changes the cache key from the canonical path to the original resolved path. That can cause the same module to be loaded multiple times when it is reachable through symlinks, virtualized paths, or other aliases (which is especially relevant with workspace/package-manager setups). If the goal is to preserve the caller's path for resolution semantics, consider still canonicalizing for `Module._cache` lookup/execution or document why duplicate module instances are acceptable.

Calling `realpath` on every absolute resolution can change the resolved location from the symlinked package path to the physical store path. That may break the package-boundary semantics Parcel relies on for workspace/Yarn setups, because later dependency lookups can become relative to the real store location instead of the logical package location. Since this issue is specifically about resolving packages relative to the user project/runtime, it would be safer to limit this normalization to the cases that actually need it (or preserve both logical and real paths separately) rather than rewriting all absolute results here.

Calling `realpathSync` unconditionally for every absolute resolution can change the resolved location from the package path the resolver intentionally returned to the physical target on disk. In workspace/PnP/symlinked setups, that may bypass package boundaries and alter which package is considered the owner of the module, which is exactly the area implicated by this bug. Can we restrict this normalization to cases where `preserveSymlinks` is disabled (or otherwise mirror the async resolver's behavior) so we don't accidentally resolve modules relative to the wrong package?

Filtering out `NODE_ENV` here can change Yarn's install behavior in ways that are unrelated to the nested-invocation issue. In particular, users running Parcel under `NODE_ENV=production` may rely on that when package managers decide whether to install devDependencies or peer/dev transitive requirements. Since the bug description is about Yarn-specific wrapper variables, it would be safer to only strip the known problematic Yarn/npm variables (or document why `NODE_ENV` must be removed), otherwise this may introduce hard-to-diagnose differences between direct `yarn add` and Parcel-triggered installs.

Using `packageManager.resolve(module.name, from)` here changes the resolution base from the previous `path.dirname(from)` to `from` itself. If `PackageManager.resolve` expects a file path this is fine, but if it treats the second argument as a basedir/package root, this can resolve a different package than before and reintroduce workspace/PnP edge cases. It would be safer to either pass `path.dirname(from)` explicitly or document/assert the expected semantics of `resolve` here.

Same concern as above: this now resolves peer conflicts via `packageManager.resolve(name, from)` instead of the old `resolve(..., {basedir: path.dirname(from)})`. If `from` is a file path, please make sure `PackageManager.resolve` normalizes it consistently; otherwise conflict detection may inspect the wrong installed package version in monorepos/workspaces.

This changes `build` to autoinstall by default, which is a behavioral change from the previous explicit `false`. Given the issue description, this can unexpectedly mutate a user's `package.json`/lockfile during production builds and may be surprising in non-CI environments. If this is intentional, it would be safer to gate it behind an explicit opt-in or at least ensure the CLI/help/docs clearly call out that `parcel build` now autoinstalls unless `--no-autoinstall` is passed.

Removing `resolve` from `@parcel/utils` looks risky unless every runtime import has already been migrated. The issue description shows the failing stack still goes through `@parcel/utils/lib/promisify.js` into `resolve/lib/async.js`, so dropping this dependency here could break existing consumers or leave transitive/undeclared usage under Yarn 2 PnP. Please verify that all `@parcel/utils` code paths no longer require `resolve`, or keep it until those call sites are removed.

Removing the `resolve`/`resolveSync` re-export from `@parcel/utils` is a potentially breaking API change. Given the issue here is about resolving `@babel/core` relative to the wrong location, deleting this export alone doesn't address call sites unless all consumers have already been updated to import from the new module. Please verify there are no remaining imports from `@parcel/utils` (including external/plugin consumers), or consider keeping a compatibility re-export and deprecating it first.

Removing `@parcel/core` from `peerDependencies` can break package-manager validation for consumers that load this packager outside the monorepo. This PR relies on explicit dependency metadata to decide what is resolved from Parcel vs the user project, and `@parcel/core` is typically part of the plugin API surface. If this package still imports types or runtime values from `@parcel/core`, it should remain a peer dependency (or be moved to `dependencies` if it must be bundled privately) rather than being dropped entirely.

Moving `@babel/core` to a peer dependency makes sense for sharing the user's Babel instance, but this also means the transformer can no longer rely on it being resolvable from its own package location. Given the issue here, any remaining `require('@babel/core')` calls inside the transformer need to be resolved via Parcel's package manager / project root rather than normal Node resolution, otherwise Yarn 2 workspaces will still fail at runtime. Please verify the runtime loading path is updated along with this manifest change.

This change also drops `@parcel/core` from `peerDependencies`. If the transformer still expects compatibility with a host Parcel version, removing that peer declaration may weaken package-manager validation and allow mismatched installs. Was this intentionally removed, or should `@parcel/core` remain alongside `@babel/core`?

Removing `postDeserialize` here looks risky given the bug being fixed. `preSerialize(config)` typically strips non-serializable/runtime-specific state, and `postDeserialize(config, options)` is what restores that state after the config crosses process boundaries. If the Babel config previously used `postDeserialize` to rebind resolution to the project/package-manager context, dropping this hook could leave deserialized configs without the information needed to resolve `@babel/core` correctly in worker processes, especially in Yarn 2/PnP workspaces. Please verify that the corresponding logic was moved elsewhere; otherwise this may regress config loading or module resolution after deserialization.

Switching to a static import fixes the Yarn 2/workspace resolution issue described in the bug, but it also removes the previous behavior of loading the project's own `@babel/core` when `babelOptions.internal` is false. That can break user configs/plugins that expect to run against the same Babel instance they were installed with, especially if the project has a different compatible Babel version than Parcel's bundled one. Can we preserve the old split behavior for user-provided configs while only forcing the bundled Babel for internal/generated configs or runtimes?

Setting `showIgnoredFiles: true` unconditionally changes behavior from the previous version, which only enabled this for `@babel/core >= 7.12.0`. Since this file now imports Parcel's bundled `@babel/core`, this is only safe if the bundled version is guaranteed to satisfy that minimum in all supported installs. If not, older bundled/core peer versions will throw on an unknown option. Please either keep the version guard or document/enforce the minimum Babel version here.

This switches all config loading to the transformer's own `@babel/core`, but the removed `postDeserialize` logic previously rebuilt serialized config items using the appropriate Babel instance after cache deserialization. Without that step, cached `ConfigItem`s may no longer be rehydrated correctly across processes, and plugin/preset functions may remain unresolved or tied to a different Babel instance. Can we confirm deserialization is still handled elsewhere before removing that path entirely?

Moving `elm` to a peer dependency means consumers must now have `elm` installed in their project for this transformer to work. That matches the new plugin-loading model, but it also changes install/runtime behavior compared to bundling it transitively. Please make sure the transformer actually resolves/invokes the user's `elm` package (or otherwise handles a missing peer with a clear diagnostic), because under Yarn 2/PnP a peer declared here but still required relative to the transformer package will fail in the same way described in the issue.

Using plain `require.resolve('elm/package.json')` here changes resolution from the user's project to the transformer's own package location. That seems to reintroduce the same class of bug described in the issue/PR: under Yarn 2/PnP or workspaces, `elm` may only exist in the consuming project, not as a dependency of this transformer. It also drops the previous `packageManager.resolve` result shape (`resolved`/`pkg`), so `result.pkg?.bin` and `result.resolved` no longer match the returned value. Please keep resolving via Parcel's package manager (or otherwise resolve relative to `searchPath`) so the binary is found in the user's environment.
