Moving `glslify-bundle` and `glslify-deps` from `devDependencies` into `dependencies` makes sense if they are required at runtime, but this change also means they will now be published and installed for consumers. Please double-check that the transformer actually imports these packages in production code; otherwise this works against the PR goal of reducing the default install size.

Importing `glslify-deps` statically here changes resolution from the user's project to the transformer package itself. That means custom `glslify` transforms/includes installed only in the app may no longer be found, and it also bypasses Parcel's auto-install/package-manager behavior that this PR relies on elsewhere. Can we keep loading this relative to `asset.filePath` (or otherwise ensure `glslify`'s internal resolution still uses the project context) so workspace/PnP setups continue to work?

Same concern as above: making `glslify-bundle` a direct import removes the previous `packageManager.require(..., asset.filePath)` behavior. If this package is expected to be optional/auto-installed or to participate in project-relative resolution under Yarn PnP/workspaces, this could regress those environments. Please verify that bundling still works when the dependency is only available from the consuming project.

Importing `graphql` statically here changes it from a user/project-resolved dependency to a transformer-owned dependency. Given this PR's goal of reducing default installs and the reported workspace/Yarn 2 resolution issues, this can break projects that rely on Parcel auto-installing optional plugins/peers into the user project unless `graphql` is now declared correctly for this package and loaded from the intended location. Please verify that `graphql` is listed in this transformer's dependencies/peerDependencies and that resolving it from the transformer package won't reintroduce the same class of resolution bug described in the issue.

This static import removes the previous `packageManager.require(..., { shouldAutoInstall })` behavior, so `graphql-import-macro` will no longer be auto-installed/resolved relative to the asset's project. If this package is meant to stay optional under the new install model, this line needs corresponding package metadata changes and compatibility checks for PnP/workspaces; otherwise GraphQL transforms may fail at module load time instead of being handled through Parcel's package manager.

Importing `sharp` at module load time changes the resolution behavior from `options.packageManager.require('sharp', asset.filePath, ...)` to Node's normal resolution relative to this package. That can break the new "resolve from the user project"/auto-install model described in the PR, especially in workspace or PnP setups, and it also eagerly loads a heavy optional dependency even when no image transform is requested. Consider keeping runtime resolution relative to `asset.filePath` (or otherwise using the package manager's resolver) so `sharp` is found in the consumer project rather than `@parcel/transformer-image`'s location.

Removing the `peerDependencies` block here also drops the explicit `@parcel/core` peer requirement for this transformer. Given the issue described, this package now appears to rely on host-provided dependencies being resolved from the consumer project, and removing peer declarations can make those requirements invisible to package managers (especially Yarn 2/PnP), leading to ambiguous or missing-module errors at runtime. If `@parcel/core` is still expected to be provided by the host, it should remain a peer dependency; otherwise it should be moved to `dependencies` and all call sites should be verified to avoid cross-package resolution problems.

Moving `less` from `devDependencies` into `dependencies` means it will now always be installed with the transformer package. Given the PR goal is to reduce the default Parcel install size by shifting optional toolchains out of core dependencies, this looks inconsistent with that strategy. If `less` is intended to be provided by the consuming project (similar to other compiler/runtime packages), it should likely be a `peerDependency` instead, or at least be handled consistently with the new plugin resolution/autoinstall model.

Switching to a static `import less from 'less'` changes resolution from the asset's project context to the transformer's own package. That works only if `less` is now declared as a direct dependency of `@parcel/transformer-less`; otherwise this can break under Yarn 2/PnP or in workspaces in the same way as the `@babel/core` regression described in the issue. Please verify `less` is listed in this package's dependencies (not just expected from the user project), or keep project-relative loading if users are meant to provide it.

Adding `@mdx-js/react` as a runtime dependency here may be unnecessary for the transformer itself. The MDX compiler can emit imports to `@mdx-js/react`, but that package is typically required by the user's app at runtime rather than by Parcel during transformation. Shipping it as a transformer dependency increases install size and can mask missing user dependencies instead of surfacing them. Please verify whether this should be a peer dependency (or left to auto-install in the user project) rather than a direct dependency of `@parcel/transformer-mdx`.

Importing `@mdx-js/mdx` directly from the transformer changes it from a user-resolved/auto-installable dependency into a hard dependency of this package. Given the PR's goal of reducing the default install size, this can break MDX projects unless `@parcel/transformer-mdx` now declares `@mdx-js/mdx` appropriately in its own `dependencies`/`peerDependencies` and the default config's `parcelDependencies`. Please verify that packaging metadata was updated accordingly, otherwise MDX support may fail or silently defeat the install-size reduction.

Adding `postcss` as a peer dependency makes sense for resolving it from the user's project, but this package still keeps `postcss` in `devDependencies` only. If the transformer code ever imports `postcss` at runtime without going through Parcel's plugin resolution, this will recreate the same class of issue described in the bug report under strict package managers like Yarn 2/PnP. Please verify that all runtime access to `postcss` is resolved from the project context, or consider documenting/guarding this expectation explicitly.

Importing `postcss` statically here changes resolution from the user's project/config context to the transformer package itself. Given this PR's plugin-resolution changes and the reported workspace/Yarn 2 issue, that can break projects that rely on their own `postcss` installation or version. If `postcss` is intended to be shared with user config/plugins, it should remain resolved relative to the asset/project (or be declared/handled as a peer dependency consistently) rather than bundled via a top-level import.

This static import has the same risk as `postcss`: it forces `postcss-modules` to resolve from `@parcel/transformer-postcss` instead of the user's project. Since CSS modules support is conditional on user config, this can fail under strict package managers (e.g. Yarn PnP/workspaces) unless `postcss-modules` is guaranteed to be a declared dependency of this package and compatible with the user's `postcss` instance. Please verify the dependency strategy here or keep project-relative resolution.

Moving `pug` from `devDependencies` to `dependencies` makes sense because the transformer requires it at runtime. However, this package still has no `peerDependencies` entry for `parcel`, unlike many other runtime-loaded plugins. Given the PR changes plugin resolution behavior based on declared dependencies/peers, it would be worth verifying that `parcel` should remain only in `engines.parcel` here and not also be declared as a peer, otherwise consumers/package managers may not validate compatibility consistently.

Importing `pug` directly here changes resolution from the user's project to the transformer package itself. That works only if `pug` is now declared as a dependency/peerDependency of `@parcel/transformer-pug`; otherwise this will recreate the same class of issue described in the bug report under Yarn 2/PnP (`tried to access ... but it isn't declared in its dependencies`). Please verify the package manifest was updated accordingly, and if users are expected to share their own `pug` instance/plugins, consider whether this should remain project-relative instead.

Using `require.resolve('react-refresh/babel')` here changes resolution to be relative to this transformer package at runtime. That can break under Yarn 2/PnP or workspace setups unless `react-refresh` is declared as a direct dependency of `@parcel/transformers/react-refresh-babel`. Given the issue is specifically about avoiding runtime-relative resolution bugs, please verify this package owns that dependency in its manifest, or keep using the package manager-based resolution so the plugin is resolved from the intended location.

Removing the `peerDependencies` entry for `@parcel/core` changes how package managers validate this plugin's host compatibility. Since this package is a Parcel transformer plugin, it is typically loaded by `@parcel/core` rather than importing it directly, so dropping the peer dep can hide version mismatches and may also affect auto-install behavior for external consumers. Please confirm this package is never resolved independently of the monorepo/default config, or keep `@parcel/core` as a peer dependency if host-version compatibility still matters.

Moving `sass` from `devDependencies` to `dependencies` makes the transformer always ship a concrete Sass implementation, which increases install size and removes the ability for consumers to control the Sass version used by Parcel. Given the PR goal is to reduce the default install and the new plugin-loading model prefers user-resolved/shared toolchains for things like compilers, should this be a `peerDependency` (optionally with a matching `devDependency` for local tests) instead? Otherwise projects that already depend on `sass` may end up with a second copy/version.

Importing `sass` statically here changes resolution from the project context to the transformer package itself. That can break the new on-demand/peer-dependency model described in this PR, because users may have `sass` installed in their project (or auto-installed there) while `@parcel/transformer-sass` does not bundle it. It also risks the same kind of workspace/PnP resolution issue mentioned in the bug report. If `sass` is intended to be user-provided, it should still be loaded relative to `asset.filePath`/the project; otherwise this package needs an explicit dependency on `sass`.

Moving `stylus` from `devDependencies` to `dependencies` makes sense for runtime resolution, but this package also needs to be reflected consistently in the default config/package metadata used for auto-install and plugin resolution. Otherwise we risk reintroducing the same class of issue described in the PR/bug, where Parcel resolves a transformer but its required peer/runtime package isn't available from the expected location.

Importing `stylus` and its internals statically here changes resolution from the asset/project context to the transformer package's own dependency graph. That fixes the Yarn 2 ambiguity issue, but it also means Stylus is no longer loaded from the user's project, so any project-specific Stylus version or plugin expecting the local copy may be ignored. Please confirm this package now declares `stylus` as a direct dependency/peer dependency and that using Parcel's bundled Stylus is intentional for all call sites in this transformer.

Adding `typescript` as a peer dependency makes sense so the transformer can resolve the user's compiler, but keeping the same package in `devDependencies` as well means this package will still install TypeScript in the monorepo and may mask resolution issues during local development/tests. Given the goal of this PR is to avoid bundling optional toolchains by default, please verify whether `devDependencies` is actually required here, or move it to the workspace root/test setup if it's only needed for development.

Importing `typescript` statically here changes resolution from the user's project to the transformer's own dependency graph. That fixes the runtime-relative lookup issue, but it also means Parcel will no longer use the project's installed TypeScript version when one is present, which can cause config parsing/transpile behavior to diverge from the user's toolchain. Given the PR description says `typescript` should be treated as a peer dependency and auto-installed into the project, this line looks inconsistent with that design. Can we preserve project-relative resolution (or explicitly document/version-pin this behavior) instead of always bundling the transformer's copy?

Replacing `@parcel/core` with `typescript` in `peerDependencies` looks incomplete. This package still appears to depend on Parcel's plugin API at runtime, so dropping the `@parcel/core` peer can allow incompatible core/plugin versions to be installed without any warning. If the intent is to add `typescript` as a peer, consider keeping `@parcel/core` here as well rather than replacing it.

Importing `typescript` statically here changes resolution from the user's project to the transformer's own dependency graph. That can break the intended behavior described in this PR, where peer deps should be resolved/validated relative to the project and auto-installed when needed. It also risks using a different TS version than the one implied by the user's `tsconfig`/tooling. If this transformer is supposed to use the project-local TypeScript, we should keep `options.packageManager.require(...)` (or another project-relative resolver) rather than a top-level import.

Removing the `MODULE_NOT_FOUND` retry here changes behavior for template preprocessors that depend on transitive packages not already present in the project. Previously Parcel attempted to auto-install the missing module extracted from the error and retried `render`; now this will just fail immediately. Given the PR's goal of resolving plugin deps from the user project, this looks like a regression for Vue preprocessors in the same class as the Babel/runtime issue. Can we preserve the auto-install/retry path (or otherwise ensure preprocessor dependencies are declared and resolved correctly) instead of dropping it entirely?

Adding `typescript` to both `devDependencies` and `peerDependencies` makes sense for local development plus consumer resolution, but this package now relies on the package manager to install a peer for consumers. If `@parcel/ts-utils` is used internally by other Parcel packages that don't declare `typescript`, Yarn 2/PNP can still surface missing-peer warnings or resolution failures depending on how it is loaded. Please verify that every package/runtime that imports this utility either declares `typescript` as a peer transitively or that this package is always resolved from the project root where auto-install can satisfy it.

Importing `typescript` statically here changes resolution from the user's project to Parcel's own dependency graph. That can break workspace/PnP setups and also means `loadTSConfig` may parse configs with a different TS version than the one actually installed in the app. Since this utility is meant to respect the project's TypeScript installation, it should continue resolving `typescript` via the package manager relative to `config.searchPath` (or otherwise explicitly document/enforce that Parcel's bundled TS is always used).

Adding `eslint` as a peer dependency makes sense so the validator can resolve the user's installation, but the current range is very narrow. Parcel users may already be on newer ESLint majors, and this would force unnecessary auto-installs or version conflicts. If the validator is compatible with newer ESLint versions, consider widening the peer range (and matching `devDependencies`) to avoid making Parcel harder to use in projects that already depend on ESLint.

Importing `eslint` statically here changes resolution from the user's project to Parcel's own dependency tree. That can break workspace/PnP setups and also means the validator may no longer use the project's installed ESLint/plugins/config ecosystem. Since this PR is specifically about resolving some tools relative to the user project, this line looks risky unless `eslint` is intentionally bundled as a direct dependency and all config/plugin resolution still works correctly from `asset.filePath`. Please verify whether this should remain a `packageManager.require(..., asset.filePath, ...)` call instead.

Adding `typescript` as a peer dependency makes sense so the validator can resolve the user's TS installation, but this package still only lists it in `devDependencies` for local development. If the default config/runtime can load this validator without auto-installing peers first, package managers with strict dependency isolation (e.g. Yarn 2/PnP) may still fail unless the consumer package explicitly declares `typescript`. Please verify that `@parcel/config-default` (or whichever config references this validator) also declares/auto-installs the peer, otherwise this change may reproduce the same class of resolution bug described in the issue.

Importing `typescript` statically here changes resolution from the user's project to the validator package itself. That can reintroduce the same class of issue described in this PR/bug: under Yarn 2/PnP or workspaces, `typescript` may need to be resolved as a peer dependency from the consuming project, and this also bypasses `shouldAutoInstall`. If the intent is to use the project's TypeScript instance, this should still go through `options.packageManager.require(...)` (or another project-relative resolver) rather than a top-level import.

This mutates `request.input` only when a node already exists in the graph, which means first-time requests won't carry any prior invalidation metadata. Given the PR description says these invalidations are used to compute/cache-check asset transforms, this asymmetry could lead to inconsistent behavior between cold and warm runs. Consider ensuring `invalidations` is always initialized (e.g. to `[]`) and verify that reusing graph invalidations here cannot accidentally carry stale entries for requests whose dependency set changed since the previous transform.

Clearing invalidations only when `startRequest` is called changes the previous behavior of `invalidateNode`, but it also means invalidation edges now survive while a request is marked invalid and before it is re-run. If a request never restarts in the current process, those stale `invalidated_by_*` edges can still affect later graph queries and startup invalidation logic. Please double-check that every path which replaces or removes a request also clears obsolete invalidations, or consider documenting why retaining them until restart is safe.

This only writes the cache entry under the post-transform invalidation hash. If the initial lookup at line 240 used `this.request.invalidations` and that set differs from `flatMap(assets, asset => asset.getInvalidations())`, subsequent requests may miss the cache entirely because they probe with the request-level hash but the entry was stored under the asset-level hash. Consider either ensuring both hashes are guaranteed to be identical here, or writing/reading consistently with the same invalidation source for this stage.

Passing `this.invalidations` directly into the child `UncommittedAsset` means the original and derived assets will share the same mutable `Map`. Any later `addIncludedFile`/`invalidateOnEnvChange` call on one asset will affect the other, which can leak invalidations across transformer results and make cache invalidation nondeterministic. Consider cloning here (`new Map(this.invalidations)`) so each asset instance owns its own invalidation set.

The comparator here never returns `0` for equal IDs, which makes the sort inconsistent when duplicate invalidations are present. That can lead to non-deterministic ordering and therefore unstable hashes for the same logical set of invalidations. Consider comparing the computed IDs explicitly and returning `0` when they match, e.g. by storing `getInvalidationId(a)` / `getInvalidationId(b)` first and using a full three-way comparison.

Changing `addIncludedFile` to accept only a `FilePath` narrows the public API compared to the previous `File` object. Given the PR description mentions included files now store a hash on the `File` node, this wrapper no longer exposes any way for transformer authors to provide metadata beyond the path. Please double-check that all callers and type definitions were updated consistently, and that the internal `addIncludedFile(filePath)` implementation is now responsible for creating/tracking the hashed `File` node; otherwise this could regress included-file invalidation.

This now relies entirely on `invalidations` coming back from `runTransform`, whereas the previous implementation derived invalidations directly from each returned asset (`includedFiles` and `invalidateOnEnvChange`). If any transformer/plugin path fails to populate `invalidations` consistently, file/env changes will silently stop invalidating this request. Consider either asserting that `invalidations` is always present and complete, or merging/falling back to the asset-derived invalidations here for backward compatibility and safety.

Making `invalidations` optional here can weaken the type contract for downstream code that now needs to handle both `undefined` and an empty list. Since this PR appears to make invalidation tracking a core part of asset requests, consider defaulting this to an empty array and making the field non-optional to avoid missed invalidation handling paths.

This assertion now checks `getInvalidations()` instead of `getIncludedFiles()`, which changes the behavior being covered by the test. Given the PR is about included-file invalidation, it would be good to keep explicit coverage that duplicate included files are still deduplicated in the included-files list as well, otherwise a regression in `getIncludedFiles()` could slip through while this test still passes.

Removing the `includedFiles` assertion weakens coverage for the regression this PR is addressing. Since the change is specifically about invalidating on included file updates, this test should still verify that Pug dependencies are tracked somehow (even if the exact shape changed, e.g. hashes instead of plain paths). Otherwise we could regress dependency discovery while still passing the suite.

Removing the `includedFiles` assertions weakens coverage for the Sass dependency-tracking behavior that this PR is specifically fixing. Even if the internal representation changed, these tests should still verify that imported Sass files are recorded as dependencies somehow; otherwise a regression where `@import` changes stop invalidating the root asset could slip through while the bundle output still looks correct in the initial build.

Removing the `includedFiles` assertions weakens coverage for the bug this PR is addressing. Since the change is specifically about invalidating caches when included files change, these tests should still verify that TypeScript dependencies/imported files are tracked as included files (or be replaced with an equivalent assertion against the new representation). Otherwise, a regression in included-file tracking could slip through while these tests still pass.

Removing `includedFiles` from `assertBundles` weakens test coverage for the bug this PR is addressing. The issue and PR both revolve around invalidation when included files change, and this helper previously allowed tests to assert those relationships directly. If no replacement assertion utility is being introduced elsewhere, we may lose the ability to verify that included-file tracking is preserved in bundle outputs. Consider keeping this support or adding an equivalent helper before removing it.

Changing `addIncludedFile` to accept only a `FilePath` narrows the API compared to the previous `File` object shape. Given the PR description mentions included files now store a hash on the `File` node, this type change makes that metadata impossible to pass through this interface and may conflict with downstream implementations or plugins that still need more than just the path. Please verify all call sites/implementations were updated consistently and consider whether this should remain a richer type (or a new overload) rather than reducing it to `FilePath`.

This branch ignores the `params` passed to `getTextContent` and always derives text from the XFA DOM. That can change behavior compared to the regular path, especially for options like `normalizeWhitespace` / `disableCombineTextItems` that callers may rely on. If those options are intentionally unsupported for XFA, it would be good to document that here or thread the relevant params into `XfaText.textContent(...)` to keep the API behavior consistent.

The condition is very broad: whenever `_htmlForXfa` is set, `getTextContent()` switches to the XFA path. Given the TODO comment below, this looks like it may also affect foreground-XFA cases once that lands. Consider guarding this with an explicit capability/state check instead of the transport internals alone, so text extraction doesn't silently return the wrong source for mixed/foreground XFA documents.

This only collects text nodes for leaf elements when `XfaText.shouldBuildText(name)` is true, but `#text` nodes are always pushed above. That asymmetry could make the XFA text mapping inconsistent with the text extraction logic used for search/highlighting, especially for text nested under elements that `shouldBuildText` would normally exclude. It would be safer to centralize the inclusion rule so the same predicate is applied consistently when building both the searchable text content and the DOM-node map.

Using `attributes.textContent` here can cause duplicate text entries when the same node also has text-bearing children, because the code still recurses into `node.children` below. That would make the generated `TextContent.items` diverge from the actual rendered text and can break find/highlight offsets. If `textContent` is meant to be the flattened text for this subtree, we should either return immediately after pushing it or skip walking the children for this branch.

This assertion hard-codes the exact localized UI string (`"1 of 7 matches"`), which makes the test brittle across locale changes or wording updates in the find bar. Since the test intent is to verify XFA search/highlighting, it would be more robust to assert on the selected match text and/or parse the count semantically rather than depending on the full rendered string.

This override disables XFA unconditionally in the generic build, which seems unrelated to the search/highlighting refactor described in the PR and changes existing behavior for all generic-viewer users. If this is intentional, it should be clearly justified in the PR/issue; otherwise it risks regressing XFA support outside of tests. Consider keeping the previous default here or gating this behind a more specific build/runtime condition.

Enabling the shared `TextHighlighter` here for XFA can race with the regular text-layer path on pages that also create a text layer, since the same instance is later passed into `TextLayerBuilder` and may be reconfigured there. It would be safer to either guard this so it only runs for XFA-only pages, or ensure any previous mapping/listeners are reset before calling `setTextMapping`/`enable()` again.

The start index here looks off by one. `clearedUntilDivIdx` is set to `match.end.divIdx + 1`, so on the next iteration `Math.max(clearedUntilDivIdx, match.begin.divIdx)` can skip clearing `match.begin.divIdx` when it should still be reset (e.g. adjacent/overlapping matches or when the previous clear ended exactly before the next match). This can leave stale highlight markup/classes behind. Consider using `Math.max(clearedUntilDivIdx + 1, match.begin.divIdx)` with `clearedUntilDivIdx` tracking the last cleared index, or initialize/update the variable so the semantics are consistent.

Calling `setTextMapping` before `renderTextLayer` populates `textDivs` means the highlighter receives an empty array reference at setup time. This only works if `TextHighlighter` intentionally relies on later in-place mutation of that same array; if it snapshots or iterates immediately, XFA/PDF highlighting will break silently. Consider moving this call until after the text layer has been rendered/populated, or document/enforce that `setTextMapping` must keep live references.

Using a blanket `::selection` rule on the entire XFA layer can override native selection styling for all interactive XFA content (including form fields) and may conflict with the browser/theme defaults. Since this change is specifically about find-result highlighting, consider scoping the selection styling more narrowly or reusing the existing viewer selection variables so the behavior stays consistent with the rest of the PDF viewer and remains accessible in high-contrast/user-themed environments.

The updated JSDoc says the first render resolves to an object with a `textDivs` property for `TextHighlighter`, but this branch now returns `XfaLayer.update(parameters)` on subsequent renders as well. If `XfaLayer.update` doesn't return the same shape (or returns `void`), callers will have to handle inconsistent result types depending on whether `this.div` already exists. Please make sure the return contract is explicit and consistent here, or document that only the initial render returns the highlighter data.

Capturing the `ready` event payload here only works if Electron emits `ready` before `__playwright_run()` starts. If `ready` has already fired before this listener is attached, `launchInfoEventPayload` will stay `undefined` and the synthetic `app.emit('ready', launchInfoEventPayload)` below will not preserve the original event data. Consider guarding for the already-ready case or documenting that the replayed event may not carry the original payload.

Overriding Electron's built-in `app.isReady()`/`app.whenReady()` globally is a fairly invasive change. Any app code that cached the original methods or relies on their exact semantics may now observe different behavior after `require(appPath)`. It would be safer to restore the original implementations after replaying startup, or at least preserve and delegate to them where possible.

This CSS fallback for the configured `testIdAttributeName` is using `escapeForAttributeSelector(...)`, which produces the internal selector syntax (`[attr="value"i]`) rather than a valid CSS attribute value. Since this branch is specifically meant to generate FrameLocator-compatible selectors, it should use the same CSS quoting path as the other attribute-based candidates (for example `quoteAttributeValue(...)`). Otherwise codegen can still emit a non-CSS selector string here for iframe test ids.

This assertion only checks the JavaScript output for the test-id case and doesn't verify that the generated selector avoids the internal selector form that caused the bug. Since the issue is specifically about `internal:` selectors leaking into frame locators, it would be safer to add explicit negative assertions (for all languages, or at least JS) that the output does not contain `internal:`. Otherwise this test could still pass if the generated code includes both the expected selector and an internal one elsewhere.

The wording here is a bit ambiguous: `there is no limit per project` can be read as if a project may use unlimited workers, while in practice it is still capped by the global `TestConfig.workers` value and available tests. Consider clarifying that the default is to inherit only the global worker cap, with no additional per-project cap unless `TestProject.workers` is set.

This truthiness check skips valid `workers` values like `0`. If `0` is intended to be rejected, it would be better to validate explicitly and throw; otherwise this currently treats `workers: 0` as if the project override was not provided at all. Consider checking `projectConfig.workers !== undefined` instead.

This change appears to move `workers` validation from the top-level config to project-level validation, but the feature request is to support both a global default and a per-project override. With the previous `validateConfig` block removed, `config.workers` is no longer validated here, so invalid top-level values could now slip through or fail later with a less clear error. Consider keeping the existing top-level validation and adding this project-level validation in addition to it.

This assigns `jobIndex` and breaks even when no free worker was found (`workerIndex === -1`). In that case, line 96 will access `this._workerSlots[-1]`, which is undefined and can crash whenever the queue has an eligible job but all workers are busy. We should only select/break on a job after confirming `workerIndex !== -1`, otherwise continue scanning or return early.

The type here is broader than the documented behavior. The JSDoc says this accepts a number or a percentage string like `'50%'`, but `string` would also allow arbitrary values such as `'foo'` at the type level. If runtime only supports percentages, consider narrowing this to a template literal type like `number | `${number}%`` (or reusing the same worker type used by `testConfig.workers`) so the declaration matches the actual API contract.

This assertion depends on a very specific ordering of console output across workers (`test1-end` immediately followed by `test2` for each project). Even with per-project worker limits, cross-worker log interleaving can be nondeterministic on slower or more contended environments, which makes this test prone to flakes. Consider asserting the relative ordering within each project (e.g. `test1-begin` before `test1-end` before `test2` for the same project) rather than exact global positions in `outputLines`.

This check assumes a globally stable event order between parallel workers (`test2-end`, then `test3`, then `test1-end`). In practice, stdout from concurrent workers can interleave differently across machines/CI, so this exact slice-based assertion may be flaky. It would be safer to verify the concurrency property more directly—for example, that `test1-begin` and `test2-begin` appear before `test3`, and that `test3` does not appear before one of the first two tests completes.

This error message is built from the original `selector`, but the new behavior is specifically about rejecting *nested* frame locators inside composite selectors while allowing identical top-level frame chains. Using the full selector here may be misleading when only one nested branch is invalid. Consider including the offending nested sub-selector/part (or both branches) in the message so users can tell which side of `or/and/has` caused the failure.

This change removes the previous unconditional validation that rejected any frame selector inside nested selectors, but it doesn't add a fallback error for the non-identical-frame case described in the PR. As written, a selector like `frameLocator('#a').locator('x').or(frameLocator('#b').locator('y'))` will now be parsed without throwing here, which seems to contradict the intended behavior and may defer the failure to a less clear stage. Consider preserving the old validation when `lastFrameIndex !== -1` and the frame prefix does not match `parts.slice(0, lastFrameIndex + 1)`, ideally with the improved error message mentioned in the PR.

This test only exercises the `srcdoc` iframe case. Since the bug report and the implementation are about comparing frame locator identity, it would be safer to cover a non-`srcdoc` iframe as well (for example via `server.PREFIX`) to ensure the behavior is not accidentally tied to inline frame content setup.

The negative case uses `frameLocator('#iframe')`, but this page markup does not define an element with `id="iframe"`. That means the failure could be influenced by a missing-frame selector rather than purely by the intended 'different frame locators are rejected' validation. Consider adding a real second iframe or assigning an id to the existing iframe so the test isolates the composite-locator frame mismatch behavior.

This rewrite replaces the original Firefox startup log entirely, which may hide the actual stderr/context users need for debugging if the failure was triggered by a similar message for a different reason. Consider preserving the original `error.logs` alongside the friendly explanation (for example, append/prepend the boxed message instead of overwriting it) so we improve the UX without losing diagnostic detail.

Appending the shared SVG defs directly to `this.highlight` fixes the iframe case, but `install()` can be called more than once because of the recreation logic above. Since this line unconditionally appends a new defs container each time, repeated installs may accumulate duplicate `clipPath` IDs in the same document and leave stale nodes behind. Consider guarding this so the SVG defs are created once per document/highlight root, or remove any existing instance before appending.

This assertion is quite implementation-specific: it hard-codes the exact `clipPath` serialization (`url("#icon-check")`), which can vary across browsers/engines or change if the icon plumbing is refactored while still keeping the dialog correct. Since the bug is about missing icons in the iframe dialog, it would be more robust to assert the presence of both required defs in the iframe and/or that the accept/close controls are rendered/visible, rather than depending on a precise computed-style string for one icon only.

`boundingBox()` can return `null` when the target is not visible or not attached yet. This helper previously had the same risk, but broadening it to accept arbitrary `Locator`s makes that easier to hit in tests. Consider asserting the box is non-null (or waiting for visibility first) so failures are clearer and we don't throw a less-informative `Cannot read properties of null` on the next line.

This wording may be misleading given the feature request explicitly discusses step timeouts being either independent from or constrained by the enclosing test timeout. Saying `Defaults to 0 (no timeout)` sounds like a step can run indefinitely, but in practice it will still be bounded by the test timeout unless the new behavior is documented otherwise. Consider clarifying the interaction with the test timeout here so users do not assume `timeout: 0` disables all time limits for a step.

Using `Promise.race([body(), timeoutPromise])` enforces the step timeout, but it does not cancel `body()`. If the timeout wins, the step is marked failed and the test continues unwinding while the original async work may still be running in the background, potentially mutating shared state or producing late errors. Is that behavior intentional? If not, this likely needs integration with the existing timeout/cancellation machinery used by tests/fixtures rather than a standalone race.

The timeout callback creates a custom `StepTimeoutError`, but this class is local and does not appear to carry any step/test context or use the framework's standard timeout error formatting. That may lead to inconsistent reporting compared to other Playwright timeouts. Consider reusing the existing timeout error type/path so reporters and error presentation stay consistent.

Adding `timeout?: number` to the public type is fine, but this change also updates the API surface and should be reflected in the generated docs/JSDoc for `test.step`. Right now the surrounding comment only says `@param options`, so users won't discover what `timeout` means, its units, or how it interacts with the test timeout. Please document the new option here (and ensure any API docs generation picks it up) to keep the declaration self-describing and consistent with the rest of the typed API.

This test includes a `console.log(result.output)` in the committed spec. That will add noise to the test output and is usually only useful while debugging failures locally. Please remove it unless the suite intentionally relies on printing runner output here.

Asserting on the full human-readable error string is brittle because timeout wording often changes across reporters or future refactors. Consider asserting on a more stable substring/pattern (for example that the output mentions the step timeout and `100ms`) or, if available, on structured failure data instead of exact text.

The new `timeout` option is exposed in the generated typings, but this signature still uses an inline object type. Since `test.step` options are part of the public API now, it would be better to reference a named exported type/interface (for example `TestStepOptions`) and document the timeout semantics there. That avoids duplicating the shape across generated/runtime typings and makes future additions less error-prone.

Wrapping `waitForTimeout` in `_wrapApiCall` will add the generic API-call logging, but unlike `waitForRequest`/`waitForResponse` there is no useful context included here. Since the issue is specifically about improving debuggability, consider passing a more descriptive API name such as `waitForTimeout(${timeout})` (or otherwise including the timeout value in the log) so the trace/debug output shows how long we are waiting.

When `urlOrPredicate` is a `RegExp`, `trimUrl()` returns `undefined`, so `waitForRequest(/.../)` still won't emit any useful debug log even though the issue asks for logging the URL *or the predicate*. Consider handling `RegExp` here as well (for example via `String(param)` with truncation) so regex-based waits are also visible in debug output.

Same gap here for `RegExp` predicates: `trimUrl()` only formats strings, so `waitForResponse(/.../)` won't produce a log line. Since the feature request explicitly mentions logging the URL or predicate, it would be better to include a representation for regex inputs too.

This changes the behavior of `Waiter.log()` globally, so every internal waiter message will now be emitted to the `api` debug channel. That may be broader than the issue requires (`waitForRequest`/`waitForResponse`) and could introduce noisy or duplicate logs for other wait-based APIs that already have surrounding `wrapApiCall` logging. Consider scoping this to the specific public APIs that were missing logs, or at least verify that this doesn't produce redundant output across all waiter usages.

This assertion hard-codes the exact truncation format (`…`) and cutoff length of the logged URL, which makes the test brittle if the log formatter changes slightly. Since the feature request is about surfacing the URL in debug output, it would be safer to assert on a stable substring (for example, that the message contains `waiting for request` and a recognizable prefix of the URL) rather than the precise elided representation.

This assertion only verifies the timeout error message, not the actual debug logging behavior described in the issue/PR. Since the feature is about adding `wrapApiCall`-based logs, this test could pass even if no debug log is emitted. Consider capturing the debug/protocol log output (or using the existing test helper for API logs, if available) and asserting that the wait call is logged there, rather than only checking the thrown error text.

This adds the `Video.delete` heading, but unlike other API entries it does not document the return type or include any language-specific usage examples. Since this PR introduces a new public API, the docs should be consistent with the rest of the class reference (for example, `- returns: <[void]>` and sample snippets), otherwise the generated API docs may be incomplete or inconsistent across languages.

When saving a remote artifact via piping, errors from the source readable are not handled. If `stream.stream()` emits an `error` before or during piping, this promise may never reject cleanly and can surface as an unhandled stream error. Consider using `stream.pipeline(...)` (or attaching an `error` listener to the source stream as well) so both read and write side failures are propagated.

This subscribes to the `'video'` channel event but intentionally ignores the payload. Adding a no-op listener can be misleading and may also hide the fact that the client is not yet surfacing context-level video artifacts anywhere. If the event is required to keep the protocol in sync, consider documenting that explicitly or storing/emitting the artifact for future consumers; otherwise it may be better to avoid registering the handler until there is actual behavior attached.

This no longer propagates the remote-browser flag to the video artifact, while the download path above does. Since the PR description says `video.path()` should throw for remotely connected browsers, the `Artifact` created here likely also needs `_isRemote` set from the browser context before calling `_artifactReady(...)`; otherwise `video.path()` may incorrectly behave as if the artifact were local.

Using `Promise.race` here can make `this._artifact` resolve to `null` as soon as the page closes/crashes, even if the video artifact becomes available shortly after page shutdown. Since `saveAs()`/`delete()` are documented as safe after the page is already closed, this introduces a race where callers may get `Page did not produce any video frames` for a valid video. Consider waiting for the artifact to be reported after close, or only resolving to `null` when we know recording was never created.

Exposing `absolutePath: artifact.localPath()` in the initializer looks inconsistent with the PR description that says `video.path()` should throw for remotely connected browsers. Sending the local path eagerly during dispatcher construction may leak a server-side path to remote clients before any guard in `pathAfterFinished()` can run. Consider omitting this field for remote connections, or making it conditional/undefined unless the path is actually allowed to be exposed.

Iterating over `context._browser._idToVideo.values()` here can emit `video` events for artifacts that have already been reported via `BrowserContext.Events.VideoStarted` if the dispatcher is created after some videos already exist and the event is also replayed/observed elsewhere. It would be safer to deduplicate by artifact/video id before dispatching, otherwise clients may see duplicate `video` events for the same recording.

Using `existingDispatcher<ArtifactDispatcher>(artifact)` here assumes the `ArtifactDispatcher` has already been created elsewhere. Unlike the download path above, this code does not create one if it does not exist, so the `video` event can fail or emit an undefined dispatcher depending on event ordering. Please ensure the artifact dispatcher is instantiated before this event is dispatched, or fall back to creating it here.

This immediate dispatch has the same risk as the event handler above: `existingDispatcher(page._video)` requires that a dispatcher for `page._video` already exists. Since the previous implementation explicitly constructed a `VideoDispatcher` in the initializer, this change may break pages that already have a video artifact at dispatcher creation time. Consider creating/registering an `ArtifactDispatcher` here if one is not guaranteed to exist yet.

Adding a new `'video'` event to `BrowserContextChannel` changes the generated protocol surface, but this hunk only updates the `on(...)` overloads and event type. Please make sure the corresponding event is also declared in the browser-context event mapping / validator schema used by the protocol generator and dispatch layer; otherwise TypeScript will compile while runtime event serialization or validation can still miss this event.

Adding a new `BrowserContext.video` event while also keeping `Page.video` introduces two protocol entry points for the same artifact. Since `Page.video` already exists below and the old `Page.video` property was removed, it would help to clarify whether both events are expected to fire, and if so, how clients should correlate the context-level event back to a specific page. Otherwise this risks protocol/client ambiguity and duplicate handling.

The error message here is download-specific, but `Artifact` is now a shared abstraction for both downloads and videos. If this path is hit for a video artifact, users will get a misleading message pointing them to `download.failure()`. Consider making this message artifact-agnostic (or parameterizing it per artifact type) so the new video API reports the correct guidance.

Emitting `BrowserContext.Events.VideoStarted` immediately with the raw `Artifact` changes the event contract from the previous `Video` object and may fire before the `pageOrError` promise resolves. If any existing listeners expect a page-associated video object or rely on the page being available when this event is emitted, this can break them. Please verify all consumers of `VideoStarted` are updated accordingly, or consider preserving the previous semantics/wrapping type here.

This `gotFirstFrame.then(...)` callback is fire-and-forget, so if `pageOrError()` rejects or `_videoStarted(...)` throws, the error will become an unhandled promise rejection. The previous code passed the promise through without creating a detached async chain here. Consider explicitly handling failures, e.g. `void gotFirstFrame.then(...).catch(() => {})` or routing the error to the existing instrumentation/logging path.

This changes the accessibility check from a boolean cast (`!!acceptDownloads`) to a negation check. If `acceptDownloads` can be the string mode used elsewhere in Playwright (for example `'accept'` / `'deny'`), `!page._browserContext._options.acceptDownloads` will treat `'deny'` as truthy and incorrectly allow access to the artifact. Please make this check explicit against the accepted value(s) so downloads remain inaccessible when the context is configured to deny them.

This changes the close path for pages that never reached initialization by forcing `_markAsError(new Error('Page has been closed'))`. Can we confirm this does not overwrite a more specific initialization failure that may already be pending (for example, a navigation/crash/protocol error)? If `_markAsError` is not idempotent or preserves the first error, this could mask the real root cause and make debugging harder. Consider guarding on the current error state as well, or documenting that close should win here.

Changing `reportAsNew` from `async` to synchronous alters its contract. If any callers still `await` it, that is harmless, but if subclasses/overrides or call sites relied on it returning a promise that resolves after all initialization side effects complete, this can introduce ordering regressions. Please verify all invocations and any overridden implementations to ensure there is no async work left that callers implicitly depended on.

The event name is being renamed from `VideoStarted` to `Video`, but this hunk also removes the only visible emitter (`videoStarted(...)`). Unless emission has been moved elsewhere in the same change, listeners on `Page.Events.Video` will never fire. Please make sure the new event is still emitted when the artifact becomes available, and that all consumers were updated consistently.

This assertion pattern can produce a false positive if `page.video().path()` unexpectedly resolves instead of rejecting, because `catch` will never run and `error` would be the resolved value. Then `error.message` may fail for the wrong reason. Consider using `await expect(page.video().path()).rejects.toThrow(...)` so the test explicitly verifies rejection and the expected message.

This assertion now checks a more generic error message (`Use saveAs()`) instead of the download-specific guidance (`Use download.saveAs()`). Since this test is for `Download`, loosening the message here may hide regressions where the API no longer points users to the correct object/method. If the implementation intentionally standardized the message across artifacts, consider adding a separate assertion that still verifies the error is clearly associated with the download API, or keep the more specific wording for downloads.

This fixed `waitForTimeout(1000)` makes the test rely on timing rather than on an observable condition, which can introduce unnecessary slowness and still be flaky on slower CI machines. If the goal is to ensure at least one video frame is produced before closing the context, consider waiting on a deterministic signal or polling for the artifact/video file to appear instead of sleeping for a full second.

The new `delete()` API is added here, but the `Video` interface docs should also clarify its interaction with the other newly introduced methods and remote connections. In particular, `path()` now throws remotely while the PR description says `delete()` is safe remotely, so consumers may need explicit guidance that `delete()` does not require a local filesystem path and can be called before or after page/context closure. Without that, the type surface is a bit underspecified compared to the behavior change in this PR.

The heading says `expect(value).toHaveScreenshot(...)`, but the examples and feature semantics only support `Page` and `Locator` subjects (`expect(page)` / `expect(page.locator(...))`). Using `value` here is misleading in the API docs and suggests broader matcher applicability than is actually implemented. Consider renaming this section to something like `expect(page).toHaveScreenshot(...)` (or explicitly documenting both `page` and `locator`).

The description for `omitBackground` appears to be a copy/paste error: it currently describes the overall assertion behavior rather than what this option does. This should explain that the screenshot omits the default white background and allows transparent backgrounds, matching the underlying screenshot option semantics.

This note now implies that array path segments are supported by `toHaveScreenshot`, but the PR description mentions file extension handling as an open compatibility concern. It would be good to clarify here whether array-based names without an explicit extension are supported the same way as string names, especially since the examples above use `.png` explicitly. Otherwise readers may assume parity with `toMatchSnapshot` that the implementation does not fully provide yet.

This implementation takes a single screenshot and immediately delegates to `toMatchSnapshot`, but the feature/issue explicitly requires `toHaveScreenshot` to be web-first and auto-retry until the golden is reached. As written, transient rendering differences won't be retried, so the matcher won't behave like other async Playwright assertions. Consider integrating the screenshot comparison into a polling/retry loop instead of capturing once here.

Requiring a `name` here seems to conflict with the intended API shown in the issue (`await expect(page).toHaveScreenshot()` should be valid and use the auto-generated snapshot name). This guard will reject the no-argument form, so it would be better to allow `undefined` and let the snapshot naming logic handle the default case.

This import should be a type-only import to avoid generating a runtime dependency from a declaration file. Since `PageScreenshotOptions` is only used in type positions below, prefer `import type { PageScreenshotOptions } from 'playwright-core';` for consistency and to prevent unnecessary emitted imports in downstream tooling.

This broadens the `get_int` path to include Python objects that can be extracted as `u64`, which fixes the reported `UInt64` case, but it also means values in `(i64::MAX, u64::MAX]` will now be routed through whatever `get_int` currently does. Please verify that `get_int` actually preserves those as unsigned values instead of still coercing via `i64` or another lossy intermediate; otherwise this condition change alone may just move the bug rather than fully fix it. A regression test with a value > `i64::MAX` and an explicit `UInt64` schema would help confirm the behavior.

This test doesn't cover the regression described in the issue: the bug occurs when `from_records` is given a Python object like `np.uint64(...)` together with an explicit schema, but here we're only passing a plain Python `int` without `schema`. That can pass even if the original bug remains unfixed. Please update the test to use `np.uint64("9908227375760408577")` and `schema={"a": pl.UInt64}` so it exercises the reported failure mode directly.

This changes `Time -> Datetime` from a cast to a hard error, but the issue/PR description only calls out forbidding `Date <-> Time`. If `Time -> Datetime` was previously supported, this is an additional breaking change that should either be justified in the PR/tests or avoided here. At minimum, please confirm this is intentional and covered by the public API/docs, since users may rely on the old behavior.

This change removes the old behavior test, but the PR changes semantics to raising on `Date -> Time`/`Time -> Date` casts. Without adding a replacement test here (or nearby) that asserts an exception is raised, we lose coverage for the regression this PR is fixing and could accidentally reintroduce the silent cast behavior later.

Changing this flag to `false` matches the reported group-by behavior, but this boolean is not self-describing and likely controls whether the expression is treated as elementwise/scalar-like in aggregation contexts. Since the bug was caused by rolling expressions being classified incorrectly, it would be safer to add a targeted regression test for `group_by(...).agg(col(...).rolling_* (..., by=...))` and, if possible, replace this positional boolean with a named option or comment to prevent similar misclassification in other `rolling_*` variants.

This test name is a bit misleading now that the fix is intended to ensure rolling expressions are treated as list-producing within `group_by().agg(...)`, not scalar-returning. Renaming it to something like `test_rolling_group_by_agg_preserves_list_output_15656` would make the expected behavior clearer and reduce confusion for future readers.

Caching the conversion function only by `type_object_ptr` is now unsafe because the selected function depends on `allow_object`. If a type is first seen with `allow_object=true`, `get_object` can be stored in `LUT`, and a later call for the same type with `allow_object=false` will still reuse that cached entry and silently accept an object that should error. Consider including `allow_object` in the cache key, or only caching decisions that are independent of this flag.

Passing a hard-coded `true` here changes DataFrame row construction to always allow object fallback for dict values. That fixes subclassed temporals, but it also broadens behavior for every unsupported Python value encountered in `dicts_to_rows`, potentially masking type errors that `strict` previously surfaced. If the intent is only to support temporal subclasses, consider threading an explicit `allow_object` policy from the caller or handling the temporal-subclass case inside `py_object_to_any_value` without enabling object fallback unconditionally for all values.

This now passes `allow_object` through to `py_object_to_any_value`, but the conversion is still hardcoded with `true` for the other flag. If that flag controls strict temporal handling/subclass detection, this is fine; otherwise it would be worth documenting what the `true` means here or replacing it with a named constant/helper to avoid making this callsite harder to reason about after the refactor.

The new `map_err(|_| ...)` discards the original conversion error entirely. That makes debugging harder and also changes behavior from the previous branch, which included the offending value's `repr()` in the message. Consider preserving the original error as context (or at least including `value.repr()` when available) so users can still tell *why* conversion failed for non-object cases.

This changes `new_from_any_values` to always call `py_object_to_any_value(..., true)`, which means object fallback is now enabled unconditionally for this constructor. That fixes subclassed temporals, but it also broadens behavior for every unsupported Python value and may silently produce `Object`-typed series where we previously raised. If that behavior change is intentional, it would be good to document it explicitly and add coverage for non-temporal unsupported objects; otherwise consider preserving the previous failure semantics and only relaxing conversion for the temporal-subclass cases.

This test no longer exercises the `allow_object=True` path even though the function name still says `_w_allow_object`. Given the PR description, the bug fix is specifically about subclassed temporal values being accepted without falling back to object handling, so the current assertion is useful, but the name is now misleading and we also lose coverage of the explicit `allow_object=True` case that the previous test had. Consider either renaming the test to reflect the new behavior or adding a second assertion that verifies `pl.lit(MyClass(*input), allow_object=True)` still produces the same temporal literal.

This assertion only checks the output schema, so it can pass even if the optimizer still produces an incorrect plan that drops columns in other cases. Since the reported bug is about optimized execution missing columns compared to `no_optimization=True`, it would be stronger to assert the actual collected frame (or compare optimized vs unoptimized results) and include at least one matching join key so the join path is exercised with non-empty output.

Using `string.IsNullOrEmpty(e.Data)` changes behavior for empty-string payloads as well as `null`. `DataReceivedEventArgs.Data == null` is the documented end-of-stream signal from `Process`, but an empty string may still be a real line from the child process. If the goal is only to prevent the NRE reported in this issue, consider checking `e.Data == null` here so we don't silently drop valid empty output/messages.

This bypasses the `DB.Stats(...)` API and reaches into `Head()` directly. That changes the behavior of `readyStorage.Stats` from "DB stats" to "head-only stats", which can silently drop any logic/aggregation the DB-level method provides now or in the future. If the intent is to preserve the previous admin stats semantics, it would be safer to delegate to `x.Stats(statsByLabelName)` instead of `x.Head().Stats(...)`.

Exposing the underlying `*tsdb.DB` here couples `promql.Test` to the concrete implementation details of `teststorage.TestStorage`. That makes this helper more brittle if `TestStorage` changes its internals, and it also weakens the abstraction that `Storage()` already provides. If this is only needed for a specific test capability, consider exposing that capability directly from `teststorage.TestStorage` (or via a narrower helper/interface) instead of reaching into the `DB` field.

Changing `New` to return `*TestStorage` instead of the `storage.Storage` interface narrows the API and can break existing tests/helpers that only depend on the storage interface. Unless callers need access to `*tsdb.DB`-specific methods, it would be safer to keep the return type as the interface and return `&TestStorage{...}` as that interface.

This now treats every `Stats()` failure as `errorInternal`, but the rest of this change relies on `tsdb.ErrNotReady` being surfaced as `errorUnavailable` via `setUnavailStatusOnTSDBNotReady`. That only works if the returned error is wrapped in `apiError.err` and still matches `errors.Cause(...) == tsdb.ErrNotReady`. Please verify that `Stats()` returns `tsdb.ErrNotReady` in the not-ready case; otherwise `/status/tsdb` will regress from 503 to 500 during startup.

Using `t.Run("")` makes all subtests unnamed, which hurts test output readability and makes failures harder to identify. The previous indexed name at least distinguished cases; consider keeping `fmt.Sprintf("%d", i)` or adding a descriptive name field to the table.

The new `dbDir` field is accepted by `NewAdmin`, but it is not initialized in the returned struct. As a result, `TSDBSnapshot` will build the snapshot path from an empty base directory and write to `snapshots/...` relative to the current working directory instead of the TSDB directory. Please set `dbDir: dbDir` here.

Comparing `errors.Cause(err)` directly to `tsdb.ErrNotReady` is brittle and ties this handler to `github.com/pkg/errors` unwrapping semantics. If the storage layer starts wrapping with standard `%w` errors or returns a different wrapper chain, this check may stop working and federate will incorrectly return 500 instead of 503. Prefer using an error classification helper that supports unwrapping consistently (e.g. `errors.Is` with wrapped sentinels, or a storage-specific helper) so the HTTP status remains stable across wrapping changes.

Minor typo in the test comment: `Request are expected...` should be `Requests are expected...`. Since this is a regression test documenting the intended validation order, it’s worth keeping the wording precise.

This new `LocalStorage` interface embeds `api_v1.TSDBAdminStats`, but the `status()` handler below also relies on `tsdb.ErrNotReady` semantics from the implementation. Since `LocalStorage` is now the contract used by the web layer, it would be safer to document that `Stats()` may return `tsdb.ErrNotReady` (or expose that behavior through the interface) so alternative implementations don't accidentally break the HTTP status handling.

Switching from direct `db.Head()` access to `localStorage.Stats()` changes the failure mode of `/status`: this endpoint can now return 503/500 where it previously always rendered as long as `h.tsdb()` was non-nil. Please verify this is intentional and covered by tests, especially during startup/replay when `Stats()` may return `tsdb.ErrNotReady`.

This TODO points out a real resource leak in the test: multiple HTTP responses are created below, but their bodies are not consistently closed. In a parallel test this can keep connections/goroutines alive and make the test flaky or inflate memory usage. Rather than leaving this as a TODO, it would be better to close every `resp.Body` immediately after each request (or use a helper that reads/drains and closes it) so the test fully releases resources.

This new pre-filter only short-circuits on `:job`, but `assert_enqueued_with` can also reject candidates by other non-deserializing fields like `:queue` (and potentially `:at` depending on how it's stored). As written, we'll still eagerly deserialize jobs that are guaranteed not to match on those fields, so a bad earlier job on a different queue can still raise `ActiveJob::DeserializationError` before we reach the real match. Consider applying the same raw-hash filtering for every attribute that can be checked without deserializing, so deserialization is only attempted for plausible matches.

The test name says `deserializable_args`, but the scenario and comment are specifically about arguments that fail deserialization. Renaming this to something like `test_assert_enqueued_with_with_earlier_undeserializable_args` would make the intent clearer and better match the regression being covered.

This assertion only verifies the happy path for a single aliased integer expression. Since the regression report is about `column_types` being unexpectedly empty, it would be safer to also assert that the hash actually contains the expected key (or has the expected size) so the failure mode is clearer, and ideally add coverage for more than one selected column/expression to ensure we don't regress on partial population again.

This expectation is now adapter-specific, but the underlying issue here is not really PostgreSQL in general — it's duplicate/unaliased result column names. Gating on `current_adapter?(:PostgreSQLAdapter)` makes the test encode a broad adapter behavior that may not hold for all PG queries or future adapter changes. Consider asserting this behavior only when the returned result metadata actually contains duplicate column names, or move this into a PostgreSQL-specific test with a clearer name so the generic calculations test suite doesn't imply all unaliased function plucks on Postgres must return strings.

This changelog entry describes the fallback as `ActiveSupport::LogSubscriber.logger`, but the PR summary says subclasses now default to their superclass's logger. That distinction matters for deeper inheritance chains (e.g. `ChildSubscriber < ParentSubscriber < ActiveSupport::LogSubscriber`), where the effective default would be `ParentSubscriber.logger`, not necessarily the base class logger directly. Consider rewording this to say it inherits from the superclass logger, ultimately falling back to `ActiveSupport::LogSubscriber.logger`/`Rails.logger`.

Using `return @logger if @logger` changes the previous memoization semantics for falsey values. If a caller explicitly sets `self.logger = nil` on a subclass to disable logging or clear an inherited logger, this method will ignore that assignment and fall back to `superclass.logger` (or `Rails.logger` on the base class). Consider checking whether the ivar is defined instead, e.g. `return @logger if instance_variable_defined?(:@logger)`, so an explicit `nil` override is preserved.

This cleanup won't run if the assertion above fails, which can leak `MyLogSubscriber.logger` into later tests and make the suite order-dependent. Wrapping the assignment/reset in an `ensure` block would make the test more robust.

The new changelog entry appears to include line-number prefixes and excessive leading whitespace (`1:`, `2:`, etc.), which doesn't match the existing markdown format and will render incorrectly. Please remove those prefixes so the entry follows the standard changelog style.

Using `||=` here will also overwrite an explicitly provided `precision: false` with `nil`, because `false` is treated as unset. Other compatibility shims in this area usually only apply defaults when the key is absent. Consider checking `options.key?(:precision)` instead, e.g. `options[:precision] = nil unless options.key?(:precision)`, so caller-supplied values are preserved exactly.

This extracted helper still returns the same generic message, so it doesn't address the Postgres-specific confusion described in the issue. Since this method is defined in the abstract adapter, consider either overriding it in the PostgreSQL adapter to explain the reduced limit (e.g. reserving characters for the `_pkey` suffix) or making the message here extensible so adapters can append adapter-specific context.

This message says PostgreSQL adds the `_pkey` suffix "when creating default indexes", but `_pkey` is specifically the suffix used for the automatically generated primary key constraint/index name. Calling them "default indexes" is a bit misleading and may confuse readers about when this limit applies. Consider wording this in terms of the automatically generated primary key name/constraint instead.

This assertion hard-codes the full PostgreSQL error message, which makes the test brittle if the wording changes slightly while preserving the intended behavior. Since the issue here is to clarify *why* the effective limit is lower, consider asserting key substrings instead (for example, the max identifier length, `_pkey`, and the effective limit) so the test still validates the important semantics without coupling tightly to exact prose.

This assertion hard-codes the full PostgreSQL error message, including explanatory prose, which makes the test brittle to small wording changes while not adding much behavioral coverage beyond the adapter-specific branch. Consider asserting the stable parts instead (e.g. that the message includes the table name, `max_identifier_length`, `name_limit`, and `_pkey`) so the test still verifies the intent without coupling so tightly to exact phrasing.

The new changelog entry appears to be misindented compared to surrounding entries (`1:*` / `2:` etc. in the diff). In `CHANGELOG.md`, formatting consistency matters for readability and generated docs, so please align this entry with the existing bullet indentation/style used by neighboring items.

This test mutates the `Sharded::BlogPost` class by defining `has_many :comments_without_query_constraints` but only restores `@query_constraints_list` in the `ensure` block. Because association definitions persist on the class, this can leak state into later tests or make this test order-dependent if the same association name is reused. Consider cleaning up the reflection after the assertion (e.g. removing the generated reflection/association method) or using a temporary model class instead.

This special-case checks `current.effectTag` to decide whether to suppress the normal bailout path, but I don't see the flag being cleared anywhere in this hunk. If `ForceUpdateForLegacySuspense` survives past the retry/commit that needed it, subsequent renders could be forced to reprocess unnecessarily. Please make sure the lifecycle of this effect tag is well-defined and that it's removed once the legacy suspense recovery render has completed.

This checks `current.effectTag` to decide whether to force work, but `effectTag` is primarily a commit-phase side-effect field and can contain stale bits from a previous render. Using it as a begin-work bailout signal risks coupling scheduling logic to unrelated effects and may accidentally force or skip work in edge cases. If this flag is meant to survive across the suspended retry, it would be safer to store it in a dedicated render-time field (or at least ensure it is explicitly cleared at the right point), rather than reading commit effect state from `current` here.

Setting `ForceUpdateForLegacySuspense` here fixes the legacy-mode bailout case, but this path now depends on that flag being reliably cleared after the retry/commit. Otherwise the source fiber could be forced to re-render on later updates even when it no longer suspended, causing unnecessary work or masking other bailout behavior. Please verify the flag lifecycle is covered end-to-end (set only for this suspended render, then cleared on the next completed pass), ideally with a regression test around repeated context updates.

Setting `ForceUpdateForLegacySuspense` on `sourceFiber` fixes the reported legacy-mode bailout, but this path now depends on that flag being reliably cleared after the retry/commit. If it isn't reset, subsequent updates could be forced unnecessarily and mask normal bailout behavior. Please double-check that all completion/commit paths consuming this flag clear it, and consider adding a regression test that verifies only the post-suspense retry is forced.

This `only: true` will cause the test runner to execute just this case and skip the rest of the suite. That’s useful while developing locally, but it should be removed before merging or it will mask unrelated regressions in `ESLintRulesOfHooks-test.js`.

Extending `isHook` to accept `JSXIdentifier` fixes the JSX-attribute case, but it also broadens hook detection to any JSX tag/prop name that starts with `use`. That can include non-hook JSX identifiers (for example custom elements or prop names) and may cause false positives unless all call sites of `isHook` are guaranteed to only pass identifiers that represent callable references. It would be safer to either constrain this to the JSX-attribute/declaration paths that motivated the change or add tests covering benign JSX names like `<Foo useData={value} />` and `<use-thing />` to ensure we don't regress lint behavior.

This new JSXAttribute branch assumes `node.parent.parent` is always a `JSXAttribute`, but function expressions can also appear inside other JSX expression containers (for example as children: `<Comp>{() => {}}</Comp>` or inside spread/other nested expressions). Accessing `node.parent.parent.type` without guarding that `node.parent.parent` exists and is the expected node shape can make `getFunctionName` brittle for other AST forms. Consider adding a null/shape guard before dereferencing `node.parent.parent` and keeping this branch narrowly scoped to actual attribute values.

This assertion only checks the insertion-effect cleanup after deleting the hidden subtree, but the issue being fixed is specifically about `React.lazy`/re-suspension semantics. In this test `ChildA`/`ChildB` are plain components resolved through `fakeImport`, so it would be stronger to also assert the full ordering around the hide/delete transition (including that layout cleanup happened earlier and insertion cleanup happens exactly once here) or add a dedicated lazy-wrapped regression case matching the reported bug. As written, this may pass without covering the original failure mode completely.

Using `dictAddOrFind()` avoids the assertion when the same forgotten node is blacklisted twice, which matches the reported crash. One thing to double-check here is ownership of `id`: unlike `dictAddRaw()`, this path may return an existing entry, in which case the freshly allocated `sds` is not inserted into the dictionary. If the dict type does not automatically free the unused key on the `find` path, this change could introduce a leak for repeated `FORGOTTEN_NODE` extensions. Consider freeing `id` when `dictGetKey(de) != id`, or using a lookup-first pattern to make ownership explicit.

Using `$+` here preserves duplicate prerequisites and their order. For a static archive target, that can accidentally add the same object multiple times if the dependency list changes later. `$(AR) $(ARFLAGS) $@ $^` is usually safer because it deduplicates prerequisites while still including all required objects.

Assigning `hdrAllocFns = *override;` without validating the input can crash callers if `override` is `NULL`, and it can also leave individual function pointers unset if the struct is only partially initialized. Since this is a global allocator hook used by the histogram library, it would be safer to reject `NULL` and either validate each callback or fall back missing entries to the defaults.

The overflow guard can itself trigger undefined behavior when `size == 0` because `SIZE_MAX / size` divides by zero. `calloc(0, n)` is valid and should typically be delegated to the configured allocator, so this check should either special-case zero first (e.g. `if (size != 0 && nmemb > SIZE_MAX / size)`) or use an equivalent formulation that avoids division by zero.

Switching from the single object file to `libhdrhistogram.a` changes the link dependency model, but this target still only depends on `$(REDIS_SERVER_OBJ)`. If the archive is not built before `redis-server`, parallel builds (`make -j`) can fail nondeterministically with a missing library. Please add the static library as an explicit prerequisite (and likewise for the benchmark target) so make knows the build order.

Registering the HDR histogram allocators from `initServerConfig()` means this global library state is reconfigured during normal server initialization rather than once at process startup. If `hdrSetAllocators()` is not idempotent or if any HDR objects can be created before this point in future refactors/tests, this can lead to allocator mismatches.
