These seek-realtime test calls appear to pass `e->ts.monotonic` instead of `e->ts.realtime`. That means the new test is not actually exercising `sd_journal_seek_realtime_usec()` with realtime timestamps, and it can miss the regression described in the issue where realtime goes backward. Please switch these six calls to use `e->ts.realtime`.

Reusing `arg_tpm2_key_pcr_values` by assigning its pointer into `arg_tpm2_hash_pcr_values` creates aliasing between two logically distinct option buffers. That can easily lead to double-free/cleanup issues later, or to surprising behavior if one of them is modified independently. It would be safer to either compute the bank from `arg_tpm2_key_pcr_values` directly at the call site, or make an explicit copy here instead of sharing ownership.

This fallback changes the effective meaning of `--tpm2-pcrs=` from "no TPM PCR binding" to "implicitly use `--tpm2-public-key-pcrs`" whenever the former is empty. That seems broader than the issue being fixed and may silently enroll a policy bound to PCR 7/public-key PCRs when the user explicitly cleared TPM PCRs. Please consider limiting this to the bank-selection logic only, instead of passing the substituted array into `enroll_tpm2()`, or add a stronger guard that distinguishes an explicitly empty `--tpm2-pcrs=` from an unspecified one.

The grammar here is off (`can contains`) and the allowed character set for HTTP header names looks too restrictive/inaccurate. HTTP field names are typically defined as `token` and allow more characters than just alphanumerics, `_`, and `-` (for example `.` is commonly used in custom headers). If the implementation really enforces this narrower set, it would be good to document that it's a systemd-specific restriction and why; otherwise the man page should be updated to match the actual parser behavior.

The allowed character set for header values is too restrictive for general HTTP headers and may reject valid configurations. In particular, common values often contain characters like `:` (already handled by the split), but also tabs or other visible ASCII not listed here, and RFC-compliant field values are broader than this whitelist. This risks breaking legitimate headers such as `Authorization`, `User-Agent`, or vendor-specific values. Consider validating against HTTP field-value rules more directly, or at least allowing the full visible ASCII range except CR/LF to prevent header injection while preserving valid use cases.

The hard-coded 40 character limit for header names looks arbitrary and is stricter than HTTP requires. There are valid custom and vendor-prefixed header names longer than this, so this may unnecessarily reject user-supplied headers introduced by this feature. Unless there's a documented systemd-specific constraint elsewhere, it would be safer to drop this limit or replace it with a much larger defensive bound.

Allowing `_` in header names is non-standard for HTTP field names. Some servers and proxies reject or normalize such headers for security reasons, so accepting them here may lead to confusing interoperability issues. If compatibility with generic HTTP infrastructure is the goal, consider restricting this to the RFC token character set (which includes `-` but not `_`) or at least documenting why underscores are intentionally supported.

This API takes raw `const char *` for both header name and value, but the declaration doesn't communicate any validation or ownership expectations. Since this helper will be used for user-configured HTTP headers, it would be good to document or enforce constraints here (for example, rejecting names/values containing `:`/CR/LF and clarifying whether the strings are copied into the `OrderedHashmap`). Without that, callers may accidentally pass invalid or non-owned data and introduce malformed requests or lifetime bugs.

Appending user-configured headers after the built-in ones means a config entry can override protocol-critical headers like `Content-Type` or `Transfer-Encoding`, which may break journal upload semantics or create hard-to-debug server-side behavior. Please either reject reserved header names in `config_parse_header`/here, or document and enforce a clear allowlist/denylist for headers that must remain under program control.

This test target only includes `test-journal-header-util.c` and `journal-header-util.c`. If `journal-header-util.c` depends on helpers from the shared journal-remote utility sources (for example common macros, string/vector helpers, or test support that other unit tests pull in), this target may either fail to link or silently diverge from how the code is built in production. Please verify the new test uses the same dependencies/includes as the main target, or add the required shared sources/libraries explicitly here.

This test accepts an empty header value, but it never verifies the resulting map contents after the earlier overwrite and duplicate-insert cases. As written, the test only checks return codes, so a regression in `header_put()` that returns success but stores the wrong value/order would go unnoticed. Consider asserting that the hashmap contains the expected final values (e.g. `Name` should be `""` after line 17, `NewName` should still be `"Val"`, and the number of entries should remain correct).

This test only verifies that the journal entry still arrives, but it doesn't actually assert that the configured custom header was sent or handled. As written, it would also pass if `Header=` were ignored entirely. Please add a check on the remote side (or server logs/request metadata) that confirms `TestHeader: TestValue` was present in the upload request, otherwise the new feature isn't really covered by this test.

When we enter `ANSI_COLOR_STATE_OSC_SEQUENCE_TERMINATING` after seeing `ESC`, any following byte other than `'\\'` is currently discarded because this state always frees the OSC buffer, resets to `TEXT`, and `break`s without reprocessing the current character. That means malformed or unsupported `OSC ... ESC <non-\\>` sequences can eat one real output byte. Previously, the `ESC` itself terminated the OSC immediately and the next byte was handled normally. Consider either rewinding/reprocessing `c` after leaving this state, or only consuming the byte when it is actually `'\\'`.

The assertion here only verifies that the selection anchor/end coordinates are decremented after the circular buffer advances. The PR description also mentions invalidating the previous selection rects, but this test doesn't exercise or assert that behavior. Consider adding a check that the old selection region is invalidated/redrawn (or at least that the selection rects themselves move as expected), otherwise a regression in the rendering invalidation path could still slip through while this test passes.

This manual `Release()` on `m_inner` is a pretty fragile lifetime workaround. It assumes `m_inner` is always initialized here and that exactly one extra reference needs to be dropped to balance the `AddRef()` above. If either assumption changes, this can turn into a null dereference or an over-release/use-after-free. Can we guard this with an assertion/comment that documents the expected refcount contract (for example, why `m_inner` is guaranteed non-null at this point and why releasing exactly once is correct), or encapsulate it in a helper so the ownership hack is less error-prone?

Using `args[0].data()` as the start of the `$*` expansion drops any original spacing between the alias name and the first argument. For input like `foo   a b`, this will expand to `a b` instead of preserving the full original argument tail (`  a b` / `   a b`, depending on expected doskey behavior). The previous implementation used the substring after the first space, so `$*` preserved the original spacing exactly. Consider slicing from `firstArgEndIdx + 1` (or the original arg-string start) instead of from the first parsed argument token.

This change removes the direct unit tests for the helper methods (`s_Tokenize`, `s_GetArgString`, and the individual macro replacement helpers). Even if the public end-to-end alias tests now cover the reported whitespace bug, dropping these focused tests reduces regression coverage for edge cases in the parsing/replacement primitives and makes future failures harder to localize. Consider keeping or updating the helper-level tests unless those APIs were intentionally removed or made inaccessible.

Renaming this helper in a public/common header can be a breaking change for all existing call sites. In this hunk I don't see a compatibility shim or corresponding updates, so please make sure every use of `clamp_slice_abs` is migrated, or consider keeping the old name as a forwarding wrapper/deprecated alias to avoid widespread build breaks.

Same concern here: changing the API name in `til.h` from `clamp_slice_len` to `safe_slice_len` will break any unchanged callers. If the intent is just to improve naming, it would be safer to preserve the old symbol as a wrapper or verify that all in-tree and downstream references are updated in the same change.

Switching from `clamp_slice_len` to `safe_slice_len` changes the behavior here from truncating an out-of-range slice to returning an empty/invalid slice when `height * softFontIndex` overflows or starts past the end of `softFontPattern`. That seems reasonable, but this line now relies on the subsequent `data.empty() || data.size() != height` check to preserve the old fallback behavior. Please confirm that `safe_slice_len` cannot throw/assert in these cases and that all callers expect invalid soft-font indices to be handled by the existing early-return path rather than by clamping to a shorter slice.

Removing the `WindowsTerminal` dependency on `Microsoft.Terminal.Remoting` is only safe if every remaining build path no longer consumes artifacts from that project. Since this PR removes the remoting projects from the solution entirely, please double-check that no project references, post-build steps, or packaging logic still expect the remoting DLL to exist; otherwise solution builds can become order-dependent or fail only in certain configurations/CI legs.

Removing `Microsoft.Terminal.Remoting.winmd` from the unpackaged distribution changes the public artifact set. If any unpackaged consumers, tooling, or registration logic still rely on this metadata file for WinRT activation or interop, this will be a breaking change. Please confirm that all remaining code paths no longer require the remoting WinMD and consider documenting the rationale here, since the surrounding comment still implies there may be exceptions to the removal list.

Removing `Microsoft.Terminal.Remoting.dll` from the required in-proc server list makes sense if the remoting/Monarch-Peasant package registration was intentionally deleted as part of this refactor. However, this test change will also stop catching cases where the manifest still accidentally includes the old DLL or where packaging cleanup is incomplete. Consider adding a complementary assertion that `Microsoft.Terminal.Remoting.dll` is *not* present in `$inProcServers`, so the package test validates both the new expected set and the removal of the obsolete component.

Removing `compatibility.isolatedMode` from the schema is a breaking settings change. If the implementation no longer supports this flag, we should also make sure existing user settings are handled gracefully (for example via migration, deprecation notes, or at least release notes), otherwise users with this property in `settings.json` may start seeing unknown-setting warnings with no guidance.

Removing the `TerminalRemoting` ETW provider from the WPR profile also removes our ability to capture traces for the new single-instance / cross-process window activation path introduced in this PR. Since this refactor replaces Monarch/Peasant with mutex + `WM_COPYDATA`, losing this provider may make regressions like window state/focus-mode restoration much harder to diagnose. If the provider is truly obsolete, please confirm it has been removed from the codebase as well; otherwise it would be safer to keep it in the profile until equivalent instrumentation exists elsewhere.

`FindTargetWindow` now unconditionally reads `_settings.GlobalSettings()` without the previous lazy initialization guard. If this method can still be reached before settings are loaded during startup / cross-process activation, this will either dereference a null settings object or use default/uninitialized windowing behavior, which matches the kind of startup-state regression described in the issue. Please make sure settings are guaranteed to be loaded before this call, or keep an explicit initialization check here.

`CreateNewWindow` no longer ensures `_settings` is initialized before consuming warning/state derived from settings. If window creation can happen before the initial settings load in the new single-threaded startup path, this risks creating a window with incomplete configuration (for example, focus mode/title bar state). Consider preserving the null/loaded check here or documenting the stronger invariant that callers must load settings first.

Renaming `GetSettings()` to `Settings()` also drops the previous `[[nodiscard]]` annotation. Since this returns the app's settings object and ignoring it is likely unintended, removing `[[nodiscard]]` weakens the API contract and can let accidental no-op calls slip through silently. Consider keeping `[[nodiscard]]` on the renamed accessor as well.

Exposing the entire `CascadiaSettings` object here is a much broader ABI/API surface than the previous set of read-only properties. That can unintentionally couple `AppLogic` consumers to the full settings model and make future settings refactors harder. If callers only need a few values (like theme / isolated mode / tray icon behavior), consider keeping narrow accessors or exposing a smaller projection instead of the full settings object.

This constructor copies `ShowWindowCommand` from the incoming `CommandlineArgs`, but the alternate constructor below leaves `_ShowWindowCommand` at its default `SW_NORMAL`. Given the reported regression around focus mode/title bar state on newly created windows, this asymmetry looks risky: windows created through the `window/content/bounds` path may ignore the caller's intended show state and come up as a normal window. Consider either accepting `ShowWindowCommand` in the second constructor as well or documenting/ensuring that this path is never used for windows that need to preserve startup/focus-mode behavior.

Making `Id` a public settable field here is risky for a remoting contract. Unlike the other members in this type, it isn't exposed as a read-only property, so callers can mutate it after construction and potentially desynchronize the window identity from the rest of the request payload. Given this PR is refactoring single-instance/window-routing behavior, an immutable ID would be safer and more consistent with the other args types.

This removes the German localization entries for `RenameFailedToast.Title` and `RenameFailedToast.Subtitle`. If the corresponding resource keys are still referenced anywhere in the app or in other locale files, this will cause a missing-resource fallback/regression for de-DE. Please confirm these keys were removed globally from the codebase and all resource files, or keep the entries until the feature usage is fully deleted.

These resource entries are being removed, but this hunk doesn't show the corresponding code paths being deleted. If any rename-failure UI still references `RenameFailedToast.Title` or `RenameFailedToast.Subtitle`, those lookups will now fail at runtime and may fall back to empty strings or trigger localization/resource errors. Please make sure all usages were removed or replace these with updated resource keys in the same change.

These localized `RenameFailedToast.*` entries are being removed from the Spanish resource file. If the corresponding resource keys are still referenced anywhere in the app, this will cause fallback to another language or missing-resource behavior at runtime. Please confirm the feature/toast was removed everywhere, or keep these keys in sync with the base `Resources.resw`.

These localized `RenameFailedToast.*` entries are being removed from the French resource file. If the corresponding resource keys are still referenced anywhere in the app, this will cause fallback to another language or missing-resource behavior at runtime. Please confirm the feature/toast was removed everywhere, or keep these translations in sync with the base `Resources.resw`.

This removes the Italian localization entries for `RenameFailedToast.Title` and `RenameFailedToast.Subtitle`. If the corresponding resource keys are still referenced anywhere in the app or still exist in the neutral/default `.resw`, this will cause the it-IT locale to fall back unexpectedly or surface missing-resource issues. Please confirm these keys were removed globally and are no longer used before deleting the localized strings.

These localized `RenameFailedToast.*` entries are being removed from the Japanese resource file. If the corresponding toast/UI strings are still referenced anywhere in the app, this will cause a fallback to another language or a missing-resource lookup at runtime. Please confirm the feature/string keys were removed globally (including the neutral/default resources and all locales), or keep these translations in sync with the code.

These localized `RenameFailedToast.*` entries are being removed from the Korean resource file. If the corresponding resource keys are still referenced anywhere in the app, this will cause lookup failures or fallback to another language at runtime. Please make sure the feature and all references were removed globally, or keep these keys in sync with the base/default `.resw` files.

These localized `RenameFailedToast.*` entries are being removed here. If the corresponding resource keys are still referenced anywhere in the app after the Monarch/Peasant refactor, pt-BR will now fall back to another language or fail resource lookup depending on usage. Please confirm the feature and all references were removed globally, or keep these strings in sync with the base `Resources.resw`.

These qps-ploc entries for `RenameFailedToast.Title` and `RenameFailedToast.Subtitle` were removed. If the corresponding resource keys are still referenced anywhere in the app, this will cause resource lookup failures or fallback behavior in pseudo-localization builds. Please make sure the keys were intentionally deleted from all `.resw` variants and that the feature/code paths using them were removed or updated accordingly.

This removes the `RenameFailedToast.Title`/`Subtitle` entries from the qps-ploca resource file. If the corresponding resource keys are still referenced anywhere in the app, this locale will now fall back to another language or fail resource lookup at runtime. Please confirm these toast strings were intentionally deleted everywhere (not just renamed/moved), and if they still exist in the default resources, keep this pseudo-localized copy in sync as well.

These `RenameFailedToast.*` resource entries are being removed from the pseudo-localized resource file. If the corresponding keys still exist in the base/default `.resw` and are still referenced by the app, this will create an inconsistent resource set and can cause fallback behavior or missing-string issues during pseudo-localization testing. Please make sure these keys were intentionally removed from all locales because the feature/string is no longer used, or keep the pseudo-localized entries in sync with the base resources.

These localized `RenameFailedToast.*` entries are being removed from `zh-CN`, but there’s no surrounding context here showing the corresponding resource keys were deleted from the app entirely. If the toast is still referenced anywhere, this will cause the Chinese locale to fall back unexpectedly or surface a missing-resource issue. Please confirm the feature/string keys were removed globally from all locales and code paths, or keep these translations in sync with the base resources.

These localized `RenameFailedToast.*` entries are being removed from zh-TW, but there’s no replacement in this hunk. If the app still references these resource keys anywhere after the Monarch/Peasant refactor, this will cause missing-resource fallbacks or blank UI in Traditional Chinese. Please confirm the feature and all lookups were removed everywhere, or keep the keys until the code paths are fully deleted.

This PR is described as removing Monarch/Peasant, but this filters file is adding `Monarch.cpp` and `Peasant.cpp` back into the project. If these types were intentionally deleted or replaced by the new single-instance/window-management path, reintroducing them here can cause the old implementation to keep building or create confusion about which code path is active. Please verify that the project file and filters stay consistent with the intended architecture and remove these entries if they are obsolete.

Same concern here: the PR title/description says Monarch/Peasant are being removed, but `Peasant.cpp` is being added to the filters. If this is only a stale project-entry change, it should be dropped; otherwise it suggests the old single-instance implementation is still part of the build, which could conflict with the new behavior and make regressions like window state/title bar issues harder to reason about.

Adding `Monarch.idl` appears inconsistent with the stated goal of removing Monarch/Peasant. If the runtime no longer uses these COM/WinRT contracts, keeping the IDL in the project can leave generated artifacts and interfaces around unnecessarily. Please confirm whether this IDL is still required, and remove it if the new single-threaded/window-emperor flow has fully replaced it.

This now forwards the drop position in screen coordinates (`CoreWindow::PointerPosition`) minus a drag offset computed from client coordinates (`GetCursorPos` + `ScreenToClient`). That mixes coordinate spaces: `pointerPoint` is screen-relative, while `_stashed.dragOffset` is relative to the client area/tab strip. Subtracting them can shift the spawned window by the non-client frame/title bar, which matches the focus-mode/title-bar regression described here. Consider keeping both values in the same space (for example, compute the drag offset in screen coordinates, or convert the final adjusted point consistently before passing it on).

This declaration still takes `winrt::TerminalApp::RequestReceiveContentArgs`, but this hunk removes the local `RequestReceiveContentArgs` type definition and its factory registration from this header. If the type was moved elsewhere, this is fine; otherwise this leaves a dangling public API reference that will fail to compile or break projection generation. Please verify the type is still declared/generated in another header included here, or update this signature to use the replacement type.

This method used to explicitly marshal back to the window's dispatcher because the comment notes it may be invoked from a background thread. Changing it to a plain `void` method and immediately touching `_root`/UI state assumes callers are now always on the UI thread, but that guarantee isn't evident from this hunk and the stale comment above still says the opposite. If any `SettingsChanged` notification is still raised off-thread, `_root->SetSettings`, dialog operations, and `_RefreshThemeRoutine()` will become unsafe. Please either preserve the foreground dispatch here or update the event/call chain and comment to document and enforce the new threading guarantee.

Changing `UpdateSettings` from `safe_void_coroutine` to a synchronous `void` method is a significant behavioral change. If the previous coroutine form was relied on to defer work until after XAML/window initialization or to avoid reentrancy during startup, making it synchronous could reintroduce ordering issues like the focus-mode/title-bar regression described in this PR. Please verify that all callers still invoke this only after the window/root has been fully initialized, or document why the async boundary is no longer needed.

Adding a new WinRT property to the IDL changes the public ABI for `ControlCore`. Please make sure the generated projections and the corresponding implementation/stub are updated everywhere this interface is consumed, and consider whether this should be added to a newer interface version instead of modifying the existing one directly to avoid breaking binary compatibility for existing clients.

Changing `TouchPressed` from `Microsoft.Terminal.Core.Point` to `Windows.Foundation.Point` alters the WinRT contract for this interface. Since `ControlInteractivity.idl` is part of the projected API surface, this can break existing consumers or create a mismatch with any generated/implemented signatures that still use the old type. Please verify that all implementations/callers were updated and consider whether this API change is intentional and version-safe.

This is another public IDL signature change from `Microsoft.Terminal.Core.Point` to `Windows.Foundation.Point`. Besides updating implementations, please confirm the coordinate semantics are still identical (e.g. float vs. integer precision, DPI/scaling expectations), because touch handling is sensitive to subtle type/space changes and this PR is fixing window/input behavior.

Using `DisplayInformation::GetForCurrentView()` here can be problematic in the single-threaded/multi-window refactor, because it depends on a current XAML view and may not reflect the actual monitor DPI for the specific control/window. If this peer is reused after a window moves between monitors, or if the call happens without the expected view context, the cached padding can be scaled incorrectly. Consider passing the effective rasterization scale from the owning control/window instead of querying global view state here.

`ContextMenuRequestedEventArgs::Position()` is already relative to the control in XAML coordinates. Adding `GetPadding()` here shifts the flyout by the terminal padding, so right-clicking near the edge of the control will open the context menu offset from the actual pointer location. This also changes behavior from the previous implementation, which positioned the menu relative to the control origin, not the text grid origin. Consider passing the scaled position through directly without adding padding.

Replacing the previous fallback with `assert(false)` + a hardcoded `{ 10, 10 }` makes this path much less robust in non-debug builds. If `MinimumSize()` is queried before `_initializedTerminal` is set, release builds will silently return an arbitrary size instead of a value derived from settings/DPI, which can cause incorrect initial layout. It would be safer to preserve the old computed fallback unless we can prove this branch is unreachable.

The border thickness arguments appear to be swapped here. `Thickness` is `{ left, top, right, bottom }`, and the old code used `{ width, height, 0, 0 }` to size the highlight to a cell. Changing this to `{ fontSize.Height, fontSize.Width, 0, 0 }` will distort the tooltip border for non-square cells.

Changing this overload from `safe_void_coroutine` to `void` removes the async boundary from a settings update path. Given the PR is collapsing window/threading behavior, this is probably intentional, but please verify the implementation no longer performs any dispatcher hops or awaits. If it still does, converting only the declaration to `void` can introduce reentrancy/order-of-operations changes during startup, which is relevant to focus-mode/title-bar initialization bugs.

Similar concern here: `UpdateAppearance` used to be coroutine-based and is now synchronous. If appearance changes were previously marshaled to the UI thread or intentionally deferred, making this `void` can change when non-client/UI state is applied relative to window creation. Please confirm the implementation is fully synchronous and always called on the UI thread, otherwise this could regress startup visual state such as focus mode/title bar visibility.

Removing the `IsolatedMode` projected setting from the view model changes the public surface exposed to the settings UI. If `isolatedMode` is still present in the schema, XAML bindings, or serialization/migration paths, this will either break bindings at runtime or make the setting silently disappear from the editor while still existing in user settings. Please verify that all references to `IsolatedMode` were removed or intentionally deprecated across the editor, schema, and docs.

Removing `IsolatedMode` from the WinRT interface is a breaking API change for any XAML/UI code or generated bindings that still reference this projected setting. If this property is intentionally being retired as part of the Monarch/Peasant removal, please make sure all consumers (including generated code, settings model/viewmodel code, and any persisted settings migration paths) have been updated in the same change; otherwise this can surface as build breaks or runtime binding failures.

Dropping the `!IsolatedMode()` guard changes behavior for isolated launches: they can now opt into persisted window layout restoration. Given the issue/PR context around new-window creation and focus mode/title bar state, this may unintentionally restore stale window chrome/state for windows that were previously expected to start clean in isolated mode. Can we confirm that isolated mode is supposed to honor persisted layouts now, and if so, add/update tests for that interaction? Otherwise this looks like a regression risk.

Removing `IsolatedMode` from the WinRT/IDL surface is a breaking API change. If this setting is still referenced anywhere in the settings parser, serialization/deserialization, defaults, or UI bindings, older configs may stop round-tripping cleanly or fail to compile at generated projection call sites. Please verify all consumers have been removed and consider whether this should remain as a deprecated/ignored setting for backward compatibility instead of being deleted outright.

This changes the behavior for out-of-range values from clamping to silently falling back to `400`. That means inputs like `0`, negative numbers, or values above `1000` will now produce a normal weight instead of the nearest valid weight (`1` or `1000`), which is a semantic change and could mask bad binding/input data. If the goal is just to avoid invalid `FontWeight` values, consider preserving the previous clamping behavior and only rounding within the valid range.

Calling `DestroyWindow` unconditionally here looks risky because `_CloseRequested` now delegates window teardown to `WindowEmperor` via a posted message. If `Close()` can be reached before the posted close is processed, or after the HWND was already destroyed, this can introduce reentrancy/double-destroy behavior and leave the app in an inconsistent state. It would be safer to guard this with a handle validity check and make ownership of HWND destruction explicit in one place.

This now treats only strictly positive numeric window IDs as valid targets. Previously, window routing went through the remoting layer, and it's not obvious from this change that `0` can never be a valid ID. If `0` is used anywhere as a legitimate window identifier, drag/drop attach will silently fall back to creating a new window instead of attaching to the intended one. Please confirm the ID contract here or relax the check to match the actual valid range.

Storing `WindowEmperor` as a raw pointer makes `AppHost`'s lifetime assumptions implicit. Since this refactor removes the old remoting ownership model and centralizes window management, it would be safer to document or encode the ownership contract here (for example with a reference, `not_null`, or a comment/assert in the constructor) so `AppHost` can't outlive the manager and dereference a dangling pointer during callbacks like summon/commandline dispatch.

Moving `CM_UPDATE_TITLE` into `BaseWindow` changes it from a shared/custom message definition to a per-class constant based on `WM_USER`. Since `WM_USER` ranges are only guaranteed to be private to a specific window class, this can become fragile if other custom messages are still defined elsewhere for the same HWND or if different window types need to interoperate. Please verify that all custom messages for this window class are now centralized here (or consider using `WM_APP`/a shared enum) to avoid collisions and subtle regressions like state updates being delivered to the wrong handler.

Removing the default `WM_DESTROY -> PostQuitMessage(0)` behavior changes the lifetime semantics of every `BaseWindow` consumer. That may be intentional for the single-threaded/window-emperor refactor, but it also means any derived window that doesn't explicitly post a quit message will leave the message loop running after the last window closes. Given the reported focus-mode/title-bar regression around reopening windows, it would be worth confirming that shutdown/teardown is now handled centrally and that all close paths still terminate the loop when appropriate.

`_OnGetMinMaxInfo` now calls `_pfnSnapDimensionCallback` unconditionally, but the previous implementation explicitly handled the pre-initialization case where this callback may not be set yet (the comment above still says that can happen during `WM_CREATE`). If `WM_GETMINMAXINFO` arrives before the page/XAML wiring is complete, this will dereference a null callback and can also produce incorrect minimum tracking sizes. Please restore the null check/fallback to `minimumWidth`/`minimumHeight` here.

Changing `minimumWidth` from `long` to `float` is a potentially risky API change for window sizing code, since Win32 sizing paths (`MINMAXINFO`, `RECT`, style calculations, etc.) are integer-based. If this value is consumed by code that ultimately rounds or truncates back to integral dimensions, using `float` here can introduce unnecessary conversions and subtle off-by-one behavior. Unless there is a specific DPI-scaling reason to keep this as a floating-point value, it would be safer to preserve an integral type or make the scaling/rounding explicit at the call site.

Same concern here: `minimumHeight` is now a `float`, but the surrounding window-management APIs are generally integer-based. Using a floating-point type for a constant that represents pixel dimensions can make later comparisons and conversions less predictable. If this is intended for DPI-aware calculations, consider documenting that and centralizing the rounding behavior; otherwise an integral type would better match the Win32 sizing model.

Removing the `NonClientIslandWindow::Refrigerate()` override also removes the `_callbacks = {};` cleanup that revoked this class's XAML/event subscriptions before the window was refrigerated. If `IslandWindow::Refrigerate()` does not now handle these `NonClientIslandWindow`-specific callbacks, stale handlers can survive across refrigerate/reactivate cycles and explain state restoration bugs like the title bar reappearing. Please confirm the callback revocation was intentionally moved elsewhere, or preserve this cleanup in the new lifecycle.

Removing the `Refrigerate()` override here looks risky given the reported regression: refrigerated windows are involved in restoring/reusing a window, and `NonClientIslandWindow` owns the custom non-client/titlebar behavior needed for focus mode. If this class now falls back to the base `IslandWindow::Refrigerate()` implementation, any non-client specific state may no longer be preserved/reset correctly, which would explain a restored window showing a standard title bar. Please verify that the base implementation fully handles `NonClientIslandWindow`'s custom title bar/focus-mode state, or keep a specialized override here.

Calling `host->Close()` before erasing the entry can re-enter `WindowEmperor` while `_windows` still contains this host. Since `Close()` may pump messages / trigger callbacks synchronously, that can lead to duplicate close handling or stale-window lookups during shutdown. Consider erasing/removing the host from `_windows` first (or guarding against re-entrancy) before invoking `Close()`.

Storing raw `shared_ptr<AppHost>`s here means `WindowEmperor` now directly owns every window object. Given this PR removes the old window-thread lifecycle and the reported bug is about stale/refrigerated windows affecting later focus-mode state, it's worth double-checking that closed/minimized/refrigerated windows are always removed from `_windows` promptly. Otherwise helpers like `_mostRecentWindow()`, `_summonWindow()`, or `GetMainWindow()` can accidentally target a no-longer-active window and reuse stale state. If ownership is not intended, consider weak references or documenting/removing entries on close deterministically.

This rewrite drops the trailing newline from the project file (`\ No newline at end of file`). That usually isn't functionally significant for MSBuild, but it creates unnecessary diff churn and can trip formatting/linters or future tooling that expects POSIX-style text files to end with a newline. Please keep the final newline when regenerating/saving this file.

Removing `GetScaleFactor()` from this interface is a breaking API change for every `IControlAccessibilityInfo` implementer and caller. If the scale factor is no longer needed, please verify that all accessibility/UIA code paths that previously depended on DPI scaling now derive it from another source; otherwise this can regress coordinate translation for screen readers or hit testing, especially in mixed-DPI/multi-monitor scenarios.

This changes the semantics from returning raw padding/scale information to returning an absolute origin, but the calculation only uses `left`/`top`. If `GetPadding()` is DPI-scaled while `GetBounds()` is in a different coordinate space, this can introduce subtle UIA hit-testing/focus issues on high-DPI or mixed-DPI setups. Please verify both values are in the same coordinate system, or document/convert here explicitly.

Replacing `GetPadding()` and `GetScaleFactor()` with a single `GetContentOrigin()` changes the provider's geometry contract quite a bit. Can you verify that all UIA bounding-rectangle calculations that previously depended on both padding and DPI scaling are still correct? Returning only an origin point may lose information needed to translate text/control coordinates on per-monitor-DPI or non-default padding scenarios, which could surface as misplaced accessibility hit-testing/focus rectangles.

This refactor changes the coordinate math from the previous mixed DIP/physical-pixel handling to a simple integer offset add. Please double-check that `GetContentOrigin()` is returned in the same coordinate space as `clientPoint`/`screenPoint` for all DPI modes. The old code explicitly applied padding and scale-factor conversion, so if `GetContentOrigin()` is still DIP-based or rounded to `til::point`, UIA text ranges can become misaligned on non-100% scaling or per-monitor DPI transitions.

Removing `unitRemoting` from the `ValidateSet` changes the public interface of `Invoke-OpenConsoleTests()`. If the corresponding test project was intentionally deleted/renamed as part of the Monarch/Peasant removal, this is fine, but otherwise this will cause existing scripts/CI invocations that still pass `-Test unitRemoting` to fail parameter validation immediately. Please confirm the test target no longer exists and update any callers/documentation in the same change.

Removing `Remoting.Unit.Tests.dll` from the unit test runner means this script will no longer execute that test suite. Given the PR is replacing/removing the Monarch/Peasant remoting architecture, this can unintentionally reduce coverage exactly where behavior changed most. If these tests were intentionally deleted or migrated, it would help to update this script accordingly (or add the replacement test assembly) so `runut.cmd` still exercises the relevant scenarios instead of silently skipping them.

Removing the `unitRemoting` test entry drops the only automated coverage for the remoting/single-instance window handoff path, which is exactly the area this PR is refactoring (`WM_COPYDATA`, window routing, startup behavior). If these tests are intentionally obsolete after deleting Monarch/Peasant, please replace them with equivalent coverage for the new implementation rather than just removing them, otherwise regressions like the focus-mode/title-bar issue may go undetected.

This change replaces `DECAWM` with lowercase `decawm`. If the spelling action is case-sensitive, this may stop whitelisting the uppercase acronym that appears in docs/comments and reintroduce failures. Consider keeping the original uppercase entry as well unless you've confirmed the checker normalizes case.

Removing the `libpopcnt` and `dynamic_bitset` license notices from `NOTICE.md` is only safe if those third-party components were also fully removed from the repository/build. Please double-check that there are no remaining source files, transitive inclusions, or packaged artifacts depending on them; otherwise this creates a licensing/compliance gap.

Removing the pre-increment `TriggerFlush(true)` changes the ordering guarantees around circular-buffer rotation. Previously, any pending renderer output was forced out before `FirstRow` advanced, which helped keep the terminal's visible state synchronized with the old buffer layout. Given the PR notes and the linked resize/reflow quirk, can we confirm that all remaining callers now emit/flush synchronously before `IncrementCircularBuffer()` is reached? Otherwise, scrolling or resize-related reflow could be generated against the post-rotation state and overwrite/misorder content.

Changing the default from `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK` to `0` appears to opt all ConPTY sessions into the resize behavior described in #14291, where enlarging the viewport can replay/reflow buffered content and overwrite the terminal's current output. Since the issue/PR context explicitly calls this out as an unresolved quirk that should be documented, dropping the flag unconditionally here is a behavioral regression unless the new VT path fully compensates for it. Please either preserve the flag by default, gate this behind an explicit opt-in/setting, or add clear documentation/tests showing why the quirk is now safe to disable.

Removing `ConptyRoundtripTests.cpp` also drops the only test coverage in this project that exercised end-to-end ConPTY output against TerminalCore. Given the PR explicitly calls out unresolved resize/reflow quirks and changes the VT translation path substantially, this creates a regression risk where those behaviors are no longer validated anywhere. If these tests were intentionally moved, please add/update the replacement project in this change; otherwise consider keeping a targeted roundtrip test here (or adding equivalent coverage elsewhere) before removing it.

Removing `oss\dynamic_bitset` and `oss\libpopcnt` from the global include path can break any project that still relies on transitive includes from this shared props file. If this is intentional cleanup, it would be safer to first verify all consumers now declare these include directories explicitly, otherwise unrelated projects may start failing to compile depending on include order.

This now treats `--vtmode` as a no-op and consumes only the flag token, but the old implementation expected a value. If any existing caller still passes `--vtmode <value>`, the value will remain in `args` and may be misparsed as a positional/client argument later. Consider consuming the optional following value as well (or rejecting it explicitly) to preserve backward compatibility and avoid argument parsing regressions.

This changes the ConPTY check from `IsInVtIoMode()` to `GetVtIo(nullptr)`, which may broaden the condition from "currently in VT I/O mode" to "a VT I/O object exists". If those are not strictly equivalent, cursor blinking/notification work could now be skipped in cases where VT I/O is present but inactive. Please confirm the semantics match here, or keep the mode-based predicate to avoid behavior regressions.

Removing `SuppressResizeRepaint()` here looks behaviorally significant for ConPTY resize handling. The issue being addressed specifically calls out that enlarging the pseudo console can emit a reflowed buffer and overwrite terminal content; this call appears to have been the guard preventing VT repaint on resize. If `_api.ResizeWindow` now performs synchronous VT translation, please either preserve equivalent suppression in the new path or document why resize no longer triggers repaint, otherwise this change may reintroduce the resize overwrite quirk.

Switching from `GetVtIo()` to `GetVtIoNoCheck()` removes the null/availability guard here. Since `_InputThread()` can run during teardown, this makes `CloseInput()` depend on `VtIo` still being present in global console state. Please confirm that `GetVtIoNoCheck()` is always valid on this path, or keep the checked accessor to avoid a shutdown-time crash/regression.

This `static_cast` assumes the state machine engine is always an `InputStateMachineEngine`. If that invariant is not strictly guaranteed, this becomes undefined behavior and is harder to diagnose than the previous callback-based indirection. Consider documenting the invariant here or using a safer access pattern/assert to catch mismatches in debug builds.

This changes the public API from a setter (`SetLookingForDSR`) to a getter (`IsLookingForDSR`) and removes the callback member, which suggests the ownership of the DSR-tracking state moved elsewhere. Please make sure all previous call sites that relied on updating this state still have an equivalent path; otherwise DSR handling can silently stop working or become stale. Given the PR/issue context around resize behavior and synchronous VT translation, regressions here could affect cursor-position queries during resize/reflow scenarios.

This `WriteFile` call doesn't handle partial synchronous writes. On pipes/files, a successful `WriteFile` can report fewer bytes written than requested, but here the byte count is discarded (`lpNumberOfBytesWritten == nullptr`) and the function returns immediately. That can truncate VT output under some handle types. Please capture the written byte count and loop until the entire `_front` buffer has been sent, or otherwise document why partial writes are impossible for this output handle.

The shrink heuristic looks inverted relative to the comment. `cap > _front.capacity() / 2` will be true when `_back` is more than half of `_front`, i.e. when the new buffer is not much smaller than the old one. To free memory only after a significant shrink, this likely wants `_front.capacity() > cap * 2` (or equivalent) instead.

Using an abbreviated function template (`auto&&... args`) in a header requires C++20. If this project still targets C++17 in any configuration/toolchain, this will break the build. Consider spelling this as an explicit template (`template<typename... Args> void WriteFormat(Args&&... args)`) unless the language standard bump is guaranteed everywhere.

The trailing cell of a full-width glyph is being marked with `COMMON_LVB_LEADING_BYTE` as well. Historically the second cell should carry `COMMON_LVB_TRAILING_BYTE`; otherwise any logic that distinguishes lead/trail cells may misinterpret the buffer contents. The same pattern appears again in the fill path below, so it would be good to fix both sites consistently.

This has the same issue as the write path above: the second half of a full-width character is tagged as `COMMON_LVB_LEADING_BYTE` instead of `COMMON_LVB_TRAILING_BYTE`. That can break width/accounting logic and makes the later `last.Attributes & COMMON_LVB_LEADING_BYTE` check ambiguous for trail cells.

These callers now acquire the console lock before invoking `FillConsoleImpl`, but `FillConsoleImpl` also calls `LockConsole()` internally. Unless the lock is explicitly re-entrant, this introduces a risk of self-deadlock and also makes ownership harder to reason about. Consider making `FillConsoleImpl` assume the caller already holds the lock, or moving all locking responsibility into the helper and removing it from the wrappers.

Mutating the memory behind a `std::wstring_view` via `const_cast` is very risky here. The comment says the backing storage currently comes from `_CONSOLE_API_MSG::_inputBuffer`, but `WriteCharsLegacy` accepts any `wstring_view`, so this silently bakes in a non-local lifetime/mutability assumption and can become UB if a caller passes immutable storage. It also means the caller's buffer is permanently modified before returning. Please avoid in-place mutation here and either write a transformed copy to `io` or add a dedicated escaping/filtering path in `VtIo`.

This `" \r"` workaround is called out in the PR description as intentionally breaking text reflow on resize. Given the linked issue about ConPTY resize/reflow quirks, emitting an extra printable space here means the VT side will diverge from the buffer contents and can overwrite/reflow incorrectly when the window grows. At minimum this should be gated behind a compatibility/ConPTY-specific quirk or documented with a stronger code comment/TODO near the emission site so the behavior is explicit and easier to revisit.

This now returns `nullptr` for any non-active `SCREEN_INFORMATION`, which is a behavioral change from the old `GetVtIo()` that always returned `_vtIo` when VT mode was enabled. Given the PR notes already call out unresolved issues around `SetConsoleActiveScreenBuffer`/buffer switching, this gating may suppress VT generation for operations targeting an inactive buffer and make those paths silently no-op instead of surfacing the need for a buffer switch. Please double-check all callers that pass a `context` here and consider documenting or asserting this contract so inactive-buffer writes don't regress unexpectedly.

`totalOffset` is derived from signed coordinate deltas and `bufferStride`, but its type is deduced with `auto`. If `bufferStride` is `size_t`, negative `offsetX/offsetY` values can be converted to a huge unsigned value before `subspan()` is called. That can happen when the request rectangle starts outside the buffer on the left/top. Please keep this as a signed index (or validate the computed offset explicitly) before converting to the span index type.

Passing `requestRectangle.Width()` as the source stride assumes the input buffer is laid out exactly like the requested rectangle. That matches the current callers, but this helper was just generalized with an explicit `bufferStride` parameter, so it would be safer to validate that the span is large enough for `height * stride` (especially after clipping) before iterating. Otherwise a malformed request can make `subspan(totalOffset, writeRectangle.Width())` walk past the provided buffer.

This unconditionally reintroduces a full viewport write on resize in VT/ConPTY mode, but the previous code explicitly skipped that when the resize quirk was enabled. Given the issue description, resizing to a larger size can cause ConPTY to emit the reflowed buffer and overwrite terminal content, so always calling `WriteToScreen` here looks like a regression. Please preserve the old guard or otherwise document/handle the ConPTY resize behavior before forcing redraws in VT mode.

Writing the title as `OSC 0 ... BEL` without escaping/sanitizing the title can break the control sequence if `title8` contains BEL or ST (`ESC \\`). Since window titles can come from applications, this should either escape/strip terminators or use a helper that guarantees safe OSC payload encoding.

This changes the check from `IsInVtIoMode()` to `GetVtIo(nullptr)`, which is a subtly different condition. If `VtIo` can exist before it is fully active, or remain allocated after teardown, `ShowWindow` may start targeting the pseudo window in cases where we are not actually in PTY/VT-IO mode. Since this method controls which HWND receives the message, it would be safer to preserve the semantic check for "currently in VT IO mode" (or add an explicit helper for that) rather than using pointer existence as a proxy.

Using `GetVtIo(nullptr)` here appears to redefine `IsConsolePty()` from a mode/state query to a null-check on the backing object. That can change behavior for callers that expect this to mean "PTY mode is active" rather than "a VtIo instance exists". If the refactor intentionally changes that contract, it would be good to document it; otherwise, consider keeping the previous `IsInVtIoMode()` semantics or introducing a dedicated `HasVtIo()` helper to avoid conflating the two.

This change removes the previous ConPTY/PTy-mode exception and now always forces a full redraw when default attributes change. In this refactor, that may reintroduce the exact resize/reflow overwrite behavior described in the issue, because a full repaint in a pseudo-terminal can emit buffer contents back to the client and clobber terminal state. If the new VT path no longer needs redraw suppression, it would be good to document why here; otherwise consider preserving the PTY guard (or an equivalent `GetVtIo(nullptr)` check) to avoid unnecessary output in ConPTY mode.

Adding a context-aware `GetVtIo` suggests callers now need buffer-specific behavior, but the old `IsInVtIoMode()` helper was removed in the same change. Please make sure the new API preserves the old null/availability checks in a single place (or document the contract clearly), otherwise call sites may start dereferencing a `VtIo*` for screen buffers where VT translation should not apply. This is especially relevant with active/inactive screen buffer handling mentioned in the PR quirks.

Dropping `--resizeQuirk` here looks behaviorally significant. The surrounding comment still says we must preserve the resizing quirk for defterm/Terminal compatibility, and the issue linked in this PR is specifically about documenting that ConPTY resize behavior rather than removing it. If this flag is intentionally no longer needed after the VT refactor, please update the comment and verify the new path still reproduces the expected ConPTY resize semantics; otherwise this may silently regress default terminal resizing.

Conditionally skipping renderer creation for VT I/O changes a long-standing invariant in this function. Please double-check that every later use of `g.pRender` is now guarded (including any code outside this hunk) and that `CodepointWidthDetector` still has a valid fallback in ConPTY mode. Otherwise width calculation may become mode-dependent or null-deref paths may remain.

This test was previously validating that `--vtmode` is parsed correctly when arguments are tab-delimited, but after changing the input to omit `--vtmode` it now only duplicates the generic tab-splitting coverage from earlier cases. If `--vtmode` support was intentionally removed, consider adding an explicit negative test that `--vtmode` is rejected/ignored; otherwise we lose coverage for tab-delimited option parsing here.

By removing `--vtmode foo` from this scenario, the test no longer exercises the important edge case described in its name: distinguishing an implicit client command line from other recognized args. As written, it overlaps heavily with the previous `foo -- bar` case. Please either keep a replacement option-bearing case or rename/remove this test to avoid losing parser coverage.

This change drops the only coverage in this block for how `--` interacts with a preceding option that consumes a value. The new command line still tests explicit client-command splitting, but it no longer verifies that parser state is handled correctly after an option/value pair. If `--vtmode` was removed, consider replacing it with another value-taking option so this edge case remains covered.

Removing `ConptyOutputTests.cpp` from the host unit test project appears to drop coverage for ConPTY output behavior entirely. Given the PR explicitly calls out several intentionally unresolved ConPTY quirks (including resize/reflow behavior), it would be safer to either migrate these tests to their new home in the same change or add a comment/reference showing where equivalent coverage now lives. Otherwise this refactor risks silently losing regression protection for the resize/output semantics discussed in the issue.

Dropping the `vt.unittest` project reference makes sense if `VtRendererTests.cpp` is being removed, but please verify that no remaining tests in `ut_host` still depend on VT test utilities or shared helpers from that project. If equivalent tests were moved elsewhere, consider linking that in the PR description to make the test coverage transition explicit.

Removing the `inConpty` variant from this test also drops coverage for the resize behavior that previously exercised `IsInVtIoMode()`-gated paths. Given the issue/PR context explicitly calls out ConPTY-specific resize/reflow quirks, this test no longer verifies that deferred main-buffer resize behaves correctly when VT/ConPTY mode is active. Please either keep a ConPTY-mode variant here or add an equivalent dedicated test so we don't regress the ConPTY-specific behavior silently.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` changes the teardown contract for this test fixture. Unless font cleanup has been moved into `CleanupGlobalRenderer()`, `CleanupGlobalScreenBuffer()`, or `delete m_state`, this can leave global font state alive across tests and introduce order-dependent failures/leaks. Please confirm the ownership transfer and, if it was centralized elsewhere, consider documenting that here or updating related tests to make the new lifecycle explicit.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` from the test class cleanup changes the lifetime management of the global font state. If this cleanup was not moved elsewhere, it can leave process-global resources initialized across tests and introduce order-dependent failures or leaks in the `ut_host` suite. Please confirm that font teardown is now handled centrally (or is no longer required) and, if so, consider documenting that in the surrounding test infrastructure to avoid hidden cross-test coupling.

Removing `CleanupGlobalFont()` from test class cleanup changes the lifetime management of a global resource. If the font state is still initialized by these tests, this can leak state across test classes or make later tests order-dependent. Please confirm that font cleanup was moved elsewhere or is no longer required, and consider adding/adjusting teardown coverage to ensure global font state is reset between tests.

This setup now uses `CreatePipe`, but the next line calls `SetNamedPipeHandleState` on `rx`. `SetNamedPipeHandleState` only applies to named pipe handles, so this will fail for anonymous pipes created by `CreatePipe` and make the test setup brittle or consistently fail. If the intent is non-blocking reads, either switch back to `createOverlappedPipe(...)`/a named pipe here, or use a different mechanism for draining the anonymous pipe.

Removing `ConptyOutputTests.cpp` from the host unit test sources drops coverage for ConPTY output behavior, which is especially relevant to the resize/reflow quirks called out in the issue and PR notes. If these tests were migrated, please update the build to include their new location; otherwise we risk losing regression coverage for ConPTY-specific output semantics.

Similarly, removing `VtRendererTests.cpp` without an obvious replacement appears to eliminate test coverage for the VT translation path that this refactor is fundamentally changing. Since the PR moves translation logic out of `VtEngine`, can we add or point to equivalent tests for the new synchronous VT emission path before dropping these entries?

Removing the fallback `PSEUDOCONSOLE_*` flag definitions from this public/static header can break consumers building against older Windows SDKs, because `ConptyCreatePseudoConsole(..., dwFlags, ...)` still exposes these flags in its API surface. In particular, the issue/PR context mentions documenting the resize quirk, but this change removes `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK` entirely from the compatibility header. If the intent is to rely on newer SDK headers, please make sure all supported build environments include them, or keep the `#ifndef` fallbacks here (or in another shared compatibility header) so downstream callers can still compile and use/document these flags consistently.

Replacing the `#include <pcg_random.hpp>` with `#pragma once` changes this header's include dependencies. The code below uses `std::enable_if_t` and `std::is_standard_layout_v`, so unless those are guaranteed to come from another common include, this header is no longer self-contained and may break translation units that include it directly. Please ensure the required standard headers (for example `<type_traits>`) are included here explicitly.

This change bypasses `SetWindowVisibility` and emits `CSI 1 t` / `CSI 2 t` directly, but those XTWINOPS sequences are not universally supported and can be interpreted differently by terminals. Since this callback is part of the pseudo-window path, it would be safer to either preserve the previous abstraction (so terminal capability handling stays centralized) or gate this write behind an explicit ConPTY/terminal-support check. Otherwise we may regress behavior for hosts that previously ignored or handled `SetWindowVisibility` differently.

Removing the `SuppressResizeRepaint()` call here looks behaviorally significant for ConPTY resizes. The previous code explicitly suppressed repaint VT after a successful resize, which seems directly related to the known issue where enlarging the pseudo console causes the reflowed buffer to be emitted and overwrite terminal content. If this suppression is now handled elsewhere, it would be good to make that explicit in the surrounding code/comments; otherwise this change may reintroduce the resize repaint quirk instead of just refactoring.

Returning `true` here changes the previous ConPTY behavior of propagating `ED 2`/erase-all to the hosting terminal. The removed comment explains why that passthrough existed: the attached terminal needs to move its own visible contents into scrollback. With this change, `_EraseAll()` now only mutates the host-side buffer and triggers a local scroll, which risks breaking scrollback semantics in ConPTY and is directly related to the resize/reflow quirks called out in the issue/PR. Please confirm that `ED` is now emitted elsewhere for PTY output, or preserve/document the passthrough behavior here.

This now unconditionally calls `TriggerScroll`, whereas the old code intentionally skipped it in PTY mode because the `ED` sequence was also forwarded and would otherwise cause the buffer to scroll twice. If passthrough has been removed as part of the refactor, this may be correct, but it also means ConPTY no longer updates the connected terminal's scrollback the way it used to. It would be good to either add a comment explaining the new synchronization model or verify with a regression test that erase-all in ConPTY still produces the expected visible/scrollback behavior.

Previously `SetClipboard` returned `false` in ConPTY so the OSC 52 operation could be forwarded to the hosting terminal, since ConPTY itself can't service clipboard requests for the remote terminal. Returning `true` here swallows the sequence in PTY mode and changes observable behavior for terminal-side clipboard integration. If clipboard forwarding is now handled elsewhere, please point to that path; otherwise this looks like a regression.

Changing this to `return handled;` means iTerm2 marks are now consumed in ConPTY instead of being forwarded after a flush. The removed code suggests the flush was needed to keep marks aligned with the correct output line in the hosting terminal. Unless there is a new generic passthrough path for these OSC actions, this likely breaks mark placement/visibility for terminals connected via ConPTY.

Same concern as `DoITerm2Action`: this now consumes FinalTerm marks in ConPTY without the previous flush-and-forward behavior. Since these marks are terminal UI features rather than conhost features, swallowing them in PTY mode seems like a behavioral regression unless another layer now emits them synchronously.

The ConPTY-specific DRCS passthrough handler was removed, so `DownloadDRCS` now only updates local state. That means the connected terminal will never receive the `DECDLD` payload or the follow-up SCS remap, which seems incompatible with the PR goal that application-generated VT should reach the terminal unmodified. If DRCS passthrough is intentionally dropped, it should be called out as a known limitation; otherwise this needs an equivalent forwarding path.

Removing the ConPTY passthrough here means color table restore reports are now parsed locally and no longer forwarded to the attached terminal. For OSC/DCS-style terminal state restoration, the remote terminal is usually the component that actually owns the visible palette, so swallowing the sequence in PTY mode may desynchronize host-side state from terminal-side state.

The old ConPTY path flushed and returned `false` so BEL/volume requests could be handled by the hosting terminal in sync with the rendered output. With that removed, `PlaySounds` is now handled only locally, which likely means no audible bell for PTY clients and reintroduces ordering issues the flush was guarding against. Please verify that terminal bell passthrough is implemented elsewhere before consuming this sequence here.

This change appears to have replaced the `SetLookingForDSR` setter with a second getter. If the corresponding declaration/call sites still expect `SetLookingForDSR(bool)`, this will break the state machine's ability to update `_lookingForDSR` and may cause DSR handling to stop working. Was this intended to be a reordering only? If not, please keep the setter implementation here (e.g. `_lookingForDSR = looking;`) and move `EncounteredWin32InputModeSequence()` elsewhere as needed.

This now unconditionally returns `true` and ignores both the string and `flush` flag, which changes the previous behavior from "forward unknown/pass-through output to the attached terminal when available" to "silently consume it". Given the PR notes that VT output should be passed through unmodified, this looks risky unless all callers have already been reworked to guarantee that passthrough is handled elsewhere. If that migration is not complete, unsupported/unknown sequences may disappear instead of reaching the terminal.

This refactor changes the semantics of `Clamp(const Viewport&)`: the previous implementation clamped each inclusive edge independently, which preserves a degenerate 1-cell viewport when `other` lies completely outside the bounds. Using an exclusive-rect intersection (`ToExclusive() & other.ToExclusive()`) can instead produce an empty rect in those cases, so callers may observe different behavior. Please verify that all call sites expect intersection semantics rather than edge-wise clamping, or add a regression test for fully out-of-bounds/zero-sized inputs before switching to this implementation.

Removing `--resizeQuirk` from the conhost command line appears to drop the only propagation path for `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK` from the public API into the headless host. Given the linked issue is specifically about documenting/preserving this ConPTY resize behavior, this change may silently ignore callers that still set that flag unless the behavior was moved elsewhere in this PR. Please either keep forwarding the flag, remove/deprecate the API surface in sync, or add a compatibility note/test proving the flag is intentionally obsolete.

This change removes the local fallback definition for `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK` and replaces it with `PSEUDOCONSOLE_WIN32_INPUT_MODE`. Given the issue/PR context is specifically about documenting the resize quirk, dropping the resize flag from this compatibility header can break consumers building against older SDKs that still rely on the constant being available here. If the intent is to add the new flag, consider keeping the `PSEUDOCONSOLE_RESIZE_QUIRK` fallback as well (with its original value) instead of replacing it, so both old and new flags remain available.

This boundary check uses `result.y > 0`, but elsewhere in this function you compare against `bufferSize.Origin()`, which suggests the viewport/selection origin may not always be `(0, 0)`. If `bufferSize.Top()` can be non-zero, this can read the wrong prior row or allow wrapping past the top of the bounded region. Consider checking against the buffer's top bound instead (for example `result.y > bufferSize.Top()`) before calling `GetRowByOffset(result.y - 1)`.

This test mutates the buffer state manually to work around `WriteLinesToBuffer` (`SetWrapForced(false); // Ugh`). That makes the test brittle because it now depends on an implementation quirk unrelated to the behavior under test, and future changes to the helper could silently invalidate these expectations. If possible, prefer constructing the buffer contents in a way that produces the intended wrap flags naturally, or add a dedicated helper for setting up wrapped/non-wrapped rows explicitly so the test setup is self-describing.

The expected word end here points to `{ 0, 6 }`, which is one row past the resized buffer height of 6 (`0..5`). If `GetWordEnd` intentionally returns an exclusive end position on the next line, it would be worth documenting that in the test because this is easy to misread as an out-of-bounds expectation. Otherwise, this may indicate the test is asserting a coordinate outside the valid buffer range.

Switching `_ignoredNamespaces` from `std::wstring_view` to owning `winrt::hstring` avoids lifetime issues, but it also changes the lookup contract. Please verify all call sites still use heterogeneous lookup-compatible key types (`hstring`, `std::wstring_view`, or `wchar_t*`) and that this header has the necessary includes for `til::transparent_hstring_hash` / `til::transparent_hstring_equal_to`; otherwise this can introduce compile or behavior regressions in namespace filtering.

This reads `DisabledProfileSources` into a fixed 512-wide-char buffer, which can silently fail for larger `REG_MULTI_SZ` values. In an enterprise policy scenario, admins may disable multiple sources and exceed this size. Consider querying the required size first (or handling `ERROR_MORE_DATA`) and allocating dynamically so all configured entries are honored.

Using an abbreviated function template (`const auto&`) in a header utility like this may be a portability issue if this code is still built under C++17 in any configuration. If the project standard isn't guaranteed to be C++20 everywhere, consider rewriting these call operators as explicit function templates instead.

Capturing `terminalPage` by value here can keep a stale page instance alive after `_root` has been replaced or torn down, and the async callback may then show the dialog on an object that is no longer the active UI. Since `App` already stores the current root, it would be safer to resolve the page inside the dispatched lambda (and optionally verify `_root` is still valid) before calling `ShowOkDialog`.

`_OnLoaded` is now the only remaining UI/page-lifecycle handler declared on `App`, while the PR description says tab management, key bindings, clipboard, dialogs, and most terminal UI responsibilities have moved to `TerminalPage`. Keeping this handler here risks leaving `App` coupled to page initialization and can make the ownership boundary ambiguous again. If this is only forwarding to `TerminalPage`, consider moving the handler itself there (or documenting why it must remain on `App`) so the separation introduced by this refactor stays clear.

`SetSettings` now overwrites `_settings` unconditionally, whereas the previous implementation only allowed it to be set once. Since `Create()` and many event handlers dereference `_settings`, reassigning it after initialization could leave the page in an inconsistent state or rebind it to a different settings object unexpectedly. If reassignment is intentional, it would be safer to also refresh dependent state immediately; otherwise consider preserving the original guard or asserting that this is only called once.

Registering the TraceLogging provider every time `Create()` is called can lead to duplicate registration / lifetime mismatches, especially since unregistration only happens in the destructor. If `Create()` can be invoked more than once for a page instance, consider guarding this call or moving registration to construction so the register/unregister pair is clearly one-time.

This dereferences the optional without checking it first. `GetFocusedProfile()` is treated as optional elsewhere in this file (`_UpdateTabIcon` checks `has_value()`), so if a tab temporarily has no focused profile this will throw/crash during title updates. Please guard the optional before calling `.value()` here as well.

`FindProfile` can return `nullptr` (you already check for that in `_CreateNewTabFromSettings` and `_UpdateTabIcon`), but this line dereferences the result unconditionally. After a settings reload or profile removal, a tab could still reference a profile GUID that no longer exists. Please null-check `matchingProfile` and fall back to the terminal title when the profile is missing.

This indexes `_tabs` without validating the result of `_GetFocusedTabIndex()`. Several callers (`GetTitle`, copy/paste, scrolling, etc.) can reach here when no tab is selected, in which case `_GetFocusedTabIndex()` returns `-1` and this becomes undefined behavior. Consider returning `nullptr`/default `TermControl` when no tab is focused and making callers handle that case.

The return value of `IndexOf` is ignored here. If `tabViewItem` is not found (for example due to a stale event firing after the item was already removed), `tabIndexFromControl` remains `0` and we'll erase the wrong tab. Please check the boolean result from `IndexOf` before erasing/removing.

Adding a non-default destructor here is a potential lifetime hazard unless the implementation explicitly unregisters all XAML/WinRT event handlers and cancels any outstanding `fire_and_forget` work. This class now owns a lot more UI/event state, so if `~TerminalPage()` is empty or incomplete, callbacks can outlive the page and access freed state. Please make sure the destructor is actually needed and that it performs deterministic cleanup for the handlers introduced by this refactor.

Exposing the internal `TabRowControl` as a raw pointer to work around event wiring leaks implementation details back out of `TerminalPage` and weakens the boundary this PR is trying to establish. It also creates ownership/lifetime ambiguity for callers. If possible, prefer forwarding the needed events from `TerminalPage` itself or exposing a safer WinRT/reference type rather than a raw internal pointer.

Importing `..\App.idl` here creates a new IDL dependency from `TerminalPage` back to `App`, which works against the goal of separating page-level terminal management from the application singleton. Since the only new app-specific type referenced below is `LastTabClosedEventArgs`, consider moving that event args type into a page-local/shared IDL (or defining a page-owned event contract) so `TerminalPage` doesn't need to depend on `App.idl`.

This cast assumes `_app.GetRoot()` is always a `TerminalPage`, but the PR description notes this is a temporary workaround while event wiring is still in flux. If the root content changes or initialization order shifts, `.as<winrt::TerminalApp::TerminalPage>()` can fail at runtime. Consider either exposing these events from `App` as a stable forwarding surface, or at least using `try_as`/null-checking here so startup doesn't hard-fail on an unexpected root element.

Calling `OnSize` directly from `WM_DPICHANGED` can cause the layout update to run twice, because Windows typically sends `WM_SIZE` as part of the same resize/snap flow. If `OnSize` has any side effects beyond pure layout, this may introduce redundant work or ordering-sensitive behavior. Consider either guarding this path so it only forces the DPI-dependent recalculation, or documenting why double invocation is safe here.

Capturing `this` directly in the render engine callback can become unsafe if the engine outlives `ControlCore` or invokes the callback during teardown. Since this callback is now registered earlier and may fire from more code paths, it would be safer to either unregister it in destruction/reset paths or capture a weak reference/lifetime-safe handle before calling `_rendererWarning`.

Changing `_rendererWarning`'s signature here introduces a new required `parameter` argument, but this header alone doesn't show whether all existing call sites and the corresponding definition were updated. Since this callback likely has multiple emitters, it's easy to miss one and break the build or lose warning context. Please verify the implementation and every invocation are updated consistently, and consider a default value or overload if some warnings don't have an associated parameter.

Changing `Result` from `uint64_t` to `HRESULT` is a good semantic improvement, but this is a WinRT event arg type and the ABI-visible property type change can break existing consumers that were compiled against the old signature. Please verify that all IDL/projection definitions and any external consumers are updated accordingly, or consider preserving the old property shape if binary compatibility is required.

Changing this property from `UInt64` to `HRESULT` is a WinRT API surface change. If `RendererWarningArgs` is consumed outside this component, this can break ABI/contracts and may require versioning or at least auditing all projections/callers for the new type. Please confirm that all existing consumers expect a signed 32-bit HRESULT rather than the previous 64-bit value.

Adding `Parameter` here makes sense for reporting invalid font names, but because this is a generic `RendererWarningArgs` type, a singular `String` may be too limiting if multiple invalid font families need to be reported (the PR description mentions listing all invalid font names). Consider whether this should be a collection or a more descriptive payload to avoid losing information when more than one parameter is involved.

The new message drops the previous fallback-font information (`"{1}" has been selected instead.`), which was useful context for users when some requested fonts are missing. If the runtime still knows which font was ultimately chosen, consider preserving that in the string; otherwise the warning becomes less actionable, especially now that fallback configuration is the feature being added. Also, the sentence reads awkwardly: `install the missing` appears to be missing the word `fonts`.

Consider checking the return value of `FormatMessageW` before constructing `msg`. If it returns 0, `len` will be zero and the formatted warning will lose the system error text entirely. A small fallback (for example, omitting `msg` or using a fixed placeholder) would make the default error path more robust, especially for HRESULTs that don't have a system message.

This dereferences the result of `query<IDWriteFont1>()` without checking whether the cast succeeded. On systems where the returned font object doesn't implement `IDWriteFont1`, this will crash before we can surface a warning.
