Please guard the query result (or use a helper that enforces/handles the interface requirement) before calling `IsMonospacedFont()`.

Creating a new DirectWrite factory and fetching the system font collection on every font-face refresh is fairly expensive, especially since this runs during initialization and whenever the font text changes/loses focus. Consider reusing an existing factory/font collection or caching them at the view-model level to avoid repeated COM setup and system font enumeration.

This lookup is now known to be incompatible with comma-separated fallback specs (as the TODO notes), but returning early here means the axes/features UI won't be refreshed/cleared consistently for valid fallback input. That can leave stale state from the previous font selection. Please either parse the primary font before lookup or explicitly clear/update the dependent UI state when the full spec can't be resolved.

Adding a custom `FontFace()` getter/setter pair here while removing the `OBSERVABLE_PROJECTED_SETTING(..., FontFace)` macro changes the notification behavior for this property. Please make sure the implementation raises `PropertyChanged` for `FontFace` and any dependent properties/warnings (`HasPowerlineCharacters`, missing/proportional font warnings, filtered suggestions, etc.), otherwise the UI may stop updating when the font list is edited.

`HasPowerlineCharacters` is now a standalone observable property on `AppearanceViewModel`, but the previous `Font::HasPowerlineCharacters()` API was removed in this hunk. Please verify this value is recomputed whenever `FontFace` changes (including fallback lists like `"Consolas, MS Gothic"`), otherwise the warning state can become stale or regress from the previous behavior.

Storing only a single `_primaryFontName` may be insufficient now that `font-family`-style fallback lists are supported. If this field is later used for validation, warning messages, or glyph capability checks, secondary fallback fonts will be ignored and the UI could report false missing/proportional/Powerline warnings. Consider either storing the parsed list or naming/documenting this field to make the single-font limitation explicit.

Binding the `AutoSuggestBox.Text` with `Mode=OneWay` means edits typed by the user won't flow back through the binding, so the view model/property won't update unless every path is handled manually in code-behind. Since this control is now the primary editor for `fontFace` (including comma-separated fallback lists), consider using `Mode=TwoWay` or explicitly documenting/ensuring that `TextChanged`, `SuggestionChosen`, and focus handlers keep `Appearance.FontFace` synchronized in all cases.

This now reads `HasPowerlineCharacters()` directly from `DefaultAppearance`, but the old implementation derived the value from the currently selected font by looking it up in the font list. If `HasPowerlineCharacters` is not recomputed whenever `FontFace` changes—especially now that `FontFace` can contain a fallback list like `Consolas, MS Gothic`—the preview can become stale or incorrect. Please make sure this property is updated from the parsed font-family list (including fallback fonts) before using it here, or keep the preview logic tied to the effective font selection.

Using a cached `HasPowerlineCharacters()` value here changes behavior from the previous on-demand font lookup. Given this PR adds multi-font fallback support, a single boolean on `DefaultAppearance` may not reflect the effective result when the primary font lacks Powerline glyphs but a fallback font provides them. Consider recalculating this based on the resolved fallback chain when profile properties change, otherwise the preview may not match actual rendering.

Changing this resource from a full warning sentence to a label-only string looks like a behavioral/UI contract change. If the existing info bar still binds to a `*.Message` resource for its body text, renaming/repurposing the old key to `*.Title` may leave the message missing or duplicated unless the corresponding XAML/viewmodel was updated everywhere. Please verify that all consumers were migrated and that a dedicated `Profile_FontFace_ProportionalFontWarning.Message` resource is no longer required.

Removing `noexcept` from the generated getter changes the exception contract for every property using this macro. The body still appears to be a simple projection (`return target.name();`), so unless `target.name()` can now legitimately throw, this broad API change may be unnecessary and could affect callers that rely on these accessors being non-throwing. If the goal here is only to make a local copy in the setter/clearer, consider keeping `noexcept` on the getter (and similarly on other trivially forwarding accessors) or documenting why these projections are no longer safe to mark as non-throwing.

Setting `backspace = true` here causes the backslash itself to be dropped from the output, but the next iteration still appends the escaped character verbatim. That means inputs like `"` or `\` lose the `\`, which is fine for CSS-style escaping, but it also means a trailing backslash at end-of-input is silently discarded. Consider handling the unterminated escape explicitly so malformed input doesn't get normalized into a different font name without any warning.

This warning is emitted whenever any fallback family is missing, even if a valid primary font was resolved and rendering can proceed normally. That may be noisy for users who intentionally provide optional fallback fonts. Consider either downgrading this to only warn when no usable font was found at all, or include enough context in the callback/message to distinguish between a fatal primary-font failure and a non-fatal missing fallback entry.

Appending the system fallback after the custom mappings means the user-specified fallback list takes precedence for all Unicode ranges, which is probably intended. However, because each custom mapping uses the full Unicode range, later custom families may never be reached if an earlier fallback family claims broad coverage. If the goal is ordered fallback across the provided list, please verify DirectWrite's mapping resolution here or add a comment/test to lock in the expected precedence behavior.

This constructor now initializes `systemFontFallback` on `_api`, but the later call sites in this hunk were switched to `_p.s->font->fontFallback` / `fontFallback1` instead. If `_api.systemFontFallback` is no longer used, keeping this initialization here risks leaving duplicate or stale fallback state around. Please either remove this assignment if the per-font fallback fully replaces it, or add/verify the code path that seeds `_p.s->font->fontFallback` from the system fallback so the renderer still has a valid fallback object before `_mapCharacters()` is called.

Returning `ShadingType::Default` here can leave `_drawBuiltinGlyph` reporting a normal text glyph even though nothing was actually rendered for this soft-font codepoint. That may cause the atlas entry to be cached as a valid glyph and suppress fallback rendering or replacement-glyph behavior. Consider signaling failure explicitly (or avoiding atlas entry creation) when `softFontPattern` is empty, so accidental U+EF2x input doesn't get treated as a successfully drawn glyph.

Changing `_drawSoftFontGlyph` to return `ShadingType` suggests callers now depend on the glyph's shading mode, but this declaration alone doesn't show whether all call sites and overrides were updated consistently. Please verify the corresponding definition and every invocation handle the returned value; otherwise it's easy to introduce a silent mismatch where soft-font glyphs still use an old/default shading path or trigger a signature inconsistency at compile time.

Changing `warningCallback` from `std::function<void(HRESULT)>` to `std::function<void(HRESULT, wil::zwstring_view)>` introduces a non-owning string view into a callback that may be stored or invoked asynchronously. Please make sure every call site only passes data whose lifetime extends through the callback invocation, or consider using an owning type here (for example `std::wstring`) if the warning text can be constructed on the fly. Otherwise this can easily become a dangling-view bug.

Changing the callback signature in the interface is a breaking API change for every `IRenderEngine` implementation and call site. Since this is a virtual method with a default body, any derived class that still overrides `SetWarningCallback(std::function<void(HRESULT)>)` will silently stop overriding unless updated, which can be easy to miss. Please make sure all implementations use `override` and are updated in the same change, or consider preserving backward compatibility with an overload/adapter if external consumers implement this interface.

Declaring this lambda `static constexpr` is risky here because it returns a `std::vector<std::wstring>`, which is not generally a constexpr-friendly type across the project's supported C++/STL configurations. Even if this happens to compile with a newer toolchain, it unnecessarily tightens the language/library requirements for a unit test. Consider dropping `constexpr` (and likely `static` as well) and keeping it as a plain local lambda.

This registry path looks incorrect for enumerating installed fonts. `Get-ItemProperty "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Fonts\*"` targets subkeys under `Fonts`, but the font registrations are typically values on the `Fonts` key itself (and often under `HKLM`, not `HKCU`). As written, this is likely to return nothing on most systems, so the script will only rely on the hardcoded fallback list. Consider querying the `Fonts` key directly and including the machine-wide location as well.

Re-enabling `NotifyFocusLeave()` directly here looks risky given the linked issue history: this call was previously disabled specifically because it caused the IME candidate UI to stop appearing. Since the PR description only validates the text-reset fix, not the focus-leave behavior, this change may reintroduce #3645/#1919. Please either keep this guarded behind a proven condition, add a regression test/repro validation for focus transitions with IME, or document why the previous failure mode no longer applies.

This restores the title propagation, but the lambda still captures `this` strongly. If a `TermControl` can outlive `TerminalPage` or fire `TitleChanged` during teardown, this risks invoking `_UpdateTitle` on a destroyed page. Consider registering this with a revoker or capturing a weak reference to `TerminalPage` as well, so the callback is safely ignored once the page is gone.

This now calls `TextBox().Focus(...)` unconditionally once `OriginalSource() == *this`, whereas the previous implementation guarded against a missing textbox with `if (TextBox())`. If `OnGotFocus` can fire before the template/child controls are fully realized, this could dereference a null control. Consider keeping the null check (or otherwise guaranteeing the textbox exists here) before calling `Focus()`/`SelectAll()`.

Adding `OnGotFocus` here changes the public surface of `SearchBoxControl`, but this hunk also removes `SetFocusOnTextbox()` and `ContainsFocus()`. If any remaining callers in `TermControl` or tests still depend on those methods, this will break the build or leave focus behavior partially migrated. Please verify all call sites are updated in the same change and that `OnGotFocus` fully replaces the old explicit-focus path.

This focus funnel only redirects when `OriginalSource() == *this`. That works for direct focus on `TermControl`, but it also means any future focus path that lands on another non-input child of the control won't be redirected. Given this PR is reworking focus ownership, it may be safer to explicitly ensure focus ends up on `TerminalInputTarget()` whenever the terminal surface itself or any non-interactive descendant receives focus, otherwise keyboard input could be lost in edge cases.

Switching from the private `SetFocusOnTextbox()` helper to `SearchBox().Focus(...)` changes behavior unless `SearchBoxControl` now forwards focus to its text box in all cases. If that forwarding isn't guaranteed, opening the search UI may focus the container instead of the editable field, which would regress typing/select-all behavior described in the PR. It would be good to confirm that `SearchBoxControl::OnGotFocus` handles this before removing the explicit textbox focus call.

Removing the early return here means `_focused` is set and the terminal/UIA path continues even when the search box owns focus. The old code explicitly avoided enabling terminal input/cursor blinking while the search box had focus. If the new `TerminalInputTarget` model fully prevents TSF/IME interception and cursor activation, that's fine, but this line is where the previous guard was removed, so it would be worth validating that search-box focus no longer causes the terminal to appear focused or consume IME state.

Placing a `UserControl` directly inside `SwapChainPanel` is risky: `SwapChainPanel` is not a general-purpose content host, and in WinUI/UWP it typically doesn't support arbitrary child content the way `Grid`/`Canvas` do. If this is intended purely as a focus target, it may be safer to overlay the focusable element as a sibling in the surrounding `Grid` instead of nesting it here, otherwise this can fail at runtime or behave inconsistently across XAML platforms.

Please verify that the actual source file was renamed to `TfEditSession.cpp` and that all references were updated consistently. This `sources` file drives the build, so if the repository still contains `TfEditses.cpp` (or the corresponding header/class names still use the old abbreviation), this will break the TSF project build on CI.

Dropping the `removeSpaces` guard changes behavior for every `_newBottomLine` case that reaches this branch, and now spaces will be emitted whenever `numSpaces > 0`. The PR description says the fix is to `removeSpaces` only on the actual bottom line, but this line no longer reflects that intent explicitly. If `numSpaces` can be non-zero when `removeSpaces` is false, this will still write trailing spaces unexpectedly. Consider keeping the condition aligned with the semantic flag (or documenting/asserting that `numSpaces > 0` is only possible when removal is intended here).

Using `name.data()` to populate a `VT_LPWSTR` `PROPVARIANT` assumes the `std::wstring_view` is null-terminated, but `wstring_view` does not guarantee that. `IPropertyStore::SetValue` expects a valid null-terminated string for `VT_LPWSTR`, so this can read past the view bounds if the caller passes a non-null-terminated slice. Consider constructing the property with an owning/null-terminated string (for example via `InitPropVariantFromString`) before calling `SetValue`.

Same concern here: `path` is a `std::wstring_view`, and assigning `path.data()` into a `VT_LPWSTR` `PROPVARIANT` relies on null termination that `wstring_view` does not guarantee. This can produce invalid reads when `SetValue` consumes the string. Prefer creating a real null-terminated buffer or using a helper like `InitPropVariantFromString`.

Marking `UpdateJumplist` as `noexcept` is risky unless the implementation catches and converts every possible exception to an `HRESULT`. This code interacts with WinRT/COM types (`CascadiaSettings`, profiles, shell link creation), and any uncaught exception would now terminate the process instead of propagating to the caller. Please verify the `.cpp` implementation is fully exception-safe under this contract, or avoid `noexcept` here.

Adding a new persisted registry key here is only one part of the feature. Please make sure `CustomGlyphs` is also wired through the rest of the console settings pipeline (defaults, serialization/deserialization, profile/global settings mapping, and any migration/compat handling with the PR-described `builtinGlyphs` setting). Otherwise this define becomes dead config surface or may introduce naming inconsistencies between Terminal JSON (`builtinGlyphs`) and the legacy registry key (`CustomGlyphs`).

This unconditionally overwrites the `customGlyphs` value on every `UpdateFont()` call with the global console setting. Since the feature is described as per-profile/font configuration (`builtinGlyphs` on the font object), this can accidentally discard the value already carried by `pfiNewFont`/`fiDesiredFont` and make later font updates ignore profile-specific state. Consider preserving the flag from `pfiNewFont` when it is already set there, or ensure the source of truth is clearly the same object that produced `pfiNewFont`.

Conditionally adding this registry mapping behind `TIL_FEATURE_CONHOSTATLASENGINE_ENABLED` can make persistence behavior build-dependent: a value written by one build may be silently ignored by another, and the setting may disappear from serialization entirely when the feature flag is off. If this is intended to be a user-facing compatibility setting, consider keeping the registry key serialized unconditionally and gating only its runtime use, so settings remain stable across feature-flag/configuration changes.

This condition no longer reads from the profile setting directly and now depends on `_hackWantsCustomGlyphs`, but in this hunk there’s no indication that `_hackWantsCustomGlyphs` is synchronized with `font->customGlyphs`/`builtinGlyphs` on every settings change. If that cache is stale, `_flushBufferLine()` can take the wrong path and either skip custom glyph rendering or keep using it after the setting is toggled. Please make sure this flag is updated as part of settings application and invalidates/redraws appropriately when it changes.

Defaulting `_hackWantsCustomGlyphs` to `true` means AtlasEngine will initially shape text as if builtin/custom glyph rendering is available, even though the comment says `BackendD2D` does not support it. If the first active backend is D2D, this can produce an incorrect initial frame until the backend switch path flips the flag and forces a redraw. Consider initializing this from the actual backend capability (or to the conservative `false`) so the first render doesn't depend on a later corrective redraw.

Setting `_hackTriggerRedrawAll` here makes `RequiresContinuousRedraw()` return `true`, but in this hunk there’s no corresponding reset back to `false`. If this flag isn’t cleared after the full redraw completes, the renderer may stay in continuous redraw mode indefinitely and waste CPU/GPU. Can we either clear it immediately after the next full-frame invalidation/render, or use an existing one-shot invalidation path instead of a persistent `RequiresContinuousRedraw()` trigger?

This now creates a new `ID2D1Bitmap`/`ID2D1BitmapBrush` every time a shaded block glyph is drawn. Since these glyphs can appear very frequently (e.g. shaded block elements used for progress bars/backgrounds), this may introduce significant per-glyph allocation overhead on the render path. Consider caching one brush per shade level per device context, or reusing a shared bitmap/brush, instead of recreating it for every rectangle.

Using `glyphIndices` to sometimes store glyph indices and sometimes raw UTF-16 code units makes the container's contract ambiguous and easy to misuse later, especially since the type remains `std::vector<u16>` and the name still implies DWrite glyph IDs. This is particularly risky for non-BMP/custom glyph cases where a single Unicode scalar may require surrogate pairs and no longer map 1:1 to a `u16`. Consider introducing an explicit tagged representation (or at least renaming/documenting this field more strongly and asserting the expected range) so downstream code can't accidentally interpret UTF-16 data as glyph IDs.

`_desiredFont.SetCustomGlyphs(_customGlyphs);` is only applied in this font-update path, while `_customGlyphs` is populated earlier from settings. Can you confirm that toggling `customGlyphs` at runtime always forces this code path to run? Otherwise the renderer may keep using the previous font settings until some unrelated font change occurs. If that’s possible, we should either include `customGlyphs` in the font-dirty/reload criteria or apply it wherever the render font is rebuilt.

This introduces a new persisted behavior flag with a default of `true`, but the naming here (`_customGlyphs`) doesn't match the setting names described in the issue/PR (`builtinBlockDrawing` / `builtinGlyphs`). That mismatch makes it harder to trace config flow and increases the risk of wiring the wrong property or confusing future maintainers. Consider renaming this field to align with the public setting name, or add clear comments if this is intentionally a different internal concept.

This adds a new member to the WinRT interface, which is a versioned public contract. Please make sure the interface versioning/UUID story is handled correctly (for example by introducing a new interface revision if required by this project’s WinRT patterns) so existing consumers don’t break when the vtable layout changes.

The new projected setting name here (`CustomGlyphs`) doesn't appear to match the setting name described in the issue/PR (`builtinBlockDrawing` / `builtinGlyphs`). If the underlying model/property was intentionally renamed, please make sure the editor XAML, serialization layer, and localized strings use the same name; otherwise this can easily lead to a UI binding that compiles but doesn't expose the intended setting consistently.

The new projected setting name here (`CustomGlyphs`) doesn't match the naming described in the issue/PR (`builtinBlockDrawing` / `builtinGlyphs`). If this is intentional, please make sure the IDL property name is aligned with the underlying settings model and JSON schema, otherwise the editor/viewmodel can drift from the actual profile property and create confusing UI/serialization behavior.

The comment here still says `Retro Terminal Effect`, but this block is for the new `Profile_CustomGlyphs` setting. Keeping the old label is misleading for future maintenance and makes the XAML harder to scan. Please update the comment to match the setting being introduced.

The help text says this only applies to "block element and box drawing characters", but the PR/issue also adds support for basic Powerline glyphs. This string will be user-facing documentation for the setting, so it should mention Powerline as well (or use a broader term like "certain box drawing, block element, and Powerline characters") to avoid underselling what the toggle actually does.

The new setting name here (`CustomGlyphs`) doesn't match the naming described in the issue/PR (`builtinBlockDrawing` / `builtinGlyphs`). Since this IDL becomes part of the public settings surface, introducing a third name risks confusion in serialization, docs, and user-facing JSON. Please align this property name with the intended external setting name before it ships, or document the mapping very explicitly if the mismatch is intentional.

The new setting name here (`customGlyphs`) doesn't match the naming described in the issue/PR (`builtinBlockDrawing` / `builtinGlyphs`). Since this string becomes part of the public settings schema, renaming later would be a breaking change for users and docs. Please make sure the JSON key and property name are aligned with the intended public setting before merging.

The new setting name here (`CustomGlyphs`) doesn't match the naming described in the issue/PR (`builtinBlockDrawing` / `builtinGlyphs`) or the validation note (`customGlyphs`). Since this is part of the public settings model, inconsistent naming will leak into JSON serialization, docs, and migration behavior. Please align the property name with the intended user-facing schema and make sure the default `true` value is an intentional compatibility choice, because enabling replacement rendering by default can change the appearance for fonts that provide their own styled box-drawing glyphs.

The new property name here (`CustomGlyphs`) doesn't match the naming used in the issue/PR description (`builtinBlockDrawing` / `builtinGlyphs`). Since this is part of the public control/settings surface, introducing a different term will make the feature harder to discover and can create confusion across the model, serialization, and docs. Consider aligning the property name with the chosen user-facing setting name before this ships.

This now returns a pointer to the inserted slot, but the implementation still calls `Traits::assign(slot, key)` instead of forwarding `key`. That drops move semantics for rvalues and can be a functional problem if `Traits::assign` is specialized to distinguish lvalue/rvalue assignment. Consider passing `std::forward<U>(key)` here (and updating the trait signature accordingly) to preserve the previous behavior.

Using unconstrained `auto` here makes this helper accept any arithmetic type, including signed/narrow types, which can change the bitwise/shift behavior through integral promotions and make invalid calls compile silently. Since surrogate handling is UTF-16-specific, it would be safer to constrain this to `wchar_t`/`char16_t` (or at least an unsigned 16-bit-like type) and consider marking it `noexcept` like the neighboring helpers.

Using the user locale here changes text shaping/number substitution behavior compared to the previous invariant `L""` locale. Since this path is also used to probe glyph/layout information, locale-sensitive fallback or shaping could make results differ across users and affect reproducibility. If the intent is only to improve font fallback for specific scripts, it would be safer to document that explicitly or limit the locale usage to the cases that need it.

Reinterpreting `_api.bufferLine.data()` as `u16*` is unsafe here. `bufferLine` is iterated above as UTF-16 code units that may include surrogate pairs, but `_mapCustomGlyphs` computes `len = offEnd - offBeg` in code units and then copies those raw 16-bit units directly into `glyphIndices`. For non-BMP custom glyphs this will emit two glyph indices/advances/colors for a single codepoint, which won't match the segmentation logic in `_flushBufferLine()`. It would be safer to decode codepoints here as well (or constrain custom glyph handling to BMP-only and assert that explicitly) instead of relying on a `reinterpret_cast` of the underlying storage.

When combining surrogate pairs for custom glyph handling, `x` is not advanced to skip the trailing surrogate. That means the low surrogate at `row->glyphIndices[x + 1]` will be processed again on the next loop iteration as a separate glyph. If `glyphsTo` can contain UTF-16 code units here, this should increment `x` (or otherwise consume both code units) after `combine_surrogates` to avoid duplicate/invalid rendering.

This reads `row->glyphIndices[x + 1]` without a visible bounds check. If a malformed or truncated surrogate pair reaches this path at the end of the mapping range, this will access past `glyphsTo`. Please guard `x + 1 < glyphsTo` and validate that the next code unit is a trailing surrogate before combining.

`glyphIndex` was widened to `u32` here, but the atlas entry key and hash trait still use `u16` (`AtlasGlyphEntry::glyphIndex` and `AtlasGlyphEntryHashTrait`). If `_drawGlyph` can now receive non-font glyph IDs such as custom/builtin glyph markers or larger DWrite glyph indices, this will silently truncate during lookup/insertion and can cause collisions or incorrect cache hits. Please make the stored key type consistent with this API, or explicitly validate/narrow before inserting into the atlas map.

The new `_glyphAtlasMap` key is only `IDWriteFontFace2*`, while the previous design also keyed by `LineRendition`. The comment above `glyphs[4]` suggests rendition is now handled inside each font-face entry, but that means all call sites must consistently index the correct slot. Please double-check that initialization/lookup paths no longer assume a single `glyphs` set per face, otherwise glyphs from different renditions can be mixed or missed.

Creating a new `ID2D1Bitmap` and `ID2D1BitmapBrush` every time we draw a shaded block glyph is likely to be very expensive in the hot rendering path. Since the shade patterns are static (only 3 variants), consider caching these bitmaps/brushes per device context or otherwise reusing them instead of recreating them for every glyph draw.

`DrawCustomGlyph` is declared without `noexcept`, while the adjacent helpers in this header are explicitly marked `noexcept`. If this function is expected to be called from the rendering hot path and only uses HRESULT/COM-style error handling, it would be safer to mark that contract here as well (or document why it may throw). Otherwise we end up with inconsistent exception guarantees in this API surface.

This new field defaults to `false`, but the PR description says the new builtin/custom glyph behavior should default to `true`. If this struct is used as the source of default generation settings, this will silently disable the feature unless every caller explicitly overrides it. Please verify the intended default here and keep the naming/default aligned with the user-facing setting semantics.

The new field defaults to `false`, but the PR description says the new `builtinGlyphs`/custom glyph behavior should default to `true`. If `FontInfoDesired` is used as the source of truth before settings are applied, this default may silently disable the feature for callers that don't explicitly set it. Please verify the intended default here and align it with the documented behavior.

The JSON key here is defined as `"command"`, but the surrounding comments and the issue/PR context still describe this field as `"action"`. If existing settings/docs/tests are still using `action`, this change will silently stop deserializing those commands and treat them as unbound. Please either keep backward compatibility by accepting both keys, or update the comments/tests/docs in the same change so the contract is unambiguous.

Removing `IconPath` from the `Command` model is a breaking API change for any XAML bindings or code paths that still expect commands to expose an icon. Given this PR is focused on key styling in the command palette, can we confirm the corresponding UI/templates and any serialization or tests were updated as well? If icons are no longer supported, it would be helpful to clean up all remaining references in the command palette view model/XAML in the same change to avoid binding failures or silent regressions.

Changing these shared direction resource strings to lowercase can have unintended side effects outside the command palette. `DirectionLeft/Right/Up/Down` look like generic resources that may also be used in command names, tooltips, or other UI where sentence casing was previously expected. If the goal is only to style key labels like `left`/`right` in the palette, it would be safer to either add palette-specific resources or handle casing in the palette UI layer so other consumers don't regress.

Removing `CommandSerialization.cpp` from the project file will stop it from being compiled into `TerminalAppLib`. If this file still contains code used by the command palette or settings/model serialization paths, this will either break linking or silently drop functionality. Please confirm the implementation was intentionally moved/deleted and that all references/tests were updated accordingly.

This branch should likely remain `else if`. As written, a C0 control character or DEL will first be ignored above, but then still continue into the parameter/intermediate/pass-through checks below because the chain is broken. For example, a C0 byte could be both ignored and then passed to `_ActionDcsPassThrough(wch)`, which doesn't match the state comment or the parser behavior in the other handlers.

This test now only verifies the `:` -> `DcsIgnore` path before terminating the DCS. That means we no longer cover the previously tested case where a valid `DcsParam` sequence can terminate directly with `ST`/`ESC \\` and return to `Ground` while preserving parsed parameters. Since the PR description says DCS parameter parsing is supported, it would be safer to keep an assertion for the normal termination path as well and add this ignore-path as an additional case, rather than replacing it.

This compares `resultPos` against a fresh `GetSize().EndExclusive()` even though `bufferSize` was just cached above. Using `bufferSize.EndExclusive()` here would avoid recomputing and keep the function consistent if `GetSize()` ever becomes more expensive or its result is expected to be used atomically within the method.

Removing `allowBottomExclusive` from the declaration changes the public interface of `TextBuffer`. If any out-of-line definition or call sites still use the old signature, this will either fail to compile or silently change behavior around exclusive-end positions. Given this PR is specifically about movement at the end of the buffer, please verify the implementation and all callers were updated consistently and that backward movement from an end-exclusive position is still handled intentionally.

This commented-out debugging block looks like leftover temporary code. Keeping `DebugBreak()` comments in a test makes the intent noisier and can easily get re-enabled accidentally later. Please remove these lines before merging.

Passing a hard-coded `true` here changes the backward line-movement semantics independently of the caller's `allowBottomExclusive` setting. That may be intentional for the end-exclusive fix, but it also means this path can now decrement into the bottom-exclusive position even when the caller explicitly disallows it, which risks inconsistent behavior with the forward path and other unit-movement helpers. If the new behavior is required, consider documenting why `allowBottomExclusive` is intentionally ignored here or constraining it to the specific end-exclusive case instead of applying it unconditionally.

This only fail-fasts when `GetProcAddress` fails, but `LoadLibraryExW` can also fail. In that case `module.get()` is null and `GetProcAddress` will set a different last-error, which makes the crash reason misleading and harder to diagnose. Consider validating `module` immediately after `LoadLibraryExW` (for example with `FAIL_FAST_LAST_ERROR_IF(!module)`) before calling `GetProcAddress`.

The return value from `SystemFunction036` is ignored here. Since the API reports success/failure via its `BOOLEAN` return, `gen_random` can silently return uninitialized/partially initialized data if the call fails. Please check the result and fail or propagate an error instead of assuming success.

Removing the `unvirtualizedResources` restricted capability (and the matching `desktop6:RegistryWriteVirtualization` opt-out above) looks risky for this feature. The PR description says the app now edits the default-terminal registration and specifically needs to opt out of registry redirection so the setting is written to the real registry location. Without these manifest entries, packaged runs may write to a virtualized location and the selected default terminal may not actually take effect outside the app. Was this removal intentional, or should these declarations remain in the dev manifest as well?

Removing the `unvirtualizedResources` restricted capability and the `desktop6:RegistryWriteVirtualization` opt-out looks risky for this feature. The PR description says Terminal needs to edit the default-terminal setting in the real registry (not a virtualized view). Without these manifest entries, packaged writes may be redirected again, which would make the UI appear to save successfully while not actually changing the system-wide default terminal. Please confirm this is intentionally moved elsewhere or retained in another manifest variant; otherwise this change can break the core functionality introduced by the PR.

Removing the `unvirtualizedResources` restricted capability appears to conflict with the PR description, which says the manifest was updated specifically to opt out of registry redirection so Terminal can persist the default-terminal setting. Without this capability (and the related `RegistryWriteVirtualization` property), writes may be virtualized again and the new UI may fail to update the real system setting outside the app container. Please confirm this removal is intentional and that registry writes still target the expected location in packaged deployments.

This returns `S_OK` immediately after `CreateProcessW`, but it never waits for `reg.exe` to finish or checks its exit code. That means callers can observe success even if the registry write fails asynchronously (or hasn't completed yet), which is especially risky if the UI saves and then immediately reloads/validates the setting. Consider waiting on `pi.hProcess` and translating a non-zero process exit code into an HRESULT.

Building the `reg.exe` command line by string interpolation is fragile here because neither the executable path nor the `/d` payload are quoted/escaped. `StringFromCLSID` is probably safe, but `%WINDIR%` can expand to a path with spaces, and `CreateProcessW` parses the command line itself when `lpApplicationName` is `nullptr`. Please either pass `regExePath` as `lpApplicationName` and quote arguments properly, or use a safer command-line builder.

Adding a defaulted `useRegExe = false` here but not on the related `s_SetDefaultConsoleById`, `s_SetDefaultTerminalById`, and `s_Set` declarations below makes the API inconsistent and can easily lead to call-site confusion. If the intent is for existing callers to remain source-compatible, consider defaulting the new parameter consistently across the full setter chain (or avoid defaults entirely and require explicit selection everywhere).

Using `GetRowByOffset(0).size()` to compute the row width can produce incorrect span lengths when the buffer contains rows with different storage widths (for example due to reflow/wrap state). `SpanLength` is then used to bound iteration in `GetPlainText`, so an underestimated/overestimated width could truncate or overrun the intended logical span. It would be safer to derive the distance using the buffer dimensions/coordinate comparison helpers rather than assuming every row has the same `size()` as row 0.

`GetPlainText` currently relies on the caller to pass `start <= end`, but the new helper is public enough that this precondition is easy to violate. In release builds the `assert` in `SpanLength` won't protect us, and a reversed range can underflow into a huge `size_t` and drive an invalid iteration/reserve. Please normalize the endpoints here (like `GetTextRects`/`GetTextSpans` do) or fail fast with explicit validation.

Returning `const std::vector<...>` by value is unusual and doesn't provide meaningful immutability to callers, while it can also inhibit move semantics in some contexts. This declaration should likely return just `std::vector<std::tuple<til::point, til::point>>` (same for the other new by-value return below) unless there's a specific reason to keep the top-level `const`.

Using `std::tuple<til::point, til::point>` for a span makes the API harder to read at call sites because it's not obvious which element is the start vs. end. Since this is a public `TextBuffer` interface, consider introducing a small named struct/alias (for example `TextSpan { til::point start; til::point end; }`) to make the contract clearer and reduce misuse.

This also returns a top-level `const` by value, which is generally unnecessary and can get in the way of efficient moves/consumption by callers. Prefer returning `std::wstring` here unless you specifically need to prevent assignment to a temporary.

If `args.ActionArgs()` is not a `ColorSelectionArgs`, this handler exits without explicitly setting `Handled(false)`. Other action handlers typically make the handled state unambiguous, and leaving it untouched can make routing/fallback behavior depend on prior state. Consider setting `args.Handled(false)` when the cast fails (or initializing a local `handled`/`res` flag and assigning it once at the end).

Using `colorFg.a == 1` as a sentinel to mean "indexed color" is pretty fragile here. `SelectionColor::Color()` returns a normal RGBA value, so this overloads a valid alpha channel value with out-of-band meaning and can silently misinterpret a real color if alpha ever stops being normalized the way this code assumes. Since the PR description already calls this hacky, it would be safer to carry the color kind explicitly on `SelectionColor` (e.g. an enum/flag plus index value) instead of encoding it in `a`.

This initializes a fresh `TextAttribute` and then always sets both foreground and background before applying it. If either `fg` or `bg` is omitted, the corresponding `TextColor` remains default-constructed, which risks overwriting the existing attribute with a default color instead of leaving it unchanged. Can we preserve the current text attributes and only mutate the channels that were actually provided?

This check treats any non-zero high byte as the "indexed color" sentinel, but the PR description says the alpha channel is being used specifically to mark indexed colors. As written, a normal 32-bit color value with a non-zero top byte (for example ARGB-style input) will be misinterpreted as an index. If the intent is to reserve a specific sentinel value (e.g. alpha == 1), this should compare against that exact value rather than `(_TextColor & 0xff000000)`.

Exposing `TextColor` as a raw `UInt32` leaks an internal implementation detail (`TextColor` from `buffer/out/TextColor.h`) across the WinRT boundary. That makes the ABI harder to understand and more brittle if the native representation changes. Could we model this as a proper WinRT-friendly type instead (for example, a discriminated shape like `{ Boolean IsIndex; UInt32 Value; }` or separate RGB/index fields), so callers don't need to know about the packed native encoding?

Using a bare `UInt32` for `matchMode` makes the API opaque and weakly typed, especially since the PR description suggests only a small set of valid modes. This would be easier to consume and safer to evolve as a WinRT enum (or at least a named type) so invalid values are less likely and the contract is self-documenting.

This public API takes a raw `uint32_t` for `matchMode`, but the PR description indicates only a small, predefined set of modes is valid. Using an untyped integer here makes the contract unclear and allows invalid values to flow through the control layer. Consider introducing a strongly typed enum (ideally a WinRT enum if this crosses the ABI) so callers get validation and the API remains extensible without relying on magic numbers.

Exposing `matchMode` as a raw `UInt32` in the WinRT surface makes the API hard to understand and easy to misuse, especially since the PR description indicates only a small set of discrete modes are valid. Consider defining a WinRT enum for the supported match modes instead. That will make the contract self-documenting, improve projection behavior for callers, and leave room to add future modes without relying on magic numbers.

This hard-codes `Search::Sensitivity::CaseInsensitive`, which means `matchMode == 1` will recolor all case-insensitive matches even when the current selection differs only by case. Legacy color selection/search behavior is typically based on the exact selected text, so this may produce surprising extra matches (for example selecting `Foo` also colors `foo`). Consider making the sensitivity configurable or defaulting to case-sensitive/exact matching here.

This new helper returns a `std::vector<std::tuple<til::point, til::point>>`, which is much less self-describing than the existing selection APIs that use named types like `inclusive_rect` or `std::pair`. Since this appears to represent selection spans and is now part of the class interface, consider introducing a small named struct/alias (for example, `SelectionSpan` with `start`/`end` fields) to make call sites clearer and reduce the risk of mixing up tuple element ordering.

Writing `spanLength` cells starting at `coordStart` assumes the target range is a single contiguous run in the buffer. That works for same-row selections, but for multi-line/non-block selections the inclusive `[coordStart, coordEnd]` range spans wrapped row boundaries and this will also overwrite the cells between the logical end of one line and the start of the next. Since this PR already introduces `_GetSelectionSpans()`, should this method iterate the per-line spans instead of doing one flat `Write`?

Using an empty string here means `ColorSelection` has no resource-backed command name and relies entirely on `GenerateName`. That can be fragile for any code path that expects every action to have a non-empty display name from this table, and it also bypasses localization unless `GenerateName` fully handles it. Please verify all consumers tolerate an empty entry, or consider adding a dedicated resource string/fallback name instead.

The fallback formatting for indexed colors can produce values like `i100` or larger, but `tempBuf` is only 9 wchar_t in `GenerateName()`. Since `swprintf_s` here doesn't receive the buffer length, this relies on the destination always being large enough and can become unsafe if an out-of-range index is ever passed through. Consider either validating/clamping the index range or changing this helper to take the buffer size and use the bounded `swprintf_s(tempBuf, bufLen, ...)` overload.

The hash for `SelectionColor` only includes `TextColor()`. From the PR description, this type is also being used to carry palette-index-based colors via extra state, so two logically different `SelectionColor` values could hash identically if they resolve to the same `TextColor()`. If equality for this WinRT type distinguishes those cases, this would violate the usual hash/equality contract and could cause subtle issues in hashed containers or action deduplication. Please make sure the hash incorporates all state that participates in equality (for example, any 'is index' flag / index value as well).

`MatchMode` is exposed as a raw `UInt32`, but the PR description indicates this is really a small set of semantic modes (currently selection-only vs all-matches, with possible future expansion). Using an enum in the IDL would make the contract self-describing, improve validation/serialization, and avoid magic numbers leaking into settings and command plumbing. If keeping it numeric for compatibility, please at least document the accepted values and define a strongly typed enum at the ABI boundary.

Removing this assertion changes an important invariant: these methods now iterate all parents, but the merge/lookup logic here was originally written assuming at most one parent. If multiple parents are now expected, can we document the precedence/ordering guarantees and add tests for conflicting inherited commands/keybindings? Otherwise this silently changes behavior from a checked assumption to whatever `_parents` iteration order happens to be.

This check happens before `userSettings.globals->_FinalizeInheritance()`, so `EnableColorSelection()` will only reflect values already materialized on the user globals object. If this setting can come from any inherited/global parent layer (defaults, fragments, etc.), the conditional injection may be skipped even though the effective setting is `true`. Consider evaluating this after inheritance is finalized, or otherwise reading the fully layered/effective value before deciding whether to add the generated actions.

Adding the WinRT property here is only part of the settings surface. Please make sure `EnableColorSelection` is also wired through the JSON serialization/deserialization layer, defaults/inheritance handling, and schema/docs generation; otherwise the new setting may compile but be ignored or unavailable to users.

This adds a new global setting, but the PR description notes `Schema updated` is still unchecked. Since this macro drives the settings model only, users may not get validation/IntelliSense for `experimental.enableColorSelection` unless the JSON schema and docs are updated as well. Please make sure the corresponding schema entry is added so the new setting is discoverable and doesn't appear as an unknown property.

This `Exec` command appears to use a typographic en dash in `–ExecutionPolicy` instead of a normal ASCII hyphen (`-ExecutionPolicy`). MSBuild will pass that through literally, and PowerShell won't recognize it as a valid parameter, which can break header generation on build. Please replace it with a standard hyphen, and ideally keep the quoting consistent with the existing target above for paths that may contain spaces.

The RGB branch of `ToJson` drops the `#` prefix, but `CanConvert`/`FromJson` only accept hex colors in `#NNN` or `#NNNNNN` form. That means serializing a `SelectionColor` to JSON produces a value that this same converter won't parse back. Please include the leading `#` here (for example `"#{:06x}"`) so serialization is round-trippable and consistent with the documented type description.

This object is missing a comma after the `action` property before the following comment. Since this file is consumed as JSON/JSONC, the current syntax is invalid and will fail to parse. The same issue appears again in the `matchMode`-only command later in the file.

This compares the scheme's current display name to the default color scheme name, which can become incorrect while the user is editing/renaming the scheme. In particular, if the default scheme is renamed in the editor before the base settings are updated, `IsDefaultScheme()` will temporarily return false and the UI may lose the default tag. Consider basing this on the underlying model identity/reference, or at least the original persisted name, instead of the mutable `_Name` view-model property.

Storing a full `Model::CascadiaSettings` inside each `ColorSchemeViewModel` may leave `IsDefaultScheme()` reading stale state if the default scheme changes elsewhere and this copy is not kept in sync. Since the PR adds `RefreshIsDefault()` specifically to react to external changes, consider holding a shared/reference-backed model (or querying through the parent page VM) instead of copying the settings object into every scheme VM.

Exposing `IsDefaultScheme` as a getter-only property is useful for the new UI, but this value depends on external state (`profiles.defaults.colorScheme`) rather than the scheme object itself. If the default scheme changes elsewhere, bindings to this property will go stale unless the implementation also raises a property-changed notification for `IsDefaultScheme` on all affected `ColorSchemeViewModel` instances. Please make sure the corresponding implementation updates/refreshes this property when the default scheme is changed.

The new "Set as default" action is enabled whenever there is a current selection, but the selected scheme may already be the default. In that case this presents a no-op action and can reinforce the original confusion around whether selecting a scheme applies it. Consider disabling or hiding this button when `IsDefaultScheme` is true, or updating the label/state to make it clear the scheme is already the default.

Setting `profiles.defaults.appearance.colorScheme` here updates the base layer, but it doesn't appear to notify any dependent view models or pages beyond the local `_AllColorSchemes` refresh. If other settings UI elements are bound to the default appearance/color scheme, they may remain stale until a broader refresh occurs. Consider raising the appropriate property-changed notification (or routing this through the existing settings mutation/notification path) so the rest of the editor stays in sync immediately.

Using the lowercase string `default` for a visible UI tag may be inconsistent with the rest of the Settings UI, where labels and badges are typically title-cased. Since this tag is meant to clarify state and address the confusion in the issue, consider using `Default` (or whatever casing matches the surrounding UI) and ensuring the same resource is reused for any automation/accessibility text if applicable.

Checking only `len(wsConfig) == 0` avoids the original panic, but this still assumes the first element has the expected shape. If the cached state is malformed in some other way, `wsConfig[0].(map[string]interface{})` can still panic. Consider validating the element type (and possibly required keys) before asserting so this path consistently returns an error instead of crashing.

This assertion can let unexpected failures slip through: if `tc.err` is non-nil but `b.Configure(conf)` returns `nil`, the condition is false and the test passes. It also doesn't fail when an error is returned but no error was expected. Consider using explicit expectation checks here, e.g. fail when exactly one of `err`/`tc.err` is nil, and only use `strings.Contains` when both are non-nil.

This iterates over *all* module calls in the parent and assigns `p.InChild.providerType` whenever the child-side local name matches. If the parent has multiple calls to different child modules that reuse the same provider local name (for example, many modules using `openstack`), this can stamp this child's provider types onto unrelated sibling module calls. It would be safer to update only the specific parent `ModuleCall` that produced `c` (e.g. by matching the call name/path) rather than every call in `c.Parent.Module.ModuleCalls`.

Adding `providerType` here introduces cached resolution state into `Provider`, but the comment notes it may be unset in some cases. That makes the zero value (`addrs.Provider{}`) ambiguous with a real resolution result and easy to misuse later. Consider either documenting the invariants more explicitly at the field level (when it is guaranteed to be populated, and what callers must do when it is not), or representing the optionality explicitly so future internal consumers don't accidentally treat an unresolved provider as resolved.

Adding `providerType` here helps preserve the resolved FQN, but this field is unexported and has no documented zero-value semantics beyond the TODO. Since the comment explicitly says it may be unset in some cases, callers inside this package can easily start depending on it and accidentally treat the zero value as a valid provider. Consider documenting the invariant more explicitly here (for example, when it is guaranteed to be populated and how to detect an unresolved value) or adding a small helper like `ResolvedProviderType()`/`HasResolvedProviderType()` to centralize that logic.

The comment says this variable "will indicate if the new collations are enabled in the TiDB cluster", but the PR/issue describe a config that only controls whether new collations are enabled on the first bootstrap. As written, this comment suggests a general runtime cluster state flag, which is broader than the intended semantics and could be misleading for future maintenance. Consider clarifying that this persisted value records whether new collations were enabled during initial bootstrap.

The comment says the `GROUP BY` clause is ignored for foldable constant expressions, but this test only covers `count(a)` on a non-empty table. Since the reported bug was a coprocessor execution error during aggregation pushdown, it would be safer to also assert the behavior for an empty table and/or another aggregate like `max(a)` to ensure we don't regress on the original failure mode while only validating `count` semantics here.

Adding a mutating `setArgs` to the shared `builtinFunc` interface makes builtin function instances mutable after construction. Since these expressions are reused by the planner/optimizer, in-place argument replacement can easily introduce aliasing bugs if a caller forgets to clone before modifying. If this setter is only needed to support cloning in `EvaluateExprWithNull`, consider keeping the mutation path internal to clone logic or documenting/enforcing that `setArgs` must only be used on freshly cloned functions.

This new regression case matches the reported bug pattern, but it uses `t2.c_str` while the issue/PR discussion is specifically about `OR` over a float column preserving `keepNull` in `IsTrue`. If `c_str` is a string column, this may exercise a different coercion path and could miss the exact regression. Consider adding a case on the same numeric/float column type as the bug report (for example `t2.b`/`col_float`) to ensure the test covers the failing `IsTrue(..., keepNull)` behavior directly.

Setting `NotNullFlag` unconditionally here looks risky. `SYSDATE()` can still evaluate to `NULL` at runtime in error paths (for example, if later evaluation/conversion fails or SQL mode changes behavior), and this PR is specifically about removing incorrect `NOT_NULL` metadata. Please verify that all `SYSDATE` variants are guaranteed non-null in every execution path before advertising `NOT_NULL`; otherwise this should be left unset or derived from the actual nullability rules rather than parser validation alone.

Setting `NotNullFlag` here mutates the constant's declared `RetType` based solely on the current datum value. That fixes many literal constants, but it can still produce incorrect metadata for expressions whose type is intentionally nullable even when the current value is non-NULL (for example, values produced from nullable expressions or reused constants in cached plans). Since the issue is specifically about JSON/nullability metadata, it would be safer to derive the flag from the expression semantics or clone-and-adjust only for true literal constants, rather than unconditionally marking every non-NULL `Constant` as `NOT_NULL` in `GetType()`.

Switching these assertions from `Assert` to `Check` makes the test continue after the first mismatch, which is useful for bulk fixture updates, but it also weakens failure localization if `err` is unexpectedly non-nil. Since `tp := p.Schema().Columns[0].RetType` depends on successful planning, consider keeping `c.Assert(err, IsNil, comment)` as-is and only using `Check` for the field comparisons, or add a guard to avoid dereferencing invalid state when planning fails.

Please double-check this expectation against MySQL behavior. `database()` can return `NULL` when no default database is selected, so marking its inferred type as `NOT_NULL` may be incorrect and could reintroduce the same class of metadata bug this PR is fixing.

This looks suspicious: `current_role()` is nullable in MySQL/TiDB when no active role is set (or can produce `NONE` depending on implementation/version), so unconditionally adding `NotNullFlag` may not match actual result metadata. It would be safer to verify the runtime field flags for this function before locking the test to `NOT_NULL`.

This test only covers `convert('{}', JSON)`, which is a non-NULL constant expression. The bug report and PR description also mention `convert(null, JSON)` incorrectly carrying `NOT_NULL`, so this case can still regress without being caught here. Please add an assertion for `select convert(null, JSON)` (and ideally a nullable JSON column like `select c_json from t`) to verify the flag is cleared for nullable JSON results as well.

Setting `NotNullFlag` purely based on `value != nil` can make the flag sticky when `tp` is reused across calls. If the same `FieldType` instance previously handled a non-NULL value and is later passed a NULL, this function never clears `mysql.NotNullFlag`, so callers may still observe `NOT_NULL` for NULL-typed results. Consider explicitly clearing the flag in the `nil` path (or resetting nullability before the switch) to make the function idempotent for reused `FieldType` objects.

This test update broadens the expected behavior of `DefaultTypeForValue` from the JSON-specific bugfix to asserting `NotNullFlag` for nearly every non-nil literal. That may be correct, but it also changes the contract for all inferred expression/result types at once and could mask regressions unrelated to JSON. Please add/keep a focused case for JSON (e.g. `BinaryJSON` / `convert(null, json)` behavior) and consider whether these non-JSON expectations should be covered in a separate change with rationale, since the issue here is specifically about unexpected `NOT_NULL` on JSON.

This adds coverage for an empty `TABLESAMPLE` argument list, which is good, but it would be worth also asserting the parser/validator behavior for the bare form `TABLESAMPLE REGIONS` if that syntax is intended to be supported. The issue description and examples mention `TABLESAMPLE REGIONS`, while the PR description and tests use `regions()`, so this test alone may hide a syntax/compatibility mismatch between documented and implemented behavior.

Returning `0` here disables *all* logical optimization whenever `TABLESAMPLE` is present. That preserves sampling semantics, but it also blocks optimizations that should still be safe (for example, predicate pushdown on conditions that only reference sampled rows) and may cause unnecessary regressions for mixed queries. Since the PR description says only operators like `Selection`, `Limit`, or `Sort` are problematic, consider introducing a dedicated opt flag to disable only the unsafe rewrite rules instead of turning off the entire logical optimizer.

Removing `SampleMethodTypeNone` from the allowed cases means any `TableSample` node that reaches preprocessing with an unspecified/default method will now be rejected as `InvalidTableSample`. If the parser can construct `TableSample` before method validation is complete, this may turn malformed or partially parsed input into a semantic error here instead of being handled earlier. Please confirm that `v.TableSample != nil` always implies an explicit method and, if not, either keep `None` here or add a clearer guard/comment explaining why `None` is impossible at this stage.

Returning the original expression here changes the previous side effect of setting `StmtCtx.MaybeOverOptimized4PlanCache = true`. If any callers still rely on that statement-context flag (instead of only inspecting the returned expression), this could silently stop marking such statements as non-cacheable. Please verify all downstream plan-cache checks were updated accordingly, or preserve the flag assignment here as well.

Disabling PointGet conversion whenever `StmtCtx.UseCache` is true looks broader than the bug being fixed. The issue description mentions only PointGet plans generated in the physical stage that are unsafe for prepared plan cache because parameter values can change the shape from range scan to point get (for example `pk >= ? and pk <= ?`). With this guard, even parameter-independent point lookups that would be safe to cache will never benefit from PointGet under plan cache, which may regress performance. Could we narrow this to only the cases where the PointGet decision depends on parameter values, or at least document why all physical-stage PointGet conversions must be disabled for cached statements?

This test drops table `t` but all subsequent statements operate on `t1`/`t2`. That looks like leftover setup from another case and means the test doesn't fully clean up the tables it actually uses. Please change this to drop `t1`/`t2` (or use unique table names) so reruns are isolated and the intent is clearer.

This changes the behavior from marking the statement as `MaybeOverOptimized4PlanCache` to bailing out of `ExtractEqAndInCondition` entirely whenever `MaybeOverOptimized4PlanCache(...)` returns true. That is a much stronger semantic change: instead of still extracting ranges while preventing plan-cache reuse, we now skip the eq/in extraction path and fall back to the original conditions. Please double-check that all callers handle this fallback correctly and that it doesn't regress non-plan-cache planning quality for the current execution. If the intent is only to disable caching, preserving the previous `StmtCtx.MaybeOverOptimized4PlanCache = true` side effect may still be necessary here.

This hunk removes `TestInFunc` entirely from this file. If the test was intentionally migrated elsewhere, please make sure the replacement still covers the same cases (mixed numeric types, decimal/time/duration/json comparisons, `hack.Slice` vs string, and the new-collation scalar/vectorized paths). Otherwise this change silently drops a lot of behavioral coverage for `IN`, which is risky given the PR is about test migration/stability rather than reducing assertions.

This test mutates the global collation switch, so it should not run in parallel. Since the goal here is to avoid instability caused by shared global state, please make that explicit by keeping this test non-parallel and consider documenting that `SetNewCollationEnabledForTest` is process-global. Otherwise a future `t.Parallel()` added here or in another test touching the same flag could reintroduce flakes.

This change removes `TestCIWeightString` entirely, but the PR description says the flaky test should be moved to a serial test rather than deleted. Dropping it here also removes coverage for `WEIGHT_STRING` under `utf8mb4_general_ci`/`utf8mb4_unicode_ci`, including the trailing-space behavior that triggered the instability report. Could we keep these assertions in a non-parallel/serial test file instead of removing them?

This loop is intended to validate all 4 appended row pairs, but it always compares `chk.GetRow(0)` against itself, so only the first case (`"a"` vs `"A"`) is exercised four times. That means the added coverage for `"À"`, emoji, and trailing-space handling is currently missing. Please use the loop index here, e.g. `chk.GetRow(i)`, so each appended row is actually tested.

This hunk removes `TestCompareString` entirely, but the issue/PR description is about stabilizing flaky tests rather than dropping collation coverage. Deleting this test also removes the only checks here for `types.CompareString` behavior across `utf8_general_ci`, `utf8_unicode_ci`, `binary`, and `CompareStringWithCollationInfo`. If the intent was to migrate it elsewhere, please keep equivalent assertions in the new location or note that explicitly; otherwise this change weakens regression coverage and could hide future collation bugs.

Adding the config field here is necessary, but this change alone may not preserve the intended `INSTANCE`-level semantics. `bool` cannot distinguish between "not set in config" and an explicit `false`, while the issue description says some instances should omit the option entirely. If later logic needs to know whether the value was configured or just defaulted, consider using a tri-state representation (for example a pointer/custom nullable type) or ensure the downstream code treats the default exactly as the intended unset behavior.

Changing this sysvar to `ScopeInstance` removes the previous `SESSION` behavior, but this new definition no longer updates `SessionVars.RcReadCheckTS`. Any code path that still reads the per-session field instead of the global `EnableRCReadCheckTS` will now see a stale/default value, which can silently change behavior after the scope migration. Please verify all call sites were switched to the instance-level source, or keep session state synchronized during session initialization / sysvar loading for compatibility.

This assertion only verifies the read path for the new instance-scoped variable, but the change in this PR is specifically about scope semantics. It would be helpful to extend the test to also verify that `tidb_rc_read_check_ts` is no longer treated as SESSION/GLOBAL settable (for example, rejecting `SET SESSION`/`SET GLOBAL` and only exposing the instance value). Otherwise this test can still pass even if the variable scope metadata is incorrect.

Adding a new package-level atomic for `tidb_rc_read_check_ts` changes the source of truth for this variable, but this hunk alone doesn't show any initialization from the instance config default. Since the issue requires this to be instance-scoped and configurable via `[instance] tidb_rc_read_check_ts = true`, please make sure this atomic is wired into config loading/bootstrap so its value reflects the instance setting on startup rather than always defaulting to `false`.

Switching from the session-scoped `sessionVars.RcReadCheckTS` to the instance-scoped `variable.EnableRCReadCheckTS` changes the source of truth here, but this also removes any session-level override behavior immediately. Please double-check that all remaining code paths, tests, and documentation have been updated consistently, because mixed usage of `RcReadCheckTS` and `EnableRCReadCheckTS` elsewhere could lead to inconsistent RC behavior depending on which path is hit.

This only initializes `EnableRCReadCheckTS` at startup. Since the variable is being changed to an INSTANCE-level setting, please make sure runtime config reload paths also update this atomic value (similar to other `[instance]` options that support `SET CONFIG`/hot reload). Otherwise the effective value may diverge from the instance config after startup, which would be surprising for operators.

This logic assumes `len(partitionIDs) == 0` uniquely identifies a global index on a partitioned table, but an empty `partitionIDs` can also happen for a non-partitioned table or due to malformed/older job args. Since the subsequent delete-range behavior differs significantly, it would be safer to gate this on explicit index/table metadata (for example, whether the table is partitioned and the index is global) rather than inferring from an empty slice alone.

Using an empty `[]int64{}` here changes the meaning of the second job arg from "all partition IDs" to "global index on the table prefix". This is subtle and easy to break because downstream code may not distinguish `nil` vs empty slice consistently, or may still interpret an empty slice as "no partitions" and skip delete-range generation entirely. Please make sure `insertJobIntoDeleteRangeTable` (and any other consumer of `job.Args`) explicitly handles this sentinel value for global indexes, or consider passing an explicit flag/structured arg instead of overloading the partition ID list.

Changing the expected count from `max(len(physicalTableIDs), 1)` to `len(physicalTableIDs) + 1` looks correct for the new global-index delete-range behavior, but this now assumes there is always one additional delete-range task beyond the partition IDs. Could you add a short comment or assert the corresponding job shape here? Without that context, this is easy to misread as an off-by-one change, and it may break non-global-index drop/truncate paths if they don't always enqueue the extra range.

This error assignment looks suspicious: `err` has not been set by `TableByID`, so on the `!ok` path we may end up propagating a stale or nil error. Please return a dedicated error here (for example, wrapping the missing table ID) so failures to resolve the table are diagnosable and don't silently lose context.

Returning an error when `partInfo` is nil may make global-index execution fail for plans that don't carry partition pruning metadata, even though the safe fallback would be to treat it as "all partitions". Since this helper is used to filter global-index results, consider handling `nil` by returning all partition IDs instead of hard-failing, unless the planner guarantees `PartitionInfo` is always populated here.

This assumes `partitionIDMap` is always initialized when a global index can return `kv.PartitionHandle`. If the map is nil, every partition handle will be filtered out (`exist` is always false on a nil map), which could silently turn valid results into an empty result set. Please guard this with `w.idxLookup.partitionIDMap != nil` or make the filtering conditional on `partitionTableMode`/global-index-specific setup so non-partitioned or uninitialized paths don't regress.

This test only validates the returned rows, but the bug being fixed is specifically about filtering selected partitions when a global index is used. Without asserting the execution plan, the query could still pass by falling back to a table/partition scan instead of exercising the global-index path. Consider adding an `EXPLAIN` assertion (or another plan check) to ensure `use index(idx)` actually produces the intended global-index lookup on the specified partition.

This call now computes `possiblePaths` before the `IsView()` / `IsSequence()` early returns. That means we may parse/apply index hints and build access paths for objects that never use them, and more importantly it can change behavior for views/sequences if `getPossibleAccessPaths` rejects hints that were previously ignored. Since views and sequences return before any path planning, it would be safer to keep this call after those branches (or guard it with `!tableInfo.IsView() && !tableInfo.IsSequence()`) unless the changed ordering is intentional and covered by tests.

This condition removes global indexes whenever `hasFlagPartitionProcessor` is set, but the PR description says global indexes should still be usable in dynamic mode when global stats are ready. As written, this looks broader than the intended fix and may regress valid global-index plans for partitioned tables. Consider gating this on the actual partition prune mode / global-stats readiness instead of only the partition-processor flag.

Passing a hard-coded `true` here changes the semantics of `getPossibleAccessPaths` for every call from the partition processor and drops the previous dependency on `SchemaMetaVersion()`. Given the PR description, global-index selection is supposed to be gated by conditions like dynamic prune mode and global stats readiness, so unconditionally enabling this flag looks too broad and may reintroduce incorrect path selection for partitioned tables in unsupported cases. Please thread the actual eligibility condition into this call instead of forcing `true`.

Assigning the hinted resource group here without validating that it exists can cause execution to fail later with a hard error, which also means `sessVars.ResourceGroupName` may be left overridden for the rest of the statement lifecycle even when the hint is invalid. Since the PR behavior explicitly returns an error for unknown groups, it would be safer to resolve/validate the group at this point (or at least avoid mutating the session variable until validation succeeds).

This changes `ErrDataTooLong` into `ErrTruncated`, which also changes the warning/error code from 1406 to 1265. The issue description only asks to include the column name/row number, while MySQL still reports `Data too long for column ...` for this case. Rewriting the error class here may break compatibility for `SHOW WARNINGS` and clients that depend on code 1406. Consider preserving `ErrDataTooLong` and only enriching its message with the column/row context.

Be careful with `TruncateWarnings(warnCnt)` here: it removes warnings from `StmtCtx`, then the code appends the rewritten warnings back. Since `warnCnt` is incremented after re-append, subsequent iterations may no longer line up with the current warning slice length, which can cause warnings to be skipped or duplicated depending on how `TruncateWarnings` is implemented. A safer approach would be to fetch only newly added warnings without mutating the existing list, or to reset the baseline after truncation/re-append based on the current warning count.

This test hard-codes the full `sql_mode` string, including `NO_AUTO_CREATE_USER`, which is deprecated/removed in MySQL 8.0 and can make the test brittle if the default mode set changes across versions. Since the behavior under test is truncation handling during `LOAD DATA`, it would be safer to set only the minimal mode(s) required for this case (for example `STRICT_TRANS_TABLES`) or preserve/restore the original session mode to avoid coupling the test to unrelated SQL mode details.

This assertion depends on the row order returned by `SELECT *` without an `ORDER BY`. Since `LOAD DATA` here reads from a wildcard over multiple files, the ingestion order may not be stable across implementations or future changes, which can make this test flaky. Consider adding `ORDER BY i` to both queries (or otherwise asserting as an unordered set) so the test only validates the loaded contents.

Same concern here: the expected rows are checked in a specific order, but the query has no `ORDER BY`. Because this test covers mixed compression with multi-file loading, relying on implicit row order can introduce nondeterminism. Please sort the result explicitly, e.g. `SELECT * FROM multi_load.t ORDER BY i`.

This test mutates the session `sql_mode` but never restores it. Because the suite reuses `s.tk`, this can leak state into later tests and make them order-dependent. Please save the original value and restore it with `defer`, or set the mode explicitly in subsequent tests.

This assertion encodes a surprising behavior—loading into a generated column list results in `<nil>` rows instead of an error or warning. Since the PR is about MySQL-compatible error reporting, it would be safer to also assert the warning/error state here (for example via `SHOW WARNINGS` or statement message), otherwise the test may pass even if the compatibility issue remains or regresses silently.

Skipping the entire `TestGeneratedColumns` body leaves the old test logic in place but unreachable, and the helper signature was partially inlined below. This makes the test file harder to maintain and can mask regressions unrelated to the stated warning-message fix. If this feature is intentionally unsupported here, consider removing or moving the obsolete test implementation in the same change, or at least adding a TODO/issue reference explaining where these cases will be covered next.

This line now hardcodes `importer.PhysicalImportMode` inside `TestGeneratedColumns`, but the test is skipped immediately above, so the remaining setup code is dead. More importantly, the previous helper accepted `importMode` and exercised different modes; hardcoding one mode here would silently reduce coverage if the skip is later removed. It would be safer to either delete the unreachable body entirely or preserve the helper-based structure until the test is re-enabled.

This updated expected plan now shows `IndexMerge` estimated rows reduced to `3.00`, but the corresponding `TableRowIDScan` below still remains at `5542.21`. Given the issue being fixed is specifically about `estRows` for `IndexMerge` and `TableRowIDScan` when limit/topN is embedded, this expectation looks inconsistent and may indicate the test is not actually covering the full fix. Please verify whether `TableRowIDScan` should also be capped by the pushed-down limit here, and update the expected output or add a dedicated case if needed.

The memory accounting here looks incorrect: `unsafe.Sizeof(h.idx)` is the size of the slice header, not the backing array element that was just appended. This will under/over-report memory and also double-count strangely as the slice grows. If the goal is to track heap entries, consider charging `unsafe.Sizeof(rowIdx{})` per pushed element (and releasing the same on pop), or account for backing-array capacity changes explicitly.

Same concern as in `Push`: `unsafe.Sizeof(h.idx)` only measures the slice header, not the memory used by stored `rowIdx` values. Decrementing by this value on every pop will make the tracker inaccurate. Please account for element size or capacity deltas instead.
