This offset/count trimming is now applied in the unordered union path as well, but without `ORDER BY` the arrival order of partial index results is not guaranteed to match the final result order. Applying a global pushed limit here can therefore drop handles that should have been returned after dedup/merge across all partial plans. Can we confirm that `pushedLimit` is only set for plans where this early pruning is semantically safe, or guard this optimization more narrowly?

This `ANALYZE` runs before any rows are inserted, so the statistics for `t` remain empty/pseudo during the actual test queries. Since these assertions depend on the optimizer choosing `IndexMerge`/`Limit`, the test can become plan-unstable and not really validate the intended costing/estRows behavior. Consider moving `analyze table t` to after the `insert` so the planner sees real stats.

This expected plan still shows `TableRowIDScan` with `estRows` 19.99 even though the issue specifically calls out that, with embedded limit/topN for IndexMerge, the row count for `IndexMerge` and `TableRowIDScan` should be bounded by the pushed-down limit (e.g. <= 2 here). Keeping the old larger estimate in the golden output may either mask the bug or indicate the fix is incomplete for this shape (`ORDER BY ... LIMIT` with a probe-side TopN). Please verify whether the optimizer should now cap this estimate and update the expected plan accordingly.

Returning early when `remainingFilters` is non-empty skips the ordered-scan column augmentation below, even when `matchProp` is true. That means `currentTopPlan` may be a `Selection` on top of `ts` without the extra PK/handle columns added for order-preserving index-merge execution. If those columns are still required by the downstream row-id scan / merge logic, this can break keep-order plans or make the `columnAdded` result inaccurate. Consider deferring this return until after the `matchProp` branch has had a chance to add the necessary columns to `ts`.

This only checks `PreparedAst.Stmt` for `*ast.AnalyzeTableStmt`. For prepared statements, the cached AST may be wrapped or may not be available in all execution paths, so relying solely on this field can make `EXECUTE` of `ANALYZE` still miss the global-analyze memory path in some cases. Please confirm this is the canonical way to identify prepared analyze statements here, or add a more robust flag during prepare time and consume that flag during execute.

This assertion dereferences `err1` unconditionally. If `tk0.Exec(sqlExecute)` unexpectedly succeeds or returns a nil error, the test will panic with a nil-pointer dereference instead of producing a clear failure message. Please add an explicit `require.Error(t, err1)` before checking the error text.

The failpoint cleanup is only executed on the happy path. If any assertion above fails, these failpoints remain enabled and can leak into later tests, causing hard-to-debug flakes. Consider registering the disable calls with `t.Cleanup(...)` immediately after each `Enable(...)` so cleanup is guaranteed.

Reassigning `memStats` here drops every field except `HeapInuse`. `runtime.ReadMemStats` populates many stats, and callers of `ReadMemStats()` may rely on them remaining intact even when the failpoint is enabled. If the goal is just to avoid mutating the original object, consider copying the full struct first (e.g. `copied := *memStats; copied.HeapInuse += ...; memStats = &copied`) so the injected path preserves all other memory statistics.

Changing `SessionID` to `atomicutil.Uint64` makes sense if it can be read concurrently here, but this also changes the field's API. Please verify all write sites now use `Store()` (and reads use `Load()`) instead of direct assignment/access; otherwise this can either fail to compile or leave inconsistent synchronization semantics across the tracker lifecycle.

This dereferences `t` before the existing nil check. `memory.MemUsageTop1Tracker.Load()` can still return `nil`, so `t.SessionID.Load()` here would panic before `if t != nil` is reached. Please move the `sessionID` load inside the `t != nil` branch (or guard it explicitly) to preserve the previous safety.

Pushing `format` into the channel by value is risky here because `rowInDisk` is reused across iterations. If `rowInDisk` contains slice fields (as `ReadFrom`/`toRow` suggest), each send may copy only the slice headers, so later `ReadFrom` calls can overwrite the backing arrays before the consumer finishes `toRow`, causing rows to be duplicated/corrupted. Please allocate a fresh `rowInDisk` per iteration or deep-copy its mutable fields before sending.

This error is written in the producer goroutine and read in the caller goroutine without synchronization. That introduces a data race under the Go memory model. Consider sending the error through a channel, or using a dedicated result struct `{format rowInDisk; err error}` so the consumer observes the final error safely.

Writing `reader.err` from the worker goroutine and reading it later via `Error()` is unsynchronized, which introduces a data race under the Go race detector. Please guard this with synchronization (for example, store the error before closing the channel under a mutex, or use an `atomic.Value`/`atomic.Pointer[error]`) so callers can safely call `Error()` after or during iteration.

Using `math/rand.Uint32()` without an explicit seed makes this test depend on the package-global PRNG state. That can lead to order-dependent behavior if other tests in the same package call `rand.Seed`, and it also makes reproducing failures harder. Consider creating a local RNG with a fixed seed (for example, `r := rand2.New(rand2.NewSource(1))`) and using that throughout these tests/benchmarks.

Incrementing the update metric before `sc.c.Put(id, tbl)` means failed or no-op cache writes will still be counted as successful updates. If `Put` can reject/skip entries (or may do so in the future), this metric will drift from the actual cache state. Consider incrementing the counter only after a successful mutation, or documenting clearly that this metric tracks attempted updates rather than applied ones.

This delete metric is incremented unconditionally before `sc.c.Del(id)`, so it counts delete attempts rather than actual removals. If the key is absent, the metric may overreport cache evictions/deletions. Consider checking whether the entry exists or moving the increment to a path that reflects a successful delete, depending on what semantics you want the metric to expose.

This test only verifies that executing the query does not return an error, but the reported bug is specifically about `DESC/EXPLAIN` failing with "Can't find a proper physical plan". As written, `SELECT 1 FROM tx WHERE a IN (1)` may not exercise the same planning path as the issue. Please consider changing this to `EXPLAIN`/`DESC` on a query that actually references the indexed column (for example selecting from `tx` with `a in (...)`) and asserting the statement succeeds, so the regression test matches the original failure mode more closely.

Returning `0` whenever either side is an MVIndex path effectively disables skyline pruning for any comparison involving MVIndex, which can keep clearly dominated MVIndex candidates alive and may regress plan selection/performance beyond the reported bug. Since the root cause is that MVIndex full scan is currently unsupported, would it be safer to treat MVIndex as worse only in the unsupported cases (for example, when it would require a full scan / cannot generate a task) instead of making all MVIndex comparisons incomparable here?

`RetrieveLocal` is now being exercised with a `SQLExecutor` instead of the full expression context, which matches the new optional-property split, but this test no longer verifies the failure mode when the required optional property is absent. Since the PR is specifically about making these capabilities on-demand, it would be useful to add a companion assertion that calling `RetrieveLocal` without `OptPropSQLExecutor` returns the expected error, so we catch accidental fallback to session-bound state in the future.

This constructor call is now duplicated in multiple retrieval paths in this file (`tidbTrxTableRetriever.retrieve`, `dataLockWaitsTableRetriever.retrieve`, and here). Since all of them derive the same SQL executor from `sctx` and immediately pass it to `RetrieveLocal`/`RetrieveGlobal`, it would be better to factor this into a small helper to keep the migration away from `GetExprCtx()` consistent and reduce the chance of future call sites handling initialization/errors differently.

This looks like a copy/paste bug: `RequiredOptionalEvalProps()` ORs `SequenceOperatorPropReader` with itself, so the `SessionVars` dependency is never declared. `evalInt()` below calls `GetSessionVars(ctx)`, so contexts that validate optional props may incorrectly think this builtin does not require session vars and fail at runtime. Please include `b.SessionVarsPropReader.RequiredOptionalEvalProps()` here.

This now unconditionally fetches the domain infoschema before evaluating any rows, but the previous implementation only depended on an optional context value and could fall back to a no-op decoder. That changes the behavior of `tidb_decode_key` in contexts where `EvalContext` does not provide `OptPropInfoSchema` (which is expected to be optional per this PR). Consider checking whether the optional infoschema property is available first and only calling `GetDomainInfoSchema` when it is, otherwise keep the old no-op fallback so expression evaluation still works in lightweight contexts.

This signature now declares `SessionVars` and `KVStore` as required optional props, but `Clone()` below still only copies `baseBuiltinFunc`. That means a cloned `builtinTiDBBoundedStalenessSig` will lose its prop readers and may fail at runtime when `GetKVStore`/`GetSessionVars` is called. Please update `Clone()` to copy `SessionVarsPropReader` and `KVStorePropReader` as well.

This still pulls `SessionVars` out of `EvalContext`, which seems to conflict with the goal of this PR (`finish to remove SessionVars and other complex objects from EvalContext`). Since the only usage here is `vars.StmtCtx` for computing `minSafeTime`, consider exposing the required statement-level data directly via a basic/optional `EvalContext` accessor instead of depending on the full session vars object.

Adding `CurrentTime()` to the required `EvalContext` surface means every existing implementation now needs to guarantee a stable timestamp for the same `CtxID`. That contract is stronger than a normal `time.Now()` wrapper, so it would be good to document whether the value is expected to be memoized lazily, fixed at context creation, and what should happen if the first call returns an error. Without that clarification, different implementations may return inconsistent values and break expression determinism.

Since `RequestVerification` is now a mandatory method on `EvalContext`, this is a behavioral change for non-SQL evaluation contexts (e.g. mock/lightning/generated-column style contexts) that previously did not need privilege machinery. If those contexts are expected to forbid privilege-dependent functions, consider documenting the required default behavior here (for example, always return `false`) to avoid ad-hoc implementations that accidentally allow access.

The descriptor string for `OptPropSequenceOperator` looks incorrect: it is set to `"OptPropDDLOwnerInfo"` instead of `"OptPropSequenceOperator"`. This will make stringification/debug output misleading and can break any tests or logic that rely on the property name mapping. Please update the `str` value to match the key.

This implementation delegates to `getStmtTimestamp`, but the PR description says `CurrentTime` should return the same value for the same `EvalContext` (same CtxID). Changing the `StringerWithCtx` interface signature is a broad breaking change for every expression implementation. Hard-coding `errors.RedactLogDisable` in `String()` changes the default behavior of every existing `String()` call site, which may still be used in EXPLAIN/logging paths and would bypass the session's redact setting. This gives explicit `resume_from_checkpoint` precedence over `last_checkpoint`, which is the right behavior, but it would be safer to validate that the provided path actually exists and looks like a Trainer checkpoint before passing it through. This test currently depends on a remote Hub model (`hf-internal-testing/tiny-bert-for-token-classification`), which makes the suite more brittle and can fail in offline or restricted CI environments. Passing all unknown kwargs straight through as generation kwargs changes the previous API contract: callers that still use `generate_kwargs={...}` will now end up forwarding a literal `generate_kwargs` argument to `model.generate(...)`, which is not what the old implementation did and can break backward compatibility. This fixes the reported `None` case for encoder-decoder models, but it also removes the previous safeguard that avoided treating any model with an `encoder` attribute as a seq2seq model. For models where `self.model.encoder.main_input_name` is not the dataset key used by `model.generate(...)`, this could now select the wrong input field. Could we keep the fallback behavior by assigning `self.model.encoder.main_input_name` only when it is truthy, and otherwise falling back to `getattr(self.model, "main_input_name", "input_ids")`? That would address the bug while preserving compatibility with models that merely expose an `encoder` submodule.

This re-injects the new `token` value back into `kwargs` as `use_auth_token`, which fixes the immediate bug but also means downstream code still never sees a real `token` argument. That can become a compatibility trap if any later path in `AutoConfig.from_pretrained` or remote/custom config loading starts expecting `token` instead of `use_auth_token`. If possible, consider propagating `token` directly (or setting both keys during the transition) so the auto classes don't keep extending the deprecated internal API surface.

This re-inserts the deprecated `use_auth_token` key into `kwargs`, which means downstream calls still won't see the new `token` argument and the deprecation path remains duplicated here. Since the issue is specifically about auto classes supporting `token`, can we pass `token` through directly to the lower-level loaders that already support it (or update those call sites in this method) instead of translating it back to `use_auth_token`? Otherwise this fixes the public API superficially but keeps the old internal contract in place.

This re-injects the new `token` value back into `kwargs` under the deprecated `use_auth_token` name, which means downstream code will still only see the legacy argument. That fixes compatibility, but it also prevents this path from actually propagating `token` to any callee that already supports it and keeps the deprecated key alive internally. If possible, prefer forwarding `token` directly (or setting both keys temporarily with a clear precedence rule) so auto classes don't lag behind the rest of the API again.

The deprecation warning here is emitted without a `stacklevel`, so it will likely point users to this helper instead of their own call site. Please consider passing `stacklevel=2` (or the appropriate level) to make the warning actionable, consistent with other deprecation paths in the codebase.

The deprecation warning here is less informative than the one used elsewhere in the codebase for `use_auth_token` -> `token`. To keep behavior and messaging consistent across `from_pretrained` entry points, consider reusing the same warning text/pattern as in `modeling_utils`/`configuration_utils` (including guidance to use `token` instead). Inconsistent warnings can confuse users and make deprecation handling harder to track.

The new error message has duplicated wording (`execute execute` and `in that repo in that repo`), which makes the message look unpolished and harder to read. Please clean up the string before merging so users get a clear actionable error.

Catching only `AttributeError` may miss other platform-specific failures from this block. On some systems `signal.signal(...)`/`signal.alarm(...)` can raise `ValueError` (for example when used outside the main thread), which would still surface as an unhelpful low-level exception instead of the intended guidance. Consider broadening the handling to include the relevant `signal` setup errors or isolating the `SIGALRM` capability check more explicitly.

Using a string literal here fixes the immediate Keras error, but it also silently instantiates a real optimizer via `compile()`. Since this branch is specifically for the no-training path, it would be safer to make that intent explicit (e.g. use a named constant/helper and add a short comment explaining the Keras compatibility issue), or avoid passing `optimizer` to `compile()` at all when `do_train` is false if the surrounding code allows it. As written, `"sgd"` looks arbitrary and may be copied elsewhere without understanding why it's needed.

Using the string literal `"sgd"` here works around the Keras error, but it also relies on optimizer name resolution at compile time and makes the intent a bit opaque. Since this branch is specifically for non-training runs, consider either skipping `compile()` entirely when `do_train` is false (if evaluation/prediction paths allow it), or at least use an explicit optimizer object / named constant with a clearer comment so future changes don't accidentally depend on this placeholder optimizer.

Using a string literal here fixes the immediate Keras error, but it also silently instantiates a real optimizer for eval/predict-only runs. Since this branch is specifically the non-training path, it would be safer to make that intent explicit with a comment or a shared constant/helper (e.g. a named dummy optimizer) so future readers don't assume SGD is semantically required for QA. Otherwise this looks like an arbitrary training choice rather than a compile-time workaround.

Using the string literal `"sgd"` as a placeholder fixes the immediate `compile()` failure, but it also relies on Keras string-to-optimizer resolution and may instantiate an unnecessary optimizer with framework-dependent defaults. Since this branch is specifically the non-training path, it would be clearer and safer to pass an explicit no-op optimizer object (e.g. `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD()`) or guard the `compile()` call so we only provide an optimizer when training. Also, the comment `Just write anything` is a bit misleading for future readers—please document that this is a dummy optimizer required because recent Keras versions reject `None`.

Adding `--do_train/--do_eval/--do_predict` here fixes the reported confusion, but this example now runs all three phases at once while still requiring `--test_file`. That makes the "quick start" command heavier than necessary and can be misleading for users who only want to train/evaluate. Consider either splitting this into separate commands (train/eval vs predict) or explicitly noting that `--test_file` is only needed when `--do_predict` is enabled.

Using the string literal `"sgd"` fixes the immediate Keras error, but it also silently changes behavior if this code path ever reaches training: the model would train with a default SGD optimizer instead of failing fast or using the configured training arguments. Since this branch is specifically the `not do_train` case, it would be safer to either omit the `optimizer` argument when compiling for eval/predict-only flows, or instantiate a clearly dummy optimizer with a comment explaining that it is only needed to satisfy newer Keras versions and must never be used for training.

Using a string literal here fixes the immediate `None` issue, but it also silently instantiates a real optimizer with default settings. Since this branch is specifically the non-training path, it would be clearer and less error-prone to use an explicit dummy optimizer object (e.g. `tf.keras.optimizers.SGD()`) and document that it's only needed to satisfy `compile()`. That avoids relying on Keras string resolution and makes the intent/version-compatibility a bit more explicit.

This loop assumes `scaling_factor` is an integer because it's passed directly as the `range(..., step=...)` argument. In configs and implementations, RoPE scaling factors are often floats, so this test will break or become less representative if `scaling_factor` is changed to a non-integer value. Consider either using an integer-only local variable name to make that constraint explicit, or rewriting the check to iterate over positions independently of `range` step semantics.

There is a typo in the comment (`exlusively` -> `exclusively`). Also, since this tensor is only used to carry dtype/device into the rotary embedding call, it may be clearer to name it accordingly (for example `dummy_x`) to avoid suggesting it contains meaningful model input.

This test uses a 1-element tensor purely to carry dtype/device, but the rotary embedding forward here only depends on `x.dtype`/`x.device` and `seq_len`. Using a scalar-like placeholder makes the test less representative and can hide shape-related regressions if the embedding implementation starts validating input rank. Consider passing a minimal hidden-state-shaped tensor instead (for example `[1, 1, head_dim]`) so the test better matches real call sites.

The loop only checks positions that are exact multiples of `scaling_factor`, so it would miss many incorrect linear-scaling implementations that happen to align on those sampled indices. Since the issue being guarded against is an extra scaling factor, it would be safer to validate a denser set of positions (or all positions) against the expected mapping `new_position -> new_position / scaling_factor` to make the test more robust.

This placeholder tensor is only used to carry dtype/device into the RoPE forward pass, but its shape is unusually minimal (`[1]`). Since these embeddings typically expect an input tensor shaped like the attention activations, using a scalar-like tensor makes the test less representative and could hide shape-dependent regressions. Consider constructing `x` with a realistic shape such as `(1, 1, head_dim)` (or whatever the forward contract expects) while keeping the same dtype/device intent.

This loop indexes `linear_cos_long[:, new_position, :]` using `range(..., scaling_factor)`, which assumes `scaling_factor` is an integer. In the public API, RoPE scaling factors are typically floats (for example `2.0` from config), so this test may not generalize if the factor is changed to a non-integer value. It would be safer to either keep the test aligned with the config-style float input and compare against a selected set of integer positions, or explicitly document/enforce that this test only covers integer scaling factors.

This test relies on `x` only for dtype/device inference, but passing a 1D tensor here makes the test tightly coupled to the current rotary embedding implementation. If `Persimmon*RotaryEmbedding.forward` starts validating input rank/shape more strictly, this could fail for reasons unrelated to RoPE scaling. Consider constructing a minimal tensor with the expected hidden/head dimensions (or using an existing helper) so the test exercises the public API more realistically.

This assertion depends on `ntk_scaling_rope.inv_freq` having already been updated by the preceding long-sequence forward pass. That makes the test a bit stateful and fragile if the implementation ever stops mutating `inv_freq` in-place (for example, if it computes scaled frequencies per-call instead). Consider asserting on the returned `cos/sin` tensors only, or explicitly documenting/checking that the module is expected to update its internal cache after a long input.

This assertion depends on `ntk_scaling_rope.inv_freq` still reflecting the values computed for the long sequence. In these rotary embedding implementations, `inv_freq` is often mutated/reset based on the most recent forward call or cache update, which can make this check brittle and couple the test to internal state rather than observable behavior. It would be safer to assert on `ntk_cos_long`/`ntk_sin_long` directly, or to capture `inv_freq` immediately after the long forward and compare that snapshot instead of the live attribute.

Using an empty `_no_split_modules` enables the tests, but it also allows `device_map="auto"` to split inside any submodule. For EfficientNet, that can place tightly-coupled blocks (e.g. MBConv / stage blocks with residual paths) across devices, which may introduce extra cross-device transfers or break assumptions in more complex variants. Could we list the smallest meaningful block classes here instead of `[]`, or at least confirm that the architecture has no residual/compound blocks that should stay together?

This change removes support for non-`str` byte-like inputs in the base64 branch. Previously, `load_image` would accept either a `str` or raw `bytes` here (`image.encode() if isinstance(image, str) else image`), but now calling `.encode()` unconditionally will raise for `bytes`/`bytearray`. Even if the public type hint is `Union[str, PIL.Image.Image]`, this is a behavioral regression compared to the previous implementation. Consider preserving the old fallback, or explicitly normalizing both `str` and `bytes` while also handling embedded newlines from `encodebytes()`.

Using a bare `assert` here is risky because Python can strip assertions when run with optimizations (`-O`), which would silently remove this validation. Since this is guarding an unsupported runtime case, it would be safer to raise an explicit `ValueError` with the same message.

The type annotation for `past_key_values` here doesn't match the structure documented just above or the rest of the GPT-style outputs. For GPT-2, this is typically a tuple of layer tuples (keys/values), not a flat `List[torch.FloatTensor]`. Using the more precise nested tuple type would keep this output consistent with existing model outputs and avoid confusion for downstream users and type checkers.
Looks like a leftover debug statement. Printing during unit tests adds noise to the test output and can hide real failures in CI logs. Please remove this `print(...)` before merging.

This example introduces `embeddeds`, but it doesn't show how the embedded fields are represented on the entity/model itself. Since the issue is specifically about using embedded entities with `EntitySchema`, readers may still be unsure whether `AddressEntity` is a full entity or an embedded schema. Consider adding a short note or a minimal class/interface example for `Person`/`Address` clarifying that `address` is an embedded object and not a relation, to avoid confusion with the existing `relations` API.

`isArray` is declared as a required property here, but embedded definitions in `EntitySchema` are typically optional/minimal and this PR description doesn't indicate array embeddeds are fully supported. Making this mandatory is likely to break existing or intended schema declarations that omit it. Consider making it optional (and defaulting to `false` in the metadata builder) unless every caller is guaranteed to provide it.

Typing `embeddeds` as `[P in keyof T]?` is stricter than the existing `columns`/`relations` shape and may reject valid schema definitions for nested or prefixed embedded properties that don't map 1:1 to a top-level key on `T`. Since embedded schemas often describe flattened columns rather than direct runtime properties, consider whether this should use a looser string index (or otherwise mirror the flexibility of the rest of the schema API) to avoid compile-time limitations similar to the original issue.

This assigns `embeddedSchema.type` directly into `EmbeddedMetadataArgs.type`, but for `EntitySchema`-based embeddeds the `type` may itself be an `EntitySchema` instance rather than a class/function. The metadata pipeline for embeddeds typically expects a constructor/target, so passing the schema object through here can break resolution later. Consider normalizing this the same way relations/entities are usually normalized, e.g. using `embeddedSchema.type.options.target || embeddedSchema.type.options.name` when `type` is an `EntitySchema`.

Allowing `type` to return `EntitySchema` is necessary for schema-based embeddeds, but this widens the contract asymmetrically: `target` was updated to `Function | string`, while `type` now returns `Function | EntitySchema`. If downstream metadata builders still assume a constructor here, this can introduce type/runtime mismatches. Consider aligning this with the existing entity-schema patterns used elsewhere in metadata args (for example, permitting the schema target/name shape consistently, or using a shared `EntityTarget`-like type) so all consumers handle schema-based embeddeds uniformly.

Creating a new `EntitySchemaTransformer` inside every recursive embedded build can repeatedly re-transform the same schema tree and may become expensive for deeply nested or frequently built metadata. It also bypasses the shared `metadataArgsStorage` used elsewhere in this builder, which could lead to inconsistent behavior if the transformer logic diverges. Consider transforming entity schemas once up front and reusing the resulting `MetadataArgsStorage` here, or at least caching transformed embedded schemas by target/name.

When `type` is an `EntitySchema`, this now returns a plain `{}` instead of an instance of the schema's target class. That can break code relying on embedded objects having the target prototype, default field initializers, or instance methods. If the schema has a `target`, consider instantiating that target here (similar to the function case) and only falling back to `{}` when no constructible target is available.

Using a lazy factory here is fine, but this test file only covers the `EntitySchema` case. Since the issue report shows users trying to reference the embedded type/class directly (e.g. `type: Metadata`), it would be helpful to add a companion test that verifies whether direct schema/class references are intentionally unsupported or supported. Otherwise this implementation may pass while still leaving the original usage pattern ambiguous.

Using the Postgres-specific column type `"double precision"` in a shared functional test can make this test fail or become inapplicable for other drivers. Since the feature under test is embedded entity schema support rather than a Postgres type mapping, consider using a cross-driver numeric type (or restricting this test explicitly to Postgres) to keep the test portable.

This test only runs on MySQL, SQLite, and Postgres, but the feature being added is schema-level and should ideally be validated across the broader set of supported drivers that handle embeddeds. Restricting the coverage here may let driver-specific regressions slip through, especially since the original issue was reported for Postgres but the implementation likely affects metadata building globally. Consider either expanding `enabledDrivers` or documenting why other drivers are intentionally excluded.

This test schema only covers the `type: () => AddressSchema` form. Since the issue/feature is specifically about embedded entities in `EntitySchema`, it would be valuable to also add coverage for the direct schema/class form if that is intended to be supported, or otherwise document that only a factory returning an `EntitySchema` works here. Without that, this test may miss the original compile-time/API-usage problem reported in the issue.

This looks like a bug: `rawFieldSpecs` is derived from `propertyPath` *after* `propertyPath` has already been truncated to `propertyPath.slice(0, spIdx)`, so this will always produce an empty string. As a result, the JSON/cast suffix (e.g. ` ->'someNb'`) is lost and the generated ORDER BY still won't include the raw field spec. You probably want to slice from `fieldSpec` (or the original `propertyPath` value before reassignment) instead.

Same issue here: `rawFieldSpecs` is taken from `propertyPath` after `propertyPath` was shortened to the substring before the first space, so the suffix is dropped. This means expressions like `form.extraSettings ->'someNb'` will still be rewritten without the JSON operator. Consider slicing from `fieldSpec` (or storing the original string before mutating `propertyPath`).

This relation is marked `eager: true`, but the issue being tested is specifically about queries that include an explicit `JOIN` plus pagination. Using eager loading here can mask whether the join in the test is actually required for reproducing the bug, since TypeORM may load the relation automatically outside the query shape under test. Consider removing `eager: true` so the test depends only on the explicit join it builds.

The inverse side of this `OneToMany` looks incorrect. It currently points to `form.id`, but the inverse callback should reference the relation property on `Form` that owns the foreign key (likely something like `form.formTmpl`). Pointing at the primary key means TypeORM won't wire up the association metadata correctly, which can make this regression test pass/fail for the wrong reason or break relation loading entirely.

The property name `publicSettings` doesn't seem to match the relation used in the issue description/query (`form.formTmpl`). If this entity is meant to back that test case, using a name aligned with the actual relation would make the fixture clearer and avoid confusion about which association is being joined.

This assertion only verifies the first row and doesn't fully prove that ordering + pagination behaved correctly. A regression here could still return the wrong second row or an unstable order while keeping `result[0]` as `40`. Consider asserting the full ordered page, e.g. that the returned `someNb` values are `[40, 7]`, to make the test cover both ORDER BY and `.take(2)` semantics.

Switching from `Promise.all` to a fully sequential loop avoids the dangling-connection race, but it also changes the behavior of `createConnections` for all callers by removing parallel connection setup. That can noticeably slow down test/bootstrap paths and may be broader than necessary for fixing the cleanup issue. Consider preserving parallelism while still waiting for every attempt to settle (for example with `Promise.allSettled` plus explicit error propagation/cleanup), so we avoid both the race and the global performance regression.

This change downgrades the package version in the lockfile from `0.3.15` to `0.3.14`, which looks unrelated to the junction-table subscriber fix and can create release/versioning inconsistencies in the PR. Unless this PR is intentionally reverting the project version, please regenerate or restore `package-lock.json` so it matches the repository's current package version.

Using `env::args()` here means the generated header reflects the current process invocation rather than the normalized `pip_compile` options that actually produced the output. With the new `--no-header` compatibility flag this is less visible, but when headers are enabled it can still produce misleading or non-reproducible output in embedded/test contexts or when arguments are rewritten before reaching this function. Consider passing the rendered command/header text into `pip_compile` from the CLI layer instead of reconstructing it here.

Adding `--no-header` for pip-compile compatibility is useful, but this flag currently has no visible alias for the existing `--header` behavior if one exists elsewhere in the CLI/API. Please verify the downstream function signature and help output stay consistent with the rest of the command surface, and consider documenting whether this is a pure compatibility alias or a new behavior toggle to avoid ambiguity for users.

Passing negated booleans positionally makes this call harder to read and easy to break if the callee signature changes. Since `!args.no_annotate` and `!args.no_header` are adjacent and semantically inverted, consider introducing named locals (for example `include_annotations` / `include_header`) or a config struct so the intent is explicit and argument ordering mistakes are less likely.

Using `parent().unwrap_or(working_dir)` means a top-level include like `-r other.txt` will still be resolved against the caller-provided `working_dir` when `requirements_txt` has no parent component. That fallback can reintroduce the old behavior for relative input paths such as `RequirementsTxt::parse("dev.txt", cwd)`, where the containing file should effectively be resolved from the file path itself. Consider normalizing `requirements_txt` against `working_dir` first (or otherwise deriving an absolute containing directory) so sub-includes are always based on the actual containing file location.

This test covers nested `-r` resolution when the root file is passed as an absolute path (`parent_txt.path()`), but it doesn't exercise the edge case introduced by the new `parent().unwrap_or(working_dir)` fallback for relative root paths. Adding a test that calls `RequirementsTxt::parse` with a relative `requirements_txt` path from a different `working_dir` would help catch regressions where includes are still resolved relative to the working directory.

This test only exercises a nested `-r` include when the top-level file is passed as a relative path from the temp dir. The reported bug was specifically about invoking the command with an absolute path to the root requirements file, so this can still miss regressions where only absolute entrypoints are handled incorrectly. Consider passing `requirements_in.path()` (or otherwise invoking `compile` with an absolute path) to match the issue scenario and verify resolution is based on the containing file rather than the current working directory.

This helper treats any expression involving `extra` and a provided extra as always true, but it doesn't check the operator. For example, `extra != "dev"` (or `"dev" != extra`) would incorrectly return `true` when `dev` is present, causing the marker to be dropped/simplified in the wrong direction. Please gate this logic on `MarkerOperator::Equal` (and consider whether `in`/`not in` need explicit handling too), or add tests covering non-equality operators.

Using a hard-coded concurrency of `50` here may be too aggressive for PEP 517 metadata extraction, since each task can spawn isolated builds, hit the network, and consume significant disk/CPU resources. For `uv pip compile pyproject.toml` this is probably fine today, but for multiple source trees this could lead to resource spikes or poor behavior on constrained systems. Consider making this limit configurable or deriving it from the build context / available parallelism instead of baking in a fixed value.

When we fall back to PEP 517 metadata extraction, the requested extras are dropped here (`extras: FxHashSet::default()`). That means downstream metadata resolution no longer knows which extras the user asked for, so optional dependencies exposed only through dynamic metadata may be omitted. Consider preserving the requested extras in the specification even when static parsing is not possible.

Conditioning extra validation on `source_trees.is_empty()` means invalid `--extra` values are no longer rejected whenever any source tree is present. Since `SourceTreeResolver` is now responsible for resolving those projects, we should still validate the requested extras after `resolve()` returns (or have the resolver surface unknown extras directly), otherwise typos in extras can silently pass for dynamic-metadata projects.

Skipping the `site_packages.satisfies(...)` fast path when `source_trees` is non-empty is the right safety fix, but it also means we now always pay the metadata extraction cost for source trees even if the installed environment already satisfies them. If that's intentional, consider adding a short comment here explaining why source trees can't participate in the existing satisfaction check; otherwise this looks like an accidental regression in the install short-circuit behavior.

This passes only `extras` into `SourceTreeResolver`, but `pip install` also accepts dependency-group style selectors elsewhere in the command surface. If source trees can expose optional dependencies through groups as well as extras, this may resolve a different dependency set than the rest of the command. Please double-check that `SourceTreeResolver` is receiving all relevant selectors, or document why extras alone are sufficient here.

Using `ExtrasSpecification::None` here means `pip sync` will ignore any extras requested on source tree inputs when resolving their metadata. That can lead to syncing an incomplete environment for inputs like `./pkg[dev]` or a source tree whose requirements were selected via extras. If `RequirementsSpecification` already parsed extras for `source_trees`, consider threading those through instead of hard-coding `None`, or explicitly rejecting extras on source tree inputs if they are unsupported.

This test now asserts a fully resolved Poetry dependency graph (`anyio`, `idna`, `sniffio`) instead of the previous warning-only behavior, but the test name/comment still only says "Compile a `pyproject.toml` file with a `poetry` section." Since the PR is specifically about falling back to PEP 517 for non-static metadata, it would be clearer to make that explicit in the test name or doc comment. Otherwise, future readers may not understand that this is validating backend metadata extraction rather than direct `tool.poetry` parsing.

Using `debug_assert!` here means absolute paths are only rejected in debug builds. In release builds, an absolute `relative_path` would bypass `site_packages.join(...)` and write outside the target environment, which is especially risky now that install locations can come from arbitrary interpreters. Consider turning this into a real runtime validation that returns an error instead of relying on a debug-only check.

This condition looks inverted relative to the comment and the previous implementation. `is_virtualenv()` returns `true` when `prefix != base_prefix`, but the old code used `if self.prefix == self.base_prefix` for the non-venv case. With the new code, virtual environments will take the `else` branch instead of the venv-specific `include/site/pythonX.Y` path described in the comment. Consider changing this to `if !self.is_virtualenv()` or swapping the branches so the venv-specific layout is only used for actual virtual environments.

This unconditional `externally_managed()` check may reject installs into any interpreter passed via the new `--python` flag, including explicit user-targeted system interpreters. That could undermine the main goal of this change (`install into arbitrary Python interpreters`) unless the intended behavior is to always enforce PEP 668 here. If that is intentional, it would be good to gate this behind an override (for example, a `--break-system-packages`-style flag) or at least ensure the CLI/docs/tests cover this new failure mode for `--python` on system Python installs.

This new guard changes `pip sync` behavior for any target interpreter, including the new `--python` flow, but it unconditionally rejects externally managed environments. That may be surprising for users who are explicitly targeting a system interpreter to install into, and it can also make `pip sync` inconsistent with other pip subcommands unless they enforce the same check. Please confirm this is the intended policy for `sync` and, if so, consider wiring through the actual `EXTERNALLY-MANAGED` message (or an override such as `--break-system-packages`) rather than returning a generic error here.

This new guard changes `pip uninstall` behavior for any interpreter marked `EXTERNALLY-MANAGED`, but the PR description and issue are about enabling installs into arbitrary interpreters. Blocking uninstalls as well may be an unintended regression, since users may still need to remove packages from such environments. If this is intentional, it would be good to align it with pip's semantics (for example, only block when not explicitly overridden) and document/test the uninstall case separately.

This change now routes all non-editable direct URL requirements through metadata lookup, which is good for enforcing `Requires-Python`, but it also removes the previous wheel-filename fast path entirely. That means direct wheel URLs now depend on the distribution being registered in `index.distributions`; if that metadata is not populated for some wheel URLs, resolution will fail with `Unregistered` where it previously succeeded by parsing the version from the filename. Please confirm that direct wheel URLs always have metadata available at this point, or keep a wheel-specific fallback that still enforces `Requires-Python` when metadata is present.

The comment says this creates an "editable package", but the test is exercising a direct URL requirement (`example @ <path>`) rather than an editable install. That mismatch makes the intent harder to follow, especially given the issue/PR scope is specifically about direct URL requirements. Consider renaming `editable_dir` and updating this comment to refer to a local/direct URL package instead.

The comment says this creates an editable package, but the test is exercising a direct URL requirement (`example @ <path>`) rather than an editable install. This mismatch makes the intent harder to follow, especially since the issue/PR is specifically about direct URL requirements. Consider renaming `editable_dir` and updating the comment to refer to a direct URL/local path package instead.

This test currently asserts the old, incorrect behavior (`success: true`) even though the issue/PR is specifically about rejecting direct URL requirements whose `Requires-Python` is incompatible. Leaving it this way means the regression isn't actually covered and the test will continue to pass if enforcement is missing. Please update the snapshot to expect a failure and verify the error message mentions the `Requires-Python` mismatch.

The comment says this creates an editable package, but the requirement written below is a direct URL (`example @ <path>`) without `-e`. That mismatch makes the test intent harder to follow, especially since this PR is about direct URL requirements rather than editables. Consider renaming `editable_dir`/updating the comment to refer to a local direct URL package instead.

The new `debug_assert!` documents the expectation well, but it only fires in debug builds. Since `parse_path` now relies on `base_dir` being absolute to resolve relative paths correctly, passing a relative base in release builds will silently produce another relative path and can lead to incorrect URL normalization. Consider either normalizing `base_dir` here (e.g. making it absolute before joining) or returning an error when it isn't absolute, so the contract is enforced consistently in production too.

This now resolves workspace member paths relative to `workspace.root()`, which is correct for workspace packages, but it changes the meaning of the `project_dir` parameter passed into `path_source`. Since `path_source` is also used for non-workspace `Source::Path` entries and its parameter is still named `project_dir`, the API is now misleading and easy to misuse. Consider renaming the parameter to something like `base_dir` (and updating call sites) to make the relative-path semantics explicit and avoid future bugs where a caller passes the wrong base directory.

This fallback appears to add the `pyproject.toml` file itself to `source_trees`, whereas the success path above uses the containing directory (`dir`). Elsewhere in this function, dynamic metadata is described as requiring a build from the project root, so using the file path here is likely inconsistent and may break downstream code that expects a source tree directory rather than a manifest file path. Consider using `dir.to_path_buf()` here as well.

This deduplication happens before `absolutize_path`, so the same workspace member can still be processed multiple times if two globs resolve to different relative spellings of the same path (for example via `.`/`..` segments or symlinks). Since the issue description requires validating workspace membership by normalized paths, it would be safer to normalize first and insert the normalized path into `seen`.

This test appears to be pointing at the `bird-feeder` package again, even though the test name suggests it should exercise installing `albatross` from within the root workspace. As written, it duplicates coverage from `test_albatross_root_workspace_bird_feeder` and won't catch regressions for selecting the `albatross` member/root package. Consider changing `current_dir` to the intended `albatross` package/root path so the assertions match the scenario being tested.

This size regression is pretty large: the `Dist` cap jumps from 248 to 336 bytes, and `SourceDist` also grows substantially. Since the goal here is to avoid reparsing URLs, it may be worth checking whether storing both the original `VerbatimUrl` and fully owned parsed fields (`Url`, `PathBuf`, boxed `GitUrl`) on every variant is more than we need. If these types are carried widely during resolution, this could have a noticeable memory impact. Consider whether some of these fields can be shared/boxed/derived lazily, or at least add a comment documenting the expected increase and why it's acceptable.

Using `Url::parse(url).unwrap()` in a test hard-codes a second parsing path that can diverge from the `VerbatimUrl` parsing behavior this change is trying to preserve. If `DirectUrlBuiltDist` is meant to carry the parsed URL corresponding to the verbatim one, it would be safer to derive `location` from the already-constructed `VerbatimUrl` (or a shared helper) so the test exercises the same conversion path as production code instead of independently reparsing the string.

The error message here is misleading. `ParsedUrl::Archive` can represent more than just HTTPS archive URLs, and this branch is really rejecting any non-archive URL shape rather than specifically non-HTTPS input. Consider changing this to something like `bail!("Expected an archive URL")` or validating the scheme explicitly if HTTPS-only is actually required.

Constructing a fresh `GitUrl` here can diverge from the original parsed URL state carried by `RequirementSource::Git` (for example, if `GitUrl` later gains additional parsed fields beyond repository/reference). Since this PR is about preserving parsed URL information across resolution, it would be safer to pass through an existing parsed git URL object from the requirement source instead of reconstructing it from parts.

This TODO points to a potential behavioral regression: `RequirementSource::Path` carries an `editable` flag, but it's being discarded and `Dist::from_file_url(..., false, ...)` hard-codes the non-editable case. If lookahead is ever invoked for editable path requirements, metadata resolution may diverge from the original requirement. Can we thread `editable` through here (or explicitly document/assert that lookahead never handles editable requirements) instead of silently dropping it?

Extension matching is case-sensitive here, unlike the `.tar` check below which uses `eq_ignore_ascii_case`. That means valid-looking inputs like `PACKAGE.WHL` or `sdist.ZIP` will now be rejected at parse time even if they may have worked elsewhere before. Consider normalizing the extension or using `eq_ignore_ascii_case` consistently for all supported distribution extensions.

The source distribution extension checks are also case-sensitive (`zip`, `gz`, `bz2`, etc.), while `is_tar` already treats the inner `.tar` segment case-insensitively. This inconsistency can cause surprising parse failures for filenames with uppercase or mixed-case extensions. It would be safer to normalize the extracted extension once before matching.

This slices off the suffix based only on the provided `extension` length, but it never verifies that `filename` actually ends with that extension. If a caller passes a mismatched extension (e.g. `Zip` for a `.tar.gz` filename), we'll silently truncate the wrong number of characters and parse garbage instead of returning an extension error. Since `parse` now accepts the extension externally, it would be safer to validate with `strip_suffix` against the expected `.{extension}` here before deriving `stem`.

Switching from `parse_url(...).is_ok()` to `looks_like_unnamed_requirement(...)` broadens the set of inputs that are treated as unnamed requirements. In particular, the new helper now returns `true` for any token containing `/` or `\\`, so plain relative paths like `foo/bar` will now produce the specific "URL requirement must be preceded by a package name" error instead of falling through to normal package-name parsing. If that behavior is intentional, it would be good to add/adjust tests for local path requirements here; otherwise this may regress valid non-URL inputs by classifying them too early.

This test now uses `https://localhost:8080/`, which changes more than just the extension-validation behavior being exercised. If URL scheme handling or HTTPS-specific formatting ever changes, this test could fail for reasons unrelated to the issue. Consider keeping the original `http://localhost:8080/` (or adding coverage for both schemes) so the test stays focused on validating the unsupported direct-URL extension/path parsing.

This snapshot now expects the verbatim URL to be rewritten to `git+https://...git`, which is a behavior change beyond just validating extensions at parse time. Given the issue is about improving the error for `uv add https://.../foo.git`, it would be worth confirming that preserving the original user input isn't important here; otherwise this snapshot may be locking in normalization that could affect display, round-tripping, or error messages.

This warning drops the parse error context that was previously available from `DistFilename::try_from_filename`, so we'll now log only the raw filename for every rejected entry. That makes it harder to distinguish unsupported extensions from other filename parsing failures and weakens diagnostics. If possible, consider returning/logging the actual parse error here (similar to the `File::try_from` branch below) so skipped files remain debuggable.

This now rejects any non-archive URL during lowering, which fixes the confusing `.git` case, but it also changes behavior for direct URLs that don't encode the distribution type in the path (for example signed/download endpoints where the filename is only present in query params or after redirects). If those are expected to remain supported elsewhere, validating purely via `url.path()` here may be too strict. Consider confirming that `VerbatimParsedUrl`/downstream resolution never relied on redirect-derived filenames before enforcing this at parse time.

Parsing the archive type from `filename` before issuing the request improves the error message, but this now rejects valid download URLs that don't expose the real artifact name in the path (for example presigned URLs, redirect endpoints, or URLs that rely on a `Content-Disposition` filename). Since this code is for managed Python downloads, please confirm all configured URLs always end with a supported source archive extension, or consider falling back to response metadata / the final redirected URL before failing here.

Using `unreachable!()` here assumes the parser can never classify a local path as a wheel, but that invariant isn't obvious from this match site and could be broken by future parser changes. If a `.whl` path reaches this branch, we'll panic instead of returning a user-facing validation error. Consider either handling `DistExtension::Wheel` explicitly or converting this into a fallible error path with a clear message so invalid/unsupported requirement forms don't crash resolution.

Same concern here: `unreachable!()` turns an unexpected wheel-classified archive URL into a panic. Since this PR is about surfacing extension problems at parse time, it would be safer to preserve that behavior with an explicit error instead of relying on an internal invariant that may drift over time.

Using `DistExtension::from_path(url.as_ref())?` here will still classify any URL ending in a supported archive suffix as an archive requirement, even if it's actually a VCS URL like `https://.../foo.git`. The issue being fixed is specifically about confusing handling of Git URLs passed without an explicit `git+` prefix, and this line appears to preserve that ambiguity because `.git` is also a valid source distribution extension. Consider ensuring direct URLs are disambiguated from Git sources before extension-based archive parsing, or rejecting plain `*.git` URLs here with a more specific error/message.

The test name/comment here is misleading: this case is exercising a URL **without** an extension, but the comment says "without unsupported extension". That reads like the extension is valid. Renaming the comment to something like "Install a package without an extension" would make the intent clearer and keep it aligned with the `no_extension` test name.

Filtering out `false` forks here changes the previous invariant that a single `ResolverMarkers::Fork(_)` path was impossible. If all candidate resolutions are unsatisfiable, this will now return an empty fork set and let execution continue, which may be interpreted downstream as "no forks" rather than "resolution impossible". Please verify callers handle an empty collection correctly, or consider surfacing an explicit error/early return when every fork is unsatisfiable.

This serialization silently drops any `MarkerTree` whose `contents()` is `None`, so the serialized sequence length can be smaller than `self.0.len()`. That can make round-tripping lossy and hide invalid/unsupported marker states in config output. Consider either serializing via `Display`/`to_string()` for every marker, or returning a serialization error when a marker has no contents instead of omitting it.

The declared option type here (`str | list[str]`) looks inconsistent with the field type and docs. `environments` is deserialized as `Option<SupportedEnvironments>`, and the surrounding schema/docs describe it as a list of markers. Unless `SupportedEnvironments` explicitly supports a single string as well, this metadata will advertise a config shape that may not actually parse or validate. Consider aligning the option metadata with the real accepted format (likely `list[str]`), or ensure the deserializer/schema also supports a bare string.

This lookup assumes the root package can be found by matching `workspace_package.root()` to `self.install_path()`. If the workspace root is virtual / non-package, or if path normalization differs, this will silently return `None` even when `tool.uv.environments` is configured at the workspace root. Since supported environments are a workspace-level setting, it may be safer to read them directly from the root `pyproject.toml` / workspace metadata rather than inferring the root package from `packages.values()`.

This fallback only uses `supported_environments` when there is no reusable lock at all. But the PR description says we should ignore the lockfile entirely when supported environments change, and `ValidatedLock::validate` now returns `Unusable` in that case. Since `reusable_lock` excludes `Unusable`, this is mostly correct, but it also means `--upgrade-package` keeps reusing the old fork markers even if the current supported environments are narrower and would allow pruning more branches. Consider intersecting reused `fork_markers()` with the current `environments`, or at least documenting why `Preferable` locks should continue to drive the initial forks instead of the user-specified supported environments.

This error only mentions the current platform, but the feature being added is based on arbitrary supported-environment marker expressions, which can also exclude a lock on Python implementation, version, architecture, etc. Consider wording this more generically (for example, "The current environment is not compatible...") so the message stays accurate when the incompatibility is not strictly platform-related.

Using `filter_map(MarkerTree::contents)` here can silently drop supported environments that don't have string contents, which may produce an incomplete or even empty error message despite the lockfile having constraints. Consider preserving all environments in the message (for example via `to_string()` or a fallback when `contents()` is `None`) so users can reliably see why their platform is rejected.

This `--locked` assertion expects a fresh resolution (`Resolved 7 packages`) even though the preceding step already wrote a matching lockfile. That makes the test less effective at verifying lockfile reuse and could mask regressions where `--locked` unnecessarily re-resolves instead of validating the existing lock. Consider asserting behavior that distinguishes reuse from recomputation (for example, no resolution work / no network access, or at least a message indicating the existing lockfile was accepted unchanged).

The comment says this should fail against an "unstable" environment, but the test and issue are about an unsupported environment constrained by `[tool.uv].environments`. "Unstable" is misleading here and could confuse future readers about what behavior is actually being validated. Consider renaming the comment (and possibly the test) to use "unsupported" or "incompatible" instead.

The documented default value here looks inconsistent with the issue/PR behavior. The description says uv resolves for all possible environments by default, but `[]` usually reads as 'no environments'. If the actual semantics are 'unset means all environments', it would be clearer to document this as something like `null`/`None`/`all environments` instead of an empty list, or explicitly explain that an empty list is treated as no constraint.

The new `environments` field is only typed as an array of strings here, so invalid marker expressions will still pass schema validation and fail later at runtime. Since this option is central to constraining resolution, it would be helpful to either document the expected grammar more explicitly here (for example, that these must be valid PEP 508 environment marker expressions) or add a validation pattern / format if feasible, to catch configuration mistakes earlier.

This import looks unused in the added logic and will likely trigger an `unused_imports` warning/error under the project's lint settings. It should be removed unless it's needed elsewhere in this file.

The new hint still doesn't address the reported editable-install case from the issue. For `uv pip install -e . --all-extras`, this branch will suggest `<dir>[extra]` syntax, but `--all-extras` has no equivalent in that form and the original problem is that editable sources should be considered valid for extras when a local project metadata file exists. If this PR is intentionally only improving messaging, the error should at least explicitly mention that `--extra/--all-extras` cannot be combined with `-e <dir>` and how to achieve the same result; otherwise this condition may be masking a functional bug rather than just a UX issue.

This assertion appears to lock in the old behavior from the reported issue: `-e <dir> --all-extras` is expected to fail with the generic "requires a pyproject.toml" message, even though the editable source does have project metadata. Since the issue specifically calls out editable installs with extras as a valid/desirable workflow, this test may be asserting the wrong outcome and could prevent the intended fix. Consider updating this case to expect success (or at least a more specific error only for truly invalid sources) once the command-line handling is corrected.

The error message has a grammar issue: `Workspace does contain any buildable packages` reads incorrectly and is likely meant to be `Workspace does not contain any buildable packages`. Since this is user-facing guidance added by the PR, fixing the wording will avoid confusion.

The error message here appears to have a grammar typo: `Workspace does contain any buildable packages.` likely should be `Workspace does not contain any buildable packages.` As written, the snapshot locks in an incorrect user-facing message, so this test may either validate the wrong behavior or need updating once the implementation is corrected.

This shell snippet uses `source /etc/os-release`, but OpenWrt's default shell is BusyBox `ash`, where `source` is not guaranteed to exist. Since this detection path is specifically for OpenWrt-like systems, using the POSIX-compatible `.` command would be safer and avoid false negatives on guests that have `/etc/os-release` but no `source` builtin.

This changes `import.meta.glob` handling from the aggregated HTML/SFC wrapper module to each extracted script block individually. That fixes the name-collision issue, but it may also change the base file used by `transformGlob` for non-HTML files with multiple script contexts (notably Vue/Svelte), because every virtual script now passes the same `path` instead of preserving whether the glob came from the wrapper or a specific script context. Please add/verify tests for `import.meta.glob` inside multiple `<script>` blocks across html/vue/svelte to ensure the generated imports and dependency scan results remain identical.

Skipping this test avoids a failure, but it also removes coverage for the inline-worker path in a PR that changes relative asset URL handling. Since the TODO says inline workers should inline assets, this can mask a regression rather than document expected behavior. Consider keeping the test and updating its assertion to match the intended inline-asset behavior, or add a dedicated follow-up test that explicitly verifies the current expected output for inline workers.

Guarding the all-reduce with `self.world_size > 1` avoids unnecessary communication, but this changes the behavior of `reduce_results=True` in the single-rank case. Please verify that any downstream code relying on the reduced tensor being a distinct buffer (or on the reduce op's autograd/distributed semantics) still behaves correctly when we now return `output_parallel` directly. A small comment here explaining that this is an intentional no-op optimization for TP=1 would help prevent confusion.

This match is too broad: `if lib_name in line` can also pick up unrelated entries whose path or soname merely contains the requested name as a substring. Since `ldconfig -p` output is structured, it would be safer to match the soname token exactly (e.g. the first whitespace-delimited field) before taking the resolved path. Otherwise we may return the wrong library path and make the integrity check flaky on systems with multiple similarly named libs.

Relying on a hard-coded `/sbin/ldconfig` path is not very portable across Linux distributions/containers; some environments only expose `ldconfig` on `PATH` or under `/usr/sbin`. Since this helper is now on the critical path for NCCL discovery, consider resolving `ldconfig` via `shutil.which()` (with a fallback list) and handling `FileNotFoundError` gracefully so we can still fall back to `LD_LIBRARY_PATH` instead of failing early.

Changing the return type here fixes the immediate constructor mismatch, but it also changes the public contract of this override from `ModelInputForCPU` to `ModelInputForCPUWithSamplingMetadata`. Please verify that the corresponding base/interface method and all call sites are updated consistently; otherwise this can introduce an LSP/type inconsistency where code typed against the base runner still expects `ModelInputForCPU`. If the richer type is now always required on CPU, it would be safer to update the shared abstraction as well or document why this override intentionally narrows/changes the return type.

Switching from `FlagInitialModulesAsUsedPlugin` to `FlagAllModulesAsUsedPlugin` fixes the reported DLL/runtime issue, but it also broadens the scope from initial modules to every module in the DLL compilation. Since the PR notes this as a breaking change because DLLs may become larger, it would be good to add or reference a regression test here that proves this is required specifically for `DllPlugin` and that `entryOnly: true` still preserves the previous tree-shaking behavior. Otherwise this change could silently disable useful pruning for all non-`entryOnly` DLL builds.

Setting `module.used = true` and `module.usedExports = true` directly here fixes the reported DLL/tree-shaking issue, but it also bypasses the normal export-usage analysis for every module in the compilation. Since this plugin is intended for DLL builds only, it would be safer to either document that scope very explicitly or guard against accidental broader use, otherwise consumers could unexpectedly disable tree-shaking and increase bundle size across non-DLL compilations.

This condition changes the reset semantics from "always clear usage before recomputing" to "preserve any truthy `used` value from an earlier phase". That matches the DLL fix, but it also means this plugin now depends on `module.used` being either `false` or a meaningful preexisting marker. If `module.used` can hold stale truthy state from a previous compilation pass/watch rebuild, those modules would be skipped from reset and remain incorrectly marked as used. Please confirm this hook only runs with fresh module state, or narrow the check to the specific DLL pre-marking case instead of any truthy value.

This file disables tests for the `0-create-dll` step entirely. Given this PR is fixing a regression around DLL side effects, skipping assertions here reduces coverage for the DLL creation phase and can let regressions slip through unnoticed. If this step truly cannot host runtime assertions, consider adding a brief comment explaining why `noTests` is required and ensure the behavior is validated from a later step in this config case.

The `test` regex is very broad and matches any path containing `0-create-dll` followed by any character before `module`/`dependency` because `.` is unescaped. That can make this fixture depend on path formatting and accidentally apply `sideEffects: false` to unintended files. Consider escaping the dot and anchoring the pattern more precisely, e.g. `/0-create-dll\.(module|dependency)$/` or matching the actual fixture filenames.

This test fixture only assigns the imported value to a local variable, which may not be enough to assert the runtime behavior that regressed in #10475. Since the bug was an `undefined` export at runtime, it would be stronger to add an observable assertion/use here (for example exporting `x` or checking a property on `constants`) so the test fails if the DLL build still drops the module or export.

This fixture file appears to include line-number prefixes (`1:` / `2:`) as literal source text. If that's intentional from the diff rendering, ignore this; otherwise the file won't be valid JavaScript/ESM and the test will fail for the wrong reason. The contents should just be the export statements without the prefixes.

Using `console.log` in a test fixture can make the test output noisy and may hide whether the regression is actually covered. If the goal is to keep `constants` as a live usage so tree-shaking doesn't remove it, consider exporting/consuming it from the test assertion instead, or add a short comment explaining that this side effect is intentional for reproducing the DLL bug.

This fixture package is missing a `name` (and ideally a `version`). While webpack can often resolve a package from the folder structure alone, omitting these fields makes the test fixture less representative of a real npm package and can cause confusion or brittle behavior in tooling that reads `package.json`. Consider adding at least `"name": "test-package"` and `"version": "1.0.0"`.

This fixture file appears to include line-number prefixes (`1:`, `2:`, `3:`) as part of the actual source. If that is intentional from the diff rendering, ignore this; otherwise the test module is not valid JavaScript and won't exercise the DLL/export behavior correctly. The file content should just be the function export without the numeric prefixes.

This fixture appears to include line-number prefixes (`1:`, `2:`, etc.) as part of the file contents. If that's intentional, the file won't be valid JavaScript and the test may fail for the wrong reason. Please remove the prefixes so the fixture contains only the actual module source.

This line appears to include a prefixed line-number string (`1:`) as part of the file contents. As written, `1:exports.noTests = true;` is not valid JavaScript and will cause the test config to fail to parse. It should likely just be `exports.noTests = true;`.

This test writes its manifest into `test/js/config/dll-plugin/issue-10475.json`, which is outside this config case directory and appears to be a shared location. That can make the test order-dependent or leave stale artifacts behind if another test reuses the same path. Consider writing the manifest into the current test folder (for example via `path.resolve(__dirname, "issue-10475.json")`) so the fixture is self-contained and isolated.

This test only verifies that requiring the DLL entry does not throw, but the reported regression was about an undefined export at runtime after tree-shaking. As written, it may pass even if some modules are still omitted from the DLL as long as the entry loads. Could we assert on an exported value or inspect the DLL manifest/runtime to ensure the affected module graph is actually present?

Using a relative path string as the `name` here makes the test depend on the exact emitted DLL filename/location from the sibling config. That can be brittle if output paths or test execution context change. If the intent is to validate the runtime contract from the generated manifest, consider deriving this from the actual DLL config/output or documenting why this hard-coded path is required for the test setup.

Using `TODO[]` here weakens the type information right where this change is expanding support to `StaticBlock`. Since the surrounding hooks and logic now explicitly handle `MethodDefinition | PropertyDefinition | StaticBlock`, this cast should be updated to that concrete union instead of a placeholder. Otherwise we lose static checking for accesses like `item.value`, `item.key`, and `item.static`, and it becomes easier to miss AST-shape differences in future changes.

This test enables the Control modifier for multi-select, but it never resets it afterward. Because the same `VisualTestContext` is reused for the subsequent scenario in this test, later `select_path_with_mark` calls depend on the modifier state leaking across steps, which makes the test more brittle and less explicit about its setup. Consider resetting modifiers after each scenario and re-enabling them only where needed.

Restoring `SCOMPARE1` here looks correct, but this assumes the save path also writes `BSA_SCOMPARE1_OFF` into the saved context. If the corresponding `_save_context` change and stack-frame offset definition are not updated in lockstep, this will read an uninitialized/incorrect slot and corrupt the restored compare state. Please verify the save-side and `BSA_*` layout were extended consistently.

The new offset macro is named `BSA_SCOMPARE1_OFF`, but the comment block above documents this slot as `SCOMPARE` and the issue/PR text also refer to the `SCOMPARE1` register specifically. It would be good to make the naming/documentation consistent here to avoid confusion when this macro is used from the save/restore assembly. If the architectural register name is `SCOMPARE1`, consider updating the comment block to match.

This comparison mixes pointer/integer types and assumes a simple address ordering to infer which stack is active. Besides being brittle, it can misclassify the active stack if PSP is temporarily outside the expected range or if future layout changes break the "privileged stack is higher" invariant. It would be safer to cast both operands explicitly to the same integer type and, ideally, verify PSP falls within the privileged stack bounds rather than only checking `>= priv_stack_start`.

Returning `-EAGAIN` for any non-binary websocket frame can cause MQTT to treat a valid control frame (for example ping/pong) as a transport read error and disconnect, which is the behavior this change is trying to avoid. Since `mqtt_read_message_chunk()` only treats `0` as a clean close and otherwise propagates negative values as errors, this should likely consume/ignore non-data frames and continue reading until a binary frame is received (or no data is available in non-blocking mode), rather than surfacing them as an error.

This still hard-codes the response path to the global UART shell backend returned by `shell_backend_uart_get_ptr()`. That fixes the console-vs-printk issue, but it doesn't address the original bug for shells running on a non-default backend or when multiple shell instances exist, because `smp_shell_tx_raw()` ignores its `arg` parameter and doesn't use the shell instance that actually received the request. Please consider passing the active shell/backend context through `arg` and transmitting on that specific device instead of always using the global UART backend.

This `if` condition is fragile in CMake. Expanding `${CONFIG_TLS_CREDENTIALS}` directly can fail or behave unexpectedly when the variable is unset or contains a non-boolean value. Since this is a Kconfig-generated CMake variable, it's safer and more idiomatic to test it as `if(CONFIG_TLS_CREDENTIALS)` instead.

Enabling `NEWLIB_LIBC` makes this test depend on a specific libc implementation, which can unnecessarily reduce board/CI coverage for a socket/TLS API test. Unless the test code actually requires newlib-specific behavior, consider using the minimal libc or omitting this setting so the test remains focused on networking/TLS functionality.

`NET_SOCKETS_TLS_MAX_CONTEXTS=3` is quite tight for a test that appears to use both a TLS client and server in the same process/threading setup. Depending on how many contexts mbedTLS allocates internally during handshake/retry paths, this can make the test flaky or harder to extend. It may be safer to leave a bit more headroom here.

This registers the client certificate using `TLS_CREDENTIAL_SERVER_CERTIFICATE`, which looks incorrect for the client-authentication cases exercised by `TLS_PEER_VERIFY_OPTIONAL/REQUIRED`. The client cert should be added with the client certificate credential type (e.g. `TLS_CREDENTIAL_CLIENT_CERTIFICATE`), otherwise the TLS stack may not present it during mutual-auth handshakes and the test can fail or not actually validate the intended path.

The `TOOLCHAIN_HAS_NEWLIB` filter makes this test unavailable on all non-newlib configurations, but the issue/PR description doesn't indicate a libc-specific dependency. If the test only needs mbedTLS/socket support, this may unnecessarily reduce CI coverage. Please confirm the actual requirement here and consider filtering on the relevant networking/TLS Kconfig capabilities instead.

This new Kconfig option appears to preserve the old non-compliant behavior, but the PR description says the controller should now accept repeated scan enable/disable requests per the Bluetooth spec.
