Since Kconfig symbols default to `n`, adding this option without selecting it anywhere means the behavior silently changes for all existing builds. If that is intentional, it would be good to make that explicit in the help text (e.g. that enabling this restores legacy strict behavior for compatibility/testing). Otherwise, consider naming it to reflect legacy behavior more clearly or setting an explicit default to avoid ambiguity.

This unconditionally converts `CMD_DISALLOWED` into success for all scan-enable requests when strict mode is off. Per the PR description/spec, only the idempotent cases should be accepted: disabling when already disabled, or enabling when already enabled (with duplicate-filter changes taking effect). As written, this can also mask unrelated `CMD_DISALLOWED` failures returned by `ll_scan_enable()`, making real controller state/conflict issues invisible to the host. Please gate this on the actual requested/current scan state, or have `ll_scan_enable()` return a more specific status for the already-enabled/disabled case.

Same concern here for extended scanning: mapping every `CMD_DISALLOWED` to success is broader than the Bluetooth-spec behavior described in the PR. It should only succeed for no-op/idempotent enable/disable requests, otherwise this may hide legitimate failures from `ll_scan_enable(cmd->enable, cmd->duration, cmd->period)` and make debugging state/race issues harder.

Conditionally compiling enum members changes the numeric values and even the presence of public API constants based on Kconfig. That can make code using `NET_OPT_*`/`TCP_OPT_*` harder to write portably and may break assumptions in switch statements or shared code paths. Consider keeping the enum values unconditionally defined in the public header and rejecting unsupported options at runtime or in the implementation when `CONFIG_NET_TCP_KEEPALIVE` is disabled.

Introducing a separate `net_context_tcp_option` enum/API for TCP-only options creates an inconsistency with the existing `net_context_set_option()` interface, which already carries socket-level options like `NET_OPT_TCP_NODELAY`. Splitting TCP options across two APIs may complicate callers and duplicate implementation paths. Please clarify why keepalive-related TCP options cannot be added to the existing option mechanism, or document the intended separation between generic and TCP-specific options.

Adding `SO_KEEPALIVE` here can create an ABI/API compatibility issue because its numeric value differs from the widely used POSIX/Linux socket constant (`9`). Applications that share code or prebuilt components with other libc/socket environments may rely on the standard value. If Zephyr intentionally diverges, it would be safer to document that clearly; otherwise consider matching the conventional constant values for compatibility.

`TCP_KEEPIDLE` is assigned `2`, but the conventional socket API value is typically `4` (with `TCP_KEEPINTVL=5` and `TCP_KEEPCNT=6`). Since these macros are part of the public API, using different numeric values may break source/binary compatibility for portable code that expects standard constants. Please verify whether these should align with the de facto Linux/POSIX values or be explicitly documented as Zephyr-specific.

Enabling keepalive by default changes TCP behavior for all existing applications and can introduce unexpected traffic, power usage, and disconnects on idle connections. Since this is a new feature, it would be safer to default this to `n` (or at least justify the default in the help text) so applications opt in explicitly.

`set_context_keepalive()` does not validate the input size before dereferencing `value`. Unlike the other keepalive setters in this hunk, this can read past the caller buffer or accept malformed option payloads. Please add a `len == sizeof(uint32_t)`/`sizeof(int)` check (matching the public API expectation) and return `-EINVAL` on mismatch.

This assumes `context->tcp` is always initialized. If a caller sets TCP options before the TCP control block exists (or on a non-TCP context), this will dereference `NULL`. Consider validating that the context is a TCP socket and that `context->tcp` is non-NULL before writing these fields, returning an error otherwise.

`TCP_OPT_NODELAY` is accepted here but handled as a no-op and still returns success. That makes the API misleading because callers cannot tell the option was ignored. If NODELAY is not implemented yet, this should probably return `-ENOTSUP`/`-EINVAL`; otherwise the actual state should be stored/applied.

Same concern as the setter path: `TCP_OPT_NODELAY` falls through as a no-op and `net_context_tcp_get_option()` returns success without populating `value`/`len`. This can leave callers reading uninitialized data. Please either implement the getter or return an explicit error for unsupported options.

Calling `tcp_conn_unref(conn)` here only drops a reference count when keepalive probes are exhausted, but it does not actively transition the socket/connection into a closed state or notify the application. That can leave the context in `NET_CONTEXT_CONNECTED` while the keepalive worker simply returns. RFC1122 keepalive failure should result in the connection being aborted/closed, so this path likely needs to invoke the normal TCP close/reset/error handling instead of just unref'ing the internal object.

This ACK check resets `keep_cur` for any ACK greater than `conn->seq - 1`, which is true for most normal ACK traffic, not specifically for responses to keepalive probes. That means ordinary ACKs can mask a dead peer and prevent `keep_cnt` from ever being reached. Consider resetting the keepalive failure counter only when the ACK actually acknowledges the keepalive probe (`SEG.ACK == SND.NXT`/probe-specific condition) or when valid peer traffic is received.

Adding `keepalive_timer` unconditionally increases the size of every `struct tcp`, even when `CONFIG_NET_TCP_KEEPALIVE` is disabled. Since the per-connection keepalive state below is already guarded by `CONFIG_NET_TCP_KEEPALIVE`, it would be more consistent to guard this timer as well to avoid unnecessary memory overhead on builds that do not enable keepalive.

This `SO_KEEPALIVE` branch is compiled in whenever `CONFIG_NET_TCP_KEEPALIVE` is enabled, but it is placed under the generic `SOL_SOCKET` handling and does not appear to verify that `ctx` is actually a TCP socket. As written, callers could potentially set `SO_KEEPALIVE` on UDP or other non-TCP contexts and get a misleading success path depending on lower-layer behavior. Please guard this with a protocol/type check (or return `ENOPROTOOPT` for non-TCP sockets) so the socket API behavior matches POSIX expectations.

Adding `IPPROTO_TCP` support here changes `getsockopt()` behavior for `TCP_KEEP*`, but there is no visible check in this switch that the underlying socket/context is actually TCP. If `zsock_getsockopt_ctx()` can be called for non-TCP sockets, these options should reject unsupported protocols with `ENOPROTOOPT` instead of delegating unconditionally.

The comment says `set conn->keep_idle`, but this path does not validate the user-supplied option length before passing `optval/optlen` down. For socket options like `TCP_KEEPIDLE`, `TCP_KEEPINTVL`, and `TCP_KEEPCNT`, it's worth explicitly checking that `optlen` matches the expected integer size and that `optval` is non-NULL, otherwise malformed callers may get inconsistent behavior depending on the lower layer implementation.

The wording here implies a strict suspend/resume pairing, but the API contract is still underspecified. Since this is a public header, it would help to document whether `resume()` is valid only after a prior `suspend()`, whether nested suspend calls are supported, and what happens if the backend was never enabled. Without that, callers may make assumptions that lead to inconsistent backend state.

Suspending the shell log backend here is not paired with a guaranteed resume on all exit paths. In this function, if `mcumgr_serial_tx_pkt()` fails before any subsequent non-mcumgr byte is processed, the backend can remain suspended indefinitely and effectively disable shell logging. Please restore the backend in this function after transmit completes (or in a scoped/RAII-style helper) so suspend/resume is balanced even on errors.

This new suspend path directly forces the backend state to `SHELL_LOG_BACKEND_DISABLED`, which is the same terminal state used by `z_shell_log_backend_disable()`. If suspend/resume is meant to be temporary, this can lose the previous state and incorrectly re-enable a backend that was intentionally disabled before MCUMGR activity. Consider introducing a distinct suspended state or preserving/restoring the prior state in the control block so `resume()` only undoes a matching suspend.

Removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable path is important for reassembly, but this change also means every extended advertising meta event is now treated as non-discardable at the H:4 transport layer. That can increase backpressure and make the behavior inconsistent with other transports if they still mark ext adv reports discardable. Please make sure the same policy is applied across the other HCI drivers / common RX paths, or document why H:4 is intentionally different.

Removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable set matches the new host-side reassembly requirement, but this changes behavior for the ESP32 transport specifically. Please verify that the RX path and event queue can now absorb bursts of extended advertising reports without overruns, since these events will no longer be dropped under pressure. If the transport still has tight buffering, we may need an explicit comment here explaining why extended reports must never be treated as discardable, to prevent future regressions.

Removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable path is important for reassembly, but this changes behavior for this controller-specific HCI transport in a non-obvious way. Please add a short comment here explaining that extended advertising reports must not be treated as discardable because dropping intermediate fragments can corrupt host-side reassembly by concatenating unrelated reports. Without that context, this looks like an accidental regression in event filtering.

Removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable path is important for reassembly, but this now makes the SPI transport treat extended advertising reports differently from legacy advertising reports. Please double-check that this behavior is intentional and documented here, because dropping only the final fragment would break host-side reassembly by concatenating unrelated reports. A short comment explaining why extended reports must no longer be marked discardable would help prevent this from being reintroduced later.

Removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable path is important for reassembly, but this change also means extended advertising events will now be allocated with the normal RX timeout/blocking behavior. Can we confirm that this is intentional for user channel mode and document it here? Otherwise this may introduce backpressure or dropped traffic in high-rate scanning scenarios compared to legacy advertising reports.

The doc comment has a grammar issue: `The reported data is a truncated.` reads awkwardly and may confuse API users. Consider rewording it to something like `The reported data is truncated.` or `The reported advertising data is truncated.` to match the meaning of the flag.

The default of `255` looks too small for the new reassembly behavior described in the PR/issue. With this setting enabled by default, any extended advertising payload larger than 255 bytes will still be truncated by the host, which undermines the goal of transparently reassembling long reports. Consider either increasing the default to the common maximum supported by the host/controller path, or making the help text more explicit that users must raise this value to benefit from long advertising data support.

The remaining capacity calculation looks wrong here. To determine how many bytes can still be appended to `ext_scan_buf`, this should subtract `ext_scan_buf->len` from `net_buf_max_len(ext_scan_buf)`, not `evt->length`. As written, if the buffer already contains reassembled fragments, `size_to_copy` can become too large and `net_buf_add_mem()` may overrun the available tailroom.

Removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable path changes flow-control behavior for extended advertising events at the H:4 transport layer. Since this PR adds host-side reassembly for fragmented extended reports, can you confirm that making these packets non-discardable is intentional and safe under RX pressure? Otherwise this may regress robustness by allowing large/interleaved extended advertising traffic to consume buffers where we previously dropped them when needed. A short comment here explaining the rationale would help, especially because legacy advertising reports are still treated differently.

Removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable set means these events will now be treated as non-discardable on ESP32. That matches the new host-side reassembly requirement, but it also changes flow-control behavior for this driver: under RX pressure, extended advertising reports can no longer be dropped even when incomplete. Please verify the ESP32 transport/controller can tolerate this without causing head-of-line blocking or starving other HCI traffic, and consider documenting why this exception is needed here so the behavior change is explicit.

Dropping `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable path changes the transport behavior for extended advertising events. With the new host-side reassembly, only fragmented/incomplete reports should be retained, but complete extended advertising reports are still high-rate scan traffic and were previously treated as discardable. As written, all LE Extended Advertising Report events now become non-discardable here, which can increase queue pressure/latency and may block on full buffers. Please verify this is intentional, or gate discardability on the report `data_status` instead of removing the event type entirely.

Removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable set makes sense for host-side reassembly, but this also changes rpmsg backpressure behavior: extended advertising reports can now no longer be dropped under load while legacy advertising reports still can. Please verify this is intentional and documented, since on systems with heavy scanning traffic this may increase the risk of queue buildup or starvation of other HCI traffic.

Removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable path changes behavior for extended advertising events on the SPI HCI transport. With the new host-side reassembly, these reports can still arrive as multiple fragments and at a high rate; if they are no longer treated as discardable, the RX thread may block instead of dropping them under memory pressure, which can stall processing of subsequent HCI traffic. If this is intentional, it would be good to document why extended advertising reports are now safe to keep/non-discardable here, or preserve discardability for incomplete fragments while only making fully reassembled reports non-discardable at a higher layer.

Removing `BT_HCI_EVT_LE_EXT_ADVERTISING_REPORT` from the discardable fast-path changes user channel behavior for extended advertising events: they will now be allocated with the normal timeout instead of `K_NO_WAIT`. Since this PR adds host-side reassembly, that may be intentional for the host stack, but `userchan` is also used to pass raw HCI traffic to userspace. Can you confirm this won't regress applications relying on non-blocking handling/dropping of LE Extended Advertising Report events under buffer pressure, or gate the change so it only affects the host reassembly path?

The doc comment has a grammar issue: `a truncated` should be `truncated` or `a truncated advertisement/report`. Since this is a public API comment, it would be good to make the wording precise and consistent with the rest of the Bluetooth terminology, e.g. `The reported data is truncated.`

The default of 255 looks too small for the feature being added here. With reassembly enabled, users may reasonably expect long extended advertising reports to be delivered intact, but this default will still truncate anything larger than the legacy-sized payload. Consider either increasing the default to match the common maximum supported by the host/controller path, or making the help text much more explicit that long advertising data will still be silently dropped unless this option is raised.

Using `k_malloc()` here introduces an unbounded, runtime allocation path in the HCI RX/scan handling logic. The issue description explicitly calls out configurable scan buffer sizing, but this list of fragmented advertisers can also grow without a bound and may fail under memory pressure. In the Bluetooth host path, Zephyr usually prefers fixed-size pools/slabs for deterministic behavior. Consider replacing this with a bounded container (e.g. slab/array controlled by Kconfig) and defining what happens when the limit is reached.

`init_fragmented_advertisers()` reinitializes the dlist head, but any previously allocated `fragmented_advertiser` nodes are not freed first. If `bt_scan_reset()` can be called after advertisers have been added, this leaks memory and also loses references to the old nodes. Please walk the list and `k_free()` existing entries before reinitializing it.

`size_to_copy` is declared as `uint8_t`, but both `evt->length` and `net_buf_max_len(...) - ext_scan_buf->len` can exceed 255 for long advertising data (the feature this PR is adding). This will wrap/truncate the computed copy length and break reassembly for buffers larger than 255 bytes. `size_to_copy` should be at least `uint16_t` or `size_t`.

Setting `CONFIG_BT_EXT_SCAN_BUF_SIZE` to 91 only exercises a very small reassembly window, while the feature and issue are specifically about receiving much longer advertising payloads (up to controller-supported long reports). With this value, the test may never cover multi-fragment accumulation beyond a single small fragment and could miss regressions in the actual long-data path. Consider increasing this buffer to a size that forces/permits real fragmented reassembly in the test, or add a comment explaining why 91 is sufficient for the intended coverage.

Using a fixed `k_msleep(10000)` makes this test very slow and potentially flaky, since it assumes the host's reassembly timeout is exactly below 10 seconds and ties the test runtime to wall-clock time. If the timeout is configurable or changes, this test may either become unnecessarily slow or stop validating the intended behavior. Consider deriving the wait from the relevant timeout configuration (with a small margin) or exposing a deterministic test hook instead of sleeping for 10 seconds.

This test is only constrained by `platform_allow`, but there is no explicit dependency on Bluetooth support. In Zephyr test metadata it's usually safer to add `depends_on: bluetooth` (or the relevant host capability) so the test is not selected in environments where the allowed platform exists but Bluetooth support is unavailable or misconfigured.

This still pushes ISO callback contexts into the shared `free_tx` FIFO used by ACL/L2CAP. The PR description says ISO should not consume any of L2CAP's buffers, but with a shared pool the separation is only by increasing the total count, not by isolating allocation. That means heavy ACL/L2CAP traffic can still starve ISO callbacks (and vice versa). If the goal is true independence, consider introducing a dedicated ISO TX context FIFO/pool and making ISO send paths allocate from that pool instead of `free_tx`.

Passing `emul->target` here changes the callback contract from the bus-specific emulator wrapper to the emulated device. That matches the PR intent, but this line assumes every registered emulator has a valid `target`. If `emul_init_for_bus(dev)` can discover legacy or malformed emulator entries without a bound device phandle, this will turn into a NULL dereference in the transfer path. Consider asserting or validating `emul->target` during registration/init so failures are caught earlier and with a clearer error.

This changes the callback contract from passing the bus-specific emulator (`struct spi_emul *`) to passing `emul->target` instead. That matches the PR goal, but it also means every `spi_emul_api::io` implementation and its function pointer typedef must already be updated consistently. If any existing implementation still expects a `struct spi_emul *`, this will compile incorrectly or break at runtime. Please make sure the SPI emulator API declaration and all in-tree users were migrated together, and consider adding an assertion here that `emul->target` is non-NULL before dereferencing it.

This macro now instantiates a concrete bus-emulator struct in the header based on `DT_ON_BUS(node_id, ...)`, but the fallback in `Z_EMUL_BUS()` expands to `-EINVAL`. If `node_id` is not on one of the supported buses, this produces invalid C in a type position (`static struct -EINVAL ...`) and the resulting error will be very opaque. Consider using a compile-time assertion or a dedicated invalid-token path that fails with a clearer message when an unsupported bus is used.

Using `DT_REG_ADDR(node_id)` here assumes the emulated device node itself always has a `reg` property matching the bus address/chip select. That aligns with the new phandle-based design only if every supported emulated device binding guarantees `reg`; otherwise this macro will break for devices whose bus-specific addressing is encoded differently or omitted. It may be safer to derive this from the referenced bus/device relationship explicitly or document/enforce the `reg` requirement for all emulator targets.

Renaming `parent` to `target` changes the meaning of this field, but the surrounding API in this header still talks about the owning/parent emulator (`const struct emul *emulator` is now passed into callbacks). If this pointer is meant to reference the top-level emulator object that owns the bus-specific node, `target` is a bit ambiguous and may be confused with the emulated device rather than the emulator wrapper. Please either keep the ownership-oriented naming consistent (`parent`/`owner`) or update the rest of the API/docs to clearly distinguish `target` from the emulated device phandle to avoid misuse.

Changing the callback signature from `struct i2c_emul *` to `const struct emul *` removes direct access to the bus-specific context, but this header still defines `struct i2c_emul` with bus-specific fields like `api` and `addr`. If existing transfer implementations or bus dispatch code still need those fields, this API change can break them or force awkward back-conversion. Please make sure the call sites are updated consistently and consider documenting/providing a helper to recover the bus-specific `i2c_emul` when needed, otherwise the new signature leaves the relationship between the generic emulator and the I2C bus node unclear.

Changing the callback signature from `struct spi_emul *` to `const struct emul *` is a breaking API change, but the surrounding SPI-emulator-specific context (`spi_config`, `spi_buf_set`) still implies this callback is invoked from the SPI bus layer. Please make sure the contract here is explicit about how implementations recover the SPI bus emulator state now (for example via `emulator->bus.spi` or equivalent) and update any helper/accessor APIs accordingly. Without that, existing implementations lose direct access to the `spi_emul` object and this header alone doesn't show a clear migration path.

This dereferences `emul->bus.i2c` unconditionally based only on `bus_type`. If the emulator was built without the corresponding bus support or the bus union member was not initialized, this can crash before we even reach the guarded registration switch below. It would be safer to either guard these assignments with the same `CONFIG_*_EMUL` checks used later, or validate that the selected bus pointer is non-NULL before writing `target`.

If `emul->init()` fails, the code still proceeds to register the emulator on the bus. That can leave a partially initialized emulator visible to the bus layer and make later failures harder to diagnose. Consider skipping the registration step (e.g. `continue`) when init returns non-zero.

This macro now passes `&api` into `EMUL_DEFINE`, but the call sites provide `bmi160_emul_api_spi` / `bmi160_emul_api_i2c`, which are declared under `#if BMI160_BUS_SPI` / `#if BMI160_BUS_I2C`. If a build enables only one bus variant, the other symbol may not exist, so this relies on the instantiation macros being perfectly gated. It would be safer to keep the API selection fully inside the bus-specific instantiation path (or add compile-time guards/asserts) to avoid accidental references to an undefined API symbol when Kconfig/DT combinations change.

This now pulls the emulator state from `espi_dev->data`, but previously it was derived from the specific emulator returned by `api->find_emul(...)`. If the eSPI controller can host multiple emulators/chip-selects, `espi_dev->data` is the controller driver's private data, not the selected emulator's `struct espi_host_emul_data`, so this can read the wrong object or even corrupt memory. Please keep using the emulator returned by `find_emul` (via its parent `struct emul`/`data`) rather than the bus device's `data` pointer here.

Dropping the `i2c_emul_register()` call here changes initialization from explicit registration to implicit registration via the new `EMUL_DEFINE(..., &bus_api)` path. If the core auto-registration is not guaranteed to have happened before this emulator is used, this will silently leave the emulator unregistered. Please make sure the new framework actually registers I2C emulators for all existing call sites, or keep/assert registration here until that behavior is enforced.

This fixes the immediate over-read by accounting for the 2-byte AD header, but it would be safer to derive the copy length from the remaining payload explicitly (for example, `MIN(len - offset, set_data->len - 2)`) and use that value consistently for both `memcpy` and `offset` updates. As written, correctness depends on earlier logic guaranteeing `set_data->len - 2 <= len`, which is easy to break in future refactors.
Minor typo in the documentation: `callling` should be `calling`. Since this is public API documentation, it's worth fixing to avoid carrying the typo into generated docs.

This now reuses the global `read_buf` from the work handler before calling `bt_gatt_notify_uuid()`. If `read_buf` is also used by the PACS read path or by another queued PAC notify work item, this introduces a race where the payload can be overwritten while notifications are being prepared/sent. Consider using a dedicated local/net_buf for notifications or otherwise serializing access to `read_buf`.

The return value from `get_pac_records()` is ignored here. If building the PAC payload fails, `read_buf.len`/`read_buf.data` may contain stale or invalid data and we will still notify clients. Please check the result and bail out (or log) on error before calling `bt_gatt_notify_uuid()`.

This comparison is intended to detect values larger than the representable positive `int32_t` range, but it uses `INT_MAX` instead of `INT32_MAX` while `ret` is explicitly `int32_t`. On platforms where `int` is not 32-bit, this can change behavior and reintroduce truncation/overflow issues. Using `INT32_MAX` here would make the bound match the destination type and the rest of the logic more precisely.

There appears to be a typo in this public enum constant: `BECK` should likely be `BACK` to match the Generic Audio Assigned Numbers naming and the surrounding constants (`TOP_BACK_LEFT`, `TOP_BACK_CENTER`). Since this is part of the public API, shipping the misspelled name will be hard to correct later without compatibility impact.

Using `enum bt_audio_location *` here is risky because the location is a bitmask and may combine multiple speaker positions. In C, enums are not guaranteed to be wide enough for all bitmask values, and this enum currently uses bits up to 27. A fixed-width integer type such as `uint32_t *` would be safer and more explicit for a flags field, especially in a public callback API.

Same concern as for `publish_location`: this value represents a flags/bitmask field rather than a single enum choice. Passing it as `enum bt_audio_location` can be misleading and may be ABI-sensitive across toolchains. Consider using `uint32_t` for the written location value in this public callback.

The new API doesn't document its return value or any validation constraints. Since this is a public header, callers need to know what errors to expect (e.g. invalid `type`, unsupported/invalid `location`, or notification failures). Please add an `@return` section and clarify whether `location` is a bitmask of `bt_audio_location` values or a single enum value, because the current signature/documentation can be read either way.

This changes the Kconfig symbol from `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITIES` to `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITY`. If the corresponding Kconfig option and all references were not renamed consistently, `capabilities.c` may stop being compiled entirely for existing configurations. Please verify that the singular symbol actually exists and that this isn't an accidental typo/regression.

Renaming this Kconfig symbol from `BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITIES` to `BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITY` is a breaking change for any existing `depends on`, `select`, or `#ifdef CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_CAPABILITIES` references outside this file. I only see the local dependency updated in this hunk, so please verify all in-tree references are migrated (and consider whether the old name should be kept as a compatibility alias) to avoid silently disabling related code or breaking downstream configs.

Removing `BT_PAC_SNK_LOC`/`BT_PAC_SRC_LOC` drops the existing Kconfig interface for setting initial PAC locations. If the new runtime-owned location state does not provide a replacement way to initialize these values from configuration, this becomes a behavior-breaking change for existing boards/apps that relied on these symbols. Please make sure there is a migration path (for example, a new init-time config symbol or documented API call during startup) and document the deprecation/removal in the Kconfig help or release notes.

This callback updates the stored location before attempting to notify listeners, but it always returns success even when `bt_audio_unicast_server_location_changed(type)` fails. That means a remote write can appear successful to the client while the required notification was not sent, which seems at odds with the issue this PR is addressing. Consider propagating the error (or at least distinguishing `-ENOTCONN`/`-EAGAIN`-style benign cases from real failures) so callers can react appropriately.
Minor style issue: this struct initializer entry is missing the trailing comma used by the surrounding entries. Adding it keeps the initializer diff-friendly and consistent with Zephyr style.

This takes the address of the temporary value returned by `sys_cpu_to_le32(location)`. If `sys_cpu_to_le32` is a macro/expression rather than an lvalue, this is not valid C and can also be fragile across toolchains. Please store the converted value in a local `uint32_t location_le` first, then pass `&location_le` to `bt_gatt_notify_uuid`.

Here `location` is declared as `uint32_t`, but `get_pac_loc()` expects an `enum bt_audio_location *`. Passing `&location` relies on enum and `uint32_t` having identical representation, which is not guaranteed in C and can trigger type-mismatch warnings or undefined behavior on some toolchains/ABIs. Consider using a local `enum bt_audio_location loc` here, validate/convert it to `uint32_t` afterward, and then notify with the converted LE value.

This mask is introduced in a public internal header but there is no indication here that `enum bt_audio_location` is limited to 28 bits by the spec. Since this macro can now be reused across translation units, it would be safer to either document why 28 is correct or derive/reuse an existing spec-defined constant if one already exists. Otherwise this becomes a magic number that is easy to misuse if the location enum or spec coverage changes.

This new public wrapper appears to be a thin pass-through to an internal PACS helper. That creates a layering concern: `unicast_server.c` now depends directly on `pacs_internal.h`, which couples the unicast server API to PACS internals. If this function is part of the public unicast server surface, consider exposing a non-internal PACS API instead, or moving the notification trigger to the layer that owns the location state, so we don't leak internal service dependencies across modules.

This looks like a copy/paste mistake: in the source PAC branch you're still passing `BT_AUDIO_SINK` to `bt_audio_capability_set_location()`. That means the source location is never updated, and if both PAC roles are enabled this second call will overwrite the sink location instead of configuring the source location. Please change this argument to `BT_AUDIO_SOURCE`.

This change drops the `const` qualifier from `source`, but `bt_audio_broadcast_source_get_base()` appears to be a read-only accessor that serializes BASE data into `base_buf`. Removing `const` unnecessarily weakens the API contract and can break existing callers that hold a `const struct bt_audio_broadcast_source *`. Unless this function now mutates the source object, it would be better to keep the parameter `const` for const-correctness and backward compatibility.

Setting `CONFIG_BT_ISO_MAX_CHAN=2` here couples the sample to exactly two BIS/ISO channels. Since this change is specifically adding subgroup support, the required channel count should match the total number of BIS streams the sample creates across all subgroups. If the sample logic is later extended to more than one stream per subgroup, this hard-coded value will become a hidden limit and can cause allocation/init failures. Consider deriving this from the sample's stream configuration or at least documenting that it must stay in sync with the total stream count.

This calculation silently truncates when the stream count is not evenly divisible by the subgroup count. In that case, the last `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_STREAM_COUNT % CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT` streams are never assigned to any subgroup, while the log message on line 119 still reports the full product. Please add a build-time/runtime check that the counts divide evenly, or distribute the remainder explicitly.

Changing `BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_COUNT` from `range 0 ...` to `range 1 ...` is a behavioral change, not just subgroup support. If any existing configurations intentionally set this to 0 to compile out broadcast sources while keeping `BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SOURCE` visible/selectable, this will now become invalid. Please confirm that 0 is no longer a supported/needed value, or document the migration impact in the Kconfig help/PR notes.

`streams_encoded` is never updated through the pointer passed into this helper. The current `streams_encoded++` only increments the local pointer variable, so the caller's offset remains unchanged and every subgroup after the first will read `source->stream_data` from the wrong index. This should likely be `(*streams_encoded)++` (and the parameter used when indexing `stream_data`) so the running stream offset is propagated back to `encode_base()`.

This array is still sized by `BROADCAST_STREAM_CNT`, but the new API introduces multiple subgroups. If `BROADCAST_STREAM_CNT` is a global stream limit rather than a per-subgroup limit, the comment here is misleading; if it is intended to be per-subgroup, this can under-allocate or over-allocate depending on how subgroup stream counts are handled. Please clarify the ownership/lifetime of this storage and whether it should be attached to each subgroup instead of the source-level object.

`subgroup_param` and `create_param` are stack-allocated and only partially initialized before being passed to `bt_audio_broadcast_source_create()`. If either struct contains additional fields now or in future API revisions, this can leak uninitialized data into the call and cause subtle behavior differences. Please zero-initialize these structs (e.g. with `memset` or designated initializers) before assigning the used members.

This new sink subgroup count is set to 1, but the surrounding comment only mentions the controller's stream limitation. Since this PR is specifically adding subgroup support, keeping the subgroup count pinned to 1 means this test configuration won't exercise the new multi-subgroup behavior at all. If that's intentional for controller-capability reasons, please add a clarifying comment; otherwise consider increasing the subgroup count in a dedicated test config so the new API surface is actually covered.

This indexes `stream_params` by subgroup index and then hard-codes `params_count = 1U`, which means only the first `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT` streams are actually assigned to subgroups. If the number of broadcast streams exceeds the subgroup count, the remaining streams are silently ignored, so the test no longer exercises all configured BIS streams. Consider distributing all `stream_params` across the subgroups (or at least asserting that the stream count matches the subgroup layout) so the test remains functionally complete for different Kconfig combinations.

This change drops the `const` qualifier from `source`, but the API name and documentation suggest this function only serializes the BASE into `base_buf` and should not need to mutate the broadcast source. Removing `const` unnecessarily weakens the contract and can break existing callers that hold a `const struct bt_audio_broadcast_source *`. If the implementation does not modify `source`, please keep the parameter `const`; otherwise the header/docs should explain why mutation is required.

Increasing the sample to 2 streams and 2 subgroups also increases the amount of advertising/ISO metadata and control state the sample needs to build. `CONFIG_MAIN_STACK_SIZE=2048` was added here, but this line only scales the TX buffer count. Please double-check that 4 buffers is sufficient for the new topology on all supported boards/configurations; otherwise the sample may fail at runtime with buffer exhaustion when both BISes are active.

This integer division silently drops any remainder. If `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_STREAM_COUNT` is not an exact multiple of `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_SUBGROUP_COUNT`, some streams will never be assigned to a subgroup, and if the subgroup count is 0 this will also divide by zero. Please add a build-time/runtime validation for these configuration values before using them here.

Changing the minimum from `0` to `1` is a behavioral change in Kconfig that can break existing configurations that intentionally disable broadcast sources by setting `CONFIG_BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SRC_COUNT=0` while keeping `BT_AUDIO_BROADCAST_SOURCE` enabled. If the intent is only to support multiple subgroups/stream-specific codec configs, consider whether `0` should remain allowed for backward compatibility, or document/migrate this change explicitly.

`streams_encoded` is never updated here because `streams_encoded++` only increments the local pointer, not the value it points to. As a result, every subgroup after the first will encode BIS-specific codec data starting from `source->stream_data[0]`, which can produce an incorrect BASE when multiple subgroups are present. This should increment the referenced counter instead (e.g. `(*streams_encoded)++`) and the indexing should use that running offset consistently.

This memcpy uses `stream_param->data_count` without any visible bounds check against the capacity of `source->stream_data[stream_count].data`. If a caller provides more BIS-specific codec entries than the destination array can hold, this will overflow. Please validate `data_count` against the destination's max element count before copying.

The state is fetched twice here: once into `broadcast_state` and again in the condition. Besides the redundant work, this can make the logged value inconsistent if state changes between calls. Reuse the cached `broadcast_state` in the condition.

Same issue as above: `broadcast_source_get_state(source)` is called again even though the result was already stored in `broadcast_state`. Using the cached value avoids duplicate traversal and guarantees the debug log matches the checked state.

This array is still sized by `BROADCAST_STREAM_CNT`, but the surrounding API change replaces the flat stream list with subgroup-based organization. If `BROADCAST_STREAM_CNT` is a global maximum rather than a per-subgroup limit, this can waste memory and makes the ownership/indexing of `stream_data` ambiguous once multiple subgroups are present. Consider storing the codec-specific stream data with each subgroup (or otherwise documenting the mapping explicitly) so the per-stream configuration stays aligned with the new subgroup model.

`subgroup_param` is a stack variable and only `params_count`, `params`, and `codec` are initialized before it is passed into `bt_audio_broadcast_source_create()`. If `struct bt_audio_broadcast_source_subgroup_param` has additional fields now or gains any later, they will contain indeterminate values here. Please zero-initialize `subgroup_param` (and ideally `create_param` as well) before assigning members to avoid passing garbage into the API.

`create_param` is also an uninitialized stack struct with only a subset of members assigned. Since this API was just expanded to support subgroup-based configuration, leaving any new/optional fields unset can lead to undefined behavior or fragile code if the struct evolves. Consider `memset(&create_param, 0, sizeof(create_param))` (or designated initialization) before populating it.

This test config now hard-codes the new subgroup count to 1, which keeps the existing single-stream setup building, but it also means the bsim audio test does not exercise the new multi-subgroup / per-stream codec configuration support introduced by this PR. If the intent is to validate the new API surface, consider adding or updating a dedicated test configuration with `*_SUBGROUP_COUNT > 1` (and matching stream setup) so regressions in subgroup handling are actually covered.

This only assigns a single `stream_param` to each subgroup (`params_count = 1U` and `params = &stream_params[i]`), so the test never exercises the new per-stream codec configuration support within a subgroup. As written, it creates one stream per subgroup, which can mask bugs in BASE generation for multiple BIS entries under the same subgroup. Consider grouping multiple `stream_params` into at least one subgroup and giving them distinct `data` values (e.g. left/right locations) so the test actually validates the feature introduced by this change.

Switching this guard to `CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS` matches the new Kconfig naming, but it also changes behavior for users still enabling the deprecated `CONFIG_PTHREAD_IPC`. Since this header advertises POSIX feature-test macros to applications, dropping the old symbol here can make `_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS` disappear during the deprecation window even though rwlock support is still configured via the legacy option. Please keep compatibility by checking both symbols (or ensure the old symbol `select`s the new one unconditionally).

Renaming the macro here without keeping a compatibility alias will break existing source that still uses `PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER`, and unlike Kconfig symbols there is no migration tooling for C identifiers. Since this PR is about standardizing/deprecating names, it would be safer to keep `#define PTHREAD_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER POSIX_RWLOCK_INITIALIZER` (possibly guarded with a deprecation note) for at least one release cycle.

Switching this `sysconf()` feature test to `CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS` is consistent with the Kconfig rename, but it may change behavior for users still enabling only the deprecated `CONFIG_PTHREAD_IPC`. Please make sure the deprecated symbol `select`s or otherwise maps to `CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS`; otherwise `_SC_READER_WRITER_LOCKS` could start reporting `-1L` even though rwlock support is still effectively enabled through the legacy option.

Switching the build guard from `CONFIG_PTHREAD_RWLOCK` to `CONFIG_POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS` is consistent with the Kconfig rename, but please verify backward compatibility during the deprecation window. If `CONFIG_PTHREAD_RWLOCK` can still be selected by out-of-tree users or older configs, `rwlock.c` may stop being compiled unless the old symbol `select`s/implies the new one. Consider keeping a temporary compatibility guard here or confirming the Kconfig layer guarantees the new symbol is enabled whenever the deprecated one is used.

The prompt string here doesn't match the symbol being introduced. `PTHREAD_RWLOCK` is mapped to `POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS`, but the description says `pthread_spinlock_t support [DEPRECATED]`, which is misleading and duplicates the next option. Please change the prompt/help text to refer to reader-writer locks (for example, `pthread_rwlock_t support [DEPRECATED]`) so menuconfig users see the correct deprecation mapping.

Defaulting this new symbol only from `POSIX_API` looks like a behavior change from the previous generated option, which effectively made rwlocks available whenever the POSIX pthread pieces were enabled. Since this PR is intended to standardize/rename Kconfig options, not change feature selection semantics, can we preserve compatibility by also selecting/defaulting this from the legacy symbol(s) or from the same lower-level dependency used before? Otherwise existing configs that rely on rwlocks transitively may silently lose `pthread_rwlock_*` support after migration.

This replacement looks inconsistent with the actual POSIX type and initializer naming. `pthread_rwlock_t` is the object type, but `POSIX_READER_WRITER_LOCKS_INITIALIZER` reads like a feature-test option rather than an initializer macro. If the goal is to keep this header test aligned with the standardized names introduced by this PR, please verify that this macro is actually defined as an initializer in `<pthread.h>`; otherwise this comment will document an invalid API and may confuse future implementers when rwlocks are added.

Removing the `mp->fs = NULL` assignment avoids the immediate NULL dereference in concurrent I/O paths, but it also changes the unmount contract: after `fs_unmount()` returns, callers can still observe a non-NULL `mp->fs` and potentially continue using a mount that has already been removed from the global list. If the intent is to make async unmount safe, this likely needs a stronger lifetime/state mechanism (for example, marking the mount as unmounting and synchronizing active operations) rather than simply leaving the filesystem pointer intact.

`best_diff_nsec` is initialized with `INT32_MAX`, but `diff_nsec` is a `uint64_t` holding nanosecond deltas. This caps the search to ~2.1s and can prevent any candidate from being selected when frame intervals are larger than that, leaving `match` unchanged. Consider initializing with `UINT64_MAX` (or `video_frmival_nsec()` of the first candidate) so all valid intervals can be compared correctly.

`video_frmival_nsec()` divides by `frmival->denominator` without any validation. Since this is a public inline helper in the API header, a zero denominator would trigger undefined behavior at every call site. Please either document that `denominator` must be non-zero and assert it here, or handle the invalid case explicitly.

This test CMakeLists only adds `src/video_common.c`, but the PR description introduces a new Twister scenario `tests/drivers/video/api/drivers.video.api`. If this scenario is expected to exercise the new virtual driver/API coverage, please verify that all required test sources are included here; otherwise the test may build without actually covering the intended cases.

Setting `CONFIG_VIDEO_BUFFER_POOL_NUM_MAX=1` makes the test configuration very restrictive and may hide issues in queueing/streaming paths that require more than one buffer in flight. If the new virtual RX/imager test exercises capture or pipeline behavior, consider using at least 2 buffers or documenting why a single buffer is sufficient for all API coverage here.

This assertion is duplicated on the next line with a more descriptive message. Keeping both doesn't add coverage and makes failures noisier; consider removing this bare `zassert_not_ok(ret)` and keeping only the message-bearing assertion.

Restricting this test to `native_sim` may prevent it from running in CI configurations where `native_sim` is unavailable or renamed (for example, `native_sim/native/64`). If the test only depends on the emulator introduced in this PR, consider using `integration_platforms` or a broader board selection instead of `platform_allow` so coverage is preserved across environments.

Setting this driver to `default y` means it will be enabled automatically whenever the matching devicetree node exists. For an emulator/skeleton-style driver, that can unexpectedly pull it into builds and increase image size or create conflicts in boards/tests that happen to define the compatible. Consider leaving this disabled by default and enabling it explicitly from test/sample configs instead.

This help text documents a hard dependency on the framebuffer being the first field of `dev->data` so another driver can `memcpy()` from it. That kind of layout coupling is brittle and easy to break during refactors. If this ABI is intentional, it would be safer to expose it through a shared struct/type or accessor rather than relying on field order documented only in Kconfig help.

Setting this symbol to `default y` means the RX emulator will be enabled automatically whenever its devicetree node exists and `VIDEO_EMUL_IMAGER` is selected. For an emulator/test helper driver, that can unexpectedly pull it into non-test builds. Consider making this opt-in (`default n`) or gating the default behind a test/emulation-specific condition so boards with the DT node don't enable it implicitly.

This casts the `void *value` pointer itself to an integer instead of dereferencing it. As written, the register will receive the pointer address truncated to `int`, not the control value provided by the caller. The same issue exists for the other control cases below. Please read the pointed-to value with the expected type (for example `*(int *)value` or a control-specific type) before passing it to `emul_imager_write_reg()`.

This self-initialization looks like a bug: `vbuf` is used to initialize itself, which leaves it indeterminate and can fail to compile depending on toolchain settings. It should be initialized to `NULL` before the loop, e.g. `struct video_buffer *vbuf = NULL;`.

Copying from `cfg->source_dev->data` assumes the source device's private `data` pointer is a raw frame buffer. That breaks driver encapsulation and is not guaranteed by the device model, so this emulator becomes tightly coupled to one specific source implementation. Could this use a defined API/shared buffer contract instead, or at least cast/access a known source-specific struct explicitly so the dependency is clear and type-safe?

There appears to be an unintended extra `child-binding` nesting here. In devicetree bindings, `child-binding` should usually be declared once, with its contents directly underneath. As written, consumers may need a grandchild node to match `video-interfaces.yaml`, which likely does not reflect the intended node structure for ports/endpoints. Consider removing the outer or inner duplicate so the binding shape matches the expected DT hierarchy.

This introduces a nested `child-binding`, which is unusual for graph-style endpoint bindings and may not match how devicetree children are validated elsewhere in Zephyr. If the intent is to describe ports/endpoints, consider aligning this with the existing video/graph binding structure (for example, a `ports` node with its own `child-binding`, then endpoint children beneath it). As written, this may either over-constrain the node layout or fail to validate the expected DTS hierarchy used by consumers.

Using `remote-endpoint-label = "application";` here looks inconsistent with the rest of the graph, where endpoints are linked to another declared endpoint label. If there is no matching endpoint node with label `application` in this overlay, the graph is incomplete and may fail validation or stop exercising the endpoint-linking logic in build/test coverage. Consider either adding the corresponding endpoint node or removing this link if the output is intentionally left unconnected.

Using `remote-endpoint-label = "application";` here looks suspicious because there is no matching endpoint label defined anywhere in this overlay. If the test relies on a complete media graph, this leaves the RX output endpoint dangling and may cause graph parsing or future validation logic to fail. Consider either defining the corresponding endpoint node in the overlay or omitting this link if the output is intentionally unconnected for the test.

Passing an integer control value via `(void *)30` is not portable and can break on architectures where pointer size/representation differs from `int`. The video control API expects a pointer to the value storage for scalar controls, so this should pass the address of a typed variable (for example `int exposure = 30; video_set_ctrl(..., &exposure)`) instead of casting the integer literal to `void *`.

Same issue here: casting the literal `30` to `void *` relies on implementation-defined behavior and doesn't match the usual control API contract of passing a pointer to the value. Please use a properly typed local variable and pass its address to avoid undefined/architecture-specific behavior in the test.

Using `#define unsigned signed` to transform `__SIZE_TYPE__` into a signed type is very fragile in a public core header. It relies on preprocessor token substitution over a compiler builtin expansion, can confuse tooling/static analysis, and may break if `__SIZE_TYPE__` is not a simple `unsigned ...` spelling. Since this type is intended to be a stable Zephyr-native ABI type, it would be safer to derive it explicitly from pointer width or introduce an arch/toolchain-provided signed-size typedef instead of this macro trick.

Adding this as a standalone `bool` makes it selectable on all architectures, but the help text says the default behavior is that `k_off_t` matches the architecture word size. Without an explicit `default` or dependency, the actual default is always `n`, which can diverge from that documented behavior on 64-bit targets if the type definition is wired only to this symbol. Please make the Kconfig semantics match the description (for example, `default y if 64BIT`) or clarify the help text to state that 64-bit offsets are opt-in even on 64-bit platforms.

Casting `offset` to `long` for `%lx` can truncate the value when `k_off_t` is wider than `long` (which is especially relevant given this PR adds support for 64-bit `k_off_t` on some targets). That would make the diagnostic misleading for large offsets. Please use a format specifier/helper that matches `k_off_t` explicitly, or cast to a guaranteed-wide unsigned type and print with the corresponding format macro.

Using `k_off_t` here removes the POSIX dependency, but this helper still computes `page_offset` with `%` and later uses `offset >> cfg->addr_width`. If a caller passes a negative offset, the modulo/shift behavior will produce invalid addressing and can underflow when converted into the I2C address bytes. Please make sure the public read/write paths reject negative offsets before reaching this helper, or add a guard here to enforce `offset >= 0`.

Casting only `offset` to `uintptr_t` still leaves the address arithmetic dependent on the type of `SOC_NV_FLASH_ADDR`. If that macro is a 32-bit integer, expressions like `SOC_NV_FLASH_ADDR + (uintptr_t)offset + i * FLASH_WRITE_BLOCK_SIZE` can still overflow before being converted to a pointer on targets where `k_off_t` is wider than the address type. To make the widening explicit and consistent, consider casting the base address to `uintptr_t` as well, e.g. `((uintptr_t)SOC_NV_FLASH_ADDR + (uintptr_t)offset)` before the final pointer cast.

Changing the local callback signatures to `k_off_t` is consistent with the layering fix, but please verify that the flash driver API function pointers in `struct flash_driver_api` were updated to the same type everywhere. If the interface still expects `off_t`, this will create an incompatible function pointer assignment at the driver API table and may only show up as a warning or subtle ABI issue depending on the toolchain.

Switching to `k_off_t` is consistent with the layering fix, but this function still mixes the offset type with `uint32_t`/address-sized arithmetic (`SOC_NV_FLASH_SIZE`, `len`, and later `SOC_NV_FLASH_ADDR + offset`). If `CONFIG_K_OFF_T_64BIT` is enabled, this can introduce signed/unsigned conversion issues or truncation on 32-bit targets. It would be safer to validate `offset >= 0` explicitly and ensure the range checks/arithmetic are done in a type wide enough for `k_off_t` before converting to 32-bit addresses.

Switching from `off_t` to `k_off_t` fixes the layering issue, but this function mixes the new offset type with `uint32_t` locals (`cur`, `next`) and later compares/adds them against `offset`. If `CONFIG_POSIX_DEVICE_IO_64_BIT` or any future configuration makes `k_off_t` wider than 32 bits, this can introduce truncation or incorrect range validation for large offsets. Consider updating the internal arithmetic and related parameters to use `k_off_t`/`size_t`-compatible types consistently.

Now that `offset` is `k_off_t`, the pointer calculation below (`SOC_NV_FLASH_ADDR + offset`) depends on implicit conversion to an address-sized integer. On 32-bit targets, a 64-bit `k_off_t` can be truncated here. It would be safer to validate/cast explicitly, or keep the arithmetic in a type guaranteed to match the address space after checking bounds.

Changing the API to `k_off_t` is good for layering, but this erase path still uses `uint32_t erase_offset` internally. If `k_off_t` is configured as 64-bit, offsets beyond 4 GiB will be mishandled or silently truncated. Please make the internal offset bookkeeping use the same width as the public parameter, or add an explicit guard that rejects unsupported large offsets.

This range check only verifies the upper bound. Since `k_off_t` is a signed type, negative offsets would still pass here (`offset + size` can remain <= flash size), and later pointer arithmetic in read/write/erase would compute an address before `CONFIG_FLASH_BASE_ADDRESS`. Please also reject `offset < 0` here (and ideally guard against signed overflow in `offset + size` as well).

Switching to `k_off_t` is consistent with the layering fix, but this driver forwards `offset` into vendor flash APIs and likely performs address arithmetic against hardware-defined address types. Since `k_off_t` may now be configurable to 64-bit, please verify that all downstream uses either accept this width or explicitly range-check/cast before passing the value on. Otherwise large offsets could be silently truncated on 32-bit platform/vendor interfaces.

Changing the API parameter to `k_off_t` is consistent with the layering fix, but this function still immediately narrows `offset` into `int v_addr = offset` (and also `size_t len` into `int v_size`). If `CONFIG_K_OFF_T_BITS=64` is enabled, larger offsets can be silently truncated here, which undermines the stated future-proofing for 64-bit offsets. Please either keep the internal variables in a width-compatible type or add explicit range checks before narrowing.

The format string no longer matches the argument types here. `len` is a `size_t`, but `%d` expects an `int`, and `offset` is now `k_off_t` while `%zx` expects a `size_t`-compatible unsigned value. This was already questionable before, but with the type migration it would be safer to use format specifiers that match the actual types (or cast to a known logging type) to avoid UB/truncation on platforms where these widths differ.

Please double-check the logging format here: `%zx` is for `size_t`, but the argument is `k_ssize_t` after this change. If `k_off_t`/`k_ssize_t` become 64-bit independently of `size_t` (as described in the PR), this debug print can misformat or truncate the offset. Consider using a format specifier/cast that is explicitly tied to the offset type.

The format string here still assumes `len` fits `%d`, but `len` is a `size_t`. This was already incorrect before the type migration, and on 64-bit targets it can truncate or trigger format warnings. Please switch this to `%zu` (or an equivalent logging-safe size_t format) while touching this code.

Casting `addr` to `k_ssize_t` and printing it with `%zx` is type-inconsistent: `%zx` expects a `size_t`/unsigned `z` argument, not a signed `k_ssize_t`. This can produce incorrect output or trigger format warnings depending on how `k_ssize_t` is defined. Since these are flash offsets/addresses and should be non-negative, it would be safer to log them as an unsigned size-compatible type (for example cast to `size_t`) or use a format specifier that matches `k_off_t` exactly.

The new cast does not match the format string here either: `%zx` expects an unsigned `size_t`, but `(k_ssize_t)addr` is signed. Please align the cast and format specifier to avoid UB/format-check issues, ideally by logging the offset as `size_t` if negative values are not expected.
Same format mismatch as above: `%zx` is for `size_t`, but the argument is cast to `k_ssize_t`. This should be updated to use a matching unsigned type/format pair.

There are two formatting issues on this line: `len` is a `size_t` but is printed with `%d`, and `offset` is cast to `k_ssize_t` while `%zx` expects `size_t`. Both should use matching format specifiers/casts (for example `%zu` for `len`, and an unsigned size-compatible type for `offset`) to avoid truncation or warnings on 64-bit builds.

This debug log still has mismatched printf types after the `k_off_t` conversion: `len` is `size_t` but uses `%d`, and `offset` is passed as `k_ssize_t` to `%zx`. Please switch to matching specifiers/casts to keep the logging portable and warning-free.

Same issue here: `size` is a `size_t` but logged with `%d`, and `addr` is cast to `k_ssize_t` while `%zx` expects `size_t`. This is especially worth fixing in common debug paths because it can hide real values on wider architectures.

The format string still uses `%zx`, which expects a `size_t`, but the argument is now cast to `k_ssize_t` (a signed type). This is a type/format mismatch and can produce incorrect output or trigger compiler warnings depending on how `k_ssize_t` is defined. Please switch to a format specifier that matches the signed offset type, or cast to the corresponding unsigned type if the intent is to print a hex address-like value.

Same issue here: `%zx` expects `size_t`, but `offset` is being passed as `k_ssize_t`. Since this is now using the native Zephyr signed type, the log format should be updated to match the actual argument type (or the cast should be changed to an unsigned type if hexadecimal formatting is required).

This log format is now inconsistent with the actual type conversion. `offset` is cast to `uint32_t`, but the message still uses `%lx`/`long` here and in the nearby verification/erase logs. Since this driver passes the offset to the QSPI IP as `uint32_t`, using a fixed-width hex format consistently (for example `0x%x` with `(uint32_t)offset`, or better `PRIx32`) would avoid misleading output and potential truncation/format mismatches when `k_off_t` is 64-bit.

Changing this implementation to `k_off_t` is consistent with the layering fix, but please verify that the corresponding public declaration in `include/zephyr/drivers/flash.h` and any syscall/marshalling metadata were updated as well. If the prototype still uses `off_t` anywhere, this will either break the build or create an ABI/API mismatch for out-of-tree users.

Changing `offset` to `k_off_t` can make it wider than 32 bits, but this range check still does `offset + size` directly against `FLASH_SIZE`. If `offset` is very large, the addition can overflow before the comparison, and if `k_off_t` is unsigned on some configurations the `offset >= 0` check becomes ineffective. Consider rewriting this as `offset <= FLASH_SIZE && size <= FLASH_SIZE - offset` (with appropriate casts / prior negativity check) so the validation remains correct across 32-bit and 64-bit `k_off_t` configurations.

Casting `k_off_t` directly to a pointer here can truncate the address on targets where `CONFIG_K_OFF_T_BITS=64` but `uintptr_t` is narrower. Since this driver stores memory-mapped flash addresses in `area_address`/`offset`, it would be safer to use an address-sized type for these fields/parameters (or add a compile-time assertion that `sizeof(k_off_t) <= sizeof(uintptr_t)`) rather than relying on `k_off_t` for pointer conversion.

Casting `_a` to `uintptr_t` before adding `CONFIG_FLASH_BASE_ADDRESS` is safer than doing arithmetic on a potentially signed offset, but this macro now relies on `uintptr_t` being available from the current include set. Please verify this file includes `<stdint.h>` (or another header that guarantees `uintptr_t`) on all supported toolchains, otherwise this layering cleanup may introduce a build break here.

Casting `info.start_offset` to `long` to match `%lx` can truncate the value on targets where `k_off_t` is wider than `long` (which this PR explicitly enables via configurable 64-bit offsets). This shell output should use a format specifier that is guaranteed to match `k_off_t`/its promoted type, or cast to a fixed-width type like `uint64_t` and print with the corresponding macro, otherwise page offsets may be displayed incorrectly on some configurations.

Switching from `off_t` to `k_off_t` fixes the layering issue, but this helper still mixes a potentially signed offset with an unsigned `size` and compares the sum against an unsigned flash-size expression. If `offset` is negative, the usual arithmetic conversions can turn it into a large unsigned value and make the check behave unexpectedly. Consider explicitly rejecting `offset < 0` before the addition, and ideally guard against overflow in `offset + size` as well.

Switching this helper to `k_off_t` is consistent with the layering fix, but please verify that all callers and related declarations in the STM32 flash driver now use the same type. In this header, `flash_stm32_write_range()` still takes `unsigned int offset`, which can reintroduce truncation or signedness inconsistencies if `k_off_t` is widened (especially with the new 64-bit option mentioned in the PR). It would be safer to keep the offset type uniform across the driver API surface.

Changing `addr` to `k_off_t` is consistent with the layering fix, but this helper compares the offset against flash sizes and likely performs arithmetic with `size_t`/device capacity fields. Please double-check that `ospi_address_is_valid()` explicitly rejects negative `addr` values before any mixed signed/unsigned arithmetic or casts; otherwise a negative `k_off_t` can be converted to a large unsigned value and incorrectly pass range checks or overflow intermediate calculations.

Casting only `addr` to `long` means the addition is still performed between `STM32_XSPI_BASE_ADDRESS` and a potentially wider/signed `k_off_t`, and only then converted for `%lx`. On 32-bit targets with `CONFIG_K_OFF_T_64BIT`, this can trigger truncation/sign-conversion warnings or hide overflow in the debug path. Consider casting both operands to an explicitly unsigned address-sized type (or using a pointer/address print helper) before the addition.

Same concern as above: with `addr` now being `k_off_t`, formatting the computed address via `%lx` after only casting `addr` to `long` is not robust across configurations, especially if `k_off_t` is 64-bit. Please cast the full expression to a well-defined unsigned address type (or use a dedicated address-format macro/helper) to avoid truncation and format mismatches.

Casting `offset` to `uintptr_t` before adding the flash base address is a good step, but the final cast to `flash_prg_t *` still converts an integer directly to a pointer. On targets where pointer width differs from `k_off_t`, this relies on implementation-defined behavior and can still truncate if `FLASH_STM32_BASE_ADDRESS` is not already pointer-sized. Consider performing the address computation entirely in `uintptr_t` (including the base address) and, if possible, validating that `k_off_t` cannot be negative before forming the pointer.

Casting `offset` through `uintptr_t` before adding the base address is the right direction, but this still relies on `FLASH_STM32_BASE_ADDRESS` participating safely in integer arithmetic. To avoid truncation/sign-conversion issues across 32/64-bit configurations—especially now that `k_off_t` may be wider—it would be safer to cast both operands to `uintptr_t` (or validate that `offset` is non-negative and in range before the cast) before forming the final address.

Casting `offset` to `uintptr_t` here avoids pointer arithmetic on a non-pointer type, but it also silently converts a potentially signed `k_off_t` to an unsigned address-sized integer. If a negative offset ever reaches this path, it will wrap to a large address and produce an invalid write. Since this function dereferences the computed address directly, it would be safer to either validate `offset >= 0` here or cast via an explicitly checked intermediate after `flash_stm32_valid_range()` guarantees non-negative offsets.

Casting `offset` to `uintptr_t` before adding the base address is a good step, but this still assumes `k_off_t` can be losslessly converted to an address-sized unsigned integer. With the new Kconfig allowing 64-bit `k_off_t` on 32-bit targets, a large offset could be silently truncated here and produce an invalid write address. Consider validating that `offset` is non-negative and fits in `uintptr_t` (or performing the range check in `flash_stm32_valid_range()` against the addressable flash window) before this conversion.

Casting `offset` to `uintptr_t` before the addition is a good step for decoupling from POSIX types, but this still silently truncates if `CONFIG_K_OFF_T_BITS=64` and `uintptr_t` is 32-bit. Since the PR explicitly adds support for 64-bit `k_off_t`, it would be safer to validate that the offset fits in the address space (or otherwise constrain this driver to addressable offsets) before converting it to a pointer-sized integer.

Casting `offset` to `uintptr_t` before adding the base address avoids the direct pointer cast from `k_off_t`, but it also silently reinterprets potentially negative offsets as large unsigned addresses. If `write_dword()` can ever be reached without a prior `flash_stm32_valid_range()` check, this becomes much harder to diagnose. Consider asserting or explicitly rejecting `offset < 0` here (or using a helper that guarantees a validated non-negative offset) so the type migration doesn't weaken the safety of the address calculation.

Casting `offset` to `uintptr_t` before adding the flash base address avoids pointer arithmetic on a signed offset type, but it also means negative `offset` values will wrap to a large unsigned address. This function appears to rely on callers having already validated the range; if that assumption is intentional, it would be safer to document it or add an assertion/check here so an invalid negative `k_off_t` cannot silently turn into an arbitrary flash pointer.

Casting `off_t` to `k_off_t` here can silently truncate or reinterpret the file offset if the two types differ in width or signedness (which this PR explicitly makes configurable). Since `disk_access_get_di()` also takes the same spinlock internally, the check/remove sequence is no longer atomic. Please perform the lookup/validation and removal while holding a single lock, or use a helper that assumes the lock is already held.

This new API is exposed unconditionally, but `apply_default_pin_state` is only meaningful for controllers that use pinctrl. Returning `-ENOSYS` here means callers now need to handle a runtime failure on platforms where the SPI driver simply doesn't implement the hook. Since the motivating use case is to let client drivers reliably restore the bus pins, consider whether this should instead have a generic fallback in the SPI core (for example, reapplying the controller's default pinctrl state when available) so the behavior is consistent across SPI drivers rather than depending on each backend to opt in.

Setting `TZ` here changes process-global state, which can leak into other tests in the same native process and make results order-dependent. Since this test only needs a deterministic timezone for the `ctime_r()` assertion, please save the previous `TZ` value and restore/unset it after the check (and call `tzset()` if needed on the host libc) so the test remains isolated.
